```

#### ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-07T16:46:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net...

I'm going to start with a suggestion that I originally had at the end:

Get copies of ANSI X3.23-1985 and ISO/IEC 1989:2002 and find out what's
actually in them.  I'm afraid you've consistently confused "conventional in
the implementations with which I'm familiar" with "standard".  If you're
indeed writing a book on Best Practices, I think you'd agree that the
investment in these two documents would reasonably be considered appropriate
to ensuring a quality product.  Go through everything you've written, and
check to see whether it's described in, and consistent with, the appropriate
standard.  This is particularly important for those things you describe as
standard in the first place.

Herewith some further comments:

> Basics, divisions and sections
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> display 'hello world'.

Standard '85 COBOL doesn't require ENVIRONMENT or DATA DIVISIONs, but does
require IDENTIFICATION and PROCEDURE.  And standard '02 COBOL requires
PROGRAM-ID even if it does allow the omission of the associated division
header.  PROGRAM-ID gets REALLY powerful in '02 COBOL.

> The data division has three sections-file, working-storage and
> linkage-in which you describe data structures. All three follow the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> passed by a calling program or dynamically allocated.  ...

Ignoring, of course, the fact that you describe the meaty details of FILEs
in the data division, and the fact that it also contains the REPORT and
COMMUNICATION SECTIONs of '85 COBOL, and the LOCAL-STORAGE and SCREEN
SECTIONs of '02 COBOL.  I know you're only talking about the ones you're
interested in, but there are more.

> Historically, Cobol was stored on punched cards and had rules saying
> columns 1-6 contained a sequence number, so you could sort the cards
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> rules went away eighteen years ago, in 1985,

Nope.  Still in '85 standard COBOL, still the default in '02 standard COBOL.
See prior message.  Overridable only in standards-compliant compilers issued
within the last ten weeks or so, or as a vendor-specific extension to
earlier ones.  The Column-72 location of Margin R did go away in '85, but
not the column locations of Margins L, C, A or B.    And Margin B (but not
Margins L, C or A, or the existence of Margin R) went away altogether in
fixed-form reference format in '02 standard COBOL.

>Position 1 is now for the asterisk formerly in column 7, line position 2
through a very big
> limit contains your program text.

Not  unless 1) ">>SOURCE FORMAT FREE" is in effect, 2) the compiler includes
both the Free-Form and Compiler Directive features of '02 COBOL, AND 3) the
"*" is followed immediately by ">".

>You can write comments to the right
> of program text by using *>.

True in '02 COBOL, but in '85 COBOL only with implementor-defined
extensions.

>Best Practice: use lower case and "free
> form", but avoid creating lines (much) longer than your text editor
> can display without scrolling.

Lower case is strictly a style issue from '85 COBOL onward; since then,
COBOL as a language is case-independent.  Free format source is only in '02
COBOL or earlier compilers that include it as an implementor-defined
extension (that fact should be getting pretty clear by now).

>
> Chapter 2
>
> Basics, data types and structures
>
 Strings:
>
> 01  a-string                           pic  x(30).     *> string
> 01  b-string                           pic  x(70).     *> string
>
> A Cobol string is fixed length, filled with spaces on the right.

Only sometimes.  STRING doesn't fill, as you point out later.  Whether and
how it's filled or not is not dependent on the USAGE, it's dependent on the
VERB.

> Convert Cobol to C
> string cobol-string delimited by '  '  low-value into c-string
> *> look for 2 spaces

Not in standard COBOL.  Use delimited by <2-character-data-name>.  This is
discussed further later on.

> .. and Pascal-style strings with an explicit length.
>     05  pascal-structure.     *> pascal-style string
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>      05  cobol-string               pic  x(30).

Are you sure *ALL* Pascal implementations comply with this?

> The rules for moving structure names are the same as
> strings-truncation when smaller, space-fill when larger.

Except when they're not ... See the rules for MOVE and the specific
exception involving group MOVEs.

> Numbers:
> 01  an-integer           comp-5  pic s9(09).     *> four bytes, 32 bits
> 01  half-word            comp-5  pic s9(04).     *> two bytes, 16 bits
> 01  quarter-word         comp-5  pic s9(02).     *> one byte, 8 bits
> 01  long-integer         comp-5  pic s9(15).     *> eight bytes, 64bits

Neither the '85 nor the '02 standard defines the internal format of COMP,
and COMP-5 is not in any of the standards at all.  These formats are always
defined by the implementor of the language for the particular intended
platform.

> These are integers stored in the machine's natural, highest-speed,
> format.

On some machines in some dialects of COBOL, maybe, but COMP-5 isn't
standard.  BINARY works pretty well on the machines I'm familiar with, and
it IS standard syntax.    But again the format is implementor defined.

> Although a 16-bit word can hold values to 32,767, your Cobol compiler
> may not allow values greater than 9,999 because pic 9(04) means four
> decimal digits.

It better, at least for DISPLAY and COMP.  If you add 1 to a PIC 9(4)
DISPLAY item containing 9999, you'd better end up with 0.  The rules about
the "odometer effect" are very, very basic to COBOL, and they'd better be
applied unless you're off in a vendor extension somewhere (like PIC 9 BINARY
EXTENDED, which allows values as high as 549,755,813,887 on any machine
worth its salt ... )

> All numbers stored in files should be this type. The number is in
> ASCII characters.

Unless it's not -- EBCDIC, for example, or a national character set, or ...

>  ... The sign is over the
> low-order digit as 'A' for +1 and 'J' for -1.

Unless it's not -- (all) the standards all allow the implementor to define
the default position and representation of the sign and allow the user to
override it with the SIGN clause for individual data declarations.

> The size limit of numbers is 15 digits plus a sign.

Nope.  '68, '74 and '85 COBOL have required implementors to allow at least
18 digits.  The '68 standard required support for at least 18 digits but
allowed more; the '74 standard made it clear that 18 digits were required
and that anything more was an extension.

>
> 01  packed-decimal-number      comp-3  pic s9(08)v9(07).
>
> Packed is an obsolete format where each digit and the sign occupy half
> a byte, called a nybble (cute, huh?).

Obsolete?  "PACKED DECIMAL" was introduced as a standard USAGE in '85 COBOL.
On some machines plain old COMP is defined as packed decimal.  On some
machines it's VERY efficient indeed.

> Historically, operations on
> packed numbers were faster than on display because machines offered
> hardware support. Modern machines do not, so there is no reason to use
> packed numbers.

Hardware support for things like decimal conversion and decimal arithmetic
(and for that matter, things like editing) is not necessarily dead!

> Indicators (flags):
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> false value.

What about USAGE BIT ('02 standard)?   Seems like it might have some sort of
use in "true-false" situations ...

> Pointers:

'02 COBOL only.  Not in '85 standard COBOL, and no guarantee that
implementations that support this syntax are compatible with each other.

> Pointers contain memory addresses, which are 32-bit.

Unless they're not.  The format of pointers is explicitly left to the
implementor by the '02 standard.

> Arrays:

The term used in the standard is "Tables".

> See pascal-structure above for a variable length array. Indexes, such
> as x-arrray, are useful only for searching. Historically, they were
> provided as a high-speed alternative to subscripts. Nowadays, linked
> lists, used extensively in the Demo, are faster and more flexible.

I doubt that.  Can you prove it mathematically in the general case?

> Floating-point:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Format is ANSI-standard, compatible with other programming languages.

Not in '85 standard COBOL, and not using that syntax in '02 COBOL, either.
FLOAT-SHORT, FLOAT-LONG, FLOAT-EXTENDED, maybe.  But even there the FORMAT
of these floating-point variables is ... wait for it ...
implementor-defined.

> move low-values to group-name            *> binary zeros, strings only

Most likely result, but not defined that way in the standard, and could be
different depending on the way the implementor handles character sets.

> move high-values to a                    *> FF, strings only

Machines and implementations that use an 8-bit character, maybe, but what
about 6-bit?  Internationalized programs?

>move x'0C' to a                          *> hex literal, strings only, will
be

Extension in '85 COBOL, uses different syntax in '02.

> compute a = (b * c) / d         *> receiving field must be number or
edited
> compute p rounded = (n * 100) / d
> compute b = a                   *> same as move a to b

... but COMPUTE has not only ROUNDED but ON SIZE ERROR, which MOVE lacks.


> add 1 to n
> subtract b from c
> multiply b by c
> divide b into c
> divide 7 into century-date giving week-number remainder da-of-week

> Note that the receiving field is right-most word in all cases.

No, not in the last case.  TO, GIVING, INTO.  It is true that MULTIPLY A BY
B puts the result in B, which is why it's probably better to use GIVING.  If
the compiler's not smart enough to treat MULTIPLY A BY B GIVING B the same
as MULTIPLY A BY B, then you need to yell at your vendor.

>Cobol offers other syntax that reverses word order. Use these forms only.

Why?  I'd say it's more useful to use the forms that are CLEAR TO YOU AS A
PROGRAMMER.   Particularly for MULTIPLY I don't think the CORRECT behavior
is OBVIOUS.

> Generally, compute is preferred over elemental arithmetic with the
> exception of 'add 1', simple addition to an accumulator and cases
> where you need the remainder. Compare Cobol's 'add a-number to total'
> to other languages' 'total = total + a-number'. Which is more verbose?

Which is clearer?  I tend to prefer COMPUTE only in cases where I have an
arithmetic expression that involves more than one arithmetic operation.

> Evaluate is Cobol's 'case statement'. It is used in place of if when
> there is more than one condition. Using it avoids inappropriate
> nesting of ifs. ...

Evaluate is actually much more powerful than e.g., Pascal or ALGOL's CASE.

> There are two types of perform: in-line loop control and a 'call' to
> another paragraph.

I think lumping "PERFORM" with "CALL" is misleading.

>In the old days, calling another paragraph was the only form.

Unless you wanted to call another program (introduced in '74 COBOL).  How
old is old?

In-line PERFORM was introduced in the '85 standard.

>Nowadays, loop control is the predominant use. Using
> perform to 'call' a paragraph is weak, like Basic's GOSUB, because it
> doesn't permit you to pass parameters. It is preferable to use the
> call verb to call local functions.

Arguable.  Depends entirely on the implementation.

>> call 'program' using a, b, c

> Passing control and parameters to another program, statically or
> dynamically linked, written in Cobol or another language. Can also be
> used to call a local function, similar to perform with the addition of
> parameters. Chapter 6 and the Demo show how to do this.

Some machines don't do "static linking" for this.  Neither '74, '85 nor '02
COBOL standards specify how programs in a run unit are linked together.

>> move spaces to a-string
> string 'Error ' error-code delimited by size into a-string *> compose
'Error xx'
> string city delimited by '  ' ', ' state space zip delimited by size
> into a-string
> *> the above delimiter is two spaces
> string cobol-string delimited by '  ', low-value into c-string

The second STRING will step on the information put into a-string.  Don't you
want a WITH POINTER clause here?

DELIMITED BY syntax allows a single delimiter; DELIMITED BY "  " (where the
literal is two spaces) is OK, but "DELIMITED BY " ", " " (two one-space
literals) is not.  Neither is "DELIMITED BY " ", LOW-VALUE.  Try DELIMITED
BY a two-character data item containing the desired value.  Or, if your
implementation allows it (it's not standard), DELIMITED BY x"2000", or
whatever syntax it uses for hex literals.

 > move spaces to input-words
> unstring input-line delimited by all space into
>     input-word (1) input-word (2) input-word (3) ...
> *> all space means multiple spaces are treated as one delimiter

Nope.  Standard disallows "ALL" before any sort of literal -- figurative or
otherwise -- in this context.  ALL <dataname>, yes; ALL <literal>, no, ALL
SPACE, no.

> Neither string nor unstring fills its output with spaces; you must
> initialize.

So MOVE is better than STRING or UNSTRING?  I think that's a bit of a broad
brush!

And do you mean the INITIALIZE verb?  I'd expect it'd most likely step on
the results, unless you mean *pre*-initialize.  Why not UNSTRING ... WITH
POINTER followed by a STRING SPACES WITH POINTER?

> Unstring lacks power. For instance, it cannot preserve quoted
> sub-strings. For non-trivial use, you'll wind up writing your own
> parser, as I did in the Demo Program. Unstring is useful for
> converting a null-terminated C-style string to a space-filled
> Cobol-style string.

Well, I just don't know what to do with that.

>inspect a-string converting
>     'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ' to
>     'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'

> Inspect is an overly complex verb which isn't particularly useful.
> You can program its functions yourself with more confidence they will
> work as desired.

I've used it often to good, and more importantly, contextually clear effect.

> It has one useful function: translating one character
> set to another.

If you're talking about your example of INSPECT ... CONVERTING, only in '02
COBOL and implementations that have extended their '85 compilers beyond the
applicable standard to include it.

>On older compilers, this function was the Transform verb.

Never heard of TRANSFORM.  Which standard was that in?  I can't find it in
'68, '74, '85 or '02.   Did find '68 COBOL's EXAMINE being replaced by the
more powerful INSPECT in '74 COBOL though.

 > goback
>
> This is how you exit, returning to the calling program or operating
> system. Goback replaces 'stop run' and 'exit program'.

Not in *standard* '85 COBOL, though many (by no means all!) implementations
provide it.

> Chapter 4

...

Well, I think I've put about as much effort into this as I can afford right
now.  See my suggestion at the beginning.

     -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T16:16:21+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b21srq$bt0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

>> add 1 to n
>> subtract b from c
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> No, not in the last case.  TO, GIVING, INTO.  

Forget the REMAINDER clause ..

> It is true that MULTIPLY A BY
> B puts the result in B, which is why it's probably better to use GIVING. 

Originally Cobol was designed to use the final word as the destination as 
this made it easier to remember:

     MOVE N         TO R
     ADD N          TO R
     SUBTRACT N   FROM R
     MULTIPLY N     BY R
     DIVIDE N     INTO R

adding a GIVING retained this convention, the result was still the last 
word:

     MULTIPLY N      BY M    GIVING R

It was was almost compromised when users wanted a DIVIDE BY, and this is 
why this must always be

     DIVIDE N   BY M  GIVING R

You cannot have DIVIDE BY without a GIVING, did you not know why this was ?

> If the compiler's not smart enough to treat MULTIPLY A BY B GIVING B the
> same as MULTIPLY A BY B, then you need to yell at your vendor.

There is more to the design of the language than just whether they 
accidentally choose the 'right' way to do it.

>>Cobol offers other syntax that reverses word order. Use these forms only.
> 
> Why?  I'd say it's more useful to use the forms that are CLEAR TO YOU AS A
> PROGRAMMER.   Particularly for MULTIPLY I don't think the CORRECT behavior
> is OBVIOUS.

Once you understand the uniform rule for the original Cobol design it gets 
easier.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T06:07:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e449e82.203517369@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b21srq$bt0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

>Once you understand the uniform rule for the original Cobol design it gets 
>easier.

The universal rule involves word order, which for Cobol is verb-subject-object
(VSO). 


People who design programming languages should have at a least a litttle
background in linquistics. Computer Science geeks who design languages have
heard of Noam Chomsky, but not read any of his books, especially his seminal
Syntactic Structures. As a result, we're stuck with 'techie' languages which
just don't get it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T16:43:59+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b21ufk$cl3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

>>> move spaces to a-string
>> string 'Error ' error-code delimited by size into a-string *> compose
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> string cobol-string delimited by '  ', low-value into c-string
 
> DELIMITED BY syntax allows a single delimiter; DELIMITED BY "  " (where
> the literal is two spaces) is OK, but "DELIMITED BY " ", " " (two
> one-space
> literals) is not.  Neither is "DELIMITED BY " ", LOW-VALUE.  Try DELIMITED
> BY a two-character data item containing the desired value.  

No.  You misread the statement, there is only one delimiter specified, the 
', ' is a literal to output:

    string city      delimited by '  ' 
           ', ' 
           state 
           space 
           zip       delimited by size
        into a-string

will result in: "NEW YORK, NY 12345" for example.

     string cobol-string delimited by '  '
            low-value 
         into c-string

will result in a nul (low-value) terminated value in c-string.

>  > move spaces to input-words
>> unstring input-line delimited by all space into
>>     input-word (1) input-word (2) input-word (3) ...
>> *> all space means multiple spaces are treated as one delimiter
 
> Nope.  Standard disallows "ALL" before any sort of literal -- figurative
> or
> otherwise -- in this context.  ALL <dataname>, yes; ALL <literal>, no, ALL
> SPACE, no.

Not according to my references:  

    UNSTRING identifier-1
          DELIMITED BY [ALL] {identifier-2} OR [ALL] {..
                             {literal-1   }
          INTO ..

>> Neither string nor unstring fills its output with spaces; you must
>> initialize.
> 
> So MOVE is better than STRING or UNSTRING?  I think that's a bit of a
> broad brush!

In what way did he say 'better' ?  I only saw 'different'.

> And do you mean the INITIALIZE verb?  I'd expect it'd most likely step on
> the results, unless you mean *pre*-initialize.  

Given that 'initialize' implies 'at the start' then why would you think 
that he would be 'initializing' afterwards (as in "step on results") ?

> Why not UNSTRING ... WITH
> POINTER followed by a STRING SPACES WITH POINTER?

I don't that would be particularly useful way of doing it, the UNSTING 
POINTER is the position in the source string, you seem to want to use it on 
the destination.

Did you mean ? :
             UNSTRING ... 
                INTO this COUNT IN this-size
             STRING SPACES 
                 INTO this WITH POINTER this-size
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T08:55:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b28lh6$17se$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b21ufk$cl3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:b21ufk$cl3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
> No.  You misread the statement, there is only one delimiter specified, the
> ', ' is a literal to output:

Right.  I did misunderstand the intent.

> > Nope.  Standard disallows "ALL" before any sort of literal -- figurative
> > or
> > otherwise -- in this context.  ALL <dataname>, yes; ALL <literal>, no,
ALL
> > SPACE, no.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>           INTO ..

I don't know what your references are.  My reference on this particular
subject is ANSI X3.23-1985 Page VI-136, 6.27, "THE UNSTRING STATEMENT",
Syntax Rule 1, which states:  "Literal-1 and literal-2 must be nonnumeric
literals and neither can be a figurative constant that begins with ALL."   I
can't see how that can be read as allowing UNSTRING ... DELIMITED BY ALL
SPACES ... as standard COBOL.  When a figurative is used (without ALL,
obviously) in this context, it stands for a single character (General Rule
8), but with ALL, how many characters would be considered to satisfy the
criterion?   If two is uncondionally sufficient, then an alphanumeric
literal "  " works fine.  If what you're really looking for is "delimited by
trailing spaces", then the REVERSE function provides this capability in
standards-conformant COBOL (the intrinsics function amendment is mandatory
for conformance to '85 COBOL, and of course the functionality remains in
2002 COBOL).

> > And do you mean the INITIALIZE verb?  I'd expect it'd most likely step
on
> > the results, unless you mean *pre*-initialize.
>
> Given that 'initialize' implies 'at the start' then why would you think
> that he would be 'initializing' afterwards (as in "step on results") ?

"INITIALIZE" is a verb that may be used anywhere in the program, and he was
indicating the necessity to pre-initialize the destination (say, to spaces)
before the two verbs within the program.

>
> > Why not UNSTRING ... WITH
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> POINTER is the position in the source string, you seem to want to use it
on
> the destination.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, probably; point being, it's doable.  Some vendors also have extensions
that make this some easier:  "FOR <numeric-size>" in both verbs, for
example.  But I agree they're not standard COBOL.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-11T06:49:16+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b28opc$1gh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b21ufk$cl3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b28lh6$17se$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

>> Not according to my references:
>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 2002 COBOL).

An optional ALL is part of the syntax.  A literal (as in literal-1) may be 
"ALL SPACES" for example.  You may not use ALL in the literal to get:

       DELIMITED BY ALL ALL SPACES

where the first ALL is part of the syntax and the 2nd ALL is part of the 
literal.  Hence they ban literals of the form 'figurative constant that 
begins with ALL'.

So DELIMITED BY ALL SPACE or DELIMITED BY ALL ", " or DELIMITED BY ALL 
Separator are available in the standard.

With this ALL any numbe of adjacent delimiters are traeated as one.
 
> "INITIALIZE" is a verb that may be used anywhere in the program, 

But it is also an english word that has a root that means 'at the start'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T13:28:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v4fr768umqbbd2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b21ufk$cl3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b28lh6$17se$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

Chuck Stevens <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:b28lh6$17se$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> "Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:b21ufk$cl3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
> > No.  You misread the statement, there is only one delimiter specified,
the
> > ', ' is a literal to output:
>
> Right.  I did misunderstand the intent.
>
> > > Nope.  Standard disallows "ALL" before any sort of literal --
figurative
> > > or
> > > otherwise -- in this context.  ALL <dataname>, yes; ALL <literal>, no,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> literals and neither can be a figurative constant that begins with ALL."
I
> can't see how that can be read as allowing UNSTRING ... DELIMITED BY ALL
> SPACES ... as standard COBOL.

My understanding is that both SPACE and ALL SPACE are
figurative constants. Bcause the format for UNSTRING uses
ALL, those figurative constants that begin with ALL are
prohibited. Essentially, the rule is stating that ALL ALL SPACE
may not occur in an UNSTRING statement; but ALL SPACE may.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T11:56:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2905k$1fcm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b21ufk$cl3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b28lh6$17se$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
My apologies to the readership.

In response to Richard Plinston, I wrote:

> I don't know what your references are.  My reference on this particular
> subject is ANSI X3.23-1985 Page VI-136, 6.27, "THE UNSTRING STATEMENT",
> Syntax Rule 1, which states:  "Literal-1 and literal-2 must be nonnumeric
> literals and neither can be a figurative constant that begins with ALL."
...

Bill Klein pointed out to me (very kindly and diplomatically, I might add!)
that what is actually nonconforming is "UNSTRING ... ALL ALL ...

The first "ALL" is part of the UNSTRING syntax, not part of the literal.
And that, for what it's worth, means that in the fragment
    UNSTRING ... DELIMITED BY ALL "Z"
'ALL "Z" ' is NOT treated as a figurative constant.

UNSTRING ... DELIMITED BY ALL SPACES is indeed allowed by the standards.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

```

major snip

> >On older compilers, this function was the Transform verb.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


    I suspect that transform was a case of IBM feeling obliged to use
an interesting assembler verb.  In IBM assembler (at least back in 360/370
days)
Transform was an assembler verb.  Not macro - just assembler.  Amounts to
using each byte as an index into a 256 byte lookup table.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T18:32:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e454bcb.247885055@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <daadnWOAhPGeXdmjXTWcog@comcast.com>`

```
"Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@comcast.net> wrote:

>
>major snip
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>using each byte as an index into a 256 byte lookup table.

Correct. Intel has the same instruction.

IBM also had Translate and Test (TRT), whose major use was not translation at
all but rather scanning for a delimiter. Intel has a similar instruction in
'repne scasb', which at least one COBOL compiler -- Realia -- uses when you
SEARCH an array of bytes looking for equal to a single value. It's very fast.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

language instruction is TR or TRANSLATE not TRANSFORM.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T23:56:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E45DF61.ED670FC@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <daadnWOAhPGeXdmjXTWcog@comcast.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
<snip> 
>     I suspect that transform was a case of IBM feeling obliged to use
> an interesting assembler verb.  In IBM assembler (at least back in 360/370
> days)
> Transform was an assembler verb.  Not macro - just assembler.  Amounts to
> using each byte as an index into a 256 byte lookup table.

Well, I don't exactly know where you get this. The "VERB" in
BAL or ALC was TR for TRanslate or TRT for TRanslate & Test.
In both cases, this is a MNEMONIC representing a specific
OPCODE.

TR was used to translate from ASCII to EBCDIC or vice versa
(as just ONE example).

TRT was used to locate a particular character(s) (say you
wanted to parse/scan some text and wanted to find all the
delimiters) by making that particular offset into the table
contain other than x'00'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>

```
Russell Styles wrote:
> major snip
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> using each byte as an index into a 256 byte lookup table.

I suspect IBM added this to their mid-60's compilers as a conversion aid 
so people upgrading to S/360's could easily translate BCD into EBCDIC.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)

"Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@comcast.net> wrote in message news:<daadnWOAhPGeXdmjXTWcog@comcast.com>...
>     I suspect that transform was a case of IBM feeling obliged to use
> an interesting assembler verb.  In IBM assembler (at least back in 360/370
> days)
> Transform was an assembler verb.  Not macro - just assembler.  Amounts to
> using each byte as an index into a 256 byte lookup table.


Actually the assembler opcode is Translate, aka TR or x'DC', not Transform.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T18:22:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e453b28.243625521@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>standard in the first place.

I would if the standards were available electronically. I'm a mobile contract
programmer who cannot transport a library of paper every time I move, about once
a year. Why do the cobbler's children have no shoes? 

When I'm not certain a feature is in the Standard, I'll change references from
"the standard" to "standard practice". Free-form is a good example. I've been
using it since 1984.

>> The data division has three sections-file, working-storage and
>> linkage-in which you describe data structures. All three follow the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>in the data division...

They have evaporated over the years. Nowadays, it's fd file-name (period). 

>and the fact that it also contains the REPORT and
>COMMUNICATION SECTIONs of '85 COBOL, and the LOCAL-STORAGE and SCREEN
>SECTIONs of '02 COBOL.  I know you're only talking about the ones you're
>interested in, but there are more.

Searching tens of thousands of programs found no use of those at Merrill Lynch,
Sears (both mainframe), US Dept of Education (Unix) and other IT shops large and
small. As I said in the introduction, this tutorial pares COBOL down to
essentials by omitting features not used in practice. 

>Lower case is strictly a style issue from '85 COBOL onward; since then,
>COBOL as a language is case-independent.  Free format source is only in '02
>COBOL or earlier compilers that include it as an implementor-defined
>extension (that fact should be getting pretty clear by now).

We users desire standards to minimize implementor-defined features. We generally
avoid features that are different between compilers.

>> A Cobol string is fixed length, filled with spaces on the right.
>
>Only sometimes.  STRING doesn't fill, as you point out later.  Whether and
>how it's filled or not is not dependent on the USAGE, it's dependent on the
>VERB.

The statement is valid in a tutorial introducing neophytes to COBOL. 

>> Convert Cobol to C
>> string cobol-string delimited by '  '  low-value into c-string
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>discussed further later on.

In the discussion below, you say '  ' (two spaces) is valid.

>> .. and Pascal-style strings with an explicit length.
>>     05  pascal-structure.     *> pascal-style string
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Are you sure *ALL* Pascal implementations comply with this?

No. I'll change "Pascal" to "variable-length strings".


>Neither the '85 nor the '02 standard defines the internal format of COMP,
>and COMP-5 is not in any of the standards at all.  These formats are always
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>it IS standard syntax.    But again the format is implementor defined.

COMP-5 means 'native' on all mainstream compilers. As you point out, COMP and
BINARY are interpreted differently by various compilers. De-facto standards are
useful knowledge for the practitioner. 

>> All numbers stored in files should be this type. The number is in
>> ASCII characters.
>
>Unless it's not -- EBCDIC, for example, or a national character set, or ...

Whoops. Change "ASCII" to "displayable".

>>  ... The sign is over the
>> low-order digit as 'A' for +1 and 'J' for -1.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>override it with the SIGN clause for individual data declarations.

Sign over low-order digit is another de-facto standard.

>> The size limit of numbers is 15 digits plus a sign.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>and that anything more was an extension.

You are correct. Thanks for catching the error. 

The limit of 15 was for packed on a mainframe, because machine language
instructions provided four bits for the length plus sign.

>> 01  packed-decimal-number      comp-3  pic s9(08)v9(07).
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>machines it's VERY efficient indeed.

I'll change the text to say packed is efficient on mainframes and possibly other
machines designed years ago. 

>What about USAGE BIT ('02 standard)?   Seems like it might have some sort of
>use in "true-false" situations ...

I'll clarify that this document describes '85 COBOL, not the '02 standard. 

>> Pointers:
>
>'02 COBOL only.  Not in '85 standard COBOL, and no guarantee that
>implementations that support this syntax are compatible with each other.

In practice, Micro Focus, IBM and Fujitsu (at least) use exactly the same syntax
in their '85 compilers. That's useful knowledge for the practitioner. 

>> Pointers contain memory addresses, which are 32-bit.
>
>Unless they're not.  The format of pointers is explicitly left to the
>implementor by the '02 standard.

Right. I'll change "are 32-bit" to "are usually 32-bit on today's machines. In
the future, the size will increase to 64-bit and beyond."

>> See pascal-structure above for a variable length array. Indexes, such
>> as x-arrray, are useful only for searching. Historically, they were
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I doubt that.  Can you prove it mathematically in the general case?

Of course not. Speed depends on efficiency of memory managers -- both hardware
and software. But knowing that C and other languages use pointers so heavily,
it's a safe bet that machines and operating systems will be optimized to handle
pointers well. 

Allocating tables of worst-case size also rests on assumptions about memory
managers' ability to swap out the unused portions. If that doesn't happen, a
high paging rate couldl negate any apparent efficiency. 

>> Floating-point:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>implementor-defined.

This is another discrepancy between imprecise ('flexible') standards and
real-world practice. The title of the paper is Best *Practices*.

>> move low-values to group-name            *> binary zeros, strings only
>
>Most likely result, but not defined that way in the standard, and could be
>different depending on the way the implementor handles character sets.

Burroughs used 'the COBOL character set', in which low-values were spaces and
high-values were EBCDIC "9". I cursed theoriticians every time I had to find a
work-around. 

>> move high-values to a                    *> FF, strings only
>
>Machines and implementations that use an 8-bit character, maybe, but what
>about 6-bit?  Internationalized programs?

6-bit? Has IBM reintroduced the 1401? 

>... but COMPUTE has not only ROUNDED but ON SIZE ERROR, which MOVE lacks.

Good point. I'll add your amplification.

>>Cobol offers other syntax that reverses word order. Use these forms only.
>
>Why?  I'd say it's more useful to use the forms that are CLEAR TO YOU AS A
>PROGRAMMER.   Particularly for MULTIPLY I don't think the CORRECT behavior
>is OBVIOUS.

The test is not what's clear to you, it's what will be clear to the NEXT
programmer who has to understand your code. A program must communicate to the
compiler, to yourself over time and to other programmers. The latter is COBOL's
strongest virtue, compared to other languages.

Consistant verb-subject-object word order improves COBOL's clarity. We say 'move
subject to object', where the receiving field is rightmost. 'multiply a by b' is
consistant with that word order. 

>> There are two types of perform: in-line loop control and a 'call' to
>> another paragraph.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>old is old?

My comment about calling another paragraph was in the context of PERFORM; it was
not in a discussion of the CALL verb. 

I think the comparison is appropriate. Every PERFORM could be replaced by a CALL
to a local function. If the function needs access to a file, one must move the
file and all operations on it into a single function, because files cannot be
passed as parameters.

>> Passing control and parameters to another program, statically or
>> dynamically linked, written in Cobol or another language. Can also be
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>COBOL standards specify how programs in a run unit are linked together.

Agreed. Most compilers do the 'linking' internally, which explains why the
function must be nested. If the compiler produced an EXTERN, the function could
be called by outside programs, thereby violating the spirit of local. 

>>> move spaces to a-string
>> string 'Error ' error-code delimited by size into a-string *> compose
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>want a WITH POINTER clause here?

They were two unrelated examples. I'll put a separator between them. 

I didn't want to convuse the reader with two meanings of the word POINTER.  In
practice, I seldom use WITH POINTER. If a string cannot be built with a single
STRING, I prefer to roll my own using subscripts. The Demo does so in
word-function, with code that could NOT be replaced by an UNSTRING verb because
it preserves quoted strings as a single word. 

>DELIMITED BY syntax allows a single delimiter; DELIMITED BY "  " (where the
>literal is two spaces) is OK, but "DELIMITED BY " ", " " (two one-space
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>whatever syntax it uses for hex literals.

The desired value is the end of the COBOL string. Granted, two spaces is an
imperfect solution because the COBOL string might contain two spaces. Moreover,
when it has a single trailing space, that space will be copied to the C string.
In my defense, line-function in the Demo shows how to scan right to left for the
last non-space character. 

> > move spaces to input-words
>> unstring input-line delimited by all space into
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>SPACE, no.

You might want to double-check. I do it all the time and it works as described.

>> Neither string nor unstring fills its output with spaces; you must
>> initialize.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>POINTER followed by a STRING SPACES WITH POINTER?

STRING SPACES, with expressed or implied DELIMITED BY SIZE, will put a single
space. One would have to write a loop to clear the receiving string one
character at a time. 

I like the idea of INITIALIZE, but don't use it because it is error-prone. It
does not clear FILLERs nor POINTERs. Hopefully, this bug was fixed in the '02
standard.

Pre-initialize is meaningless. Initialize means done at the beginning. One
cannot do something before the beginning. 

>>[INSPECT] has one useful function: translating one character
>> set to another.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>applicable standard to include it.

In practice, all major '85 compilers have that feature, and syntax is identical.

>>On older compilers, this function was the Transform verb.
>
>Never heard of TRANSFORM.  Which standard was that in?  I can't find it in
>'68, '74, '85 or '02.  

It was an IBM mainframe COBOL invention which has been available since the days
of '68 COBOL. Micro Focus and Realia copied it.

>Well, I think I've put about as much effort into this as I can afford right
>now.  See my suggestion at the beginning.
>
>     -Chuck Stevens

Thank you for the lengthy critique. I appreciate the time you spent.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T15:41:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gI2dnXYYs-jB5NijXTWckg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e453b28.243625521@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net>

> >>> move spaces to a-string
> >> string 'Error ' error-code delimited by size into a-string *> compose
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >The second STRING will step on the information put into a-string.  Don't
you
> >want a WITH POINTER clause here?
>
> They were two unrelated examples. I'll put a separator between them.
>
> I didn't want to convuse the reader with two meanings of the word POINTER.
In
> practice, I seldom use WITH POINTER. If a string cannot be built with a
single
> STRING, I prefer to roll my own using subscripts. The Demo does so in
> word-function, with code that could NOT be replaced by an UNSTRING verb
because
> it preserves quoted strings as a single word.

Here's one example using POINTER for which I haven't been able to find an
easier method to accomplish.

   Move 1 To MYPOINTER.
   Perform varying MYINDX from 1 by 1 until MYINDX > 500
      Unstring MYTEXT delimited by SPACE into MYWORD(MYINDX)
            with pointer MYPOINTER
   End-perform.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T11:50:12+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b241lb$lc4$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e453b28.243625521@news.optonline.net> <gI2dnXYYs-jB5NijXTWckg@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

> Here's one example using POINTER for which I haven't been able to find an
> easier method to accomplish.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>    End-perform.

You may want to make this DELIMITED BY ALL SPACE to reduce the number if 
blank words that you extract between the actual words.  It may also make it 
faster as it will skip all the trailing spaces at the end of MyText rather 
than all the work required for several hundred MyWord()s, it would be 
faster to space fill MyWords before this code.  Of course you should also 
change the UNTIL to "MyPointer > MyText-Length OR MyIndex > 500", but you 
should have this anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T23:38:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e45950a.11071972@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e453b28.243625521@news.optonline.net> <gI2dnXYYs-jB5NijXTWckg@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>   End-perform.

If MYTEXT contains less than 500 words and spaces, what happens when you get to
the end? You'll go into an endless loop or abend with 'pointer out of range'. 

Didn't you mean to say, "until MYINDX > 500 or MYPOINTER > length of MYTEXT'?

Suggestion: try 'delimited by ALL SPACE' to avoid all those time-wasting strings
filled with spaces. Change MYINDX to MYINDX-LIMIT so you know how many words
there are, don't have to look at all 500 in subsequent code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T19:30:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eTCdndFF_KVHMtijXTWciw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e453b28.243625521@news.optonline.net> <gI2dnXYYs-jB5NijXTWckg@giganews.com> <3e45950a.11071972@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net>

> >
> >Here's one example using POINTER for which I haven't been able to find an
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> If MYTEXT contains less than 500 words and spaces, what happens when you
get to
> the end? You'll go into an endless loop or abend with 'pointer out of
range'.
>
> Didn't you mean to say, "until MYINDX > 500 or MYPOINTER > length of
MYTEXT'?
>
> Suggestion: try 'delimited by ALL SPACE' to avoid all those time-wasting
strings
> filled with spaces. Change MYINDX to MYINDX-LIMIT so you know how many
words
> there are, don't have to look at all 500 in subsequent code.

Jeeze! The example wasn't meant to be production code - just a concept!

"If MYTEXT contains less than 500... you'll go into an endless loop..." In
our case MYTEXT is exactly 500 bytes long, so your observation is a no-op.
Even if MYTEXT were only 30 bytes, there would be no endless loop or pointer
out of range.

"Didn't you mean to say..." No, I didn't.

"...try 'delimited by ALL SPACE'..." Why? There are no imbedded multiple
spaces in our application, nor are there any trailing blanks. But you didn't
know that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T13:06:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VpicnYMwc_M5O9ujXTWchw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e453b28.243625521@news.optonline.net> <gI2dnXYYs-jB5NijXTWckg@giganews.com> <3E468634.8141B144@netbox.com>`

```

"B Berman" <bberman@netbox.com> wrote in message
news:3E468634.8141B144@netbox.com...
> JerryMouse wrote:
>
> > Here's one example using POINTER for which I haven't been able to find
an
> > easier method to accomplish.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not having used pointers before, what does it accomplish in your example ?

It's a replacement for the following:

Unstring MYTEXT delimited by SPACE into
   MYWORD(1)
   MYWORD(2)
   MYWORD(3)
   MYWORD(4)
   MYWORD(5)
   MYWORD(6)
   MYWORD(7)
   MYWORD(8)
   MYWORD(9)
   MYWORD(10)
   MYWORD(11)
   MYWORD(12)
   MYWORD(13)
   MYWORD(14)
   MYWORD(15)
   MYWORD(16)
   MYWORD(17)
   MYWORD(18)
   MYWORD(19)
   MYWORD(20)
   MYWORD(21)
   MYWORD(22)
   MYWORD(23)
   MYWORD(24)
   MYWORD(25)
   MYWORD(26)
   MYWORD(27)
   MYWORD(28)
   MYWORD(29)
   MYWORD(30)
   MYWORD(31)
   MYWORD(32)
   MYWORD(33)
   ...
   MYWORD(500)
   end-unstring

In other words, using the WITH POINTER clause, the unstring command begins
at the byte specified by the pointer variable and increments the pointer as
it moves bytes to the receiving field(s). The delimiter (SPACE in this
example) is counted in the pointer variable but is not moved to the
receiving field.

Here's an example:

MYTEXT = "Now is the time..."

MOVE 1 TO MYPOINTER. {don't forget to initialize to the 1st byte subject to
moving}
Unstring MYTEXT delimited SPACE into
   WORD1
   WORD2
   WORD3
   WORD4 with pointer MYPOINTER.

At the conclusion of the command:
WORD1=Now
WORD2=is
WORD3=the
WORD4=time

Like STRING, there are additional options available.
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302091550.2b348ba6@posting.google.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote

> At the conclusion of the command:
> WORD1=Now
> WORD2=is
> WORD3=the
> WORD4=time

The important thing is that MyPointer now has a value of 17 and doing
a subsequent UNSTRING starts where the previous one left off.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T23:32:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e46e433.96886824@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e453b28.243625521@news.optonline.net> <gI2dnXYYs-jB5NijXTWckg@giganews.com> <3E468634.8141B144@netbox.com>`

```
B Berman <bberman@netbox.com> wrote:

>JerryMouse wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Not having used pointers before, what does it accomplish in your example ?

COBOL has two interpretations of the word POINTER. Do not confuse this one with
a variable of type (USAGE IS) POINTER. This one is a subscript; the other is a
memory address.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T15:39:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0302081539.106a4676@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e453b28.243625521@news.optonline.net>`

```
I think that your attempt to write such a book is commendable, but
that you should express gratitude to and not offend those people who
have spent time to produce some quite lengthy constructive
suggestions.  That doesn't mean that you must always agree with them,
but all the contributors so far are very knowledgeable.

> I would if the standards were available electronically. I'm a mobile contract
> programmer who cannot transport a library of paper every time I move, about once
> a year. Why do the cobbler's children have no shoes? 
> 
You can currently get both the 1985 and the 2002 standards from ISO
and ANSI, though the 2002 standard is quite expensive.

> When I'm not certain a feature is in the Standard, I'll change references from
> "the standard" to "standard practice". Free-form is a good example. I've been
> using it since 1984.
> 

I would suggest that you use the term "my recommended practice" as it
would be less misleading to novices.  Your intro could explain that
your recommended practices have been developed from substantial
experience with a variety of organisations and interaction with
colleagues.

I think you should make the distinctions clearer between what is
allowable by the standard and what you personally recommend.  It is OK
for you only to consider a subset of the standards for your
discussion, provided it is not implied that there are are no other
relevant features.  Is is very questionable to recommend implementor
extensions to the standard without making it clear that that is what
they are and which implementors provide them.  Different implementors
may even provide extensions that look similar, but have subtle or even
not so subtle distinctions.

Rober
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T01:47:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e45b2c8.18687249@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e453b28.243625521@news.optonline.net> <fcd09c56.0302081539.106a4676@posting.google.com>`

```
robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones) wrote:

>I think that your attempt to write such a book is commendable, but
>that you should express gratitude to and not offend those people who
>have spent time to produce some quite lengthy constructive
>suggestions.  That doesn't mean that you must always agree with them,
>but all the contributors so far are very knowledgeable.

I tried to avoid offending Mr. Stevens, who provided some useful feedback. I
concur that he and others who responded are very knowledgeable. 

>> I would if the standards were available electronically. I'm a mobile contract
>> programmer who cannot transport a library of paper every time I move, about
once
>> a year. Why do the cobbler's children have no shoes? 
>> 
>You can currently get both the 1985 and the 2002 standards from ISO
>and ANSI, though the 2002 standard is quite expensive.

Are they available electronically, or only on paper?

>> When I'm not certain a feature is in the Standard, I'll change references
from
>> "the standard" to "standard practice". Free-form is a good example. I've been
>> using it since 1984.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>colleagues.

Noted. When I went through the paper, there were only a few references to
"standard". I deleted or changed them.

>I think you should make the distinctions clearer between what is
>allowable by the standard and what you personally recommend.  It is OK
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>not so subtle distinctions.

I do not recommend implementor extensions unless they are identical on Micro
Focus, IBM and Fujitsu (at least). At that point they become 'de-facto
standards'. Pointers and free-form are good examples. The document is written
for practitioners, not theoriticians. We cannot wait seventeen years for
standards committees to issue a new release. We wish you'all would get it in
gear and publish more frequent updates.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T00:11:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E45E2FF.B617E4EF@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e453b28.243625521@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
<MAJOR snippage>

Packed Decimal may not be implemented on Intel, but is/was
on WANG (ASCII based, but used the same format as IBM did
for Packed Decimal). AS/400 also uses it. I'm not sure about
UNISYS.

6 bit machines may actually be 36 bit machines (e.g.,
Honeywell, Univac, UNISYS).

Also something you said in one of these posts about industry
practices. REPORT WRITER is something you said is not an
industry practice based on three places you listed? Is that
true?

GE uses Report Writer (I know, because I wrote quite a bit
of it in a Honewell to IBM migration). Then there was ADP as
I recall. I have worked for a few other places that used it
but just can't remember when and where.

Frankly, as I said in a prior post on this topic, I think
you might need a bit more exposure to the "real" world. You
might be quite shocked to see things that are done in IMS
shops (or DL/I in VSE), CICS based shops, etc.

Oh, and before you go off on mainframes again - regardless
of what Micro$**t says, you might want to check and see how
much of banking relies on mainframe processing, along with
major manufacturers, several Universities (which left the
mainframe world to come back to it w/in 18 months!), and so
forth.

The mainframe world does not move fast for a reason. We'd
like to think we've learned a few things after all the grief
we've been through from the '60s to today. Change for the
sake of change has cost people jobs, caused companies to
cease to exist. And it causes too many headaches for people
who like their data processing operations to be the most
boring thing going on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 1. Seeking feedback

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T10:50:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e463270.51372086@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e441e82.170744776@news.optonline.net> <b21k1c$2lsc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e453b28.243625521@news.optonline.net> <3E45E2FF.B617E4EF@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
><MAJOR snippage>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>UNISYS.

Actually, Intel does offer a little machine language support for packed. When I
said "modern machines", I was thinking of Sun SPARC, which is really fast. 

>Also something you said in one of these posts about industry
>practices. REPORT WRITER is something you said is not an
>industry practice based on three places you listed? Is that
>true?

Yes.

>GE uses Report Writer (I know, because I wrote quite a bit
>of it in a Honewell to IBM migration). Then there was ADP as
>I recall. I have worked for a few other places that used it
>but just can't remember when and where.

Don't you just hate memory lapses? :)

>Frankly, as I said in a prior post on this topic, I think
>you might need a bit more exposure to the "real" world. You
>might be quite shocked to see things that are done in IMS
>shops (or DL/I in VSE), CICS based shops, etc.

Forty years isn't enough? I managed a VSE shop for ten years, where I did heavy
replacement of CICS internals. The 4361, which ran at 1 MIPS, supplied  more
than 100 terminals with .9 second response time, with a standard deviation of
less than .5 sec. You don't get that kinda performance with dead-stock IBM
software. 

I've also worked on Burroughs, Honeywell (before GE), RCA and CII-Bull mainframe
platforms, as well as Big Iron MVS. 

>Oh, and before you go off on mainframes again - regardless
>of what Micro$**t says, you might want to check and see how
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>forth.

I'm not down on mainframes so much as the culture surrounding them. Mainframe
programmers are, for the most part, mediocre. In their heyday -- '60s and '70s
-- mainframes attracted bright talent, at least in systems programming.
Nowadays, the bright people are working elsewhere.

>The mainframe world does not move fast for a reason. We'd
>like to think we've learned a few things after all the grief
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>boring thing going on.

Except when management wants something changed NOW, but  IT methodologies such
as CMM and ISO-9000 say it will take 3-6 months. That's a serious disconnect
between IT and the real world. In the last two places where I worked, Accenture
and EDS got fired because their methodologies made changes too slow.

It's all about evolution vs. revolution. I usually come down on the side of
RAPID evolution. Have you read Christopher Alexander's "Notes on the synthesis
of form"? (Harvard Press) Although written by an architect, it's a classic on
the topic of system development.

Robert
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
<snip>
> 
> Forty years isn't enough? I managed a VSE shop for ten years, where I did heavy
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> software.
<snip>

Forty years doing the same 6 months of work does not mean
you have 40 years of exposure (if you get my drift).

I've been a software developer, hardware manufacturer's
software writer for two different companies. Then there are
all the companies that I've been on assignment to (as well
as Gubment agencies). 25+ years of programming in COBOL,
RPG, FORTRAN, ALC, BAL, C, C++, as well as several utility
languages. I've worked on 36 bit machines, 16 bit machines,
20 bit machines, 31 bit machines, 32 bit machines and 64 bit
machines.

But what does all of that have to do with the subject at
hand? You are writing a book (and I commend you for that,
and allowing us to crucify your baby) about Best Practices.
But to do this, don't you need to know the number of shops
that do this or that? That use this or that feature but not
another, and why?

There are those who make a religion out of the "GO TO" verb.
Others who made a religion out of ALTER. Then there is the
top down, bottom up, and sideways structured methodologies.

While I might come across as a PIA, the real aim here is to
get all the info you can from curmudgeons like myself to put
out the best book you can. 
<snip>
> I'm not down on mainframes so much as the culture surrounding them. Mainframe
> programmers are, for the most part, mediocre. In their heyday -- '60s and '70s
> -- mainframes attracted bright talent, at least in systems programming.
> Nowadays, the bright people are working elsewhere.
<snip>
<soap box mode>
AMEN! I taught COBOL for a semester. Out of a class of about
50, 10 of them were programmers or would make programmers.
The 25-30 of them couldn't figure out how to write a COBOL
program when 80% of it had been specifically given in the
examples used in the class lectures!

Most of the COBOL programmers that I deal with today would
have been fired before they made it through probation, were
this the mid-'70s thru mid-'80s in the shops where I worked.
And many of the SR. Systems Programmers that I come across
today are what I consider to be JR Systems Programmers. The
best they can do is run SMP/E and a few utility programs.
Very few can explain why the system operates the way it does
(so they can't possibly tune the sucker!).

<\soap box mode>
