###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 2.

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-07T17:15:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b21ln5$2n0a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e92.170760373@news.optonline.net> <b21b6q$2g6f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e443e08.178815890@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e443e08.178815890@news.optonline.net...

> You are correct. Not for the first time, we have a disconnect between
Standards
> Commitee and the real world.

Personally, I find allowing FLOATING indicators in FIXED form a really neat
feature.  I do think that if you're in FREE-FORM reference format it's
reasonable to expect that you'd conform to those rules, including the format
of comments intended for use in FREE-FORM reference format, namely, using
the FLOATING indicator "*>".

Otherwise there are some ambiguities.  I'm reasonably certain the following
demonstrates my point.  Consider each of the following source images as
starting in Column 1:
    123456 >>SOURCE FORMAT FREE
    COMPUTE A = B
    * C
    . *>

    >>SOURCE FORMAT FIXED
    123457     COMPUTE A = B
    123458     * C.  *>

Should these two examples produce the same result?  I contend that they
should.  If the "*" that's in Column 1 in the first example introduces a
comment, the whole line should be treated as a comment, and thus the two
COMPUTEs should produce different answers.  If you want "*" in Column 1 to
be a comment in the general case, you break multiplication and
exponentiation in arithmetic expressions, as well as floating-asterisk
PICTUREs.

I don't think that's all that great an idea, I don't think that's what the
programmer who (for whatever reason) coded such a thing had in mind, and I
don't think the casual reader of such a statement would presume right off
the top that A = B even if C is not equal to 1.

If you're in free-form reference format, and you want a comment in your
source, it's appropriate to use the format for comments that's appropriate
for use in free-form reference format, and I don't think requiring that, at
the expense of breaking PICTURE ***,***... as well as COMPUTE ... * and **,
is the "evidence of a disconnect between Standards Commitee and the real
world" that you claim it to be!

Maybe you as a COBOL programmer with a "wish list" for the standard don't
have to think about such possibilities, but the folks who are trying to
write rules that don't cause such bizarre side effects certainly do!

What implementations / platforms actually do treat the asterisk in Column 1
(without ">") as a comment?  And what do they do with PICTURE and arithmetic
expressions in those cases?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 2.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T12:01:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e464321.55646579@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e441e92.170760373@news.optonline.net> <b21b6q$2g6f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e443e08.178815890@news.optonline.net> <b21ln5$2n0a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>PICTUREs.

There are two schools of thought. One holds that line terminators can be
discarded, that the program can be regarded as a one-dimensional stream of
bytes. In that case, comments must be explicitly terminated, as seen in C's /*
.. */. 

The other holds that line terminators are significant. They terminate a comment,
as seen in C++'s "//"

If line breaks can terminate a comment, they should also be significant in
initiating one. 

Because COBOL subscribes to the second school of thought -- line break
terminates a comment -- it would be symmetrical to provide * in position 1 for
initiating one. 

>What implementations / platforms actually do treat the asterisk in Column 1
>(without ">") as a comment?  And what do they do with PICTURE and arithmetic
>expressions in those cases?

Realia and Micro Focus, in my personal experience, probably others. They expect
free-form text to start in position 2 rather than 1. I know, it's like the old
days except seven 'card columns' have been collapsed down to one. 

The chance of inadvertantly coding an * in column 1 is miniscule in real-world
practice. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 2.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T16:39:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b29gne$1q65$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e92.170760373@news.optonline.net> <b21b6q$2g6f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e443e08.178815890@news.optonline.net> <b21ln5$2n0a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e464321.55646579@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e464321.55646579@news.optonline.net...

> If line breaks can terminate a comment, they should also be significant in
> initiating one.
>
> Because COBOL subscribes to the second school of thought -- line break
> terminates a comment -- it would be symmetrical to provide * in position 1
for
> initiating one.

COBOL does so only because the entire line is a comment -- allowing any
permissible characters in the comment.  The problem with asterisk in Column
1 of source images in free-form reference format is that you can't tell
whether it's a syntactic component or not.  An implementation that treats
Column 1 as a fixed-form reference indicator is not an implementation of
free-form reference format, it's an extension that's incompatible with
standard COBOL.

What character or mechanism terminates a line is up to the implementor.  It
may be a line feed, it may be a carriage return, it may be the end of the
physical record.  I don't agree that a leading "*" as an indication of a
comment is symmetrical with the end of the line, nor do I agree that a "*"
in Column 1 (but not a leading "*" that happens to be in Column 2) is
consistent with the idea of "free-form reference format.

I can see some benefit to the idea of providing "embedded comments" (in
addition to the 2002-standard "inline comments"), but such an implementation
would restrict the contents of such a comment because the syntax would of
necessity require a specific character or character sequence to terminate
the comment.

> The chance of inadvertantly coding an * in column 1 is miniscule in
real-world
> practice.

Minuscule in hand-coded programs, perhaps (but only perhaps).  But I've seen
many COBOL '74 and '85 programs in which all of the code is generated source
not intended for manual maintenance, and in which the generated source is as
dense as the compiler allows.  In such an environment, the generator would
have to be able to detect that the arithmetic expression it was about to
generate included a multiplication, and thus the generator would have to
"escape" to a new record to circumvent the ambiguity between the leading
asterisk as introducing a comment and the leading asterisk as a part of an
arithmetic expression.  In my particular case, a high percentage of the code
that the compilers I work with are required to compile is just such
generated code.   While it could be argued that no reasonable programmer
trying to create readable code would ever do such a thing, the underlying
assumption that every program being "consumed" by the COBOL compiler was
written by a human trying to produce a legible, elegant COBOL program is
simply invalid.  It may have been generated by a program designed to
conserve every bit of disk space with nary a side glance at legibility. The
rules of free-form reference format -- including the use of "floating"
indicators in that context -- actually make the job of the latter much
easier.


Note also that in '02 COBOL numbers can reach 31 (from 18) digits, and
picture character-strings can go up to 50 (from 30) characters.  Thus,
"***,***,***,***,***,***,***,***,***,***.99CR" is perfectly reasonable as a
numeric-edited PICTURE character-string in that dialect.  Try fitting that,
and much else, in a single line of a traditional 80-column-oriented source
images.

Even with free-form source images (which according to the standard can't
exceed 255 characters), it is not at all inconcievable that a programmer
would end up having the PICTURE in one source image and the associated
character-string on the next for similar formatting and pretty-text
alignment reasons.

Because the chances are minuscule does not mean that the compiler designer
need not provide for them, and I don't think the "right answer" is to treat
"*" in Column 1 of free-format reference format as introducing a comment
unless Column 2 contains ">".   And if the distinction is that "*" in Column
1 introduces a comment but "*" in Column 2 does not, well, folks, that's not
a FREE-form reference format, that's a variation of FIXED-form reference
format in which the sequence number field is simply deleted.   A free-form
reference format that is truly free form is not dependent on column
positions.   At all.

      -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Practices - Cobol. Part 2.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T23:52:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302102352.5f3634a7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3e441e92.170760373@news.optonline.net> <b21b6q$2g6f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e443e08.178815890@news.optonline.net> <b21ln5$2n0a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e464321.55646579@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> There are two schools of thought. ... 
> as seen in C's /* .. */. 
> as seen in C++'s "//"

Actually C++ has both of these and many C implementions support //
 
> If line breaks can terminate a comment, they should also be significant in
> initiating one. 

Huh ? Why _should_ they ?
 
> Because COBOL subscribes to the second school of thought -- line break
> terminates a comment -- 

Actually Cobol changed from one to the other.  Originally Cobol had
the NOTE statement which started a comment ended by end of sentence or
end of paragraph.  later the column 7 '*' was introduced, and NOTE was
dropped.

> it would be symmetrical to provide * in position 1 for
> initiating one. 

In what way is '*' symetrical with \n ?
 
> Realia and Micro Focus, in my personal experience, probably others. They expect
> free-form text to start in position 2 rather than 1. I know, it's like the old
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> practice. 

What was poor about MF Cobolo is that when they introduced '*' in
Column 1 as a comment marker it also applied to fixed format source.

With older compilers columns 1-6 were completely ignored unless the
first line had a sequence number or somesuch.  Cols 1-6 thus could be
used, for example, for version control.

I was looking after some code that occasionally had notations in 1-6,
sometimes this began with an '*'.  The new compiler introduced some
strange bugs that had to be resolved by processing all the source code
to remove col 1-6, especially '*'s.

"Miniscule", I think not.
```

---

<a id="t-1f86dbfbc1"></a>
## Thread: Selecting a compiler — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Selecting a compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-07T19:30:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b21mjq$hk1$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I just thought that I would raise some "questions" as a follow-on to another
thread in CLC.  These are some of the questions that I think people *might*
consider when selecting a COBOL compiler:

1) Are there more than one compiler vendor who actually sells a "supported"
compiler for the environment that you plan on developing upon? How about
running on?  (These may or may not have the same answer.)

2) Are you looking for a compiler to "learn" on? create production code
with?  create software that you plan on selling?  (The answers to these
questions may influence such issues as to whether you care about "run-time
fees"? run-time performance? debugging tools?  easy to find samples?  easy
to find maintenance programmers? etc)

3) Do you think that you might EVER need to "port" your source code to
another compiler? to another run-time environment? to an "upgraded"
operating system/environment?

4) How important is ILC (inter-language communication) to your expected use?
If at all important to what type of "languages"?  C?  C++? Java? PL/I?
Assembler (which variety)? etc?

5) What type of non-COBOL "things" might you need/want to interface with?
SQL? printers (and what type)? proprietary systems such as IMS or CICS or
Windows API's or Oracle etc?

6) Are there contractual issues for your expected applications e.g. "must
conform to ANSI Standard for US government contract?)  Must provide
user-documentation or source code in escrow?  Must maintain at least 3
back-levels of source code?

7) Do you have any "internationalization" issues expected for your COBOL
programs? for "things" that your COBOL program must interact with?  If so,
are there Unicode or similar issues that your COBOL compiler must deal with?

8) What type of support do you "need" from your compiler vendor?  (24x7?
email responses within 2-3 business days?  open-source?)

9) What are your cost constraints on the compiler? on the run-time? on
run-time distribution?

   ***

Probably LOTS of other questions arise to others.  The chances are that if
you are in a "mainframe" environment, you have no "individual" choice in
what compiler you use - on the other hand, if you are looking for a
"workstation" compiler, you (usually) get what you pay for - and need to
worry about what you plan on doing with your compiler output (if anything).
```

#### ↳ Re: Selecting a compiler

- **From:** "Merlin Moncure" <merlin@rcsonline.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T15:46:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v5d3u46373o68f@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b21mjq$hk1$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
An interesting post!  I recently got a new job and inherited a huge cobol
source code library.  My project does it all, accounting, inventory, factory
manangement, you name it.  I never used cobol previously, having worked
mostly in c++.   My company uses AcuCorp cobol, the only one I know, so my
answers will be based on my perspectives gained using that platform.

To me, the #1 issue with COBOL is vendor lock in.  Just as an example,
AcuCorp's compiler costs 1000$, when you can download any number of free C
compilers off the internet.  To avoid lock in you have to avoid using any
vendor specific tools but these very tools are required to make a 'modern'
looking application in COBOL.  I like freedom to be able to migrate my
applciation to different platforms with minimal effort.


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b21mjq$hk1$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net...
> I just thought that I would raise some "questions" as a follow-on to
another
> thread in CLC.  These are some of the questions that I think people
*might*
> consider when selecting a COBOL compiler:
>
> 1) Are there more than one compiler vendor who actually sells a
"supported"
> compiler for the environment that you plan on developing upon? How about
> running on?  (These may or may not have the same answer.)

AcuCobol runs on most windows/unix platforms.  A lot of the GUI stuff only
works on windows.  Vanilla cobol should run just about anywhere, but
personally I wouldn't suggest starting a new project in vanilla cobol.
There are some free compilers you can download which are pretty functional,
but I don't see a use for them beyond academic purposes.

>
> 2) Are you looking for a compiler to "learn" on? create production code
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> operating system/environment?

Never, ever say no to this answer. Otherwise you are looking at a rewrite at
some later date.

>
> 4) How important is ILC (inter-language communication) to your expected
use?
> If at all important to what type of "languages"?  C?  C++? Java? PL/I?
> Assembler (which variety)? etc?

This is extrememly important to me. COBOL can no longer be counted on to
deliver every aspect of an application.  It is simply one tool in a toolkit.
COBOL has some very useful contructs for expressing business rules and will
therefore always have a use, but being able to introduce different languages
will make you a more versatile programmer.  Without ILC, you have to wait
for your vendor to deliver new technologies (for example, xml-edi) and that
is not a good thing.

Assembler has no place in a business application anymore.  C is terrible at
business apps but is good at performing workhorse tasks like dynamic
allocation.  C++ is a good general purpose lanugage, but is complex.  I
think C# is a beautiful language, (never used java) but as of Feb '03 thats
only on windows.

AcuCorp cobol has a very elegant syntax for calling DLLs (in windows), you
just CALL the dll to load it, then you can CALL its procedures.  This is one
of the best things about that platform.

>
> 5) What type of non-COBOL "things" might you need/want to interface with?
> SQL? printers (and what type)? proprietary systems such as IMS or CICS or
> Windows API's or Oracle etc?

SQL is a replacement for COBOL I/O operations, namely read, write, rewite,
open, close, and start.  One SQL statement can replace literally hundreds of
lines of code in a program.  Another benefit of sql is that it is 'set'
based programming (some call it declarative programming).  It is proven that
errors are easier to catch and fix at the set based level and set level
programs have a much smaller recurring cost than procedural programs.  SQL
statements are also much friendlier to database transactions.

If your platform has an elegant syntax for executing sql statements from
within a cobol program, congratulations.  You enjoy the best of both worlds.
Expect to pay top dollar for this from a commercial vendor.  Performance is
an issue here, too.  Some sql->cobol solutions are real dogs in the
performance arena and should be avoided.

>
> 6) Are there contractual issues for your expected applications e.g. "must
> conform to ANSI Standard for US government contract?)  Must provide
> user-documentation or source code in escrow?  Must maintain at least 3
> back-levels of source code?

I've never done such work, but CVS is an excellent choice for managing
historical source code revisions.  I installed it here at the office and it
has already paid off in a big way,  catching strange bugs introduced
inadvertantly by developers.

>
> 7) Do you have any "internationalization" issues expected for your COBOL
> programs? for "things" that your COBOL program must interact with?  If so,
> are there Unicode or similar issues that your COBOL compiler must deal
with?
>
> 8) What type of support do you "need" from your compiler vendor?  (24x7?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> run-time distribution?

Solutions for these problems are highly vendor specific.  Just do your
research and keep your options open.  At this point in the game, I think
there are few reasons to start a new project in cobol.  One thing I have
noticed about cobol is that everything seems to cost more, all the way
through the development process.  One good thing about cobol is that there a
lot of developers who have business app experience to draw from.   There
might be some reasons to choose cobol  at the enterprise level.  Generally,
though, I would only choose cobol only if there was no other alternative.

However, I would recommend that every programmer learn cobol.  At first
glance it seems very outdated, but it has a certain charm that comes through
after a while.  Even the early standards were very ahead of their time in
certain respects.  If you program in the business world the chances are very
good you will run across some cobol code sooner or later.   It is quite
common these days that businesses are porting their cobol programs to
another language/platform.  Knowing cobol would be an crucial skill in
getting hired for those projects.

Merlin
merlin.moncure@rcsonline.com
>
>    ***
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> worry about what you plan on doing with your compiler output (if
anything).
>
> --
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Selecting a compiler

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>

```
Merlin:

I agree wholeheartedly with your assessment that the #1 issue is
vendor lock in.

That's why you should take a look at the Flexus COBOL tools.

Because we employ the use of COBOL CALL USING statements for our
tools, they are COBOL compiler independent, operating system
independent and even character mode to GUI independent.

Our tools work GREAT with the Acucorp line of compilers, from the
older ones to the GT compiler to the newer Extend compilers.  But they
also work with Micro Focus compilers, Fujitsu compilers, Liant/RM
COBOL compilers, ICOBOL compilers, CA-Realia COBOL compilers and IBM
Visual Age COBOL compilers.  We still have customers who use our tools
on compilers which are no longer supported by the compiler vendor,
such as mbp Visual COBOL.

If you have any questions at all, please send a private e-mail to me.
I'll be happy to assist.

…[146 more quoted lines elided]…

Bob Wolfe
```

<a id="t-5184c34c9d"></a>
## Thread: NetX/Dialog System: ListView - Part-1 — 2 msgs

### NetX/Dialog System: ListView - Part-1

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T10:13:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2501356.1044699183@dbforums.com>`

```

Problem:

The field of a listview is alphanumeric, soon a column containing dates
with the format DD/MM/YYYY when sorted by the header they would be for
us unsorted as dates and ordered alphabetically.

solution:

we created an extra column in the listview with size zero and containing
the dates in the format YYYY/MM/DD.
imagine: the column 4 would have the format DD/MM/YYYY and the column 7
would have the format YYYY/MM/DD.
we altered the control program generated by Dialog System in the
following way:

  place-storage section.
       01 num-col pic x(4) comp-5.
--------------------------
   Register-Callbacks section.
          MOVE '"' & ProgramID & z'Clicked"' TO MessageName
          INVOKE EntryCallback "new" USING MessageName
                                 RETURNING aCallback
          MOVE P2ce-clicked To i
          INVOKE aControl "setEvent" USING i aCallback
          INVOKE aCallback "finalize" RETURNING aCallback
       *>
-------------------------------------
       sortOnColumn section.

       Entry ProgramID & "Clicked" USING anEvent.

           PERFORM GetDataBlockPointer

           INVOKE anEvent "getSelectedItem" RETURNING num-col

           if num-col = 4
              Move 7 To num-col
              Invoke aControl "SortOnColumn" using num-col
           End-If

           EXIT PROGRAM
           .
      *>----------------------------------------------------------------


Marcos A.S.
Brasil
```

#### ↳ Re: NetX/Dialog System: ListView - Part-1

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T10:19:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2501357.1044699578@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2501356.1044699183@dbforums.com>`

```

NetX/Dialog System: ListView - Part-1

Problem:

The field of a listview is alphanumeric, soon a column containing dates
with the format DD/MM/YYYY when sorted by the header they would be for
us unsorted as dates and ordered alphabetically.

solution:

we created an extra column in the listview with size zero and containing
the dates in the format YYYY/MM/DD.
imagine: the column 4 would have the format DD/MM/YYYY and the column 7
would have the format YYYY/MM/DD.
we altered the control program generated by Dialog System in the
following way:

local-storage section.
       01  num-col         pic x(4) comp-5.
--------------------------
   Register-Callbacks section.
          MOVE '"' & ProgramID & z'Clicked"' TO MessageName
          INVOKE EntryCallback "new" USING MessageName
                                 RETURNING aCallback
          MOVE P2ce-clicked To i
          INVOKE aControl "setEvent" USING i aCallback
          INVOKE aCallback "finalize" RETURNING aCallback
       *>
-------------------------------------
       sortOnColumn section.

       Entry ProgramID & "Clicked" USING anEvent.

           PERFORM GetDataBlockPointer

           INVOKE anEvent "getSelectedItem" RETURNING num-col

           if num-col = 4
              Move 7 To num-col
              Invoke aControl "SortOnColumn" using num-col
           End-If

           EXIT PROGRAM
           .
      *>----------------------------------------------------------------


Marcos A.S.
Brasil
```

<a id="t-e0f230fd42"></a>
## Thread: NetX/Dialog System: ListView - Part-2 — 1 msgs

### NetX/Dialog System: ListView - Part-2

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T10:44:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2501433.1044701082@dbforums.com>`

```

NetX/Dialog System: ListView - Part-2

Problem:

when we used the command READ PREVIOUS in a listview the order not
always leaves the form that we wanted. We would have the illustration
1.jpg for instance:

Solution:

If we click in the header Item the order it will be corrected. But we
suppose that want the order to come out correct without the user's
interference.

those commands can be used inside of your CBL. doesn't need to be
necessarily the control program. We would have the illustration 1a.jpg
for instance:

   local-storage section.
       01  num-col         pic x(4) comp-5.

       procedure division.
              .
              .
              .
              move 1 to num-col
              Invoke lview-01-objref "SortOnColumn" using num-col
           .
Marcos A.S.
Brasil
```

<a id="t-4d1798c0a6"></a>
## Thread: OT: The Global Job Shift — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: The Global Job Shift

- **From:** none@none.com (Freewheelin)
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T19:47:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e455c31.23089798@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Article on IT outsourcing offshore...

BusinessWeek Online  The Global Job Shift   Feb 3, 2003

http://www.businessweek.com/magazine/content/03_05/b3818001.htm
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: The Global Job Shift

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T23:08:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e458e01.9271325@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e455c31.23089798@news.earthlink.net>`

```
none@none.com (Freewheelin) wrote:

>Article on IT outsourcing offshore...
>
>BusinessWeek Online  The Global Job Shift   Feb 3, 2003
>
>http://www.businessweek.com/magazine/content/03_05/b3818001.htm

The article is accurate. Every month, the well-known financial research data
provider where I work moves jobs to Bangalore and Manila. They are mostly
clerical and programming grunt work. Indians are good programmers who willingly
do the dirtiest jobs, such as 24/7 production support, without complaint.
Russians are even better programmers, but they have OPINIONS which they aren't
afraid to voice. Managers don't want to hear opinions, especially those
suggesting Americans are intellectually inferior. 

Years ago, when I lived in Japan, a girlfriend asked me to help her younger
brother with his high-school homework. He was in the equivalent of our eleventh
grade. I was astounded to see him doing differential equations. When I asked
whether he was in an accelerated  program, the answer was no, this was standard
high-school curriculum. I thought to myself, "We're sunk. Some day these people
are gonna bury us." Now that we see it happening, we have only ourselves and our
school systems to blame. 

Fortunately for us COBOL crankers, and getting back on topic, foreigners usually
don't know COBOL. They know C++ and later technologies. As a result, our jobs
are secure, although they'll probably pay less because top management thinks of
'programmer' as a single skill without regard to language. When the tide goes
down, all boats are lowered at the same rate. 

Robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: The Global Job Shift

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T06:46:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E45F916.5070604@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<3e455c31.23089798@news.earthlink.net> <3e458e01.9271325@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> none@none.com (Freewheelin) wrote:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>http://www.businessweek.com/magazine/content/03_05/b3818001.htm

(snip)

> Fortunately for us COBOL crankers, and getting back on topic, foreigners usually
> don't know COBOL. They know C++ and later technologies. As a result, our jobs
> are secure, although they'll probably pay less because top management thinks of
> 'programmer' as a single skill without regard to language. When the tide goes
> down, all boats are lowered at the same rate. 

More reason, from management's perspective, to stop using COBOL.

The following paragraph from the BW report caught my eye:


FEBRUARY 3, 2003

COVER STORY

The New Global Job Shift

...

It's globalization's next wave--and one of the biggest trends reshaping 
the global economy. The first wave started two decades ago with the 
exodus of jobs making shoes, cheap electronics, and toys to developing 
countries. After that, simple service work, like processing credit-card 
receipts, and mind-numbing digital toil, like writing software code, 
began fleeing high-cost countries.

   ---------------------------------------------------------

How many of us have found programming to be "mind numbing digital toil"? 
I've always found the actual work (design, programming & testing) to be 
challenging and satisfying. Color me green, 'cause I'm a real dinosaur.

;)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: The Global Job Shift

- **From:** "Rick S." <ricks123@hotpop.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T18:12:00+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b26252$nmb$1@news-reader10.wanadoo.fr>`
- **References:** `<3e455c31.23089798@news.earthlink.net> <3e458e01.9271325@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote

> Years ago, when I lived in Japan, a girlfriend asked me to help her
younger
> brother with his high-school homework. He was in the equivalent of our
eleventh
> grade. I was astounded to see him doing differential equations. When I
asked
> whether he was in an accelerated  program, the answer was no, this was
standard
> high-school curriculum. I thought to myself, "We're sunk. Some day these
people
> are gonna bury us." Now that we see it happening, we have only ourselves
and our
> school systems to blame.

Please, Robert, sincerely, don't think I am disagreeing with you very
much, but there are some things on the other side of the equation. Perhaps,
maybe.

One can wonder if the same sort of educational system that can teach
differential equations to 11th graders will also do something to the
mind that makes innovative thinkers outsiders. A lot of new stuff does
come from the U.S. these days. We're a wild and crazy nation... and
that does have a few benefits, perhaps.

Second of all, what percentage of the population really needs to be
able to do differential equations ?  Reading, writing, basic math, write
HTML code, the ability to operate a forklift truck ... these are really
important skills.

Don't misunderstand, I agree there is a demand for highly educated
people, but the question is, what percentage of the population needs
to be that way ?

>
> Fortunately for us COBOL crankers, and getting back on topic, foreigners
usually
> don't know COBOL. They know C++ and later technologies.

Most of the COBOL systems I have worked on are "in-house"
systems, very closely tied to that way they do business at that one
company. Often there is only a small group of people that even
understand what the business end of the system is supposed to do.

Sometimes too they are ordinary middle-class folks. You know, "Betty,
that's been with the company for 22 years"... she's the one that knows
the system, and how to improve it. And test it, et cetera. Her "comfort
level" is greater if she often sees the systems people.

For generic "high-tech" software... India might be the answer, but for
most COBOL systems, I am not so sure. Close relationships between
the systems development folks and the end users is perhaps, or
probably, the best way to get the best system.

Sure the guys that write SAP, they can work in India, but highly
specialized systems, tailored to individual work environnments, that's
different.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: The Global Job Shift

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T22:44:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e46d887.93898055@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e455c31.23089798@news.earthlink.net> <3e458e01.9271325@news.optonline.net> <b26252$nmb$1@news-reader10.wanadoo.fr>`

```
"Rick S." <ricks123@hotpop.com> wrote:

>One can wonder if the same sort of educational system that can teach
>differential equations to 11th graders will also do something to the
>mind that makes innovative thinkers outsiders. A lot of new stuff does
>come from the U.S. these days. We're a wild and crazy nation... and
>that does have a few benefits, perhaps.

 I used to work for a company named Lucid which wrote most of the software in
the Sharp Wizard and Casio Boss. Japanese managers from both companies said they
preferred American programmers because Japanese programmers were good at
following instructions but unimaginative. 

We're not schooled to be creative. I too learned differential equations in
eleventh grade .. because I taught myself using library books. High-school math
teachers weren't impressed. They often kicked me out of class for being too
creative, they called it "disruptive", even though my average test scores were
100. They rewarded people who used orthodox, textbook methods to solve problems.


Lucid had 20 programmers, every one a superstar. The place dripped with ego. If
you said something dumb or incorrect, they'd shove it down your throat without
mercy. Out of curiosity, I asked each about his background and education. Not
one had a degree in computer science. Music and philosophy were the most common
majors. Many were college drop-outs and a few, like me, hadn't even finished
high-school. The company founder and resident visionary had been a shepherd
hearding sheep around the English countryside before designing a program which
won PC Magazine's Application of the Year award -- the Lucid 3D spreadsheet.
They were all self-educated misfits, and they were the best. 

>Second of all, what percentage of the population really needs to be
>able to do differential equations ?  Reading, writing, basic math, write
>HTML code, the ability to operate a forklift truck ... these are really
>important skills.

You forgot to mention pulling cable. 

>Don't misunderstand, I agree there is a demand for highly educated
>people, but the question is, what percentage of the population needs
>to be that way ?

The higher the percentage pushed through college, the lower standards must be
set. 

There is a difference between education and job training. Society would be
better if everyone were educated. But I concur with your point -- few need
higher math. 

>> Fortunately for us COBOL crankers, and getting back on topic, foreigners
>usually
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>level" is greater if she often sees the systems people.

I've had the same experience. Problem is, when Betty is asked how the system
could be improved with a new design, she describes the old system with some
minor incremental changes. She can't think outside the box. If she feels
threatened, as so often happens, she'll withhold critical information. You or I
must reverse-engineer the old system to reveal what it does. 

>For generic "high-tech" software... India might be the answer, but for
>most COBOL systems, I am not so sure. Close relationships between
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>different.

Peoplesoft is written in COBOL. It and SAP can be tailored to individual work
environments without being written from scratch. They are not limited to generic
applications such as accounting; they can handle apps with significant variation
such as order entry and inventory. 

Large IT shops discourage user interaction except in formal conferences.
Further, there is often a struggle for control i.e. internal decision making.
The user wants to keep it; management and IT want to take it away. For instance,
I was in a group which spent a year developing a new child abuse system for the
State of New York, in order to make them compliant with Federal mandates
(SACWIS). The users -- case workers -- refused to use it because it took too
much control away from them. As a result, the State is paying millions in
penalties. [Penalties are public information, available on the Web.] I hasten to
mention that the server side was written in COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: The Global Job Shift

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** madodelNOSPAM@ptd.net (Mark Dodel)
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T00:41:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cLdq6jdb1N4Q-pn2-1XndbHF5LQv1@localhost>`
- **References:** `<3e455c31.23089798@news.earthlink.net> <3e458e01.9271325@news.optonline.net> <b26252$nmb$1@news-reader10.wanadoo.fr> <3e46d887.93898055@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Sun, 9 Feb 2003 22:44:02 UTC, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) 
wrote:

-> Large IT shops discourage user interaction except in formal conferences.
-> Further, there is often a struggle for control i.e. internal decision making.
-> 

In the shops I have worked in (not really large shops, but all 
mainframe), there was always an ongoing transformation of how IT 
interfaced with users.  One year we'd interact directly with users, 
the next it was only through managers, the next only through 
"coordinators", then only through meetings, and back and forth.  To me
it always worked best when the middle layer was eliminated and we 
could talk directly to the person that was going to be using the 
systems we were developing.  Am I one of the few lucky enough to have 
experienced that?

Mark
```

```
Yes, you are. IT managers don't want you talking to users because a( you might
say something stupid which embarrasses the department and b) you might make
promises which managers don't want to keep. 'I can fix that by changing one line
of code'. Managers want that one-line change run through the full test cycle,
which might take 3-6 months.
```

```
Been there, done that.  I've had management that felt the need to make
it all appear far more complex then it needs to be just to make their 
budgets look appropriate.  And I've had the PHB who promised things 
that were extremely difficult, in ridiculously short time frames, just
to make points with some other PHB.  The best managers were the ones 
that were just happy to have the work done right and could care less 
about the fiefdom building game.  


Mark
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: The Global Job Shift

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T20:30:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e480bf8.172614407@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e455c31.23089798@news.earthlink.net> <3e458e01.9271325@news.optonline.net> <b26252$nmb$1@news-reader10.wanadoo.fr> <3e46d887.93898055@news.optonline.net> <cLdq6jdb1N4Q-pn2-1XndbHF5LQv1@localhost> <3e4708ba.106238311@news.optonline.net> <cLdq6jdb1N4Q-pn2-0wbSWpi1dGW0@localhost>`

```
madodelNOSPAM@ptd.net (Mark Dodel) wrote:

>On Mon, 10 Feb 2003 02:04:43 UTC, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) 
>wrote:
>
>> Yes, you are. IT managers don't want you talking to users because a( you
might
>> say something stupid which embarrasses the department and b) you might make
>-> promises which managers don't want to keep. 'I can fix that by changing one
line
>-> of code'. Managers want that one-line change run through the full test
cycle,
>-> which might take 3-6 months. 
>-> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>about the fiefdom building game.  

A lot of management overhead is butt-covering rather than empire-building. They
don't trust a single programmer to get it right. They want many eyes and hands
reviewing code and testing programs in the hope errors will be caught before
going into production. In my experience, shops like that have an error rate the
same or higher than shops with old-fashioned methodology, and users are less
satisfied because simple changes take so long. Ironically, when errors are
discovered in high-methodology shops, everyone is afraid to fix them because
they are 'outside the scope'. I just fix them and keep my mouth shut. 

I once worked on a program which had comments filled with spelling errors, which
I corrected. A manager ran a diff, found the changes, and made me change them
back. They were 'outside the scope', therefore I was wasting time.

The same shop had a function which subtracted two dates. The second date was
supposed to be before the first, in other words the answer was always positive.
When the function detected a higher second date, it exited. It didn't return an
error nor clear the answer, which contained the result from the previous call. I
fixed the function to work correctly, returning a negative in that case. The
systems programmer who 'owned' the function refused to install my fix because
some program MIGHT be depending on the abberant behavior. 

How can we expect quality from that kind of corporate culture?

Robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: The Global Job Shift

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@sbcglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-11T09:32:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E49178D.AD9784D2@sbcglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3e455c31.23089798@news.earthlink.net> <3e458e01.9271325@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
<snipped>
> Fortunately for us COBOL crankers, and getting back on topic, foreigners usually
> don't know COBOL. They know C++ and later technologies. As a result, our jobs
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Robert

3 & 4 years ago I took a part time position at the local Metro Comm. 
College. I was a lab assistant for windows, and language tutor for
COBOL, BAL, and intro classes to C, C++, and Pascal. The local 
university had killed their mainframe and COBOL classes the year 
before.

Over 1/3 of each COBOL and BAL class was Russian. The second year
COBOL students were 1/2 Russian. This ratio was about the same for 
both years. CICS classes were starting after I left, but I would guess
that the same students would attend those too. 

The american students just wanted to pass the class without working too 
hard, and that was their only guideline as far as studying and 
developing solutions, or learning. The Russian students were VERY 
aggressive, and demanded to know why? They also tried other methods 
and "out of the box" designs to see how they worked. 

I've ranted about this before, but I still think that our higher
education system has watered down the technical requirements so far
that the average graduate is a really a BA with a few programming
classes. A sad statement on our technology training.

Design and testing seems to be kept in the US for now, but if it
gets moved to foreign countries, I'm glad I have all my accounts
with money in them restricted from electronic transfers. Even if 
I could get higher interest and more options using electronic
accounts, I have paper documents and certificates! (I guess the 
only better thing would be a cement mattress, but they are too hard
to sleep on :-)

Gary
```

<a id="t-807e3c6b1b"></a>
## Thread: Lesson Learned — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Lesson Learned

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T15:58:45-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Of6cnd_zToUk4NijXTWchQ@giganews.com>`

```
We distributed a new application about 18 months ago.

Since then, we've fixed an number of reported problems and added a number of
features, distributing a new version about ever 6-8 weeks.

Then, in an Epiphany, a moment of clarity, it came to us: why not distribute
this stuff over the web?

Oh, obvious, you say. Everybody does it. Well, not us dummies. We were so
far down in the weeds we overlooked the practicality, thought web
distribution would not be able to easily handle the
installation/repair/replace facility of a full-blown inventory application,
or would require diversion of massive programming effort to implement.

Then we looked again.

It wasn't all that hard.

The creation of a couple-hundred CDs and the printing of same, labeling of
envelopes, update documents, postage, blah-blah-blah -- all went away.

On our last distribution we added code allowing the user to access the
download area of our web site, FTP the patch file, and apply same to every
machine on the network.

Since then, our updating protocol involves uploading the patch to our
website and launching several hundred emails - taking, in total, about 30
minutes.

Boy are we embarrassed we didn't do this sooner. We're not so embarrassed,
however, that we can't pass on what we think (now) is a good idea.

GOOD IDEA FOLLOWS:

For a new application, build in a web-update feature from the get-go. A
small investment up front pays off in much time saved later. I bet there are
even update-from-the-web packages available that only require bolting down
and you'll have everything you need.
```

---

<a id="t-588826f445"></a>
## Thread: Q: (IF WS-VAR NOT = SPACE) does not work in DBCS context — 2 msgs

### Q: (IF WS-VAR NOT = SPACE) does not work in DBCS context

- **From:** gastie <gastie@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T23:14:39+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E45814F.8050904@t-online.de>`

```
Hi everybody,

as a novice working with ILE-COBOL I encountered a problem adapting 
codes to DBCS (Double Byte Character Set).

On AS400 DBCS strings (say Chinese or Japanese) begin with a shift-out 
byte hex-valued '0E' and end with a shift-in '0F'.

Oft there are code lines like

IF WS-VAR1 not = SPACE


With possibly WS-VAR1 containing DBCS strings, this does not work any 
more if somehow shift-out or shift-in slip in.

One solution is to scan every byte and replace shift-out and shift-in 
before comparing the string to SPACES, which I believe will reduce 
performance and make new codes unbearably long.

Anyone can help me on that?
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: (IF WS-VAR NOT = SPACE) does not work in DBCS context

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T23:50:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e45968b.11457511@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E45814F.8050904@t-online.de>`

```
gastie <gastie@t-online.de> wrote:

>Hi everybody,
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Anyone can help me on that?

It's not slow if you say
inspect a-string converting x'0E0F' to '  '  *> two spaces
if  a-string equal to spaces ...
```

<a id="t-7589d10008"></a>
## Thread: Serial Number Request - NOT MINE — 10 this month / 13 total

_Thread started [2003-01](2003-01.md#t-7589d10008)._

#### ↳ Re: Serial Number Request - NOT MINE

- **From:** gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-08T22:23:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2502735.1044743015@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3E368A70.8080904@rochester.rr.com>`

```

It not possible to use a Fujitsu Cobol serial number, specially if you
are installing v6.1.. The protection scheme is enough difficult to
crack, because the site key given during the installation, can change
from machine to another, also using the same serial number. There is
also another consideration: supposing your are able to install the
product, it will run only for 30 days, then it needs to be activated
sending a request to Fujitsu registration server, to obtain a valid
activation key to unlock the product for an unlimited use.
The Fujtsu cobol proctection scheme is hard to crack.
There is final consideration that each programmer should be think: Do
you like to see your work freely distributed on the web? I think not!
So a product copyrighted must be purchased and regularly licensed.

An advice: don't waste your time..

Gianni
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Serial Number Request - NOT MINE

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T08:11:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e4b52a8.4806527@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E368A70.8080904@rochester.rr.com> <2502735.1044743015@dbforums.com>`

```
gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>It not possible to use a Fujitsu Cobol serial number, specially if you
>are installing v6.1.. The protection scheme is enough difficult to
>crack, because the site key given during the installation, can change
>from machine to another, also using the same serial number. 

The protection code will be packaged as a separate function. Find the place
where it calls that code and change it to 'mov eax, 0'. 

Robert
```

```
That statement may constitute an illegal act under the Digital
Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA) in that it is the publication of a
mechanism designed to circumvent the copyright protection mechanism of
the intelectual property of another party.

complete crap and has absolutely no chance of working is not a
defense.  Ain't American Law wonderful.
```

```
Snort - the only thing the DMCA is protecting is the votes some congress critters hope to have in the next election. I don't steal
software, and I don't pirate software.

I *do* however, make backup copies of everything, including the thousands of CD's around here that
have music on them, as well as every hunk of software I have purchased either for personal use, or use in the business.

The only thing I don't have backups of are VHS tapes and DVD's. I intend to do something about that soon, but I just haven't figured
out what the most economical method of doing anything is.

If I have to spend $16 to get a copy of the latest Dixie Chicks CD, for which production and distribution costs account for less
1/5th of the price, then I am damn sure going to make a backup for when one of the kids (or me) leaves the disk in the truck. Do you
know what a couple hours in the hot Texas summer sun can do to a CD?

Personally, I don't care how many millions of dollars George Lucas gets for Star Wars movies, nor do I care home many millions of
dollars Bill Gates looses to piracy. We don't steal and to be perfectly honest, with the number of nasty bugs in Microsoft software,
anyone who does steal from them is stealing only a pile of unsupported trouble.

And to tie this a little tiny bit to being on topic, I strong disagree with the PC and Workstation COBOL vendors who feel that
everyone is out to steal their software and hurt their profits. When one of them goes solidly out of business, I intend to buy the
rights to their compiler and market it in the way I believe will provide both a nice income stream, and also treat developers and
users much more fairly.
```

## Thread: String To Integer? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### String To Integer?

- **From:** yauyau <member23982@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T14:09:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2503685.1044799758@dbforums.com>`

```

How to convert a String to Integer?

Suppose I store "1994" in a String variable YEARSTR,
How can I convert it to integer and store in a variable YEAR?

I have tried
..
YEAR        PIC 9(10)
YEARSTR   PIC X(10)
..

MOVE YEARSTR TO YEAR

but when I display the value of YEAR, I find a zero at the end of year:

1994     0

and if I add 1 to YEAR, I won't get 1995.Instead, I get something like:

1994     1
```

#### ↳ Re: String To Integer?

- **From:** gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T21:31:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2503699.1044826269@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2503685.1044799758@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi Yauyau

This is a little solution to your problem.

PERFORM VARYING K FROM 10 BY -1 UNTIL YEARSTR(K:1) NOT = SPACES
      CONTINUE
END-PERFORM

MOVE YEARSTR (1:K) TO YEAR...

That's all

An advice: of course, the YEARSTR field must have at least 4 chars.

Hope in this help.

Gianni
```

#### ↳ Re: String To Integer?

- **From:** yauyau <member23982@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T04:58:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2503726.1044853131@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2503685.1044799758@dbforums.com>`

```

Thank you~~^^
```

```
01  var-b            pic 9(?????).

move function numval(var-a) to var-b

Sample:

var-a 01/01/2003           new value 01012003
var-a pic x(80) value "4" new value 4

Marcos Antonio de Souza
Brasil
```

#### ↳ Re: String To Integer?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T16:30:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R12dnfxLm44nutWjXTWchw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<2503685.1044799758@dbforums.com>`

```

"yauyau" <member23982@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:2503685.1044799758@dbforums.com...
>
> How to convert a String to Integer?
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1994     1

First, don't do it like that. Don't move alphanumeric fields to numeric
fields. The main reason for avoiding this practice is you can't guarantee
the content of the alphanumeric field. In your case, what if YEARSTR
contained "Wombat?"

Secondly, you've hit the confusion the compiler faces: It thinks your entire
YEARSTR is a number and forces the last byte to be a 'positive' integer
(zero). The compiler further believes the intervening bytes to be zeros. If
you added 11 to YEAR, you'd get: 1994    11.

If you can't use FUNCTION NUMVAL, then you'll have to decide the size of the
sending field on your own and move it to the appropriate-sized receiving
field.

Alternatively, you can move non-blank bytes (from right to left) to the
receiving field.
```

#### ↳ Re: String To Integer?

- **From:** "kmj" <k.moeller@mssys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-18T13:56:36+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2taib$gcq$04$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<2503685.1044799758@dbforums.com>`

```

Try this

01 filler.
  05 YearStr  Pic x(004).
  05 Year-N  pic 9(004) redefines YearStr.

or

01 YearStr.
  05 Year-N  pic 9(004) .

news:2503685.1044799758@dbforums.com...

…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **From:** "Rick S." <ricks123@hotpop.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T17:30:56+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b25vo3$imr$1@news-reader11.wanadoo.fr>`
- **References:** `<b1a0d1$gg6$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
"max Boettner" <max.boettner@t-online.de> wrote in message
news:b1a0d1$gg6$03$1@news.t-online.com...
> How can a cobol program pass a variable to JCL, so that JCL can use the
> content?

It's about a week late for this, but I noticed that nobody in here
mentioned the obvious detail that, at least as far as my VS COBOL
II book is concerned, where they write:

"in a COBOL program you can move a value from 0 to 4095
to a special register called RETURN-CODE. RETURN-CODE is
automatically passed to the operating system and becomes visible
as COND CODE for the step at which the program is ececuted".

But that much, one assumes, was obvious, I guess. And maybe it
has even changed a bit with more recent compilers ?  But I don't
think so... probably still like that, I'd guess.
```

## Thread: How to insert variable no. of spaces in string? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to insert variable no. of spaces in string?

- **From:** yauyau <member23982@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T19:19:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2503691.1044818366@dbforums.com>`

```

I want to insert a variable number of spaces at start of a String

SPACENO    PIC 99.

STRINGLINE  PIC X(80)

I can only think of using perform loop to make this...

PERFORM VARYING J FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL J > SPACENO
                        STRING " " INTO STRINGLINE
                  END-PERFORM
STRING "abc" INTO STRINGLINE

but it seems once I call STRING next time, it can't concatenate...it
"OVERWRITE" the original string in STRINGLINE.
>___< I have no idea.......May anyone help me,please?


//P.S. The assignment have to be handed in this week...and I still
thinking hard on string concatenation.....my God.........
```

#### ↳ Re: How to insert variable no. of spaces in string?

- **From:** gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-09T21:44:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2503701.1044827075@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2503691.1044818366@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi again

It's near to previous solution....

Suppose you need to insert 10 space chars before some values:

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  strspace          pic x(10) value spaces.
01  strvalue          pic x(10) value "Yauyau".
01  strResult         pic x(20).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      STRING   strspace
                    strvalue          DELIMITED SIZE
                    INTO              strResult

You should see the result like: "          Yauyau".

Another way is insert a variable number of spaces like:

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  strspace          pic x value space.
01  strvalue          pic x(10) value "Yauyau".
01  strResult         pic x(20).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
STRING   strspace    (1:10)   *> this means, put 10 spaces
              strvalue          DELIMITED SIZE
                    INTO              strResult


Hope in this help

Gianni
```

#### ↳ Re: How to insert variable no. of spaces in string?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-11T07:47:29+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b28s6g$31i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2503691.1044818366@dbforums.com>`

```
yauyau wrote:

> 
> I want to insert a variable number of spaces at start of a String
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> STRING "abc" INTO STRINGLINE

The WITH POINTER clause of the STRING statement specifies the position to 
start stringing.

       MOVE SPACES     TO StringLine
       STRING "abc" 
          INTO StringLine
          WITH POINTER SpaceNo

As the pointer is updated it can be use to concatenate using several 
separate STRING statements.  Output areas for STRING and UNSTRING should 
always be set to an initial value, usually spaces.
```

---

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol GUI interface on Linux

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-11T14:47:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g13i4v0n2gr8573ehk0njl9m1dju90r8j1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<2503702.1044827707@dbforums.com> <2512003.1044950024@dbforums.com>`

```
gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>Hello Jerry
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Gianni

Gianni:

We are working with Shawn right now to support KOBOL with our product
line.

Once we have support for KOBOL, our Web Client product could be used
to display interactive data entry screens within a KDE or Gnome
compatible web browser on Linux.

The beauty of this solution is that you can still use the same screen
definitions and same COBOL source program to also run a native Windows
version of your program (if you choose to do so).  You can also take
advantage of our Thin Client product and run your KOBOL program on a
Linux server and display the user interface screens across TCP/IP on
Windows based PC in 100% Windows GUI.  Again.....you use the same
source program and the same screen definitions.

It makes absolutely no sense to maintain multiple versions of your
program if you need to support multiple environments or multiple
compilers.  We solve that problem for you.

Contact me by personal e-mail if you have additional questions.




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

- **References:** `<2503702.1044827707@dbforums.com> <2512003.1044950024@dbforums.com> <g13i4v0n2gr8573ehk0njl9m1dju90r8j1@4ax.com> <217e491a.0302111155.741806d3@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>and it allows several programs to access one file with no locking.

Richard:

I'm not sure.  That's a question for Shawn Gordon.  Because it only
takes us a day or two to support additional COBOL compilers, it
doesn't cost us a lot to offer support for as many compilers as we
possibly can.

That's our primary motivation to support KOBOL.  It makes it a bit
easier for our customers knowing that they can switch compilers very
easily and without re-writing a lot of screen handling code.

Compilers have come and have gone and it's difficult when you are
forced to re-write a lot of source code.  That's why we try to support
as many as possible.






Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
Hi,

GUI for Cobol with Linux

a) Kobol - TheKompany will realize this year an interface between
           Kobol and QT-Libraries (KDE). I have tested to link Kobol-calls
           into MF-runtime, it is possible, so we shall see what the future
           will bring (but I mean the Qt is not easy for a Cobol programer)

  (MF,Acu) - Tcl/Tk is running on every platform - you can download it
           from http://sourceforge.net/projects/legacytcl.
            Tcl/Tk is on every Linux distribution. Put "wish" on the shell
           and you can start to build the first widgets interactive ...
Ciao
```

## Thread: Cobol to ActiveX (Foxpro?) — 17 msgs

### Cobol to ActiveX (Foxpro?)

- **From:** tickin@hotmail.com (Zack)
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T07:05:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95a74459.0302100705.6e9e86a0@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi folks,

  Firstly, I have to say I've got very little programming experience
under my belt except for a few batchfiles and some goto's on an Apple
IIe  :o)  So please forgive me if my question(s) are a little twitish.

  I'm trying to help a friend who is moving his info off an IBM MVS
Cobol (jcl) environment into, hopefully, a Windows Foxpro environment.
 The University that ran his small programs is moving to a Unix system
and is booting their few remaining clients (he may be the only one
left).  So, he's stuck...

  The gist of it is, is there a fairly easy way of converting his
existing Cobol programs into something that will run in Foxpro
(version 6)?  He has copies of the Cobol progams in 'text' form (I'm
not sure if that is the correct lingo) and he has the compiler too
(I'm not sure what product or version it is).

  Should I be thinking along the lines of translating the lines from
Cobol's format to an ActiveX format (I read somewhere that Foxpro will
run an ActiveX routine)?  Is that the path of least resistance (it
goes without saying that I haven't the faintest idea how to do that)? 
Or is there some sort of utility that would make the job easier?  I'm
completely out of my realm here...

  Any help you folks can send me would be greatly appreciated..
including a pointer to the effect that I have successfully
(unfortunately) posted to the wrong news group.

Thanks,
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol to ActiveX (Foxpro?)

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T17:16:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<T7R1a.2539$PH1.1655314@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<95a74459.0302100705.6e9e86a0@posting.google.com>`

```
"Zack" <tickin@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:95a74459.0302100705.6e9e86a0@posting.google.com...
>   The gist of it is, is there a fairly easy way of converting his
> existing Cobol programs into something that will run in Foxpro
> (version 6)?

No. Foxpro has its own programming language, which is not COBOL or even
"COBOL-like."

> He has copies of the Cobol progams in 'text' form (I'm not sure if that is
the correct lingo) and he has the compiler too
> (I'm not sure what product or version it is).

This is good, because this sounds like the 'source code.'  You definitely do
NOT want to lose this, as it more than likely contains details of the
business logic which are not documented anywhere else.

>   Should I be thinking along the lines of translating the lines from
Cobol's format to an ActiveX format

Cobol "format?" Active-X "format?"     Whatever you mean by "format",
"COBOL" is a programming Language, "Active-X" is an adjective describing a
certain type of executable file (if you really want to know, it's an
in-process OLE server).  You cannot translate/convert/port from a
programming language to a file specification... that's like asking if you
should convert your apples and oranges into dump trucks.

> Or is there some sort of utility that would make the job easier?  I'm
completely out of my realm here...

Yes, I'd have to agree.  You need to find someone a lot more knowledgeable
about "software development" in general and the business system in specific.
I'm not even sure there is a 'correct' newsgroup for this kind of thing.
But, since you are starting with a COBOL system which needs maintenance,
comp.lang.cobol is about a good a place to start as any.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol to ActiveX (Foxpro?)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T17:51:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e47e56f.162748553@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<95a74459.0302100705.6e9e86a0@posting.google.com>`

```
tickin@hotmail.com (Zack) wrote:

>  I'm trying to help a friend who is moving his info off an IBM MVS
>Cobol (jcl) environment into, hopefully, a Windows Foxpro environment.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>completely out of my realm here...

The path of least resistance is forget Foxpro. Just compile them with a PC-based
COBOL compiler. If he uses Micro Focus, he might not have to change a single
line of code. 

Also, he can easily duplicate JCL functions by using the Windows Scripting Tool
or even (gag) DOS .bat files.

Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol to ActiveX (Foxpro?)

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T16:14:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cicnSLCAfD9udWjXTWchA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<95a74459.0302100705.6e9e86a0@posting.google.com>`

```

"Zack" <tickin@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:95a74459.0302100705.6e9e86a0@posting.google.com...
> Hi folks,
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks,

No. Apples and monkeys-beating-drums.

He needs a COBOL compiler for Unix, not a different programming language.

He can:
1. Reprogram his existing program's concepts into a new language (Foxpro) -
if the new language can handle the concepts. This is very, very close to
starting over.
2. Get a new compiler to allow his existing source code to work with the new
operating system. This will entail some (usually small amount of ) work on
his part in adjusting his existing program.

Even if he opted for choice #1, it is problematic whether Foxpro is capable
of doing everything his COBOL programs accomplish. Foxpro is not a Turing
machine; COBOL is.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol to ActiveX (Foxpro?)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-11T12:37:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e48ec6a.230081822@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<95a74459.0302100705.6e9e86a0@posting.google.com> <8cicnSLCAfD9udWjXTWchA@giganews.com> <95a74459.0302102056.e38f846@posting.google.com>`

```
tickin@hotmail.com (Zack) wrote:

>  Please correct me if I've misunderstood but, as far as I can tell
>from your  comments, I should be able to move his data files
>(presently in ASCII format) onto a Windows (98? 2K?) PC; then compile
>his existing Cobol programs (presently in 'text' form) using a program
>called Micro Focus; 

Files on the mainframe use the EBCDIC character set (not format). There are many
tools for converting EBCDIC to the ASCII character set. It was probably done by
the mainframe folks who offloaded his files and handed them to him. 

>and once that done, I should be able to 'copy and
>paste' his JCL functions into a Windows Script and run the whole
>process from that setup, and on a single Windows computer.  Is that
>the idea?  Or am I missing a step or two or five.

JCL requires a rewrite, not a cut and paste. Doing so is relatively easy. 

The COBOL source programs will require little or no modification.

>  Also, on the topic of Micro Focus, I had a quick look at their
>website and I'm assuming that the compiler Robert mentioned is
>contained within their "Developer Suite".  But I got the impression
>that the package was designed to run on a Unix or Linux system... is
>that correct?  Or will it run on a single Windows system too?

Yes, Micro Focus compiles and runs on Windows. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ JCL conversion easy? was Re: Cobol to ActiveX (Foxpro?)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-02-11T09:57:23-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E490143.B60E8FCF@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<95a74459.0302100705.6e9e86a0@posting.google.com> <8cicnSLCAfD9udWjXTWchA@giganews.com> <95a74459.0302102056.e38f846@posting.google.com> <3e48ec6a.230081822@news.optonline.net>`

```
Converting batch command streams from one environment to another is not
necessarily easy.  IBM mainframe JCL of either the MVS (now z/OS) or VSE
variety is especially obscure and each contains functions not found in
other environments. 
The utilities such as sorts, file copy utilities or report writers may
not be present in the new environment or have different syntax (the
HP-UX version of SYNCSORT comes to mind where it has a modern and
readable syntax as opposed to the obscure position, length mess on z/OS
and presumably VSE).  Depending on how much JCL is used and the amount
of change being made to the application the exercise could be quite
interesting.  

My comments about mainframe JCL come from 10 years of DOS for S360
(releases 18 - 26) and 17 years experience from MVS to OS390 where I was
technical support either as an applications programmer or systems
programmer. 
Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
> tickin@hotmail.com (Zack) wrote:
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL conversion easy? was Re: Cobol to ActiveX (Foxpro?)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-11T22:45:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e497a5b.266423611@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<95a74459.0302100705.6e9e86a0@posting.google.com> <8cicnSLCAfD9udWjXTWchA@giganews.com> <95a74459.0302102056.e38f846@posting.google.com> <3e48ec6a.230081822@news.optonline.net> <3E490143.B60E8FCF@istar.ca>`

```
The main feature of JCL is the DD statement, whereby an internal file
name/handle is associated with a 'real' external file name. All one needs to do
is replace 'DD internal EFN='external'' with 'SET internal=external'. How hard
is that?

Few use IF statements in IBM JCL because they're so lame. That will not be an
issue. 

As you say, the biggest potential challenge is third-party tools. I doubt the
college professor's home-grown system uses them heavily, or at all.

Robert

"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

>Converting batch command streams from one environment to another is not
>necessarily easy.  IBM mainframe JCL of either the MVS (now z/OS) or VSE
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Files on the mainframe use the EBCDIC character set (not format). There are
many
>> tools for converting EBCDIC to the ASCII character set. It was probably done
by
>> the mainframe folks who offloaded his files and handed them to him.
>> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL conversion easy? was Re: Cobol to ActiveX (Foxpro?)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-12T09:18:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C2B10BE95B16DAA0.80278EF38C8F0095.68FAC2C92EF2FB9C@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<95a74459.0302100705.6e9e86a0@posting.google.com> <8cicnSLCAfD9udWjXTWchA@giganews.com> <95a74459.0302102056.e38f846@posting.google.com> <3e48ec6a.230081822@news.optonline.net> <3E490143.B60E8FCF@istar.ca> <3e497a5b.266423611@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Tue, 11 Feb 2003 22:45:58 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
enlightened us:

>The main feature of JCL is the DD statement, whereby an internal file
>name/handle is associated with a 'real' external file name. All one needs to do
>is replace 'DD internal EFN='external'' with 'SET internal=external'. How hard
>is that?
>

The DD statement, as you should know, is only valid in z/OS operating
systems.  It has no place in VSE.  Also, I would hardly call it the
main feature of z/OS JCL.  I would tend to think EXEC PGM= to be the
main feature of any z/OS JCL block.  Those DD statements don't mean
diddly squat without telling the system what uses them (unless they
are part of an IEFBR14 job stream of course).
 

>Few use IF statements in IBM JCL because they're so lame. That will not be an
>issue. 
>

And of course you have a survey or some other means to justify calling
IF statements in JCL lame?  I think not.  I've seen countless sets of
conditional JCL (usually PROCs but not always) that use the IF.  It is
a very nice way to bypass steps or do other things depending on
conditions that happened prior to the IF.  It is by no means lame.
Limited in scope, maybe, but not lame.

>As you say, the biggest potential challenge is third-party tools. I doubt the
>college professor's home-grown system uses them heavily, or at all.
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> Robert

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I don't mind the rat race, but I could do with a little more
cheese.
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL conversion easy? was Re: Cobol to ActiveX (Foxpro?)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-15T00:26:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e4d894b.149887685@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<95a74459.0302100705.6e9e86a0@posting.google.com> <8cicnSLCAfD9udWjXTWchA@giganews.com> <95a74459.0302102056.e38f846@posting.google.com> <3e48ec6a.230081822@news.optonline.net> <3E490143.B60E8FCF@istar.ca> <3e497a5b.266423611@news.optonline.net> <C2B10BE95B16DAA0.80278EF38C8F0095.68FAC2C92EF2FB9C@lp.airnews.net> <3e4ac867.351953389@news.optonline.net> <95a74459.0302140559.39ad2338@posting.google.com>`

```
VSE was an old operating system from the '70s. It was replaced by MVS, now
called z/OS, in the '80s. It's like the difference between DOS and Windows. VSE
was originally called "DOS". VSE is inexpensive; z/OS is expensive .. several
thousand dollars per month, except for universities who get a deep discount. 

Internationally, VSE is is the most used mainframe operating system .. solely
because it's cheap. In the US, only a handfull of companies use it. Possibly the
largest is Winn-Dixie (supermarkets) out of Jacksonville, Fl. No university
would use VSE because a) it's been obsolete for 20 years and b) they can get
z/OS for the same price.

IBM updates the z/OS COBOL compiler frequently. The university's compiler is
eleven years old because the univerity opted-out from maintenance. 

This sounds like a religious war where Unix-heads are expunging the infidel
mainframers. That's ok, the COBOL programs will run the same on Unix or PC
platforms with MUCH lower cost. 

Robert
```

```
In article <95a74459.0302120408.111ace92@posting.google.com>,
Zack <tickin@hotmail.com> wrote:

[snip]

>  If that's a mistake (please let me know) I'm afraid at the moment I
>have no other choice but to start that way - I was evidently wrong in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

This situation ('people... less than interested in providing any sort of 
help') may be a variant of Rule Number Zero.

Rule #0 (it is too lazy to be Rule #1) is:

'Nobody wants to do any work.'

Corollary 1 to Rule 0 is 'Some people will expend more energy to avoid 
doing work than actually doing the work would have required them to 
expend.'

DD
```

<a id="t-5ee3cf7544"></a>
## Thread: multidimensional table — 10 msgs

### multidimensional table

- **From:** yauyau <member23982@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T16:01:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2503891.1044892919@dbforums.com>`

```

I am using Fujitsu COBOL85.

I want to access an element of multidimensional table {which I use as an
array in other language}, but I am not familiar how to access data in a
multidimensional table.


000470 01   DATEVL IS GLOBAL.
000480      03 MONTH OCCURS 12.
000490         05 WEEK OCCURS 6.
000500            07 WKDAY OCCURS 7 PIC XX.

If I want to assign '1' to DATEVL[1,1,7], should I write the
following code?

MOVE '1' TO DATEVL(1 1 7 )
```

#### ↳ Re: multidimensional table

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-10T18:35:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e47edd1.164894920@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2503891.1044892919@dbforums.com>`

```
yauyau <member23982@dbforums.com> wrote:

>I am using Fujitsu COBOL85.
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>MOVE '1' TO DATEVL(1 1 7 )

Yes. Stylistically, most COBOL programmers put a space after the L and
comma-space after the two 1s. 

Your problem may be the order in which elements are stored in memory. There is
no standard for that. Your other language may use a convention different from
Fujitsu. You should write test programs which construct the array in COBOL,
display it in the other language. 

Robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: multidimensional table

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
> Stylistically, most COBOL programmers put a space after the L and
> comma-space after the two 1s.

or put a space before the subscript ?  or did you just make that up ?

> Your problem may be the order in which elements are stored in memory.
> There is no standard for that. 

There certainly is in COBOL.

> Your other language may use a convention
> different from Fujitsu. You should write test programs which construct the
> array in COBOL, display it in the other language.

I didn't see anywhere that he wanted to interface any languages.
```
