##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/DIALOGUE System

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T23:33:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E4C2B7C.3A8C309@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<Wdz2a.904$I06.332@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <a0Odnau-V_LBNtajXTWckA@giganews.com>`

```


JerryMouse wrote:

> "Eddie Veness" <e.veness@blueyonder.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:Wdz2a.904$I06.332@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Are you trying to scare him to death Jerry :-) He did say he was a 'beginner'.
Hopefully he's OK. I'm not familiar with Dialog System (Pete and Thane both
Fujitsu users know more about Dialog System than I'll ever know - or want to
know !). For  each control the DS system allows him to add in-line code to do
his validity checks. If that's not clear then Calin can jump in and expand.

>
> The user leaves field A, triggering some kind of lost-focus event. Your code
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> What's needed (I'm told) is a robust VALIDATE method as in VB.

I agree with you gained focus, lost focus and the Event Loop are a real pig,
depending upon what you are trying to do. For the following reasons I use both
'gain' and 'lost' events :-

- Gain Focus - I find it most irritating to backspace to 'clear' an existing
field; so I have two options.  (1) 'clear' or 'setEmpty' object - accepts fresh
input from user. From memory that is the way it works with COBOL Screen
Section.  (2) Insert mode - don't clear the field, say for SIN, VAT, GST and any
other big numbers. Allow the user to 'insert' new characters as appropriate.

- Lost Focus (1) Do I want to return directly to Business Logic now - user has
entered a new PrimeKey - so check it exists and either display or accept input
for new record. (2) For convenience (some 70% of the application's input) is
decimal inches/cms - 9.999 or 99.999 - which are entered as integers - on lost
focus redisplay with decimal point. (3) Third possibility - user clicks on a
checkbox or radio button - this gives two options (a) Retain value selected
until PB-OK entered or (b) must return the check box/radio button value NOW as
Business Logic may determine that additional controls (currently hidden) now
need to be 'activated' with set enable and show.

I  have real fun using both 'gained' and 'lost'.  For your ALLOW-FOCUS flag I
utilize methods "accessSystemEvents" and "cancelAccessToSystemEvents" for the
next object(control).

I try to stay within the logic of the Dialog - such as redisplaying decimal
numbers, and same applies to dates where user enters for YYMM a value of '1' -
then I will re-display as 'Jan 2001'. Kind of goes against the grain but for the
most part I'm currently opting for validating input when user enters PB-OK which
triggers the return to the Business class.

I wouldn't claim my approach is the simplest - but with a lot of buggering
around it works !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: Logarithm — 3 msgs

### Logarithm

- **From:** berny <member24880@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T07:54:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2523393.1045122848@dbforums.com>`

```

I'm trying to found a function in cobol to compute
the logarith of a number in a specified base

y = LOG(number, base)

Does anyone know how i can do that ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Logarithm

- **From:** "Don Higgins" <don.higgins@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T08:30:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2g6gg$iv5$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<2523393.1045122848@dbforums.com>`

```
Here is sample source to calculate log to any base in
Micro Focus Cobol:

  identification division.
       program-id. TESTLOG1.
       environment division.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
       data division.
       file section.
       working-storage section.
       77  c1-x       comp-1 value 1000.  *> number
       77  c1-b       comp-1 value 10.    *> base of log
       77  c1-loge-x  comp-1.  *> log base e of x
       77  c1-loge-b  comp-1.  *> log base e of b
       77  c1-logb-x  comp-1.  *> log base b of x (= 3)
       procedure division.
           display "TESTLOG1 starting"
           display "c1-x number   = " c1-x
           display "c1-b base     = " c1-b
           compute  c1-loge-x = function log(c1-x)
           display "c1-loge of x  = " c1-loge-x
           compute  c1-loge-b = function log(c1-b)
           display "c1-loge of b  = " c1-loge-b
           compute  c1-logb-x     =   c1-loge-x / c1-loge-b
           display "c1-logb of x  = " c1-logb-x
           display "TESTLOG1 ending"
           goback
           .
The results using Mainframe Express 2.5 SP1 were:

Micro Focus IDE V3.0.G3
RUN TIME ENVIRONMENT Copyright (C) 1984-2003 Micro Focus International Ltd.
URN AXCGG/AA0/00000
TESTLOG1 starting
c1-x number   =  .10000000E 04
c1-b base     =  .10000000E 02
c1-loge of x  =  .69077554E 01
c1-loge of b  =  .23025851E 01
c1-logb of x  =  .30000000E 01
TESTLOG1 ending

Hope that helps:

Don Higgins
don.higgins@microfocus.com
02/12/03

news:2523393.1045122848@dbforums.com...

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Logarithm

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T14:24:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2524328.1045146263@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2523393.1045122848@dbforums.com>`

```

Microfocus Cobol



The LOG10 function returns a numeric value that approximates the
logarithm to the base 10 of argument-1. The type of this function
is numeric.

General Format

FUNCTION LOG10 (argument-1)

Arguments

1.      Argument-1 must be class numeric.
2.      The value of argument-1 must be greater than zero.

Returned Values

1.      The returned value is the approximation of the logarithm to the
        base 10 of argument-1.
2.      Floating-point format is used for numeric non-integer results.


-------------------------------------------------------------

The LOG function returns a numeric value that approximates the
logarithm to the base e (natural log) of argument-1. The type of this
function is numeric.

General Format

FUNCTION LOG (argument-1)

Arguments

1.      Argument-1 must be class numeric.
2.      The value of argument-1 must be greater than zero.

Returned Values

1.      The returned value is the approximation of the logarithm to the
        base e of argument-1.
2.      Floating-point format is used for numeric non-integer results.


Marcos Antonio de Souza
Brasil
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus:File status 9/025

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T07:09:52+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2gn4s$gl4$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2523922.1045136090@dbforums.com> <2523927.1045138472@dbforums.com>`

```
mfcobol2002 wrote:

> I have that problem today. a workstation uses, it liberates, the times
> leaves and if other to use the same file or registration of the
> mistake 9/025.
> I used cbl_alloc_mem and it didn't solve.
> what do make?

If you are using cbl_alloc_mem in your program and it gives a completely 
spurious error, such as as 9/025, or otherwise behaves is really strange 
ways then it is probably because you are not handling the allocated memory 
correctly.

Using pointers allows you to accidentally write to _any_ location and not 
just the places that the compiler sets for you.  If you have coded your 
program wrongly then you could be overwriting data that doesn't belong to 
you.
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-3 or COMP-4

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-02-16T10:47:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2535627.1045392465@dbforums.com>`

```

as it is a new resource I wanted to know if it would be worthwhile to
know. another friend explained to me and the best option is the comp-5.
Thank you for the attention.

I lower his answer.


In the original question a piece of coding was included with the
question "correct or not".

I'd say: No, not correct. COMP-4 means integer so no "V" (assumed
decimal position) may not be part of the PICTURE clause.

Comp-3 in a Micro Focus environment is only usefull when you want to
efficient memory usage.
E.g:

01  pretty-big-array occurs 10000 indexed by whatever-idx.
    03 whatever-amount pic S9(9)V9(2) usage display.
    03 whatever-percent pic 9(2)v9(1) usage display.

This array occupies a lot of RAM.
Changing the usage display into comp-3 will divide the memory
consumption by 2, but calculations will not be efficient (yes very
efficient on mainframes, but no Micro Focurs there).

When you want best of both worlds you could code:

01  pretty-big-array occurs 10000 indexed by whatever-idx.
    03 whatever-amount-in-cents pic S9(11) comp-5.
    03 whatever-promille        pic  9(04) comp-5.

promille english? don't know. hope you understand what I mean

But now you must change your compute's. You gain on speed and
memory usage, but you loose the easy and intuitive way you can
calculate in cobol.

I am learning JAVA now and I am suprised that the easy way in which
cobol can cope with all kinds of numeric data and "tricks"  with
redefines and so on are not part of this new language. Never realized
how "easy & relaxed" cobol really is!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-3 or COMP-4

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-16T15:16:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fXN3a.1801$UF6.596331@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<2524327.1045145919@dbforums.com> <2535627.1045392465@dbforums.com>`

```
"mfcobol2002" <marcos_as@terra.com.br> wrote in message
news:2535627.1045392465@dbforums.com...
>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> decimal position) may not be part of the PICTURE clause.

The PICTURE IS clause for items with USAGE IS COMP, COMP-3, COMP-4 and
COMP-5 certainly may have a "V" to represent an implied decimal point. The
"integer representation" is only for internal purposes, and the compiler
WILL generate code to divide by ten the correct number of times for all
MOVE, COMPUTE, ADD, SUBTRACT, etc statements.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-3 or COMP-4

```
>

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

So what? The OS is going to handle memory management anyway.

>
> When you want best of both worlds you could code:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> calculate in cobol.

You don't always gain in speed and memory usage. Much depends on the
calculation - for example, mixing data types (COMP-3 = DISPLAY + COMP-4) and
what COBOL has to do 'under the hood' to convert various data types in
preparation for the desired calculation.

You never lose the easy way to 'code' the calculation.

>
> I am learning JAVA now and I am suprised that the easy way in which
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
you might enjoy this link.

http://home.nc.rr.com/deh/GALLERY/INDEX.HTM

Dang I think I wrote code for most of that stuff.

PatH...who feels a little older today
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: trip down memory lane

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T09:35:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e4cb873.96416237@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E4BB703.6DC345EE@certcoinc.com>`

```
Pat Hall <phall@certcoinc.com> wrote:

>For some of you old timers out there.  DD this may be you<G>.  I thought
>you might enjoy this link.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>PatH...who feels a little older today


Excepting the 1401, this is all NEW stuff. I wish they had pictures of the 704,
650 and especially the 7090 data-entry keyboard, which looked like a piano
keyboard with 36 keys. A proficient operator keying six bits at at time with
three fingers of each hand looked like he was playing a piano. Another nice
touch would be a 7044 console with 2-3 Selectric typewriter guts in the
background, awaiting duty when the operator terminal failed. A 407 plugboard
would be ultime de la ultime from that era. 

Moving forward (time waits for no man), the RCA 301 deserves a museum of its own
for the mickey-mouse peripherals it fielded. For instance, a disk drive made by
Wurlitzer that looked and functioned like a juke box. And a CPU that stopped and
turned on a red light when an I/O error occurred. I imagine conferences at RCA
headquarters in the David Sarnoff Building where high-level decisions were made
.. 'We don't have time for that error-recovery crap. Our ship date is next week.
Just do something to make the problem go away.' Those were heady times. 

Robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: trip down memory lane

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T09:59:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j633a.52036$zF6.3486025@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3E4BB703.6DC345EE@certcoinc.com> <3e4cb873.96416237@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message news:3e4cb873.96416237@news.optonline.net...
>
> A 407 plugboard would be ultime de la ultime from that era.

I vividly remember the daily, sometimes hourly, battles
to keep other people from ripping down standard boards
used on a daily basis so that they could run one-shot jobs.

Some of the actual physical tug-of-war matches were legendary.
Eventually, we resorted to lockable covers to protect the payroll boards.
Amazing, but 100% true.

The ne plus ultra however, occurred when my supervisor
interspersed-master-card-gangpunched the job cost master file.

The noise from the IBM 519 reproducing card punch was thunderous
in its final few moments.  The sight of my rather portly supervisor
flying across the machine room to hit the Stop button in a red-faced panic
is etched on my mind for all time.  A priceless memory.
```

```
>> 
>> Excepting the 1401, this is all NEW stuff. I wish they had pictures of the
704,
>> 650 and especially the 7090 data-entry keyboard, which looked like a piano
>> keyboard with 36 keys. A proficient operator keying six bits at at time with
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>foodman123.com/history.htm

Excellent page. Thanks for the pointer. All except IBM gave prices! 

I had a 1650 which ran the catylitic cracker at the Richfield (now ARCO)
refinery in San Pedro, Ca. It was and is one of the largest refineries in the
US. Normally we turned crude oil into octane and heptane, which are the
components of gasoline. Occasionally,  to show our chemical virtuosity, we
produced other products such as tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), the active
ingredient in pot. A 55 gal drum was probably worth tens of thousands. We just
flushed it down the sewer and congratulated each other on a job well done. 

Rolling on the floor laughing.

Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: trip down memory lane

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-02-16T07:01:16+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<20030216020116.14441.00000609@mb-mw.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3E4BB703.6DC345EE@certcoinc.com>`

```
Yes that did bring back memories.  I operated and coded for those - and like
the ones in the pictures they had red panels. I also remember that when we
upgraded from a 360/30 to a 360/40 they brought one with yellow panels - dang
thing never worked correctly until they replaced the panels with red ones.  The
panels were replaced because another operator kicked in one of them in
frustration :)

Brings back memories of my first night on the job (3rd shift) as an operator
trainee and watched as the senior operator crashed 3 disk drives and 2 packs,
then going on to first shift and the string that held the pencil to the
clipboard we used as our schedule get wrapped around the emergency pull knob
and shut everything down, and the day the motor on the drive that contained the
sysres pack caught fire, the day security escorted the IBM CE off the premises
because he IPL'd the machine after maintenance when he was specifically told
not to because we hadn't pull the data from the CICS log files yet.  Ah, those
were happy times :)
```

<a id="t-62a497279f"></a>
## Thread: Cobol conversion from OS/2 to Windows XP — 3 msgs

### Cobol conversion from OS/2 to Windows XP

- **From:** keith.schoen@mto.gov.on.ca (Keith Schoen)
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T10:35:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3756b8b2.0302131035.320da5f1@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi there,

I'm on a COBOL conversion project where we have been asked to convert
the existing OS/2 cobol code(coded in CA-Realia with a screen io
product called SCREENIO) to a Windows XP version of Cobol.

Currently we are looking at Fujitsu Cobol compiler with SP2 to draw
the screens.
I was wondering if anyone has done this sort of thing before and could
recommend a type of compiler and screen painter. (Or even if a product
exists that can take the current OS/2 code and translate it to work on
Windows XP)

thanks,
Keith
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol conversion from OS/2 to Windows XP

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T13:42:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2gsg1$v4c$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3756b8b2.0302131035.320da5f1@posting.google.com>`

```
CA-Realia's code is pretty much an "emulation" of IBM mainframe code.
Fujtisu provides "full" support for all IBM dialects *thru* VS COBOL II.
Therefore, I don't think that you should have much difficulty with the COBOL
source conversion (possibly NONE).

As far as ScreenIO vs sp2 - I think that Bob Wolfe probably can help you
with what tools are available for that - or how much effort it would take.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol conversion from OS/2 to Windows XP

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T19:46:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6rsn4vkf126t5068tgbkbs9e4f92pisf3r@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3756b8b2.0302131035.320da5f1@posting.google.com>`

```
Keith:

I have worked with Chris Wiles on this project in the past and
assisted another one of your associates just the other day.

I'm not sure if you are aware of this or not, but we have a screen
converter for Screenio screens and an emulator which allows you to run
your existing code and use COBOL sp2 for your GUI screens.

Contact me at rtwolfe@flexus.com for additional details.  We also
gladly provide technical support services during the evaluation
period.

keith.schoen@mto.gov.on.ca (Keith Schoen) wrote:

>Hi there,
```

<a id="t-25d90568c0"></a>
## Thread: IMS/DC: Use of SPA area — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IMS/DC: Use of SPA area

- **From:** alexmcshane@blueyonder.co.uk (Alex McShane)
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T11:28:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dc8c3ee.0302131128.3894077f@posting.google.com>`

```
Overview:
Whilst developing a new Pension Quotes system, we have been asked to
improve the robustness of the existing system.
An immediate problem with the latter is that due to the large amount
of working storage required, system abends are frequently encountered.
(It is written in OS/VS COBOL and therefore is constained by 24-Bit
addressing)
 
Possible Solution
For the given IMS/DC Transaction, define a SPA scratch area.
The working Pension Quote array can be logically broken down into
various areas.
Due to the limitation of the remaining storage "window" of the OS/VS
COBOL programs to the SPA, "paging" would have to be used to Write and
Read the array.
That is, Build Sect-1, Read, free storage, Build-Sect-2, Read, Free
storage, etc.
   
Requested Advice:-
1) Is it possible to define a SPA scratch area that is greater than
32768 bytes?
   For example, 1Mbyte.

2) Is it possible to associate a Transaction with multiple SPA areas?
   For example, four
```

#### ↳ Re: IMS/DC: Use of SPA area

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T13:01:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E4C079A.A9A50F39@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<dc8c3ee.0302131128.3894077f@posting.google.com>`

```
Alex,
Aren't you considering upgrading to a supported run time (Language
Environment) and COBOL compiler?  There is no reason to be constrained by
24-bit addressing limits.  It is often surprisingly easy to migrate a
COBOL program upwards, even to the latest COBOL compiler (Enterprise COBOL
for z/OS and OS/390 V3R2, if you weren't aware).

You aren't still using the version of the operating system and IMS for
which the application was originally written, are you?  Then why haven't
you kept the application tools current, too?  (This is not a stab at you
or your company, but it is one of the most overlooked aspects of system
maintenance.  My company has customers with tens of thousands of COBOL
load modules that are still OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL II, etc., etc.)

Alex McShane wrote:

> Overview:
> Whilst developing a new Pension Quotes system, we have been asked to
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>    For example, four
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IMS/DC: Use of SPA area

- **From:** alexmcshane@blueyonder.co.uk (Alex McShane)
- **Date:** 2003-02-16T03:47:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dc8c3ee.0302160347.6f0b3924@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dc8c3ee.0302131128.3894077f@posting.google.com> <3E4C079A.A9A50F39@attglobal.net>`

```
Thanks Colin for taking the time to reply. 
The Client has for some time being using Z/OS operating system and
COBOL 2 in
new applications.
Even for the old applications, the IMS/DC Regions are defined with 8MB
of storage.
The problem is that many of the old OS/VS COBOL programs are written
in a generated code called Delta or Assembler. Although several years
ago the Client embarked on a project to automatically migrate the
Delta to COBOL II, after a couple of years, it was aborted!
As I was saying, the new development of this Pension Quotes systen
entails using all the latest features of MVS and COBOL II, including
dynamically allocating arrays.
However, in the meantime, the existing system has operating problems
due to complex Quotes occassionally requiring more storage than can be
read by the OS/VS COBOL program.

I think there is a DB2 solution to their immediate problem entailing
using
Global Temporary tables.  

Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote in message news:<3E4C079A.A9A50F39@attglobal.net>...

…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
```

<a id="t-94f706fa72"></a>
## Thread: [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card — 21 msgs

### [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T15:36:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2gvk5$f7f$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

All righty... here's the situation.  There's an input file, flat,
containing a bunch of records from different locations, indicated by a
four-character numeric.  I've been asked to write a program to split this
file into two files, one containing a set of specified locations, the
other containing the rest.

(As per Standard Practise Here... the specified location numbers are keyed 
into a PDS member which is loaded into a working-storage table in the 
program; each input record's location number is then matched against the 
table for appropriate processing.)

Anyhow... the order of the records in the output files is the reverse of 
that of the input file.  I asked if this would have any effect on future 
processing and got a resouding 'That's A Goooooood Question... why don't 
you just put them back into original order.'

Fortunately both output file formats have a large-enough chunk of filler 
that I can put in a sequencing-number during processing; since current 
processing has shown an average file to be about 20,000 recs I have put in 
an 8-digit display numeric counter in the last 8 bytes of the outrecs... 
read an inrec, look up the location in the table, format it according to 
SEARCH result, add 1 to the sequence, move the sequence to the end of the 
record, write the appropriate output, etc.

Next, of course, comes a SORT on those last 8 characters DESCENDING... 
hey, presto, everything is in order.

Next comes a FileAID step to turn the sequence numbers back into spaces... 
gotta be neat about it, don'cha know.

That was all set-up... here's the question.

The FileAID control-card will allow for two ways to do the same thing.  I 
can code it as:

$$DD01 UPDATE  REPL=(493,C'        ')

... which specifies REPLacing whatever is at position 493 with the 
following Character string, in this case 8 spaces... or I can code it as:

$$DD01 UPDATE  REPL=(493,X'4040404040404040')

... and the difference (for those who speak EBCDIC) is obvious.

Being of the Olde Schoole, taught to mark a space with a slashed 'b', I am 
tending towards the latter example; leaving a bunch of... nothing in a 
control-card, to me, screams out 'HERE!  HERE!  SCREW ME UP *HERE*!'

On the other hand, of course, is... why specify hex when you can use 
character?  Trying to be fancy and 'dirty', perhaps... and in JCL, too!... 
well, what can you expect from a COBOL-jockey, anyhow...

Anyhow, that's what I have.  Comments are invited, fire away... and 
thanks much for your time.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <yaeger@us.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T13:24:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E4C0CFF.8020800@us.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<b2gvk5$f7f$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>...
>Next, of course, comes a SORT on those last 8 characters DESCENDING... 
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>
If you're doing a sort on the sequence numbers anyway, why not blank 
them out in the same pass using the OUTREC statement instead of doing a 
separate pass with FileAid?  With DFSORT, you have lots of  choices for 
how you code the blanks, such as:

  OUTREC FIELDS=(1,492,8X)
  OUTREC FIELDS=(1,492,C'        ')
  OUTREC FIELDS=(1,492,8X'40')
  OUTREC FIELDS=(1,492,8C' ')
  OUTREC FIELDS=(1,492,X'4040404040404040')
  OUTREC FIELDS=(1,492,500:X)

Note that you can put the column number before the blanks if you like as 
well, e.g.

   OUTREC FIELDS=(1,492,493:8X)

Just a suggestion.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T21:35:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2hkm8$a8s$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b2gvk5$f7f$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E4C0CFF.8020800@us.ibm.com>`

```
In article <3E4C0CFF.8020800@us.ibm.com>,
Frank Yaeger  <yaeger@us.ibm.com> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

[snip]

>>The FileAID control-card will allow for two ways to do the same thing.  I 
>>can code it as:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>$$DD01 UPDATE  REPL=(493,X'4040404040404040')

[snip]

>>Anyhow, that's what I have.  Comments are invited, fire away... and 
>>thanks much for your time.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>separate pass with FileAid?

Because in the two-years-and-change that I've been there I've only managed 
to teach folks to accept and use FileAID; I haven't been able to get 
OUTREC constructs similarly accepted.

It used to be 'FileAID?  I dunno, that might get difficult for the 
maintenance-programmer... better write a simple COBOL program that 
anybody'll understand.'

Now it is 'INREC/OUTREC definitions?  I dunno, that might get difficult 
for the maintenance-programmer... better write a simple FileAID for it.'

Thanks for your suggestion, though... now, back to my question, though... 
what is your opinion about C'  ' vs 
X'4040'?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T09:12:23-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<b2itg7$idf$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b2gvk5$f7f$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E4C0CFF.8020800@us.ibm.com> <b2hkm8$a8s$1@panix1.panix.com> <01c2d3d5$6ae0b000$ad93f243@chottel>`

```

Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:
>My first thought was a COBOL internal sort.  Generate the numbers in the
>input procedure and wipe them out in the output procedure.  

An excellent suggestion but... this is a No Internal Sorts shop.

Thanks for your suggestion, though... now, back to my question, though...

What is your opinion about C'  ' vs X'4040' in a control-card?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T22:29:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v4oo16il7ucheb@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b2gvk5$f7f$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E4C0CFF.8020800@us.ibm.com> <b2hkm8$a8s$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b2hkm8$a8s$1@panix1.panix.com...
[...]
> Thanks for your suggestion, though... now, back to my question, though...
> what is your opinion about C'  ' vs
> X'4040'?

As a zero-year programmer on FileAID, I might find that
C'  ' is easier to understand with a comment indicating
its correct size. In the absence of a comment, that which
I can see, X'4040', is easier to count than that which I
cannot see.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T09:39:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2iv29$peo$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b2gvk5$f7f$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E4C0CFF.8020800@us.ibm.com> <b2hkm8$a8s$1@panix1.panix.com> <v4oo16il7ucheb@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <v4oo16il7ucheb@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b2hkm8$a8s$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>cannot see.

Mr Smith, are you sure you should be posting this?  It is clear and 
unambiguous and directly addresses the question... thus far most of the 
responses have been of the 'The method you have suggested works and you 
have asked a question about the aesthetics of a small part of it... so why 
don't you use another method entire?' sort.

(Oh... and then there's the occaisional 'You have posted all the work 
you've done and asked for comments and opinions... so I'll tell you to do 
your own homework, haw haw haw!')

I have, however, come back into work today and reviewed my data - thanks 
much to whoever it was who expressed confusion about my output-record 
order! - and found that I was wrong in my evaluation (my error and 
apologies to those who wasted time due to my mistake); the output files 
are in the correct order...

... *except* for the header records.  The input file contains a header 
with the batch's count; as the input is split the counter is appropriately 
decremented and a second header's count for the second file is 
incremented.  This allows for slightly easier solution which is less 
machine-intensive but more 'verbose' in the programming and JCL involved:

Add two more SELECTs and FDs for output files which will contain only the 
headers for OUTFIL1 and OUTFIL2

Write all data recs to one file and as part of 9999-EOJ write the header 
to another (both files defined as &&TEMPs).

Follow the step to run the actual splitting job with two IEBGENERs to 
concatenate the headers and the datas into two complete files, viz.

//SYSUT1 DD DSN=&&HDR01,DISP=SHR
//       DD DSN=&&DAT01,DISP=SHR
//SYSUT2 DD DSN=OUTPUT.DSN01,DISP=(,CATLG,DELETE) etc.

...

//SYSUT1 DD DSN=&&HDR02,DISP=SHR
//       DD DSN=&&DAT02,DISP=SHR
//SYSUT2 DD DSN=OUTPUT.DSN02,DISP=(,CATLG,DELETE) etc.

Thanks much for your input!

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T12:03:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v4q7nv704380cd@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b2gvk5$f7f$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E4C0CFF.8020800@us.ibm.com> <b2hkm8$a8s$1@panix1.panix.com> <v4oo16il7ucheb@corp.supernews.com> <b2iv29$peo$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b2iv29$peo$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <v4oo16il7ucheb@corp.supernews.com>,
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
> >
> ><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message
news:b2hkm8$a8s$1@panix1.panix.com...
> >[...]
> >> Thanks for your suggestion, though... now, back to my question,
though...
> >> what is your opinion about C'  ' vs
> >> X'4040'?
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> don't you use another method entire?' sort.

I do not normally respond to posts referring to JCL. This is, in part,
due to my limited exposure to such arcana (in the loose sense of
'a secret and powerful remedy, esp. a universal cure for all diseases'),
briefly, some 20 years ago. My posting was done on impulse. I shall
endeavor to curb such impulses in the future.

[...]
> Write all data recs to one file and as part of 9999-EOJ write the header
> to another (both files defined as &&TEMPs).
>
> Follow the step to run the actual splitting job with two IEBGENERs to
> concatenate the headers and the datas into two complete files, viz.

Perhaps I can redeem myself with an unsolicited solution that
violates the two-year programmer rule.

Split the data into files where the first record in each file is a
dummy header. Maintain the actual headers in WS. After all
data has been split, open these sequential data files for I-O,
read the dummy headers, then rewrite from the actual headers.

This eliminates an obvious and, for 20K records, fast step,
and creates the possibility that future maintainers may have
reason to 'scratch their heads.'
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CF497CDBBA162F7E.544F1A1C575E82FA.57F3AEED757BC2F2@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<b2gvk5$f7f$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E4C0CFF.8020800@us.ibm.com> <b2hkm8$a8s$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
On 13 Feb 2003 21:35:52 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com enlightened us:

>In article <3E4C0CFF.8020800@us.ibm.com>,
>Frank Yaeger  <yaeger@us.ibm.com> wrote:
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

Which you use kind of depends on the abilities and knowledge of those
who maintain production JCL.  Using my own experiences, he/she who
maintains the JCL is usually of the supervisory level and is somewhat
knowledgeable in regards to EBCDIC and parameter cards, a C'  ' or
X'4040'.  Therefore, if it were me and for my own ease of use, i.e.
being able to know exactly how many spaces, I'd opt for the X'4040'
notation.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I don't mind the rat race, but I could do with a little more
cheese.
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T13:24:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E4C0D21.A12C41FF@attglobal.net>`

```
Please do your own... no wiat, that's for students, isn't it?

Why not do the whole operation in a SORT?

If you have a table of number values to include in one file, convert it to
SORT includes.  Tell SORT to write both the included and the excluded records
to output files.  Also tell SORT to do a COPY operation, which should maintain
the input order of the records.  (I don't understand why output records would
be out of order in your program, by the way.)

Should be no need to add a sequence field to the records, or to clear it back
out.

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> All righty... here's the situation.  There's an input file, flat,
> containing a bunch of records from different locations, indicated by a
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T21:58:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2hlvp$ed1$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b2gvk5$f7f$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E4C0D21.A12C41FF@attglobal.net>`

```
In article <3E4C0D21.A12C41FF@attglobal.net>,
Colin Campbell  <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote:
>Please do your own... no wiat, that's for students, isn't it?

Please note that a sample of the code already attempted is included as per 
FAQ suggestion.

>
>Why not do the whole operation in a SORT?

Because there's a bit more that needs to be done beyond what I bored folks
with here... the input file's batch number needs to be checked against the
previous month's-worth of header records for duplicate batch numbers
(previous batch headers are kept in single-record GDG generations; during
0100-HOUSEKEEPING these are read into a working-storage table and the
results are SEARCHed for current file's header batch number (technique
sound familiar? they like to do things that way, load a table with values
and search... some programs contain hand-coded binary searches, can you
guess why?), the same program is to be used for a half-dozen departments,
each of which sends a file with a *slightly* different format (leading to 
constructs like 

IF DEPT-1 OR DEPT-2 OR DEPT-3
    MOVE WS-INREC-DESCRIPTION   TO  WS-OUTREC-DESCRIPTION.
IF DEPT-4 OR DEPT-5 OR DEPT-6
    MOVE WS-INREC-FILLER(1:24)  TO  WS-OUTREC-DESCRIPTION.

(Yes I *know* there is an EVALUATE for *just* this sort of situation... 
and when I used it I got 'Well, I dunno... maybe you should change this to 
a series of simple IFs to keep it simple for the maintenance-programmer.')

>
>If you have a table of number values to include in one file, convert it to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>out.

Ummmm... perhaps you are right and I should re-examine what I did; I 
incorporated sequencing because the last record I would write to both 
files was the header-record (because it contained counts for the input 
file which had to be decremented for output-file writes).  After the run I 
looked at the output and saw the header at the bottom and thought 
'Hmmmmm... kinda backwards, that be' and it could be, in good programmer 
fashion, that I've generated a coding solution for a problem that doesn't 
exist.  Sitting here, now, a few hours later, with a snoring puppy in my 
lap gives a different perspective on the situation.

Thanks for your imput!

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] A Matter of Style - FileAID Control Card

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-02-19T14:37:59-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E53CF07.8BA2C96F@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<b2gvk5$f7f$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Given your description of the problem, I have a few questions.

1.  Why are the output files in a different sequence from the input
file?  This implies that records are stored in a table or something else
is being done.

2.  If the output records are to be sorted, could this not be done in
the COBOL program with one or two sorts?

3.  If you are using a separate sort why not just blank out the sequence
number using an OUTREC statement?  Both SYNCSORT add-ons and the Sort
Enhancements Frank Yeager has for IBM's DF/Sort may allow this to be
done in a more documented fashion than the field displacement, field
length, field type, syntax of basic sorts.
  

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
> All righty... here's the situation.  There's an input file, flat,
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

```
I have, however, come back into work today and reviewed my data - thanks

order! - and found that I was wrong in my evaluation (my error and 
apologies to those who wasted time due to my mistake); the output files 
are in the correct order...

... *except* for the header records.  The input file contains a header 
with the batch's count; as the input is split the counter is appropriately 
decremented and a second header's count for the second file is 
incremented.

--end quoted text

>

>the COBOL program with one or two sorts?

--begin quoted text:

An excellent suggestion but... this is a No Internal Sorts shop.

--end quoted text

>
>3.  If you are using a separate sort why not just blank out the sequence
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>length, field type, syntax of basic sorts.

<http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=b2hkm8%24a8s%241%40panix1.panix.com&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text:

Because in the two-years-and-change that I've been there I've only managed 
to teach folks to accept and use FileAID; I haven't been able to get 
OUTREC constructs similarly accepted.

It used to be 'FileAID?  I dunno, that might get difficult for the 
maintenance-programmer... better write a simple COBOL program that 
anybody'll understand.'

Now it is 'INREC/OUTREC definitions?  I dunno, that might get difficult 
for the maintenance-programmer... better write a simple FileAID for it.'

--end quoted text

Thanks much for your input!

DD
```

- **References:** `<t0V2a.3653$Wy1.26917@newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <3E4C53CD.7040903@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <01c2d3d1$5317f5e0$ad93f243@chottel> <3E4C7ADB.6071D37C@shaw.ca> <b2i17g$o37$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Orignial poseter,

You have links to the formula.  Rather that the exact answer here are
examples of similiar calculations.  Between the two you should be able to
make a start. I.E. if you still have problems your next post should contain
code reflecting your attempt to solve this :-)

public class FinancialCalculations{

   public static double calculateMonthlyPayment(double loanAmount,
   int months, double monthlyInterestRate){
      double monthlyPayment = loanAmount * monthlyInterestRate/
         (1 - 1/Math.pow(1+monthlyInterestRate, months));
      return monthlyPayment;
   }

   public static double calculateFutureValue(double monthlyPayment,
   int months, double monthlyInterestRate){
      int i = 1;
      double futureValue = 0;
      while (i <= months) {
         futureValue = (futureValue + monthlyPayment) *
         (1 + monthlyInterestRate);
         i++;
      }
      return futureValue;
   }
}
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Function present value

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T09:48:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2ja4q$2c76$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<t0V2a.3653$Wy1.26917@newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <iP73a.3845$Wy1.28116@newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```
IThe current ISO standard describes the PRESENT-VALUE function as having at
least two parameters, and defines it similar to the following:

    For two parameters:
        (argument-2 / (1 + argument-1))

    For three parameters:
        (argument-2 / ((1 + argument-1) + argument-3 / (1 + argument-1) **
2)

    For n parameters, n > 3:
        FUNCTION SUM (
            (argument-2 / (1 + argument-1) ** 1),
            (argument-3 / (1 + argument-1) ** 2),
            ...
            (argument-n / (1 + argument-1) ** n))

I'd think that description ought to be sufficient to get you started.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: Function present value

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T10:49:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<322878ABC1B25A95.490AF233CD543728.BE95EBFE0D967814@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<t0V2a.3653$Wy1.26917@newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```
On Thu, 13 Feb 2003 17:25:59 -0500, "Thomas Li" <aizhongli@sprint.ca>
enlightened us:

>Can somebody tell me how to calculate FUNCTION PRESENT VALUE (parameter1,
>parameter2, ...) in a mathematical formula?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Here are a couple of ways.  You didn't specify what type of interest
was being used, so the formula differs.

The present quantity P which in n years will accumulate to the amount
A(n) [read as A times n] at the rate of interest r:

At Simple Interest:  P = A(n) divided by 1 + nr [read as n times r]

At interest compounded annually:  P = A(n) divided by (1 + r) to the n
power

At interest compounded q times a year:  P = A(n) divided by (1 + r
divided by q) to the n times q power.

Further, P is called the present value of A(n) due in n years at rate
r.  The true discount is D = An - P

HTH

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I don't mind the rat race, but I could do with a little more
cheese.
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

<a id="t-271615539a"></a>
## Thread: Internet Communications Interface Agent for COBOL — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Internet Communications Interface Agent for COBOL

- **From:** Neil Nelson <news_replies@dslextreme.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-13T19:58:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v4oqa97pk0t8fe@corp.supernews.com>`

```

The ICI-ICSA Group has download software available for

* P2P Authenticated and Private Internet Communications

The reason this notice is being posted to comp.lang.cobol
is because where COBOL is used on an Internet connected
PC or can provide files to such a PC it will be very easy
to utilize the ICSA for Internet communications as an
interface to COBOL programs.

Interactive Internet communications using COBOL can be
easily created by having a COBOL program write a short
file to the ICSA input folder using the following format.


ToName James_Smith
ToPath folder_path/file
""Put this message on James_Smith's computer in the remote
folder and with filename of folder-path/file.


ToName is the ICI Name of your Partner to whom you want
to send a message.

ToPath is the folder and file name on your ICI Partner's
computer where the message should be put.

Several formats are shown at

http://www.aimetasearch.com/ici/setup_opsall.htm#14

And then if a COBOL program running the destination
computer is looping and looking for a file at the ToPath,
the communication can be completed and a reply sent in
the same manner.

The software currently available at the ICI-ICSA site uses
the above method with Visual Basic.

http://www.aimetasearch.com/ici/index.htm

Primary Features:

* Instant Messaging (Chat)

* File Transfer

* Picture Transfer (drag and drop: gif,jpg,bmp)

All communications are shared-password authenticated,
automatically compressed during communication, and
may be weak or strong SSL encrypted.

* ICSA1: password authentication only

* ICSA2: plus SSL weak encryption (exportable)

* ICSA3: plus SSL strong encryption (U.S. only)

Each version will work with the others at the best
encryption level both communicating versions have.

The download procedure registers your ICI Name and the
ICI Server manages the dynamic IP addresses so that you
and others only need to use your ICI Name.

The ICI Console allows you to select ICI Names from
those you enter (with passwords) and automatically make
a connection with the selected person when that ICI Name is
logged onto the ICI System.

In the works:

* ICI Browser: Provides browser capability for HTML
  pages on a remote computer.

* User Defined Broadcast Groups: Send messages to two
  or more people at the same time.
```

```
David Kinston wrote:

> Where can I get documentation for this?
> I have my original disks but no manuals.
>

The only luck you are likely to have is if Microsoft have some dusty
copies in their basement - they no longer offer technical support.
Bearing in mind this is their 're-badged' version of Micro Focus DOS V3
- the manuals will be very similar. The only major difference is that
Microsoft have their own Programmers' Workbench (what we would now call
an IDE (Integrated Development Environment). The PWB includes the Editor
and the compiling/linkage features specific to the Microsoft version of
the compiler..

> Or
>
> Is there an inexpensive (or free?) compiler
> that is fully functional?
>

Depends what you mean by the phrase 'fully functional' - could be COBOL
85 or are you after a compiler, which with extensions, is closer to the
newly announced COBOL 2002 Standard. I'm not sure but *think* Fujitsu's
V3 is still available as a freebie - but remember they are now up to
Verson 7.. Alternatively both Fujitsu and Micro Focus have 'student'
versions of their current compilers around $100. However, these are
'learning' tools and you can't distribute commercial applications.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Calls

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T12:12:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2jijp$2i5h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2529510.1045225663@dbforums.com> <2531154.1045248954@dbforums.com>`

```

"poopiehead" <member15404@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:2531154.1045248954@dbforums.com...
>  MOST PEOPLE THAT
> PROGRAM IN COBOL TEND TO WRITE IN UPPERCASE.

Ever since the 1985 standard, lower-case characters and their corresponding
upper-case characters have been considered equivalent in COBOL words (that
includes reserved words as well as user identifiers).   Thus, if "most
people" who program in COBOL feel they are required to write in upper-case
characters exclusively, either they or the compilers they use are, dare I
say it, embroiled in trailing-edge technology.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Hmm, I write my COBOL sentences in upper case, but all my comments in mixed
case. Keeps the clients happy. (IBM mainframe types who do not understand
the words "lower case"). Makes the comments stand out.

But be prepared to...

1.  Demo the use of the TSO editor block commands "ucc" and "lcc".
2.  Remind the clients that when they exit the TSO editor from editing a
COBOL program, ALWAYS CHECK YOUR CAPS LOCK SETTING *BEFORE* EDITING JCL. (Or
watch the screen when typing that JCL). The JCL reader does not like
lower-case at all.

MCM
```

## Thread: OS/390 COBOL copybook cross reference — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### OS/390 COBOL copybook cross reference

- **From:** richgee2@yahoo.com (Rich Gierak)
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T10:57:28-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3708ea7a.0302141057.41486c49@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm working with COBOL for OS/390 & VM v2.1.1 and seeking a cross
reference, in the compile listing, of copybooks and which lib they
came from.  I can't locate a compiler option which generates this. 
Anybody know of one?  Thx, Rich.
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 COBOL copybook cross reference

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T13:16:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2jfbe$7be$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3708ea7a.0302141057.41486c49@posting.google.com>`

```
Rich,
  I assume that you are looking for which DDName - not which DSName the copy
members come from.

If so, then I would suggest doing an ISPF exclude, then find of the word
"IN".

If you want to know which DSName they came from, I don't know of any tool
(although there might be one) that would do this.  You could create your own
LIBEXIT routine to do this - but even there it would MOSTLY handle DDName
not DSName.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 COBOL copybook cross reference

- **From:** richgee2@yahoo.com (Rich Gierak)
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T16:26:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3708ea7a.0302141626.69325227@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3708ea7a.0302141057.41486c49@posting.google.com> <b2jfbe$7be$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Thanks Bill,
I know the DDNAME to be SYSLIB.  I should have mentioned that I'm
compiling with batch JCL.  I would like to see DSName or at least a
positional indicator of the library in SYSLIB.  ie. SYSLIB(+2).

>   I assume that you are looking for which DDName - not which DSName the copy
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 COBOL copybook cross reference

- **From:** "Larry Kahm" <lkahm@heliotropicsystems.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-17T21:44:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hJc4a.14994$Yv2.14523@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<3708ea7a.0302141057.41486c49@posting.google.com>`

```
Rich,

The COBOL compiler, by itself, does not provide a copy member
cross-reference.

Serena Software's ChangeMan ZMF COBOL compile process has a program, called
SERCOPY, which expands the copy members used in a program and provides the
data set and PDF statistics for each component.

That's about the only program product I know of that does so.

Larry Kahm
Heliotropic Systems, Inc.

"Rich Gierak" <richgee2@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:3708ea7a.0302141057.41486c49@posting.google.com...
> I'm working with COBOL for OS/390 & VM v2.1.1 and seeking a cross
> reference, in the compile listing, of copybooks and which lib they
> came from.  I can't locate a compiler option which generates this.
> Anybody know of one?  Thx, Rich.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qXq4a.11$hv3.4930@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3708ea7a.0302141057.41486c49@posting.google.com> <hJc4a.14994$Yv2.14523@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`

```

this information
```

<a id="t-dfce22944c"></a>
## Thread: Implementors confused about NEXT SENTENCE — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Implementors confused about NEXT SENTENCE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T13:12:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2jf37$6at$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow-up on another thread, I did want to confirm that at least one
"early implementor" of the '85 Standard *did* mis-understand the difference
between CONTINUE and NEXT SENTENCE.

In its first (and it was - I believe THE first) '85 Standard compiler, Micro
Focus treated a "next sentence" as the same as a CONTINUE.  This was
"particularly" obvious - because they also had an extension where "Next
Sentence" was a valid imperative statement - allowed within the conditional
phrase of any verb.

HOWEVER, this "error" was corrected quite early (certainly before 1990).
Micro Focus did, in its usual "support ALL upward compatibility" manner,
provide an "OLDNEXTSENTENCE" directive to support those who "wanted" a "Next
Sentence" to work as a "CONTINUE".   This was not the default - and is
clearly marked as non-ANSI/ISO conforming.

I don't know if there were any other implementors that had the same problem
or not - but it did show that (contrary to what I said in one of my other
posts) that there was at least some room for "confusion" in the Standard -
even among those who know COBOL and "standard-ize" well.
```

---

<a id="t-6baeb31d78"></a>
## Thread: New DFSORT/ICETOOL Features (Feb, 2003) — 5 msgs

### New DFSORT/ICETOOL Features (Feb, 2003)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T13:28:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2jg0h$pvo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<OFDE42E6A4.2037E542-ON88256CCD.005CDBDF-88256CCD.005D0516@us.ibm.com>`

```
This note appeared in the IBM-MAIN list-server.  I thought that it might
also be of interest to some (IBM) COBOL people as well.  From a "brief"
glance at the documentation, I don't see any "COBOL" or "internal sort"
specific enhancements - but I do think that the general enhancements may
still be of interest in many IBM mainframe shops - that don't read
"IBM-MAIN".

Please pass this on "within your shop" - as you think it appropriate.
```

#### ↳ Re: New DFSORT/ICETOOL Features (Feb, 2003)

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <yaeger@us.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T12:39:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E4D541A.8060400@us.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<OFDE42E6A4.2037E542-ON88256CCD.005CDBDF-88256CCD.005D0516@us.ibm.com> <b2jg0h$pvo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

>This note appeared in the IBM-MAIN list-server.  I thought that it might
>also be of interest to some (IBM) COBOL people as well.  From a "brief"
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
Thanks for posting this, Bill.  I posted it on the ibm-main and racf-l 
lists (as well as several help boards) where DFSORT/ICETOOL are 
frequently discussed.  I decided not to post it to the Cobol lists 
because DFSORT/ICETOOL is only discussed on them infrequently.   You're 
right that there are no specific enhancements for COBOL this time, but 
the new SPLICE, SAMPLE, REPEAT, SPLITBY, and arithmetic operations with 
fields and constants (with or without symbols) features might be of 
interest to those COBOL programmers who are open to using DFSORT features.

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team
Specialties: ICETOOL, OUTFIL, Symbols, Migration
=> DFSORT/MVS is on the WWW at http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort
```

<a id="t-f7e6fb0d94"></a>
## Thread: Reflection Ability — 36 msgs

### Reflection Ability

- **From:** dynamosteve92@yahoo.com (DynamoSteve)
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T14:13:28-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b63a55.0302141413.740eddfe@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi All,  I have a question regarding Cobol's capabilities.  First a
disclaimer - I know very little, if anything about Cobol.  I'm a
development manager that is having to work with a Cobol dev team.  I
am trying to learn a thing or two about the language's ability.  I
know the answer to my question may depend on what flavor of Cobol is
in use - I can't answer that so if you can at least give me some
general advice I would appreciate it.

My question - does Cobol have reflection/introspection abilities
similar to Java.  What I want to do is set the value of a "variable"
in Cobol to a certain other value based on the value of a key
variable.  In psuedo-code what I'm talking about is this.

FNAME PIC X(30)
KEY   PIC X(5)
.
.
.
KEY = "FNAME"
FIND_VARIABLE(KEY) = "STEVE";
// The FIND_VARIABLE() language construct "points" at the variable
FNAME because that's the string value of the variable KEY that was
passed in.

In other words, I want to set the value of the variable who's name
coincides with value of the KEY variable.  Generally speaking, can
this be done?

Thanks in advance,
-Steve.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reflection Ability

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-15T02:49:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e4daa14.158282266@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<36b63a55.0302141413.740eddfe@posting.google.com>`

```
dynamosteve92@yahoo.com (DynamoSteve) wrote:

>Hi All,  I have a question regarding Cobol's capabilities.  First a
>disclaimer - I know very little, if anything about Cobol.  I'm a
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>this be done?

Yes, it can be done. The mechanism is different depeding on where the value is
coming from:
, An environment variable
. An internal table
. A COBOL indexed file
. A database

Robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reflection Ability

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-14T23:21:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302142321.278440d2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<36b63a55.0302141413.740eddfe@posting.google.com> <3e4daa14.158282266@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >In other words, I want to set the value of the variable who's name
> >coincides with value of the KEY variable.  Generally speaking, can
> >this be done?
> 
> Yes, it can be done. 

I think that you misunderstand the question.  Cobol does not support
introspection.

> The mechanism is different depeding on where the value is
> coming from:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> . A database

None of those.  He wants to have a Cobol user word (variable name) in
a text string and use this to obtain the value of that named item from
where the compiler has put it in memory.

The name would need to be looked up the the compiler's data map, or
possibly the debug information file, but this is (usually) not
available to the running program in any way that is supported by the
language's syntax.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reflection Ability

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-15T14:03:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e4e48b2.4932204@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<36b63a55.0302141413.740eddfe@posting.google.com> <3e4daa14.158282266@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302142321.278440d2@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>language's syntax.

The COBOL program can store variable names and pointers to their values in a
table, look them up with a SEARCH verb, SET the ADDRESS OF an abstract variable
in LINKAGE SECTION to the pointer, and perform operations on it. 

It could also do that with environment variables, where the OS looks up the name
and returns its value. Granted that is not a COBOL language feature, but can be
called by a COBOL program (as well as a Java program).

Of course, it could WRITE name-value pairs to an indexed file and retrieve them
with a READ.  

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reflection Ability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-15T10:56:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302151056.182d0eae@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<36b63a55.0302141413.740eddfe@posting.google.com> <3e4daa14.158282266@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302142321.278440d2@posting.google.com> <3e4e48b2.4932204@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >The name would need to be looked up the the compiler's data map, or
> >possibly the debug information file, but this is (usually) not
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> in LINKAGE SECTION to the pointer, and perform operations on it. 

Introspection is where this is done by syntax supported by the
language.  What you are suggesting is writing the interpreter for a
scripting language in Cobol and then introspection will be supported
in _that_.

> It could also do that with environment variables, where the OS looks up the name
> and returns its value. Granted that is not a COBOL language feature, but can be
> called by a COBOL program (as well as a Java program).

This is because the environment strings support having the name
available.  If Cobol variables were implemented like that then (such
as may be done in a scripting language) it would satisfy his needs (or
wants), however environment variables are _not_ Cobol variables.
 
> Of course, it could WRITE name-value pairs to an indexed file and retrieve them
> with a READ.  

When you have found out what this discussion is actually about then
please do come and join in.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reflection Ability

- **From:** dynamosteve92@yahoo.com (DynamoSteve)
- **Date:** 2003-02-15T06:52:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b63a55.0302150652.65d16368@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<36b63a55.0302141413.740eddfe@posting.google.com> <3e4daa14.158282266@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302142321.278440d2@posting.google.com>`

```
Ok, so if we can't do that can you offer some further advice on how to
efficiently accomplish something in Cobol?  We are going to have a
string of data coming in to the program.  The string will be available
through a variable (user word?).  The format of the string will be
similar to a URI --key1=value1&key2=value2&key3=value3 -- not exactly
like that but close enough.  The key names passed in any one string
are basically random.  They could be any combination of up to 100 or
so.

Apparently the Cobol folks have a few routines to efficiently split
this string apart into individual key/value pairs.  They were planning
on doing a giant if then... else if... else if... type of thing to
assign the value to the variable identified by the key.  Again, in
psuedo-code, something like this ..

INPUT = "key1=value1&key2=value2"
FNAME PIC X(30)
LNAME PIC X(30)
KEY PIC X(30)
VALUE PIC X(3)
WHILE more pairs available in INPUT {
  split the next pair into KEY and VALUE
  IF KEY == "FNAME" THEN
    FNAME = VALUE;
  ELSE IF KEY == "LNAME" THEN
    LNAME = VALUE;
  ELSE
    error becasue key is unknown
}

Simple enough with two possible keys, but how would you approach this
when the potential keys are many?

Thanks again to all.


riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0302142321.278440d2@posting.google.com>...
> robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> language's syntax.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reflection Ability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** jp <jpbeaud@gmx.fr>
- **Date:** 2003-02-15T17:52:27+01:00

- **References:** `<36b63a55.0302141413.740eddfe@posting.google.com> <3e4daa14.158282266@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302142321.278440d2@posting.google.com> <36b63a55.0302150652.65d16368@posting.google.com>`

```
I am not sure, I have well understood what you want, but I think it is something like:

You know how much key and value there are at maximum:

01 INPUT-DATA Occurs 100.
    05 KEY pic X(30).
    05            pic x.
    05  VALUE pic X(30).
    05            PIC X.


INITIALIZE INPUT-DATA
MOVE INPUT INTO INPUT-DATA
PERFORM VERYING i from 1 by 1 until ( i > 100 OR KEY  (i ) = SPACES  )
    Evaluate True
    When Key(i) = 'FNAME'
        Move Value (i) to FNAME
    When KEY(i) = 'LNZAME'
        Move Value (i) to LNAME
    When Other
        DISPLAY 'ERROR!!!'
    End-Evaluate
END-PERFORM






>
>
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> > language's syntax.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reflection Ability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-15T11:32:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XRudndR-jvSf59OjXTWciQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<36b63a55.0302141413.740eddfe@posting.google.com> <3e4daa14.158282266@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302142321.278440d2@posting.google.com> <36b63a55.0302150652.65d16368@posting.google.com>`

```

"DynamoSteve" <dynamosteve92@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:36b63a55.0302150652.65d16368@posting.google.com...
> Ok, so if we can't do that can you offer some further advice on how to
> efficiently accomplish something in Cobol?  We are going to have a
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks again to all.

The COBOL folk are on the right track - except the multiple if-then-else
construct is kinda kludgy.

What you are trying to do is realtively common as a transaction stream. For
example:

EMP-NO:1234,HRS-WORKED:4.5,BASE-PAY:12.50,EXP:16.50^EMP-NO:4321...

or

N=1235,H=4.5,R=12.50,E=16.50^N=4321....

Yeah, this sort of thing happens every day. Remembering that COBOL is file
and record oriented:

1. Decompose stream into 'input records'
2. Decompose individual record into values for specific data fields
3. Apply the values to the master record

I encourage you to make haste with this project:

As of Friday, Feb 14th:

"Hours after a federal court of appeals blocked an order forcing Microsoft
to incorporate Sun Microsystems' Java programming language, the Redmond,
Wash.-based software giant went ahead anyway removing its own Virtual
Machine (VM) (define) from its Windows XP operating system."

"As a result of SP1a, Microsoft will no longer provide Microsoft VM as a
download off of its Windows Update site. No new Microsoft product will
include the Microsoft VM, including Windows Server 2003 and Windows 2000 SP4
[nor inlcude Sun's rendition of JVM]"

Story at:
http://www.internetnews.com/bus-news/article.php/1578841

Java's a goner.
```

```
Ah, I knew it was a matter of time before a cocky, full of himself,
prick would chime in with some wonderful advice.  I'll tell you DD,
when I run across assholes like you hiding behind the anonymity of the
net, I tell myself "there's a person who knows he doesn't have the
skills, knowledge or technique to function in the real world".

Now, at which point was it that I said I was in charge of a Cobol
(cased this way on purpose in hopes that something as piddly as that
will annoy the shit out of you) team?  I didn't.  In fact the
development team for this project consists of mostly Java folks. 
However, we have a need to use some logic located in legacy cOBOL
apps.  I'm simply trying to understand what the coBOL'ers are telling
me so I can understand their logic when the estimate scope and time.

I am second-guessing nobody.  In fact the coboL team is, in my
extremely non-skilled, ignorant and technique-less opinion, quite
competent.  Do they know all the answers? No.  Are the unsure of the
best architecture to accomplish what we need? Yes.  Is that a crime?
No.

Ok, DD.  Time to pony up.  You're obviously King cObOl so tell us all,
exactly how would you do this?  Surely someone as sharp as you can
cook something up.  Otherwise, shut the hell up.  We've each taken our
public whack... email me privately and I'll be happy to frustrate
myself fow weeks to come trying to knock some sense into you.

Warmest regards,
-Steve.
```

```
DynamoSteve <dynamosteve92@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:36b63a55.0302151715.52b8ca6b@posting.google.com...
[...]
> Now, at which point was it that I said I was in charge of a Cobol
> (cased this way on purpose in hopes that something as piddly as that
> will annoy the shit out of you) team?  I didn't.

Richard already mentioned this; but I will mention it, again.
You wrote, "I'm a development manager that is having to work
with a Cobol dev team." While it may have been clear to you,
it was not clear to me that there were two teams.

>  In fact the
> development team for this project consists of mostly Java folks.
> However, we have a need to use some logic located in legacy cOBOL
> apps.  I'm simply trying to understand what the coBOL'ers are telling
> me so I can understand their logic when the estimate scope and time.

I once estimated a project at 16 weeks. The project was to
interface a device to a terminal. My part was the firmware.
An associate called the manufacturer, of the device, and
asked how long it should take. Their response was 30 minutes.

This discrepancy caused the associate to question my
competence. The associate was unaware that the 30 minutes
was to connect the device to a PC using Visual Basic controls
that were supplied with a SDK. My estimate had to include
writing and testing 50 C functions because VB will not run on
an 8051 microprocessor.

You may not be able to understand their estimates by asking
questions in a news group. This is because, you can never
provide to those, in the news group, all that is required to
educate you to the other team's logic.

> I am second-guessing nobody.

I hope that is true. Raising anything that you believe you
learned here may cause friction between the teams.
```

```
>
> Give me a break. "dynamosteve" is hardly less "anonymous"
than "docdwarf."
>
> How would I do this ["reflection/introspection"]?

I don't want to get in the middle of the debate in this thread
(whatever it is,) but this remark interests me:

> No COMPETENT COBOL programmer - or consultant - would ever
find himself in
> need of such a tool, because there are so many better ways
for the COMPETENT
> COBOL programmer to debug his or her program.

> Of course, you wouldn't know that, because even INCOMPETENT
'any language'
> programmers - and self-proclaimed experets and consultants -
know what they
> don't know; or at a minimum, they know THAT they don't know.

I'm not a COBOL programmer by any rigorous standard, but can
see how reflection could be made use of.  (Again, this is a
sincere request for discussion, not an attempt to support any
viewpoint other than my own.)

For instance, if you could inspect the structure and values of
your working storage, you could write a generic error handler
that would "core dump" your program on fatal errors.  I've used
similar mechanisms, where I can, and it's been a tremendous
boon to debugging code that has erred in the field.

The ability to reflect your code has to be paid for with an
increased overhead, so perhaps it's not worth it -- but does
wanting/exploiting such a feature qualify as incompetence?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reflection Ability

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-17T10:51:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302171051.3308aaee@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<36b63a55.0302141413.740eddfe@posting.google.com> <3e4daa14.158282266@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302142321.278440d2@posting.google.com> <36b63a55.0302150652.65d16368@posting.google.com> <b2lv8l$2e2$1@panix1.panix.com> <36b63a55.0302151715.52b8ca6b@posting.google.com> <fXN3a.1802$UF6.596037@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b2onaq0o5s@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
"Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote 

> The ability to reflect your code has to be paid for with an
> increased overhead, so perhaps it's not worth it -- but does
> wanting/exploiting such a feature qualify as incompetence?

Different languages are developed specifically because they offer
different facilities.- this leads to solutions being structured in
different ways.  In fact it is probably that a particualr solution
could not be easily coded in existing languages that leads to new
languages.

Reflection seems to be useful when interacting with code that is
alien.  That is as a means of a program to determine the properties
and functions of something with which interaction is required.

It is unlikely to be of any use where there is complete control over
all the code in a system, or even where there are adequate specs that
can be relied on for code that you don't have control over.

As it happens I doubt that Reflection/introspection would be the
solution that was asked for given the actual problem.  Now that _is_
incompetence.
```

```
I've seen systems that have attempt to retrofit reflective
services into an application -- with varying degrees of
success.  There's no argument from me that deciding to do so
should carefully look at benefit vs. overhead, benefit vs.
effort, etc, to see if it's worth doing.  Something I was
hoping to dialog on, though, is that if this feature is
provided by the language (or environment,) would it still be of
no use to the COBOL programmer.

is

properties

required.

control over

specs that
> can be relied on for code that you don't have control over.

"core dump" example?

the
> solution that was asked for given the actual problem.  Now
that _is_
> incompetence.

Again, I'm not trying to estimate DynamoSteve's competency, I
just though this would an interesting avenue of discussion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reflection Ability

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-17T15:29:27-08:00

- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302171529.3a4bc96@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<36b63a55.0302141413.740eddfe@posting.google.com> <3e4daa14.158282266@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302142321.278440d2@posting.google.com> <36b63a55.0302150652.65d16368@posting.google.com> <b2lv8l$2e2$1@panix1.panix.com> <36b63a55.0302151715.52b8ca6b@posting.google.com> <fXN3a.1802$UF6.596037@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b2onaq0o5s@enews1.newsguy.com> <217e491a.0302171051.3308aaee@posting.google.com> <b2rcgv05b5@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
"Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote 

> Would it be incompetent to use reflection in the aforemention
> "core dump" example?

I have no idea how you were thinking of making this useful.  A core
dump is a static file on the disk, it cannot reflect itself because it
won't execute.

Certainly it would be useful to map a core dump back to the original
source code via some sort of debugger that has access to compiler and
linker maps, but how would reflection be used ?

Or were you just thinking that intertwining the variable names as text
in between the actual variables would give you the ability to follow a
hex dump better ?
```

- **References:** `<36b63a55.0302141413.740eddfe@posting.google.com> <3e4daa14.158282266@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302142321.278440d2@posting.google.com> <36b63a55.0302150652.65d16368@posting.google.com> <b2lv8l$2e2$1@panix1.panix.com> <36b63a55.0302151715.52b8ca6b@posting.google.com> <jgCdnWHlT5pXdtKjXTWcjA@giganews.com> <217e491a.0302171017.1330d4e0@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0302171017.1330d4e0@posting.google.com...
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote
>
> > ** Microsoft announced last Thursday that Microsoft would not ship JVM -
> > either theirs or Sun's - in any future edition of Windows (starting with
XP
> > SP1a). Java's on the way out.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> detect and ensure that it doesn't run.

Nah. Microsoft's not trying to punish its enterprise customers; Microsoft is
trying to punish Sun Microsystems. Enterprise customers are just 'collateral
damage.' (Although I would point out, Java users are not completely
'innocent victims' inasmuch as they allied themselves with Sun at the
get-go. Java users, in this regard, are like the Palestinians during the
Gulf War: their support of Iraq was based more on hope than reason and they
ended up getting screwed.)

I can't agree that Microsoft is 'forcing' a choice between Java and
Windows - Microsoft is certainly STRONGLY pushing toward Dot-Net, but one
can still download JDK from Sun - as long as Sun stays in business, that is.

If Microsoft is on the way out, it won't be too bad; we still have Fujitsu's
Linux COBOL. By the way, is anybody working on a COBOL compiler for Java?
(Before anyone says: "Java's not an operating system you twit!" I'll admit
that I thought that was the only reason Microsoft would wage war against
Sun, to prevent competition in the OS market.)

And Microsoft certainly won't refuse to run Java. Unless of course Microsoft
determines that doing so would adversely affect the user's computing
experience.

I predict:
1. COBOL will outlast Java.
2. COBOL might outlast Microsoft.
3. COBOL will outlast Sun Microsystems.
4. COBOL will outlast the sun.
```
