## Thread: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2 — 34 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

- **From:** rhinten@yahoo.com (RickH)
- **Date:** 2003-02-19T09:40:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com>`

```
We have just migrated to IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2 (from IBM VS COBOL
II Rel 4.0, I think) under OS/390 V2 R10.  We have a program reading a
VSAM KSDS file sequentially which worked fine before, but with
Enterprise COBOL we get a S0C6 error if the READ statement does not
have an 'AT END' clause.

In other words, the old code was:

READ SACNT.
IF SACNT-FILE-STATUS = 10 
   MOVE 'Y' TO SACNT-EOF.

The new code is:

READ SACNT
   AT END NEXT SENTENCE.   <<-- without this clause, we get S0C6
error.
IF SACNT-FILE-STATUS = 10 
   MOVE 'Y' TO SACNT-EOF.

I thought the idea was to move to using FILE STATUS to test for the
results of I/O on all files.  Are we doing something wrong?  Or is
this something to do with Enterprise COBOL?

Rick Hinten
Old Republic Insured Automotive Services
Tulsa, OK, USA
```

#### ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-19T11:59:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b30gm1$qjb$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com>`

```
Something is VERY odd about this report.  Using "NEXT SENTENCE" with an "AT
END" shouldn't even get a clean compile (with any IBM compiler).  Can you
check your "good" and "bad" source code again.

My *guess* and it is only a guess is that you actually have the READ inside
a loop and somehow you are doing your "loop" one more time after the at end
status is actually reached *or* that you are not getting a "00" file status
code on your OPEN statement (with the S0C6 situation).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-20T01:01:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5428fd.390085362@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b30gm1$qjb$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Something is VERY odd about this report.  Using "NEXT SENTENCE" with an "AT
>END" shouldn't even get a clean compile (with any IBM compiler). 

Why not? There is a period after NEXT SENTENCE. 

>My *guess* and it is only a guess is that you actually have the READ inside
>a loop and somehow you are doing your "loop" one more time after the at end
>status is actually reached *or* that you are not getting a "00" file status
>code on your OPEN statement (with the S0C6 situation).

The READ is not inside a loop because it has periods galore. Even if it were in
a PERFORMed paragraph, the code seems to be setting the EOF indicator correctly.

This is a report of non-compliant 'vendor' implementation .. you don't test for
end of file, we kick you off. I believe an earlier COBOL standard made testing
for AT END mandatory. I always write 'at end continue end-read if file-status
equal to '10' ..'.

Robert

>
>--
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-20T14:50:52+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b31cac$utu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b30gm1$qjb$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5428fd.390085362@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Something is VERY odd about this report.  Using "NEXT SENTENCE" with an
>>"AT END" shouldn't even get a clean compile (with any IBM compiler).
> 
> Why not? There is a period after NEXT SENTENCE.

NEXT SENTENCE is not a statement, it is the clause of the IF and SEARCH 
statements which may be used there only if they are not terminated by scope 
delimiters.

The NEXT SENTENCE clause can not be arbitralily attached to different 
statements (unless there is a non-standard extension, such as in MF).

This has been pointed out to you before, but it seems to take several 
repititions before it sinks in.

> This is a report of non-compliant 'vendor' implementation .. you don't
> test for end of file, we kick you off. I believe an earlier COBOL standard
> made testing for AT END mandatory. I always write 'at end continue
> end-read if file-status equal to '10' ..'.

From the '85 standard:

  "The INVALID KEY phrase or the AT END phrase must be specified if no
   applicable USE procedure is specified for filename."

Please do get yourself some reference material that tells you what is, or 
is not, part of standard Cobol, your guesses and misinformation are not 
particularly useful to anyone.

> if file-status equal to '10' ..'.

When testing file status for success or end-of-file, only the first 
character should be tested for '0' and '1' respectively.  This is because 
status codes other than '00' or '10' indicate these by providing additional 
information.  For example: 02, 04, 05, 07, 14.
```

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:b31cac$utu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
<snip>
>
> When testing file status for success or end-of-file, only the first
> character should be tested for '0' and '1' respectively.  This is because
> status codes other than '00' or '10' indicate these by providing
additional
> information.  For example: 02, 04, 05, 07, 14.
>
Richard,
  I certainly agree with you about testing the first byte for a "successful"
(file status "0x") I/O operation.  *HOWEVER*, in this specific case, the
code was supposed to be testing for an "AT END" situation.  I believe that
"10" is the only possible status for this.  A "14" is a (relatively odd to
me) case of a relative file situation with a "too large" key.  This is
"grouped" in the "AT END" section of file status - but I don't think really
applies in this case.
```

```
>Robert Wagner wrote:

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>delimiters.

You are saying it's not an imperitive STATEMENT because it doesn't have a verb.
It sure looks like an imperitive GO TO NEXT SENTENCE to me. COBOL abandoned
linguistic purity long ago. 

>The NEXT SENTENCE clause can not be arbitralily attached to different 
>statements (unless there is a non-standard extension, such as in MF).
>

>repititions before it sinks in.

us to catch on. Remember, we're COBOL programmers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ More on NEXT SENTENCE (was: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-20T19:38:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b33vut$cl2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b30gm1$qjb$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5428fd.390085362@news.optonline.net> <b31cac$utu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e557514.26717477@news.optonline.net>`

```
The code

   Go To Next-Sentence   (where Next-Sentence with a hyphen is defined as a
procedure)
     *is* a valid COBOL statement (and if followed by a separator period is
a COBOL sentence)

  In the COBOL segment

    ... At End
           Next Sentence

"Next Sentence" is not valid as Standard COBOL has no "Next Sentence" option
for the "AT END" phrase.  On the other hand, in Micro Focus, there *is* an
explicitly documented extension of a "Next Sentence" statement that may be
used anywhere that any imperative statement may be used.  This extension
transfers control to an "implicit" continue immediately before the next
separator period.  As also stated elsewhere, I know of no other compiler
vendor with a "Next Sentence" statement extension - however, there might be
one.  I do know for a fact that Enterprise COBOL V3R2 (from the subject of
the original thread) does NOT have such an extension.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More on NEXT SENTENCE (was: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T09:01:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b35m1i$2h9q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b30gm1$qjb$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5428fd.390085362@news.optonline.net> <b31cac$utu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e557514.26717477@news.optonline.net> <b33vut$cl2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b33vut$cl2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...
> As also stated elsewhere, I know of no other compiler
> vendor with a "Next Sentence" statement extension - however, there might
be
> one.

Unisys MCP/AS COBOL85 allows this as an extension in AT END under READ and
RETURN; in INVALID KEY in READ, WRITE, and REWRITE; in the various verbs
supporting DMSII; and probably in more than a few other places for
compatibility with MCP/AS COBOL74 and COBOL(68) as well.  I *suspect* that
in general Next Sentence is allowed as an extension "by default" on any
exception-handling code, and for those cases in which the compiler disallows
it specific code is introduced in context to do so.

     -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> You are saying it's not an imperitive STATEMENT because it doesn't have a verb.

Am I really ?  Actually I thought that I was saying that NEXT SENTENCE
is not an imperitive statement because it isn't an imperitive
statement in '85 Cobol, it is a phrase in the IF and SEARCH statements
that can be used instead of an imperitive or conditional statement.

Is it too much effort for you to look it up in a reasonable reference
rather than just mumbling half-remembered misinformation ?

> It sure looks like an imperitive GO TO NEXT SENTENCE to me. 

Surely it only looks like part of 'GO TO NEXT SENTENCE', the last two
words.  What it should look like is 'THEN NEXT SENTENCE' or 'ELSE NEXT
SENTENCE'.

> COBOL abandoned linguistic purity long ago. 

What is your evidence that it ever had or wanted 'linguistic purity' ?
especially any form of this that resemble your ramblings ?

> Don't castigate us high-school drop-outs. It just takes a little more time for
> us to catch on. Remember, we're COBOL programmers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-19T20:21:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b31e2e$ii9$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b30gm1$qjb$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5428fd.390085362@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e5428fd.390085362@news.optonline.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> >Something is VERY odd about this report.  Using "NEXT SENTENCE" with an
"AT
> >END" shouldn't even get a clean compile (with any IBM compiler).
>
> Why not? There is a period after NEXT SENTENCE.
>

 <snip>

Robert - others have (in SEVERAL posts) explained your misconception about
"NEXT SENTENCE" (in this case).  I did want to "add" (as I  have mentioned
in another note - and others have mentioned in this part of the thread) that
Micro Focus has (and has had for many years) an extension of a "NEXT
SENTENCE" statement (which *does* go to a period - unless the
OLDNEXTSENTENCE directive is specified).

This note was specifically talking about IBM COBOL compiler - where this
extension has NEVER existed.  If, for any reason, you missed the original
note and hadn't realized that your Micro Focus knowledge isn't relevant,
then just check out the subject line.

   ***

Having read your latest exchange with Chuck Stevens, I just can't say how
strongly I think you need to get a MUCH better understanding of what is and
is not in current ('85 and 2002) and past COBOL ANSI/ISO Standards as well
as what it does and does not mean to be a "conforming compiler" versus
"conforming source code" versus "conforming run units".  These are WELL
defined things and you seem to be quite out of sync with "the real world" of
COBOL and its Standards.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-19T21:51:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b31jcq$8rj$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b30gm1$qjb$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5428fd.390085362@news.optonline.net> <b31e2e$ii9$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Oh come on you guys "at end next sentence" is perfectly
good COBOL. Jeez. It compiles fine for me and it clearly
compiles for the original poster as well. Any imperative
statement is acceptable in an "at end" phase and "next
sentence" certainly qualifies.

The problem is the guys job abends without it. Why? Has
something else changed in the program too? What is the
access? I'm wondering if it was changed from sequential
to dynamic and really needs "next record" rather
than "at end"? An access mode problem is the only thing
I can think of that would cause something like this.

All the Best!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-19T23:15:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b31o9q$2kh$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b30gm1$qjb$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5428fd.390085362@news.optonline.net> <b31e2e$ii9$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <b31jcq$8rj$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`

```
Ron,
  I can guarantee that you don't have *any* IBM COBOL compiler - if you get
a clean compile with

      Read file-name
         At End
                Next Sentence


Much less the compiler that this "guy" is asking about.   My guess is that
you have Micro Focus COBOL - if you actually have tried this - as that is
the ONLY compiler that I know of that has this particular extension.

P.S.  Please note that my original note did indicate that my guess was that
the S0C6 was due to a "loop into the READ" after EOF - but without seeing
valid source code that DOES compile with the compiler in the subject line of
this thread - it is hard for me to give better advice.

P.P.S.  Assuming that you have compiled any program with this construct and
gotten a clean compile (and if my guess is right that it is compiled with
Micro Focus), then I suggest that you try compiling it again with the
directives

  $SET NOMF ANS85 FLAGSTD(H)  FLAGAS(E)

and see if you come back and still say that

"Oh come on you guys "at end next sentence" is perfectly good COBOL"

in a READ AT END phrase
```

#### ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-02-19T18:21:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030219132109.11052.00000655@mb-dh.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com>`

```
Rick Hinten writes ...

>We have just migrated to IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2 (from IBM VS COBOL
>II Rel 4.0, I think) under OS/390 V2 R10.  We have a program reading a
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>this something to do with Enterprise COBOL?

Try putting the file status value in single quotes. That is:

READ SACNT.
   IF SACNT-FILE-STATUS = '10'
       MOVE 'Y' TO SACNT-EOF.

Just a thought. If you are actually coming from an earlier release of COBOL II,
the old compiler let you mix data types, but the new one is much more
stringent. Almost certainly you've defined SACNT-FILE-STATUS as PIC XX. [If you
defined it as PIC 99, you might have other problems.]

Worth a shot, anyway.

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
```

#### ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-20T13:26:28+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b317c3$sj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com>`

```
RickH wrote:


> The new code is:
> 
> READ SACNT
>    AT END NEXT SENTENCE.   <<-- without this clause, we get S0C6

NEXT SENTENCE is not a statement it is the clause of an IF or SEARCH 
statement as long as these are not used with scope delimiters.

You cannot use NEXT SENTENCE just anywhere, you should use CONTINUE.

> error.
> IF SACNT-FILE-STATUS = 10
>    MOVE 'Y' TO SACNT-EOF.

When testing file status for success or end-of-file you should only use the 
first byte.  The 2nd byte may have aditional information, for example '02', 
'14'.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-20T09:35:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19FF68AADB68561E.12C4395243DE95B3.CF5086312F800CBF@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b317c3$sj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
On Thu, 20 Feb 2003 13:26:28 +1300, Richard Plinston
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> enlightened us:

>RickH wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just to add what the IBM COBOL for OS/390 manual says about AT END:

For sequential access, the AT END phrase must be specified if no
applicable USE AFTER STANDARD EXCEPTION procedure is specified for
file-name-1.

 AT END condition: If the file position indicator indicates that no
next  logical record exists, or that an optional input file is not
present, the following occurs in the order specified:


1. A value, derived from the setting of the file position indicator,
is placed into the I-O status associated with file-name-1 to indicate
the at end condition.


2. If the AT END phrase is specified in the statement causing the
condition, control is transferred to imperative-statement-1 in the AT
END phrase. Any USE AFTER STANDARD EXCEPTION procedure associated with
file-name-1 is not executed.


3. If the AT END phrase is not specified, a USE AFTER STANDARD
EXCEPTION procedure must be specified with this file, and the
procedure is executed. Return from that procedure is to the next
executable statement following the end of the READ statement.



If the AT END phrase is not specified, a USE AFTER STANDARD EXCEPTION
procedure does not have to be associated with file-name-1.


When the at end condition occurs, execution of the READ statement is
unsuccessful. The contents of the associated record area are undefined
and the file position indicator is set to indicate that no valid next
record has been established. Attempts to access or move data into the
read record area following an unsuccessful read can result in a
protection exception.


If an at end condition does not occur during the execution of a READ
statement, the AT END phrase is ignored, if specified, and the
following actions occur:


1. The file position indicator is set and the I-O status associated
with file-name-1 is updated.


2. If an exception condition that is not an at end condition exists,
control is transferred to the end of the READ statement following the
execution of any USE AFTER STANDARD EXCEPTION procedure applicable to
file-name-1.


If no USE AFTER STANDARD EXCEPTION procedure is specified, control is
transferred to the end of the READ statement or to
imperative-statement-2, if specified.


3. If no exception condition exists, the record is made available in
the record area and any implicit move resulting from the presence of
an INTO phrase is executed. Control is transferred to the end of the
READ statement or to imperative-statement-2, if specified. In the
latter case, execution continues according to the rules for each
statement specified in imperative-statement-2. If a procedure
branching or conditional statement which causes explicit transfer of
control is executed, control is transferred in accordance with the
rules for that statement; otherwise, upon completion of the execution
of imperative-statement-2, control is transferred to the end of the
READ statement.


Following the unsuccessful execution of a READ statement, the contents
of the associated record area are undefined and the file position
indicator is set to indicate that no valid next record has been
established. Attempts to access or move data into the record area
following an unsuccessful read can result in a protection exception. 

Some info on FILE- STATUS:

The FILE STATUS clause monitors the execution of each input-output
operation for the file.


When the FILE STATUS clause is specified, the system moves a value
into the status key data item after each input-output operation that
explicitly or implicitly refers to this file. The value indicates the
status of execution of the statement. (See the "status key"
description under "Common processing facilities" in topic 6.1.8.9.)


data-name-1
The status key data item can be defined in the Working-Storage,
X Local-Storage, or Linkage sections as either of the following:


    * A 2-character alphanumeric item
    *

A 2-character numeric data item, with explicit or implicit USAGE
IS DISPLAY. It is treated as an alphanumeric item.

Each byte has a meaning.  A table of those meanings can be found here:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGYLR205/6.1.8.9.1?SHELF=&DT=20000927030801

HTH

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Vulcanization: The process whereby rubber grows pointed ears and 
starts saying "Live long and prosper". 
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-20T12:43:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302201243.66d5941d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b317c3$sj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19FF68AADB68561E.12C4395243DE95B3.CF5086312F800CBF@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com> wrote 

> When the at end condition occurs, execution of the READ statement is
> unsuccessful. The contents of the associated record area are undefined
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> protection exception.

I find this difficult to believe that it is actually true.  For
example if it is an ISAM file then it must be valid to move a new key
into the record area for a START or READ to follow, or even to move in
a complete new set of data for a WRITE.

While a CLOSE may reasonably make the record area disappear and thus
cause a protection violation if it is attempted to be accesses a READ
AT END may cause the record area to have invalid or random data, it
certainly should not make the record area inaccessible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T09:54:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F217AD81EBB57B1.53ACD56B12A0C208.08DA534D99F60837@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b317c3$sj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19FF68AADB68561E.12C4395243DE95B3.CF5086312F800CBF@lp.airnews.net> <217e491a.0302201243.66d5941d@posting.google.com>`

```
On 20 Feb 2003 12:43:01 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
enlightened us:

>SkippyPB <swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com> wrote 
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>certainly should not make the record area inaccessible.

Believe me it is true.  Before I learned better, I had it happen to me
a few times.  This condition happens if you try to access the data
described in the FD area.  That is, if you try to access the record
after AT END has happened and that record description is in the FD.
If you've been doing READ record INTO ws-storage-area, and you
encounter AT END, the data in ws-storage-area is as it was on the
previous read because AT END would keep anything from being moved into
ws-storage-area.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

starts saying "Live long and prosper". 
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T11:37:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302211137.6af75fa6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b317c3$sj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19FF68AADB68561E.12C4395243DE95B3.CF5086312F800CBF@lp.airnews.net> <217e491a.0302201243.66d5941d@posting.google.com> <3F217AD81EBB57B1.53ACD56B12A0C208.08DA534D99F60837@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com> wrote 

> >> Attempts to access or move data into the
> >> read record area following an unsuccessful read can result in a
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> ws-storage-area.

Your anecdote does not cover the issue that I was taking.

Perhaps I should be more clear.

I agree that after a READ AT END the record area may be unchanged and
contain the previous record, or it may contain garbage or anything
else. That is quite normal.

However, it states "Attempts to .. move data into the read record area
.. can result in a protection exception."

In other words the code below may abend, or at least not work:

      SELECT afile .. ORGANISATION INDEXED ACCESS SEQUENTIAL ..
      ....
      OPEN INPUT afile
      PERFORM 
          UNTIL EoF

           READ afile
               AT END
                   SET EoF TO TRUE
           END-READ
       END-PERFORM

       MOVE New-Key      TO afile-Key
       START afile
            ....

This is a perfectly valid sequence of operations on the file and yet
it states that there may be an abend (protection exception) before the
START.

Now it may be that a protection violation will occur after the file is
CLOSEd because the record area is freed from memory.  The Cobol manual
states this may occur.  That is quite different from a simple failed
read.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T14:17:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b361hr$d42$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b317c3$sj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19FF68AADB68561E.12C4395243DE95B3.CF5086312F800CBF@lp.airnews.net> <217e491a.0302201243.66d5941d@posting.google.com> <3F217AD81EBB57B1.53ACD56B12A0C208.08DA534D99F60837@lp.airnews.net> <217e491a.0302211137.6af75fa6@posting.google.com>`

```
At first I thought that Skippy was right - but looking at the '85 Standard
any compiler that works the way he describes is NOT conforming.   The
following is the *correct* wording from the '85 Standard (which corresponds
with what Richard was saying),

Page IX-31, GR12:

"(12) Following the unsuccessful execution of a READ statement, the content
of the associated record area is undefined, the key of reference is
undefined for indexed files, and the file position indicator is set to
indicate that no valid next record has been established."

Note well: that does *NOT* say that the "record area" is unavailabe - rather
that the CONTENTS of the record area are "undefined".

FYI -
  As Skippy originally mentioned an IBM language reference, I just checked
the most current one - and at:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR10/6.2.28.6
.1

It does include the (non-conforming as far as I can tell) words

 "Attempts to access or move data into the read record area following an
unsuccessful read can result in a protection exception."

It would be VERY interesting for some current IBM customer to ask them if
they really think this is '85 Standard conforming.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-22T02:12:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I0B5a.10460$F%.1101623@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b317c3$sj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19FF68AADB68561E.12C4395243DE95B3.CF5086312F800CBF@lp.airnews.net> <217e491a.0302201243.66d5941d@posting.google.com> <3F217AD81EBB57B1.53ACD56B12A0C208.08DA534D99F60837@lp.airnews.net> <217e491a.0302211137.6af75fa6@posting.google.com> <b361hr$d42$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The reason behind this is that the record area is implemented as a pointer in a DCB macro, which means that the when the contents of
the record area are undefined, it is definitely "unavailable."  The pointer is pointing to garbage or whatever. There are some
special manipulations that take place to preserve the record area.

As to whether or not it is conforming, well, from the point of view of a mainframe it is. Undefined and Unavailable are as close to
synonymous in this case as it gets. In terms of workstation COBOL, in particular where DCBs are not the normal way to open files, I
just don't know.

I do know it makes technical sense in the mainframe world. though. :)

-Paul


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:b361hr$d42$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net...
> At first I thought that Skippy was right - but looking at the '85 Standard
> any compiler that works the way he describes is NOT conforming.   The
…[86 more quoted lines elided]…

```

```
OS390, z/OS) in accessing the record area after end of file went away
with VS COBOL II and later.  It definitely went away for VSAM.  I
believe that I ran tests to verify this a couple of years ago but this
could just be a sometimer's moment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-22T21:28:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e57eb71.188115176@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b317c3$sj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19FF68AADB68561E.12C4395243DE95B3.CF5086312F800CBF@lp.airnews.net> <217e491a.0302201243.66d5941d@posting.google.com> <3F217AD81EBB57B1.53ACD56B12A0C208.08DA534D99F60837@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com> wrote:

>On 20 Feb 2003 12:43:01 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
>enlightened us:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>after AT END has happened and that record description is in the FD.

This happens because IBM compilers using SOME file systems set a pointer into a
buffer rather than moving the record into storage allocated locally like
working-storage. When a file with 'access is sequential' (stated or implied)
reaches the end, some file systems aggressively close it and release its
buffers. 

If this becomes a problem, the solution is READ .. INTO. Why would you want to
access a record which isn't there? Perhaps to fill it with high-values, to
simplify program logic. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 6)_

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> If this becomes a problem, the solution is READ .. INTO. Why would you want to
> access a record which isn't there? Perhaps to fill it with high-values, to
> simplify program logic. 

No.  The example was supplied.  It is to move in a new key and to
START so that the file can, for example, be read in a different
position.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-23T23:04:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e59508f.279550921@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b317c3$sj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19FF68AADB68561E.12C4395243DE95B3.CF5086312F800CBF@lp.airnews.net> <217e491a.0302201243.66d5941d@posting.google.com> <3F217AD81EBB57B1.53ACD56B12A0C208.08DA534D99F60837@lp.airnews.net> <3e57eb71.188115176@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302222235.fd3c2f3@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
>> If this becomes a problem, the solution is READ .. INTO. Why would you want
to
>> access a record which isn't there? Perhaps to fill it with high-values, to
>> simplify program logic. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>position.

The original complaint concerned S0C6 when using AT END NEXT SENTENCE. Then
SkippyPB quoted an IBM COBOL manual which began "For sequential access, the AT
END phrase must be specified if no
applicable USE AFTER STANDARD EXCEPTION procedure is specified for
file-name-1.", and went on to mention lost addressability after the end
condition. You replied you doubted that because the record needed to be
available for another START or READ. 

You made two errors here:

1. The original complaint was about S0C6, which indicates Program Check. Memory
protection errors are S0C4 (read) and S0C5 (write). 

2. The manual quotation was talking about _input_ files opened for _sequential
access_. Not files opened I-O and not files with dynamic access.

I tried unsuccessfully to clarify this, and mentioned in passing READ .. INTO as
a solution to the lost addessability problem on _sequential_  files. 

In practice, VSAM files do not lose addressability to the record area, even when
ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL. Only SAM files lose addressability. One cannot do a START
on a SAM file. 

The answer to the original question is that the COBOL run-time must have been
unhappy with NEXT SENTENCE, sent an error message to who-knows-where, and issued
a Program Check. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T14:13:52+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3brm5$kdn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <3e57eb71.188115176@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302222235.fd3c2f3@posting.google.com> <3e59508f.279550921@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>No.  The example was supplied.  It is to move in a new key and to
>>START so that the file can, for example, be read in a different
>>position.
 
> The original complaint concerned S0C6 when using AT END NEXT SENTENCE.
> Then SkippyPB quoted an IBM COBOL manual which began "For sequential
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> You made two errors here:

No. Wrong.  I didn't make any 'errors' at all.
 
> 1. The original complaint was about S0C6, which indicates Program Check.

That is quite irrelevant, the discussion had moved on from there, do try 
and keep up.
 
> 2. The manual quotation was talking about _input_ files opened for
> _sequential access_. Not files opened I-O and not files with dynamic
> access.

My example specifically had 'ACCESS SEQUENTIAL' and 'OPEN INPUT'.

You do not need to open a file I-O, or have dynamic access, in order to be 
able to use START.

> I tried unsuccessfully to clarify this, and mentioned in passing READ ..
> INTO as
> a solution to the lost addessability problem on _sequential_  files.

READ ... INTO, is _not_ a solution for setting the key prior to doing a 
START.  The new key _must_ be moved to the actual key area in the record in 
the FD and not to some working-storage area.

You neither 'clarified' nor 'solved', but just completely missed the point.

> In practice, VSAM files do not lose addressability to the record area,
> even when ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL. 

Yet the manual makes no such distinction, which is why I disbelieved that 
the manual was correct.  You now confirm that I was right to question what 
the manual stated.
 
> The answer to the original question is that the COBOL run-time must have
> been unhappy with NEXT SENTENCE, sent an error message to who-knows-where,
> and issued a Program Check.

This had already been discussed and resolved in spite of your misleading 
and unhelpful input which was:

WMK >> Something is VERY odd about this report.  Using "NEXT SENTENCE" 
WMK >> with an "AT
WMK >> END" shouldn't even get a clean compile (with any IBM compiler). 
 
RW > Why not? There is a period after NEXT SENTENCE. 

At least you appear to have learnt something about NEXT SENTENCE now.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e5987df.293712369@news.optonline.net...
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
> >Robert Wagner wrote:
> >
 <snip>
>
> Mr. Klein thought the 'error' should have been detected at compile time.
In
> fact, it was detected at execution time. This was NOT already discussed,
it is
> original to this thread.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Mr. Klein *guaranteed* that the code - as posted - would not compile -
therefore, none of us could really answer the original question.  I did ask
(as others did) to see what the REAL code was that was causing the S0C6.  I
did (in my original post) give a GUESS as to what might be happening.

If you can give any indication of ANY IBM compiler - much less the one
mentioned in the subject line - that actually would give a clean compile
with the original source code (and thereby get a run-time S0C6) - then
someone might be able to be more to the point in a reply.

If anyone wants to see the LRM from the product in the subject line, you can
see it online at:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR10/CCONTENT
S

A simple "online search" at that site will show that the "NEXT SENTENCE"
phrase (not statement) is mentioned in that manual at (and ONLY at) the
following places.

 (for the SEARCH statement)

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3lr10/6.2.32.2

(for the IF statement)
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3lr10/6.2.19


and in other parts of "6.2.19" and "6.2.32" (as well as the Glossary and
the Appendix on IBM extensions where it documents the use of "NEXT SENTENCE"
with END-IF)

P.S.  The term "SAM" also  is *not* relevant to Enterprise COBOL V3R2.  It
is only (currently) used with IBM VSE compilers - not those on z/OS.   The
subject also makes it clear that the original discussion was *not* talking
about QSAM - but VSAM - although from the little portion posted, it isn't
possible to tell if it was talking about ESDS (Organization Sequential -
Access Sequential)  *or* if it was talking about KSDS (Organization
Indexed - Access Sequential).

I would, however, actually be interested if anyone has heard back from the
original poster.  I am certain if "we" could see the actual Enterprise COBOL
V3R2 COBOL source code that received a "clean compile" - then we might be
able to help with the S0C6.
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <3e57eb71.188115176@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302222235.fd3c2f3@posting.google.com> <3e59508f.279550921@news.optonline.net> <b3brm5$kdn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e5987df.293712369@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >You neither 'clarified' nor 'solved', but just completely missed the point.
> 
> No, you missed the point. You are talking about VSAM files when the point is
> about SAM sequential files. 

Here is from the original posting:

>> We have a program reading a VSAM KSDS file sequentially

Here is from the posting on the manual that started this sub-thread:

>> Just to add what the IBM COBOL for OS/390 manual says about AT END:

>> For sequential access, the AT END phrase must be ...

This is about _sequential_access_, there is no mention of SAM
sequential files anywhere until you made it up.

> Mr. Klein thought the 'error' should have been detected at compile time. In
> fact, it was detected at execution time. This was NOT already discussed, it is
> original to this thread. 

In fact you failed to notice the actual conditions stated:

>>> READ SACNT
>>>    AT END NEXT SENTENCE.   <<-- without this clause, we get S0C6
error

_With_ the AT END NEXT SENTENCE clause there will be a compiler error.

_Without_ the clause (as specified in the question) they get a S0C6
error when they run the program.  This is obviously because doing a
random READ (without AT END or USE ..) gives a run time error.

Nowhere did anyone imply that the S0C6 was the result of compiling the
NEXT SENTENCE and running it.

> I answered the poster's question while you and others talked around it.

What utter nonsense. You completely failed to even read the question
correctly. The actual question was answered immediately by:

WMK>> Using "NEXT SENTENCE" with an "AT END" shouldn't even get a
clean compile (with any IBM compiler).

RP>> You cannot use NEXT SENTENCE just anywhere, you should use
CONTINUE.

Your 'contribution' was not knowing Cobol well enough to know that
NEXT SENTENCE was invalid in standard Cobol:

RW>> Why not? There is a period after NEXT SENTENCE. 

And then completely failing to notice that it was about a VSAM file
and 'solved' the wrong problem.

Your claim is completely fraudulent.
```

```
>>>>    AT END NEXT SENTENCE.   <<-- without this clause, we get S0C6

Whoops. I did misremember the original complaint. My apologies. 

Still, the answer is the same. The COBOL run-time committed suicide because it
couldn't handle AT END. 

>_With_ the AT END NEXT SENTENCE clause there will be a compiler error.
>
>_Without_ the clause (as specified in the question) they get a S0C6
>error when they run the program.  This is obviously because doing a
>random READ (without AT END or USE ..) gives a run time error.

Sequential, not random.

>WMK>> Using "NEXT SENTENCE" with an "AT END" shouldn't even get a
>clean compile (with any IBM compiler).
>
>RP>> You cannot use NEXT SENTENCE just anywhere, you should use
>CONTINUE.

It used to work, in COBOL '74, when NEXT SENTENCE could be used anywhere and was
almost the same as CONTINUE (except for those pesky periods).

>And then completely failing to notice that it was about a VSAM file
>and 'solved' the wrong problem.

The sidebar about lost addressability distracted me. VSAM files don't have that
problem. 

>Your claim is completely fraudulent.

Studying for the bar, are you?

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T15:30:21+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ekhs$qnj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <3e5987df.293712369@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302232307.37f9fea5@posting.google.com> <3e5aa9e4.11892861@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>WMK>> Using "NEXT SENTENCE" with an "AT END" shouldn't even get a
>>clean compile (with any IBM compiler).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> and was almost the same as CONTINUE (except for those pesky periods).

No. Wrong. Once again: NEXT SENTENCE in standards for '74, '85 and '2002, 
and probably in all previous standards, is a phrase of the IF statement and 
of the SEARCH statement.

NEXT SENTENCE is not an imperitive statement, nor even a statemnet.

However, there are some compilers, such as MF, that offers NEXT SENTENCE as 
a statement as an _extension_.

Please do go an read an introductory text on the basics of COBOL.
```

- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com> <b317c3$sj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19FF68AADB68561E.12C4395243DE95B3.CF5086312F800CBF@lp.airnews.net> <217e491a.0302201243.66d5941d@posting.google.com> <3F217AD81EBB57B1.53ACD56B12A0C208.08DA534D99F60837@lp.airnews.net> <3e57eb71.188115176@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302222235.fd3c2f3@posting.google.com> <3e59508f.279550921@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Sun, 23 Feb 2003 23:04:26 GMT robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>1. The original complaint was about S0C6, which indicates Program Check. Memory
:>protection errors are S0C4 (read) and S0C5 (write). 

S0C6 is a specification exception, which is caused by either branching to a
bad address, a non-aligned special instruction such as {LPSW,CS,CDS} or using
an odd register when the instruction requires a register pair.

S0C4 is an omnibus protection error on read or write when either the key
disallows the requested access or the storage is not allocated.

S0C5 is an addressing exception which does not occur on MVS^W OS390^W zOS in
DAT mode.

   [ snipped ]
```

#### ↳ Re: File Status error with VSAM files in IBM Enterprise COBOL V3R2

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T14:34:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0302241434.6c4103b7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<52179075.0302190940.321f3e0b@posting.google.com>`

```
In the interest of actually correcting your problem, might I suggest
that the LE facilities provide much better diagnostics that the Cobol
file codes.  If you just remove the file status clause and change your
code to read:

 READ SACNT
   AT END 
     MOVE 'Y' TO SACNT-EOF
 END-READ

<style-guide content-type="opinion/religious">
This would be much better if it was anonymous with conditionals, e.g.
'SET SACNT-EOF TO TRUE', this keeps maintenance programmers from every
doing 'MOVE 'X' TO SACNT-EOF and really screwing things up.
</style-guide>

LE will nicely diagnose your SOC6 for you with a very precise and easy
to understand message.

Regards,

Joe Zitzelberger

> II Rel 4.0, I think) under OS/390 V2 R10.  We have a program reading a
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: Parametrizing a JCL — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Parametrizing a JCL

- **From:** chuck_leviton@yahoo.com (charles leviton)
- **Date:** 2003-02-20T07:31:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d280a253.0302200731.1a56e21f@posting.google.com>`

```
I want to run a job that uses different datasets depending on a
parameter.  The name of the dataset will be of the form
"HLQ.parm.other qualifiers", where the only change in the Dataset name
will be the "parm".  The operator will be able to change it prior to
running the job.

My first question is where can I place this parameter?  Which leads me
to the next question, viz can the DSN be like a variable where the
name is resolved only when the job is executed?

I hope I have phrased the question in an understandable fashion.  To
give an example, I have a program that processes a payroll file.  On
different days I want to process payrolls for different locations but
I want to use the same job and supply the location name as a
parameter.
```

#### ↳ Re: Parametrizing a JCL

- **From:** Terry Sambrooks <terry@kmsitltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-02-20T11:43:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5505aa$1_5@news.onlynews.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0302200731.1a56e21f@posting.google.com>`

```
Charles,

There are a number of ways that symbolic parameters or some other type
of variable may be of use.

I know that some Batch Scheduling systems, such as OPC or whatever its
called these days, cater for variables and a word with your friendly
Operations team may assist in this area.

Beyond that JCL itself caters for Symbolic Parameters which are
basically variable with a name upto 7 characters long preceeded by a
single ampersand (&), so

// DSN=HLQ.&PARM..OTHER.QUALIFIERS

would be OK.

Assuming for a moment that catalogue procedures are being used, then if
the value of &PARM is more often one value rather than another, then a
default can be established on the PROC statement within the procedure.

Any default can be overridden at run time on the invoking EXEC statement
for the procedure without having to amend the procedure itself.

There are other examples of symbolic parameter use on my WEB site at:

www.kmsitltd.co.uk/jclex5s.html

I hope this is of use.

Regards - Terry
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parametrizing a JCL

- **From:** "Thomas Charles" <pakku@soccermail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T03:37:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<J9h5a.26673$9i7.1786309@twister.southeast.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0302200731.1a56e21f@posting.google.com> <3e5505aa$1_5@news.onlynews.com>`

```
"Terry Sambrooks" <terry@kmsitltd.co.uk> wrote in message
news:3e5505aa$1_5@news.onlynews.com...
> Charles,
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You can use the SET statement which will let you bypass the PROC.  The
syntax is fairly simple, you set a variable to a value in a "step" prior to
the one you want to execute your program in.  Then in that step you use the
usual & notation to refer to the variable.
If you want to totally eliminate operator intervention you could consider
using a parmlib that you can change yourself.  Then you would need to write
a program of some sort that will read this parmlib and output a dataset
name.  But I am not certain how you could use a variable (symbolic
parameter) from an earlier step as a DSN name.
```

#### ↳ Re: Parametrizing a JCL

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-20T23:29:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E55AB0E.EBE5502C@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0302200731.1a56e21f@posting.google.com>`

```
charles leviton wrote:
> 
> I want to run a job that uses different datasets depending on a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> parameter.

.
.
.
//PAYROLL  PROC SLQ=DFW
//S1       EXEC PGM=????
//INPUT    DD  DSN=HLQ.&SLQ..OTHER.QUALS,DISP=...
.
.
.
//         PEND ,


The PEND is only needed if this PROC is used inline. There
is another way to do this:

//xxx JOB ....
//*
//  SET PARM=DFW
//*
//S1      EXEC PGM=????
//INPUT   DD  DSN=HLQ.&PARM..OTHER.QUALS,DISP=...
.
.
.

I hope this helps.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parametrizing a JCL

- **From:** chuck_leviton@yahoo.com (charles leviton)
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T07:13:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d280a253.0302210713.7095989e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0302200731.1a56e21f@posting.google.com> <3E55AB0E.EBE5502C@ix.netcom.com>`

```
> 
> .
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> --
Thank you all, the Proc and the SET still require operator
involvement.  Since Thomas mentioned it I am curious to know if it can
be avoided.  The suggestion seems intriguing.
Thomas (or anyone else), how exactly could I have 1 step of a job
output a DataSet name (after reading a Parmlib) which can be used in a
later step.

TIA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parametrizing a JCL

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T21:44:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5AD875.7249D1AB@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0302200731.1a56e21f@posting.google.com> <3E55AB0E.EBE5502C@ix.netcom.com> <d280a253.0302210713.7095989e@posting.google.com>`

```
charles leviton wrote:
<snip>
> Thank you all, the Proc and the SET still require operator
> involvement.  Since Thomas mentioned it I am curious to know if it can
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> TIA

If you read a parmlib member, you can put the DSN you are
after into a file:

//PARMDATA DD
UNIT=SYSDA,DSN=&&DSNDATA,SPACE=(TRK,1),DISP=(,PASS),
//            DCB=(LRECL=??,RECFM=FB,BLKSIZE=0)


Successive steps can read this and get the data set name
they need from the file and then do dynamic allocation for
the DSN. However, your shop may or may not have the level of
the COBOL compiler & Runtime that supports dynamic
allocation (which may mean you will have to go to an ALC
subroutine).

Otherwise, you are in the position of generating the JCL
from w/in the COBOL program of a one step JOB and have that
JOB write to the INTRDR.

Were you in a VSE shop you might get away with generating
the JCL on the fly...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parametrizing a JCL

_(thread depth: 4)_

#### ↳ Re: Parametrizing a JCL

- **From:** weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T15:16:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b35frj$b95$1@news.utelfla.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0302200731.1a56e21f@posting.google.com>`

```
charles leviton <chuck_leviton@yahoo.com> wrote:

: I want to run a job that uses different datasets depending on a
: parameter.  The name of the dataset will be of the form
: "HLQ.parm.other qualifiers", where the only change in the Dataset name
: will be the "parm".  The operator will be able to change it prior to
: running the job.

: give an example, I have a program that processes a payroll file.  On
: different days I want to process payrolls for different locations but
: I want to use the same job and supply the location name as a
: parameter.

I might be misunderstanding what you are asking here, but why don't
you use a runcard containing the parameter?

Ex:

//STEP01 EXEC PGM=PAYAB01
//STEPLIB DD (blah blah)
//SYSOUT  DD SYSOUT=*
//INFILE  DD DSN=PAY.PF010,DISP=OLD
//RUNCARD DD *
01    <= LOCATION CODE 
/*
 
HTH!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parametrizing a JCL

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T20:56:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0302212056.45316a62@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0302200731.1a56e21f@posting.google.com> <b35frj$b95$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
Hi Ubie,

If entering the parm data into a dataset is acceptable to you, you can
write a CIBOL pgm that will construct job JCL and submit it to an
internal reader.

The general logic of the pgm is to read your parm data and use it to
construct a DD stmt that contains a DSN unique to that run. That and
the other JCL stmts would be written to a SYSOUT file with an INTRDR
clause. Closing that file triggers the submission of the JCL to a JES
reader and eventually executed.

Each time this pgm is executed the parm data supplied by you is used
to create the desired DSN.

Search the archives here using INTRDR as the argument, I'm sure you'll
find many hits to provide the coding details you'll need.

HTH, Jack.

P.S. Some may say this is frowned upon, but it's used every day in
CICS environments.


weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous) wrote in message news:<b35frj$b95$1@news.utelfla.com>...
> charles leviton <chuck_leviton@yahoo.com> wrote:
> 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> HTH!
```

## Thread: Call for Papers (Future of COBOL) — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Call for Papers (Future of COBOL)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-20T13:32:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b33ahd$828$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Call for Papers


Members of the COBOL standards community (INCITS/J4 and ISO/IEC
JTC1/SC22/WG4) together with COBOL Expo 2003 would like to know what your
vision is for the COBOL language 5 years from now.  There will be a workshop
on Wednesday June 25, 2003 at the University of Nevada, Las Vegas (UNLV).



Special topics of interest include, but are not limited to, immediate needs
like XML and future ones like asynchronous processing and a macro/template
facility.



Papers must be sent by April 30, 2003 to Don.Schricker@microfocus.com.
Papers may be submitted electronically in Word, PDF, or HTML format.  For
other formats, check with Don Schricker.



Relevant technical papers will be available on the web.  Time may not permit
presentations at the workshop on all the papers submitted.  Those who are
chosen to present their papers will be notified by Friday May 23, 2003.



Everyone is encouraged to attend the presentations at the workshop on June
25, 2003 of the selected papers.



All submitted papers must contain the following:



Paper title:  ____________________



The submitter (hereinafter the "Author"), desiring that the paper identified
above (hereinafter the "Paper") be presented at COBOL Expo 2003 and appear
on the INCITS/J4 web site with other submitted papers, hereby grants
non-exclusive permission to INCITS/J4 to distribute the Paper, or portions
thereof, at COBOL Expo 2003, to publish the Paper on the web, and to use the
Paper as the basis for part of a COBOL standard or technical report.



The Author retains copyright, right to transfer the copyright to other
parties in the future, right to use any and all portions of the Paper in
future publications by Author.



The Author warrants that he/she is the author and/or proprietor of the
Paper; that he/she has full power to make this agreement; that the Paper
does not infringe any copyright, trademark, or patent; and that he/she has
not granted or assigned any right on the Paper to any person or entity that
would interfere with this grant of permission.



Don Schricker
Micro Focus
Product Manager, Language Standards
Chairman J4, the COBOL technical committee of INCITS
Don.Schricker@microfocus.com
http://www.cobolstandards.com
http://www.microfocus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: API & Cobol

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T22:11:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Hux5a.15176$UF6.1586255@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<nKq5a.3571$EM4.2033677@newsserver.ip.pt> <7au5a.15064$UF6.1559973@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <1fu5a.3583$Ac1.2175044@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```
"pty0" <xxx@clix.pt> wrote in message
news:1fu5a.3583$Ac1.2175044@newsserver.ip.pt...
> I want to know  exactly in a net how many users are  accede a certain
> program at the same time (EXE) .
> The "exe" be in a server that is shared for the several users .
> With API programing.

Windows systems?

AFAIK the ONLY way to do this is to code something into the server program
to keep track.  And for that, there are only about umpty-nine different ways
to do it, each perfectly valid and reasonable.

Maybe there is some kind of intrinsic utility or language element in the
.NET family, since crossing CPU boundaries is what .NET is all about. But
the standard Windows API does not have anything which can go out and query
how many users are running program "X" (unless you run an interrogation on
each CPU in the network and report it back to the server - which brings us
right back to where we started: you have to code "something" in the server
to do this. Maybe a disk file, maybe a memory-mapped file, maybe a semaphore
if you want to control the user count, lots of other ways.

Enumwindows (a suggestion you received) is only valid within a single CPU..
which brings us back to the above..you'd have to run EnumWindows on each CPU
on the network and have that enum report back somewhere - presumably the
server.

One last thought... maybe your network software has an API which keeps track
of this. Check there.

If that doesn't pan out and you need someone to design and write this for
your server, I'd be happy to offer a proposal.
```

```
updated/deleted and you can think that the program is still open

for example - on program start I create a file called "users.dat" - on exit
I remove the file to show not in use but if computer crashes the file is
still there - tricking me into thinking the program is still running
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: API & Cobol

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T16:01:28-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302211601.7a601db5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<nKq5a.3571$EM4.2033677@newsserver.ip.pt> <7au5a.15064$UF6.1559973@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <Ilv5a.186458$SD6.9913@sccrnsc03>`

```
"Steve" <Steven_31@aol.com> wrote

> I have tried that too and an abend does not allow the file to be
> updated/deleted and you can think that the program is still open
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> still there - tricking me into thinking the program is still running

With a reasonable operating system you can get each copy of the
program to open a single isam file on the server, create a unique
record there and then read it with lock.  At end of run delete record
and close.

If the program crashes or otherwise stops then the operating system
removes the lock.  The count would thus be, not the number of records,
but the number that are still locked, any unlocked record can be
deleted.

However, I don't think that Windows does this, record locks may
survive after the process has crashed.
```

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<Io6dnVe2kKe9X8ujXTWchA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<nKq5a.3571$EM4.2033677@newsserver.ip.pt> <7au5a.15064$UF6.1559973@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <Ilv5a.186458$SD6.9913@sccrnsc03>`

```

"Steve" <Steven_31@aol.com> wrote in message
news:Ilv5a.186458$SD6.9913@sccrnsc03...
> I have tried that too and an abend does not allow the file to be
> updated/deleted and you can think that the program is still open
>
> for example - on program start I create a file called "users.dat" - on
exit
> I remove the file to show not in use but if computer crashes the file is
> still there - tricking me into thinking the program is still running

Create the file every 'x' interval.

When it's time to check, if the time between now and the last time the file
was created is greater than 'x', the program responsible for creating the
file has crashed.
```

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the file has crashed.

That relies on the program actually being in a position to be able to do 
that within the end of the last interval 'x' and some allowance 'y'.

With a batch program, for example, this would be relatively easy because 
code could be put into the processing cycle to check whether the time is 
between 'x' and 'x+y'.

An interactive program may be waiting for user input and no code will 
execute until the user hits a key and the focus is on that program.  Even 
then it would be an onerous task to build the check into every possible 
part of the program that may be active.

It would be necessary to build in a timer interrupt routine and have the OS 
activate the program whenever the timer interval specified expired. This 
would require that it be written to, say, be a real Windows program so that 
the API and callbacks can be used.  It would also be necessary to ensure 
that the filehandling system is reentrant as the timer may expire during 
the program a file access.
```

#### ↳ Re: API & Cobol

- **From:** "Calin @ Work" <dontemailme@work.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T18:07:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
There are more questions here, only one of them being: is your exe ran from
the server or from individual workstations?

If it's from the server, maybe you can create a little app that you can
start as a service on your server to track the number of yourprogram.exe
that run at a certain time.  This app would have a "howManyUsers" entry
point/method that would return the required info.

If it's from the workstation, try taping into your service and call/invoke
the "addUser" entry point/method.

Good luck!
Calin, TORONTO
```

```
Windows as host. In which case there is an article of how to use the
Windows API to achieve this information from ACUCOBOL-GT. 

Obviously, the article and the related source examples has the angle of
ACUCOBOL-GT, I do believe however, that it should be possible to
translate this to other COBOLs with not too much effort.

	http://www.acucorp.com/company/newsletter

	2002 Issue 3 - Cobol And The Need For Interoperability

This is the artcle.

To get to see the code, go to:

	http://www.acucorp.com/support/public/sample_programs/index.php

In the Combo Box, select "Programs Featured in Acucorp News"

Eventually get the zip archive "speaking.zip"
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T16:22:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b36frf$lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com...

> The very best way is to not use indexes at all (unless absolutely
necessary
> as in a SEARCH) - use subscripts.

Not on all machines.  Some systems and implementations differentiate between
the two, and indices can be much more efficient than subscripts   This isn't
always meaningful, but it can be in critical-path environments.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>

```
>
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not on all machines.  Some systems and implementations differentiate
between
> the two, and indices can be much more efficient than subscripts   This
isn't
> always meaningful, but it can be in critical-path environments.

Nah (assuming, of course, the implementor didn't do something really dumb
like using display subscripts).

If there's any doubt, a 5-line timing program would settle the matter for
the suspect machine. Once it is verified that subscripts and indexes are
equivalent, one can purge indexing from the brain, thereby making room for
other stuff.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Louis Krupp <lkrupp@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-02-23T21:58:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E59A65E.4090601@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <b36frf$lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oKydndQWy-SfH8qjXTWcjA@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> Nah (assuming, of course, the implementor didn't do something really dumb
> like using display subscripts).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> other stuff.

Purge away if you can;  some of us are stuck with it, leaving little
contiguous space for whatever's next.

To the question of whether or not subscripts and indexes are
equivalent:

I did a timing test (on an Alpha) with something like three billion
repetitions of "move something to a(i)" where "i" was an index vs.
a subscript.  The indexes were about 30% faster (10 seconds vs. 13).

Are they equal?  No.  Are they equivalent, in the sense that it's
not likely to matter one way or the other?  I'd have to admit that
they are.

Is that likely to stop anyone who reads this group from using
indexes?  No.  It's not in our nature.  As long as indexes exist
and are marginally faster and show us to have a slightly broader
command of the language and enhance our self esteem and improve
our chances of breeding, we'll use them.

You can't argue with biology.

Louis Krupp
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T09:07:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3djfr$1l49$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <b36frf$lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oKydndQWy-SfH8qjXTWcjA@giganews.com>`

```
Unisys MCP/AS COBOL(68) and COBOL74 both unconditionally set indices to the
actual offset of the data item being indexed, not to the subscript value.
For example
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:oKydndQWy-SfH8qjXTWcjA@giganews.com...

> Nah (assuming, of course, the implementor didn't do something really dumb
> like using display subscripts).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> other stuff.

Unisys MCP/AS COBOL74 unconditionally stores an INDEXED BY index in an
on-stack integer variable, and the value stored in it reflects the offset
from the first occurrence in the table as well as the table stride.  By
default, the range of the index is checked on reference *by the hardware* to
ensure that it does not go outside the bounds of the RECORD, but it is not
so rigorous as to insist that it may not go outside the bounds of the TABLE.

Subscripts are stored wherever they're declared, in whatever format they're
declared, and always represent a table entry occurrence NUMBER in that
format, so when used as a subscript the value must undergo a multiply and an
add to convert its value into an offset to the desired data item.  Though
it's possible to get a subscript quite close in performance to an index by
declaring it 77  USAGE BINARY EXTENDED PICTURE S9(11) and by setting the
Compiler Control Option $OPTIMIZE to defeat range checking on subscripts,
you still have the distinction that the stored value in an INDEX is an
OFFSET to the beginning of the desired field, and the stored value in a
SUBSCRIPT is an OCCURRENCE NUMBER of the desired field, and however little
the multiply (of the stride) and the add (of the table's offset) might add,
unless the table offset is zero and the stride is one there is *some* more
cost associated with the subscript.

As I said before, it may be negligible in general, but in a critical path
that difference may not be.

Unisys MCP/AS COBOL85 includes some, but not all, of these differences (it
allocates memory differently from its older brethren).

I don't think that many people would be interested in an object code
comparison between access by an index and access by a subscript (of whatever
USAGE), but while the "best case" for subscripting is not all that much
worse than the indexing case, trust me, the "ordinary case" and "worst case"
for subscripting are indeed CONSIDERABLY less efficient than indexing.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:b3djfr$1l49$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
> Unisys MCP/AS COBOL(68) and COBOL74 both unconditionally set indices to
the
> actual offset of the data item being indexed, not to the subscript value.
> For example
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Nah (assuming, of course, the implementor didn't do something really
dumb
> > like using display subscripts).
> >
> > If there's any doubt, a 5-line timing program would settle the matter
for
> > the suspect machine. Once it is verified that subscripts and indexes are
> > equivalent, one can purge indexing from the brain, thereby making room
for
> > other stuff.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> default, the range of the index is checked on reference *by the hardware*
to
> ensure that it does not go outside the bounds of the RECORD, but it is not
> so rigorous as to insist that it may not go outside the bounds of the
TABLE.
>
> Subscripts are stored wherever they're declared, in whatever format
they're
> declared, and always represent a table entry occurrence NUMBER in that
> format, so when used as a subscript the value must undergo a multiply and
an
> add to convert its value into an offset to the desired data item.  Though
> it's possible to get a subscript quite close in performance to an index by
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the multiply (of the stride) and the add (of the table's offset) might
add,
> unless the table offset is zero and the stride is one there is *some* more
> cost associated with the subscript.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> comparison between access by an index and access by a subscript (of
whatever
> USAGE), but while the "best case" for subscripting is not all that much
> worse than the indexing case, trust me, the "ordinary case" and "worst
case"
> for subscripting are indeed CONSIDERABLY less efficient than indexing.
>
>     -Chuck Stevens

I trust you, Chuck. And thanks for the exposition on the internals of how
things work.

Now, what was the timing result?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T16:20:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ecro$26i7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <b36frf$lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oKydndQWy-SfH8qjXTWcjA@giganews.com> <b3djfr$1l49$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <DGydnYLUZJ3cOcejXTWckw@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:DGydnYLUZJ3cOcejXTWckw@giganews.com...

> I trust you, Chuck. And thanks for the exposition on the internals of how
> things work.
>
> Now, what was the timing result?

With everything else made as much alike as possible (usage chosen to provide
the most efficient possible numeric description for the subscript, bounds
checking defeated for both index and subscript), I consume something over
40% more CPU using a subscript than I do using an index.  And that's a
best-case scenario for the subscript, as I see it.

Note that for best results the subscript needs to be a good old 77-level
item.

Change the level number from 77 to 01 and leave everything else the same,
and the subscript performance penalty can increase to about 115% (over
double the CPU consumption).

Put the subscript in a record, located something other than an even multiple
of six characters from the beginning of the record, and it increases to
150%.

Turn that record item into USAGE COMP or DISPLAY, and the subscript penalty
goes to nearly triple (196%).

And I haven't even added range checking yet!

Deleting INDEXED BY clauses and 77-level numeric items from all COBOL
programs (of either current dialect) at a Unisys MCP/AS site because those
syntactic constructs are outdated relics with no defensible reason for
continued existence would border on sabotage, pure and simple.

Know your machine.  That doesn't mean you have to make use of every nook and
cranny of every extension to standard COBOL.  But it does mean it's a good
idea to know which standard features of the language are geared for
performance and which are not, in the particular implementation you're
dealing with.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes
