- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZEidncEYorUxf8ejXTWcjg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <b36frf$lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oKydndQWy-SfH8qjXTWcjA@giganews.com> <b3djfr$1l49$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <DGydnYLUZJ3cOcejXTWckw@giganews.com> <b3ecro$26i7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
> >
> > Now, what was the timing result?
>
> With everything else made as much alike as possible (usage chosen to
provide
> the most efficient possible numeric description for the subscript, bounds
> checking defeated for both index and subscript), I consume something over
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Put the subscript in a record, located something other than an even
multiple
> of six characters from the beginning of the record, and it increases to
> 150%.
>
> Turn that record item into USAGE COMP or DISPLAY, and the subscript
penalty
> goes to nearly triple (196%).
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Know your machine.  That doesn't mean you have to make use of every nook
and
> cranny of every extension to standard COBOL.  But it does mean it's a good
> idea to know which standard features of the language are geared for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens

Chuck, Chuck, Chuck...

In your first paragraph you assert subscripts take 40% more time than
indexes. That's cool - I believe it. But I'm still after "40% of what."

If, for example, it's 10 seconds vs 14 seconds for 100 transactions, that's
fairly dramatic. If the difference is 10 seconds vs. 14 seconds for 1
billion iterations that's no difference at all.

In my view, coding for micro-efficiency is inefficient. First, optimizing
compilers are way smarter about that sort of thing and secondly, for
mission-critical applications (in telecommunications, for example, where the
transaction MUST be moved out of the way because more spam is flooding in),
C or assembly is probably the language of choice anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T09:05:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3g7ot$cl8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <b36frf$lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oKydndQWy-SfH8qjXTWcjA@giganews.com> <b3djfr$1l49$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <DGydnYLUZJ3cOcejXTWckw@giganews.com> <b3ecro$26i7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ZEidncEYorUxf8ejXTWcjg@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:ZEidncEYorUxf8ejXTWcjg@giganews.com...

> Chuck, Chuck, Chuck...
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> If, for example, it's 10 seconds vs 14 seconds for 100 transactions,
that's
> fairly dramatic. If the difference is 10 seconds vs. 14 seconds for 1
> billion iterations that's no difference at all.

The program I built has one hundred instantiations of the test statement in
a paragraph that's PERFORMed one million times.

The base test was "MOVE QUOTES TO SUBJECT.", in which SUBJECT is declared as
a thirteen-character item starting at the tenth byte (one-relative) of a
record.

For the base test, the result was 10.8 CP seconds.

Changing the test to MOVE QUOTES TO ANTECEDENT (AN-INDEX), where ANTECEDENT
is a table of 13-character items starting at the 22nd byte of a record,
occurring 100 times, indexed by AN-INDEX, raises that figure to 11.5 CP
seconds.

Changing the test to MOVE QUOTES TO ANTECEDENT (77-SUBSCRIPT), where
77-SUBSCRIPT is a 77-level item declared PICTURE S9(11) BINARY EXTENDED,
raises that figure to 16.3 CP seconds.

It is the comparison of the last two that led to the 40% conclusion.  If you
want to look at the subscripting/indexing in isolation, the "base line" test
includes just about all of the "administrative overhead.  Thus, while
indexing in this test took about 0.7 seconds for 100 million iterations, the
most economical form of subscripting took 5.5 seconds.

> If, for example, it's 10 seconds vs 14 seconds for 100 transactions,
that's
> fairly dramatic. If the difference is 10 seconds vs. 14 seconds for 1
> billion iterations that's no difference at all.

Which is why I indicated that it may be important in critical applications.
We have recently gone through a benchmark of the relative efficiency of code
generated by several compilers, and have come to the conclusion that for
large data-base-oriented applications the efficiency of the generated code
is *largely* immaterial, for the program spends precious little time in its
own code.  Nonetheless, there *are* cases where efficiency in particular
paths through particular programs becomes important.

>In my view, coding for micro-efficiency is inefficient.

Spending a lot of time coding for micro-efficiency for its own sake is
indeed inefficient.  Solving a mission-critical problem that happens to be
an efficiency issue is not necessarily inefficient, nor can it be said that
it is NEVER cost effective.   I would agree that it's much LESS necessary
than many programmers would have it be.

> First, optimizing compilers are way smarter about that sort of thing

Well, they can be, depending on the platform and the desired result.
Depends on what the particular optimizing compiler is optimizing -- could be
processor utilization, memory utilization, I/O efficiency, whatever.  I have
seen compilers that basically said "Well, I know what you *said*, but I've
concluded that what you really must have *meant* was ...", and I'm not all
that fond of that approach!   I'm for putting the keys to efficient
programming in the hands of the programmer.  Our COBOL74 compiler turns some
01-level elementary COMP and DISPLAY numeric items into 77-level USAGE
BINARY TRUNCATED items for efficiency (sometimes, if they're not
destinations and it's the third Tuesday in November and they have VALUE
clauses, into compile-time constants), but the rules are so convoluted even
the maintenance programmers on the compiler don't know them all off the top
of their collective heads.  Thus, making a seemingly unrelated change --
deleting a prior declaration in WORKING-STORAGE, or adding some particular
statement in the PROCEDURE DIVISION -- can undo this optimization without
notice.   The COBOL74 compiler (and the COBOL(68) compiler before it) has
been doing this sort of thing for decades, so it's not going to change, but
I'm honestly rather glad that the Unisys MCP/AS COBOL85 compiler is less
likely to second-guess the programmer in that respect!

> and secondly, for
> mission-critical applications (in telecommunications, for example, where
the
> transaction MUST be moved out of the way because more spam is flooding
in),
> C or assembly is probably the language of choice anyway.

No, not on the machines I work on, and thus not on all systems.

We do have a C compiler, but it is not wildly popular; it was used primarily
to "port" an application that happens to have been written in C.  We have
been unable to demonstrate a market demand for a C++ compiler on our
platform at all.  And we have never, EVER had an assembler on this system.
Everything is written in a higher-level language, including the operating
system.

Had you said "ALGOL", there are applications that are better suited to that
language, but I would still argue that in most instances for example a COBOL
MOVE or simple STRING is no less efficient than an ALGOL REPLACE, and the
choice of language on Unisys MCP/AS systems is driven more by the
*capabilities* of the language and the applicability of those capabilities
to the task at hand than by the *performance* of its generated code.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T10:14:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3gbq1$fb6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <b36frf$lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oKydndQWy-SfH8qjXTWcjA@giganews.com> <b3djfr$1l49$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <DGydnYLUZJ3cOcejXTWckw@giganews.com> <b3ecro$26i7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ZEidncEYorUxf8ejXTWcjg@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:ZEidncEYorUxf8ejXTWcjg@giganews.com...


> In your first paragraph you assert subscripts take 40% more time than
> indexes. That's cool - I believe it. But I'm still after "40% of what."

Another few statistics.

The original figure of 0.7 seconds for 100,000,000 references to an INDEX
was derived from a figure of 10.8 seconds for 100,000,000 MOVE statements
using a simple variable as a destination, and the comparison of that figure
to 11.5 seconds when the destination was indexed.  The base line, all the
overhead *except* for the actual cost of indexing/subscripting, is thus 10.8
seconds.

In increasing order of processor resource consumption:

Using an index without bounds checking on the reference:  0.7 seconds.
Using a 77-level BINARY item without bounds checking: 5.5 seconds.
Using an index with bounds checking on the reference:  10.6 seconds.
Using an 01-level BINARY item without bounds checking:  13.9 seconds.
Using a 77-level BINARY item with bounds ckecing:  14.6 seconds.
Using an 03-level BINARY (unaligned) item without bounds checking: 18.1
seconds.
Using an 01-level BINARY item with bounds checking: 22.3 seconds
Using an 03-level COMP item without bounds checking: 23.8 seconds.
Using an 03-level BINARY (unaligned) item with bounds checking: 25.4
seconds.
 Using an 03-level COMP item with bounds checking: 30.0 seconds.

Notice that bounds checking on an index costs nearly the same as a
13-character MOVE, and you can get nearly three such MOVEs for the price of
using a COMP item in a record as a subscript.   And once I factor out the
"baseline", it turns out that the least expensive subscripting is nearly
*eight times* the cost of indexing, taken in isolation.

Again, I agree, if your program's spending all its time waiting for
something-or-other (be it I/O complete or some response from the data base
system), it's not likely to matter, and barring any specific indication that
there is a performance problem with the program I wouldn't suggest a
programmer worry about it.  If, however, your machine is approaching 100% CP
utilization over significant periods of time and your company is about to
spend Quite A  Few Large upgrading it, it might be a good idea to figure out
what programs are soaking the processor and see if spending a few days
making performance improvements would help!

On every system, there are things that are efficient and things that are
not.  They differ from system to system.  On some systems there is no
efficiency difference between subscripts and indices.  On such systems,
perhaps the only real utility for INDEXED BY is in SEARCH.

Personally, I feel the fact that the standard allows the implementor to
define both the format and content of an index data item (see e.g. ISO/IEC
1989:2002 page 308, 13.16.36, OCCURS clause general rule 2, which is the
definition for "index"), is a specific and explicit invitation for
implementors to define them in ways that are most efficient on the
particular operating environment.  I would encourage people to use indices
where applicable on the grounds that the standard specification is such that
it is *very* difficult for subscripting to outperform indexing, all other
things being equal, and *very* easy for the implementor to have indexing
outperform subscripting.  I can't see an instance in which indexing is more
expensive than subscripting, I do see benefits for using indices, they're
still standard COBOL, and I see no signs whatever of them disappearing from
standard COBOL anytime soon.    The arguments I've heard against using them
do not make sense to me.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T12:31:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3gcq7$ilq$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <b36frf$lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oKydndQWy-SfH8qjXTWcjA@giganews.com> <b3djfr$1l49$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <DGydnYLUZJ3cOcejXTWckw@giganews.com> <b3ecro$26i7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ZEidncEYorUxf8ejXTWcjg@giganews.com> <b3gbq1$fb6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
I know that Chuck has made this clear in most (if not all) of his notes in
this thread - but I thought that I would "top post" an item that SOME
readers in CLC may have missed.  Chuck is talking about the Unisys compilers
that he is INTIMATELY familiar with.  As he has pointed out (frequently) any
programmer who is actually trying to "micro-tune" a program, needs to do
similar exploration on their operating system - with their specific
compilers and compiler options.

Just as an example,  IBM mainframe compilers (to the best of my knowledge)
will show NO difference in subscript performance between 77-level subscript,
01-level subscripts, and even non-01 level fields that are of the same usage
and on "full word boundaries".  This is just ONE example of where a code
difference on one platform might make (significant?) run-time performance
differences - but on another platform would show no change - or possibly
even a "reversed" performance change.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

```

On 25-Feb-2003, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> I can't see an instance in which indexing is more
> expensive than subscripting, I do see benefits for using indices, they're
> still standard COBOL, and I see no signs whatever of them disappearing from
> standard COBOL anytime soon.    The arguments I've heard against using them
> do not make sense to me.

The only way indices are more expensive than subscripts is because they are
marginally more difficult to understand and debug.  Since programming keeps
getting more expensive and CPU time keeps getting cheaper, the value in using
indices keeps dropping.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T20:29:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030225152921.26864.00002416@mb-mq.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ZEidncEYorUxf8ejXTWcjg@giganews.com>`

```
In article <ZEidncEYorUxf8ejXTWcjg@giganews.com>, "JerryMouse"
<nospam@bisusa.com> writes:

>In your first paragraph you assert subscripts take 40% more time than
>indexes. That's cool - I believe it. But I'm still after "40% of what."
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>C or assembly is probably the language of choice anyway.

One of the nice things about the Unisys compiler is that it has a compile
option $STATISTICS that builds in additional code for generating paragraph
and section usage/timing information.  The Sections consuming the top 90%
of processor utilization are reported separately for more detailed analysis.

This facility has been useful for demonstrating the differences in execution
time on a per iteration basis as well as a cumulative per run cycle basis.

I would imagine that Chuck's statistics are based on numbers from this type 
of facility.  His performance ratios appear to be consistent with what I found
when attempting to do performancing tuning of software in an IBM OS/390
environment.   Many of the Index vs subscript issues are mentioned in the
white papers on performance tunig for OS/390.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T13:36:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3gnkg$ni9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<ZEidncEYorUxf8ejXTWcjg@giganews.com> <20030225152921.26864.00002416@mb-mq.aol.com>`

```

"Sff5ky" <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
news:20030225152921.26864.00002416@mb-mq.aol.com...

> One of the nice things about the Unisys compiler is that it has a compile
> option $STATISTICS that builds in additional code for generating paragraph
> and section usage/timing information.  The Sections consuming the top 90%
> of processor utilization are reported separately for more detailed
analysis.
>
> This facility has been useful for demonstrating the differences in
execution
> time on a per iteration basis as well as a cumulative per run cycle basis.
>
> I would imagine that Chuck's statistics are based on numbers from this
type
> of facility.  His performance ratios appear to be consistent with what I
found
> when attempting to do performancing tuning of software in an IBM OS/390
> environment.   Many of the Index vs subscript issues are mentioned in the
> white papers on performance tunig for OS/390.

I agree that $STATISTICS is useful, and it's a good guess that I might have
used this mechanism.  But there are some good reasons I didnt *in this
instance*.  .

The first, and probably the most important, is that $STATISTICS itself
imposes some significant overhead.  It is VERY useful for comparing the
performance of various parts of a program, and even with comparing programs
with one another, but the figures it produces can be misleading when taken
as an absolute, because of that overhead.    The COBOL74 manual even points
out that this option should not be used in production code because of that
overhead.

Second, the granularity of the ACCUMPROCTIME attribute (as retrieved from
what in ALGOL is available directly from the TIME function with a parameter
of 12) makes it difficult to draw conclusions from fine differences in the
values taken during program execution. The reason that's the case is, as far
as I'm concerned, that incrementing the value of this particular register
for each of the active tasks on the system is not the MCP's highest
priority, and a corollary to that is that I have occasionally seen cases in
which two retrievals of this register in the same program obtained the same
value, even when the calls were separated by significantly more than the
2.4-microsecond "ticks" in which the value is presented.

Thus, I would be very comfortable drawing *relative* conclusions from
$STATISTICS output, but I would personally want to have my object code
rather more "stripped down" before I would derive the more absolute numbers
I was looking for here.

In this instance, I wrote programs that were designed to minimize overhead
and maximize the impact that I was trying to measure.  I have found from
experience that when I'm testing a verb I get pretty reliable and
reproducible absolute performance numbers if I write a program that has
nothing more than 100 instantiations of the verb in a paragraph, minimal
initialization of data items, and a single PERFORM that paragraph a large
number of times.  Unless the verb does VERY little, 100 instantiations
within the paragraph usually turns out to be enough to mask the overhead of
the initialization and the PERFORM.

The "PT" portion of CANDE's end-of-task output has all the information I
need, reflecting as it does the "final" (and accurate) value of the
ACCUMPROCTIME attribute for such a program, and requiring as it does a
program that runs long enough that a single fractional digit is sufficiently
granular without being excessively precise.

   -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-22T15:26:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VEM5a.15753$UF6.1700128@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com...
>
> "Ace11" <member25371@dbforums.com> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> The very best way is to not use indexes at all (unless absolutely
necessary
> as in a SEARCH) - use subscripts.

Not quite directly on point... but interesting and/or instructive
nonetheless....

When I first did some COBOL work for a new client a number of years ago, it
was because their staff COBOL programmer did not have the required expertise
in ANSI EDI.  (That's kind of a niche thing, so OK, fine, I can see bringing
in an outside resource, no problem). FWIW, IBM mainframe, all COBOL shop.

So I create the subsystem, which included some modifications to some pretty
vanilla COBOL programs. As clients are wont to do, when I said, "done" the
staff programmer checked over my source code to make sure it was
maintainable.

This programmer had been handling all the COBOL programming at this firm for
nine (9) years. Imagine my surprise when I was approached with, "This all
looks good, but one thing I do not understand: What does this statement do?
I've never seen SEARCH in a COBOL program before."

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-23T17:51:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3bmqn$1kobcv$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3tqf5vcdq89u1jr9j6dvnea11s5sk86378@4ax.com> <b399le$70u$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
So, did he get an answer????

If not, you can SET a working storage variable to an index.
IE:

SET WS-FIELD TO TBL-INDEX.

You can also use the SET to manipulate the index (to a degree).  Such as:

SET TBL-INDEX TO 1.
SET TBL-INDEX TO WS-FIELD.
SET TBL-INDEX UP BY 1.
SET TBL-INDEX UP BY WS-FIELD.

Well, you probably get the idea by now...

Dave

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T17:00:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>
>"Ace11" <member25371@dbforums.com> wrote in message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>     since index values cannot be manipulated directly ?

>The very best way is to not use indexes at all (unless absolutely necessary
>as in a SEARCH) - use subscripts.

I agree. The problem with indexes is that they are bounds-checked when you
modify them, not when you use them. Suppose you want to write your own search on
a table with 100 elements. You would write:

perform varying an-index from 1 by 1 until 
    antecedent (an-index) = subject  or an-index greater than 100
    continue
end-perform

That's ok. But if you roll your own like this:

set an-index to 1
perform until antecedent (an-index) = subject or an-index greater than 100
    set an-index up by 1
end-perform

The program abends when the index is SET to 101, even though you never reference
element 101. It doesn't matter whether the compiler option for bounds checking
is on or off. All compilers do it this way because the standard requires them
to. 

What poor design.

Moreover, indexes were invented to provide higher speed than subscripts, yet
bounds checking cannot be turned off. Running on a modern machine, including
mainframe, which can multiply in one clock cycle or less, it is quite plausible
that subscripts will be _faster_ than indexes because of bounds checking.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T09:28:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3dknl$1m6m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net...

> I agree. The problem with indexes is that they are bounds-checked when you
> modify them, not when you use them.

Not on all systems, and the standard does not specify when (or even whether)
index bounds are checked.

> The program abends when the index is SET to 101, even though you never
reference
> element 101.

Abend?  What's abend?  Another one of those universals that every
implementation has?

For me, this program gets P-DS'ed at the reference to antecedent with an
expression (the index) out of range.

> Moreover, indexes were invented to provide higher speed than subscripts,
yet
> bounds checking cannot be turned off.

It can't?  And here I've been laboring under the impression that $RESET
BOUNDS (INDEX) did that in COBOL85!  More to the point, I haven't been able
to find a way to get bounds checking on indices in COBOL74 turned ON on my
system.  Can you 'splain how to do that so I can figure out a way to turn it
off again if I want to?   ;-)

> Running on a modern machine, including mainframe, which can multiply in
one clock cycle or less, it is quite plausible
> that subscripts will be _faster_ than indexes because of bounds checking.

With a few more assumptions, maybe -- like, indices have bounds checking
unconditionally and subscripts don't, like indices and subscripts are both
stored in the same format, like the subscript's declared in the way that's
most convenient for execution-time efficiency rather than ease of debugging
or presentation, and so forth.   Not all those assumptions are valid.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T01:22:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5ab04a.13531717@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3dknl$1m6m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>index bounds are checked.

The Micro Focus manual, under SET, says:

"If index-name-1 is specified, the value of the index after the execution of the
SET statement must correspond to an occurrence number of an element in the table
associated with index-name-1. The value of the index associated with an
index-name after the execution of a PERFORM or SEARCH statement may be set to an
occurrence number that is outside the range of its associated table."

That looks like it is quoting the standard, but I cannot find the same language
in the '89 standard. Was it in the '85 standard?

I encountered the same manditory bounds checking on an earlier IBM mainframe
compiler. I find it hard to believe compiler vendors are making this stuff up.

>For me, this program gets P-DS'ed at the reference to antecedent with an
>expression (the index) out of range.

Mine gets P-DS'ed when I SET the index to 101, not when I reference entry 101,
which I never do.

>> Moreover, indexes were invented to provide higher speed than subscripts,
>yet
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>BOUNDS (INDEX) did that in COBOL85! 

Compiler switches are not standardized. 

>> Running on a modern machine, including mainframe, which can multiply in
>one clock cycle or less, it is quite plausible
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>or presentation, and so forth.   Not all those assumptions are valid.

In my experience, indexes are always stored at offset into the table, as you
described Unisys doing. If they were the same as subscripts, what's the point?

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T13:33:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302251333.369ee65@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3dknl$1m6m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5ab04a.13531717@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >> I agree. The problem with indexes is that they are bounds-checked when you
> >> modify them, not when you use them.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> associated with index-name-1.

This doesn't say that it will be bound checked and/or cause an abend
as you asserted.  It also does _not_ say that the index _won't_ be
bound checked when the table is accessed as you also asserted.

The implication of SET that results in a value that is not an
occurance is that it _may_ become undefined as to actual value.

> I encountered the same manditory bounds checking on an earlier IBM mainframe
> compiler. I find it hard to believe compiler vendors are making this stuff up.

So, as usual, based on a single instance decades or so ago, you
generalised to "all compilers" and always.

Nowhere does the standard impose any form of bound checking, it is
only you that is 'making this stuff up'.

> Compiler switches are not standardized. 

So, does that mean you will admit to being wrong about "all compilers"
and "bound checking cannot be turned off" ?

I thought not.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T10:32:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5c7bd6.1416357@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3dknl$1m6m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5ab04a.13531717@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251333.369ee65@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> "If index-name-1 is specified, the value of the index after the execution of
the
>> SET statement must correspond to an occurrence number of an element in the
table
>> associated with index-name-1.
>
>This doesn't say that it will be bound checked and/or cause an abend
>as you asserted. 

The word "must" means mandatory. 

>Nowhere does the standard impose any form of bound checking, it is
>only you that is 'making this stuff up'.

Two current IBM COBOL manualsl contain the same language:

"The receiving field can be specified by index-name-3.  This index-name
   value both before and after the SET statement execution must correspond to
   the occurrence numbers in an associated table.

The value for an index-name after execution of a SEARCH or PERFORM
   statement may be undefined; therefore, a Format 1 SET statement should
   reinitialize such index-names before other table-handling operations are
   attempted."

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/AM0C2000/4.30.2?DT=19930204110557
http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGYL1101/3.31.1?SHELF=&DT=19930312093006

Note that it says an index may be out of range (undefined) after a SEARCH or
PERFORM, but not after a SET. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

On 26-Feb-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Note that it says an index may be out of range (undefined) after a SEARCH or
> PERFORM, but not after a SET.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T17:30:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5cf4b9.32368165@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3dknl$1m6m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5ab04a.13531717@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251333.369ee65@posting.google.com> <3e5c7bd6.1416357@news.optonline.net> <b3ikrj$q74$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On 26-Feb-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>1)????

In the old days, doing so caused abend. We've seen how that changed in 1989.
Subtle changes in standard language moved bounds checking from SET to usage, so
apparently it's ok now. Unfortunately, current vendor manuals are still quoting
language from the '85 standard. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T10:50:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302261050.fbc6b2b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3dknl$1m6m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5ab04a.13531717@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251333.369ee65@posting.google.com> <3e5c7bd6.1416357@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >This doesn't say that it will be bound checked and/or cause an abend
> >as you asserted. 
> 
> The word "must" means mandatory. 

But doesn't specify any penalty, such as an abend.
 
> >Nowhere does the standard impose any form of bound checking, it is
> >only you that is 'making this stuff up'.
> 
> Two current IBM COBOL manualsl contain the same language:

But don't mention any requirement for bound checking.

> Note that it says an index may be out of range (undefined) after a SEARCH or
> PERFORM, but not after a SET. 

Which indicates that the only sensible place for bound checking would
be at the usage of the index in a table access.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T12:59:27-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3j2rd$bk$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3dknl$1m6m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5ab04a.13531717@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251333.369ee65@posting.google.com> <3e5c7bd6.1416357@news.optonline.net>`

```
But notice that it says NOTHING about what happens when the rule is
"violated" during the SET statement.  To find out about that, check out the
reference at:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/2.4.48

"If SSRANGE is in effect at compile time, the range-checking code is
generated. You can inhibit range checking by specifying CHECK(OFF) as a
run-time option. This leaves range-checking code dormant in the object code.
Optionally, the range-checking code can be used to aid in resolving any
unexpected errors without recompilation.

If an out-of-range condition is detected, an error message is displayed and
the program is terminated.

Remember: You will get range checking only if you compile your program with
the SSRANGE option and run it with the CHECK(ON) run-time option."

Once again, you have "jumped to a conclussion" that because something
SHOULDN"T be done - that it will lead to "bad results".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

```

On 26-Feb-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> But notice that it says NOTHING about what happens when the rule is
> "violated" during the SET statement.  To find out about that, check out the
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> SHOULDN"T be done - that it will lead to "bad results".

With subscripts, SSRANGE abends occur when the table is referenced, not when a
value is defined for the subscript.   Since indices can apply to more than one
table, I would assume that the same case would have to be true for indices as
well.

e.g.
move 0 to mysubscript
display 'so far so good'
display my-record(mysubscript).

We will get 'so far so good' displayed before the SSRANGE abend.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T13:07:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3jaab$2frg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3dknl$1m6m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5ab04a.13531717@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251333.369ee65@posting.google.com> <3e5c7bd6.1416357@news.optonline.net> <b3j2rd$bk$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <b3j89m$5g1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b3j89m$5g1$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> Since indices can apply to more than one table, ...

No, I don't think that's true, at least not for standard  COBOL.

ANSI X3.23-1985 page VI-27, 5.8, THE OCCURS CLAUSE, Syntax Rule 14:
"Index-name-1 must be a unique word within the program."

The '74 standard is worded similarly.

ISO/IEC 1989:2002 page 70, 8.3.1.1.1.11, User-defined words entry for
"Index-name" states "An index-name identifies an index associated with a
specific table."

A given index-name applies to one, and only one, table.  That's one reason
for index data items, to provide a mechanism allowing a value arrived at in
one index to be used for a different table (by setting the index-data-name
to the value of the first index-name, then setting the second index-name to
the value of the index-data-name).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> Thanks for the correction, but my point was it abends when you SET the index to
> 101 rather then when you use it. 

Can you list the systems that this _actually_ occurs on.  It certainly
doesn't on any that I care about.  Nor can I think of any reason why
it should.

Also note that the VARYING _will_ set the index to above its maximum
value and yet there will be no abend.

Is this just another myth that you decided on decades ago and have
never bothered to update ?

One reason why I think that you are quite wrong, yet again, is that an
index can be used on several tables of different sizes:

        01  T-index                       USAGE INDEX.
        01  Table-1.
            03  T1-Data                   OCCURS 100.
        01  Table-2.
            03  T2-Data                   OCCURS 200.
            ... 
            SET T-index      TO 150

Tell me how _any_ system can determine to abend when this line is
executed.

And also specify how the system will 'know' not to bother checking the
access to the table:

            MOVE T2-Data(T-index)    TO ...

The bound check can _only_ be on the access into the table.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T11:50:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ghes$j3f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com...

In Robert's defense here:
>
> Can you list the systems that this _actually_ occurs on.  It certainly
> doesn't on any that I care about.  Nor can I think of any reason why
> it should.
...

There are some confusing terms and even perhaps some conflicting definitions
here.

Most strictly stated, an "index" (as distinct from an item of "class index"
or "category index"!!) is the index-name referenced in the OCCURS ...
INDEXED BY index-name.  They are unique to the data declaration containing
them, and their value reflects the location of the specified item in an
implementor-defined way.  An "index data item" is a data item explicitly or
implicitly declared as USAGE INDEX (including index-valued functions).

What I thought we were talking about here is the former, not the latter.
Indeed, I don't see just a whole bunch of unique functionality associated
with USAGE INDEX data items offhand; when they are used to reference table
entries, they function as subscripts, not as indices.  SETting an index data
item to the value of a index converts the implementor-defined value in the
source to an occurrence number in the destination, but it does that even if
the destination's a plain old integer.  While I see a whole lot of value in
INDEXED BY, on the implementations I'm familiar with USAGE INDEX doesn't
offer anything over PIC S9(11) USAGE BINARY EXTENDED, save portability.

Unisys MCP/AS COBOL85 does not do a range check on SET index TO statements
(even with range checking enabled) for the very simple reason that the only
time the value of an index actually matters is when it's referenced.  Unisys
sees no particular reason to prevent you from doing something like
    SET INDEX-NAME TO 1.
    SET INDEX-NAME DOWN BY 47.
    SET INDEX-NAME UP BY <data item containing 47>.

The rule in COBOL85 (ANSI X3.23-1985 Page VI-128, 6.23.4 SET statement,
General Rule 2) that states that the value to which the index-name is SET
must fall within the range of the valid occurrence numbers for the
associated OCCURS clause is a "general rule" that does not specify a
penalty.  The results of violating such rules are undefined.

ISO/IEC 1989:2002, Pages 521, 14.8.35, SET statement, General Rule 2c
explicitly allows an index to be set outside the bounds of the table when
the destination is an index-name and the source is either a regular data
item or an index data item.  Arithmetic expression sources and SET UP/DOWN
BY statements have different rules in this regard, but the bottom line is
that the current standard explicitly allows a SET to cause an index-name to
be outside the bounds of the table.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Standard names (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T13:08:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3j3b2$93n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <b3ghes$j3f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5c8aca.5244651@news.optonline.net>`

```
(While getting slightly tired of teaching Robert Wagner to "think" before he
posts)

The portion of the name
   "ISO/IEC 1989:2002"

that has "1989" is *not* a reference to any year.  The reference to "2002"
is a reference to a year.  The 1989 is simply an arbitray number used in
describing ISO (not ANSI) Standards.   For example 10646 is the ISO Standard
for character set representations (similar to Unicode).  The part of the
ANSI numbering that corresponds to this part of the ISO number is "X3.23"

There was an ANSI (not ISO) amendment to the '85 Standard that has "1989" (a
year) in it.  That was

  "ANSI X3.23a-I989 -
   American National Standard
   for Information Systems -
  Programming Language -
  Intrinsic Function Module for COBOL"

Given that you (Robert) seem to have troubles referencing the "real"
Standards but do use the IBM mainframe manuals, you might want to check out
the "naming" section for ANSI, ISO, and FIPS Standards at:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3lr10/APPENDIX
1.7
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard names (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b3j3b2$93n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...

> that has "1989" is *not* a reference to any year.  The reference to "2002"
> is a reference to a year.  The 1989 is simply an arbitray number used in
> describing ISO (not ANSI) Standards.

Witness for a clearer example the ISO designations found on the upper right
hand corner of the covers of the '74 and '85 COBOL standards:  "ISO 1989-
1978" and "ISO 1989- 1985" respectively.  I ordinarily refer to them by
their ANSI designations (ANSI X3.23-1974 and ANSI X3.23-1985 respectively),
but the ISO 1989 designation's been around since considerably before 1989 C.
E.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard names (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T06:30:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5daafb.30914451@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <b3ghes$j3f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5c8aca.5244651@news.optonline.net> <b3j3b2$93n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
My comment on the1989 standard was based on reading the (slow-loading) document
posted at:

http://www.techstreet.com/direct/ISO+IEC+CD+1_8-1989.pdf

Looking more closely, I now see it is a draft of the 2002 standard, as you said.


In that case, all '85 compliant compilers are out of step with the '85 standard
with respect to bounds checking of indexes. The standard says they "must" be
checked when set (with no penalty for failing to do so), whereas the 2002
standard omits that language. 

In practice, all compiler compaies switched to checking 'on reference' some ten
years ago.

Robert


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>(While getting slightly tired of teaching Robert Wagner to "think" before he
>posts)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Yet again, Robert Wagner can't read or understand what he is told (was: Standard names (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T06:00:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3kuke$cad$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <b3ghes$j3f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5c8aca.5244651@news.optonline.net> <b3j3b2$93n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5daafb.30914451@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert,
  Are you simply illeterate?  There is no requirement for boundary checking
in the '85 Standard  compiler!!!

It doesn't matter how many times you claim there is - you are wrong.  You
have been told that you are wrong.  The text of the '85 Standard has been
quoted to you.  It has been explained in simple terms that saying that
something must not happen DOES NOT mean that a violation of the rule MUST
result in its detection.

The exact place that this is stated in the '85 Standard is in

 "1.7 RELATIONSHIP OF A CONFORMING PROGRAM TO A CONFORMING IMPLEMENTATION"

on page I-9

where it says,

"The translation of a conforming source program by a conforming
implementation and the subsequent execution of the resultant object program
is defined only to the extent specified in Standard COBOL."

When you find ANY place where it tells what WILL happen (i.e. be detected)
when a boundary violation occurs in the '85 Standard, then post it.  As this
doesn't exist, just SHUT UP about what you are wrong about.

People don't get "upset" with you when you make an "honest" mistake or state
your opinion.  When, however, you PERSIST it stating as "fact" what you have
already been shown you are wrong about, then that really does lower any
credibitility that you might still have.

  Your "*must*" below says that they must check for such a violation
(whether or not there is a penelty).  That is wrong - will continue to be
wrong - and qualifies as mis-representation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard names (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T09:53:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ljas$109r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <b3ghes$j3f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5c8aca.5244651@news.optonline.net> <b3j3b2$93n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5daafb.30914451@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e5daafb.30914451@news.optonline.net...
> In that case, all '85 compliant compilers are out of step with the '85
standard
> with respect to bounds checking of indexes.

No, they're not.  The standard says this is an execution-time rule and what
the particular implementor decides to do about it is up to the implementor.
The standard in fact can be interpreted to allow the burden of preventing
subscript and index range errors to fall squarely on the programmer's
shoulders, just as it does for overlapping PERFORMs as Bill Klein pointed
out.

> The standard says they "must" be checked when set

No, the standard says they must be in range at execution time once the set
statement is done.  That's not the same.

> (with no penalty for failing to do so),

No, the penalty for doing so is not defined by the standard but by the
implementor, as for other similar "must" specifications in GENERAL rules.

> whereas the 2002 standard omits that language.

Yes, my guess is because an interpretation made it clear that it was not
appropriate.  It introduces some arbitrary and unnecessary limitations on
user code -- like for example initialization of variables for a manual
SEARCH.  Note that the 2002 standard DOES introduce a formal exception
handling process, and bounds checking for subscripts and indices is covered
thereby.

> In practice, all compiler compaies switched to checking 'on reference'
some ten
> years ago.

They did?  Really?  Then why can I find no indications in the symbol files
or the patch histories that our COBOL68 and COBOL74 compilers have changed
in this regard since they were first written (thirty-plus, and twenty-plus,
years ago respectively)?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard names (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T04:38:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5EE6CD.E0917083@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <b3ghes$j3f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5c8aca.5244651@news.optonline.net> <b3j3b2$93n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5daafb.30914451@news.optonline.net> <b3ljas$109r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jtqcnThLHobySMOjXTWcpw@comcast.com>`

```


Russell Styles wrote:

>         In practice it does not really matter.  As long as this sort of code
> can
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> have portable code.

Question - how important is portable code, particularly as the majority here are
probably mainframers ?

- Installations - Howard writes code which contains useful 'Reusables', so he
*might* write it to exclude extensions. He's an employee so the installation
owns his code. Legally he is forbidden from taking an armful of source with him
when he heads for a new job in California.

- Contractors - like DD and I think Skippy - do they deliberately write their
code as 'portable' so that they can take it with them to another installation.
Again legally the contractor owns the code not the contractee.

- Mainframe Installations - how frequently do they swatch from one mainframe to
another. It's not like nipping into the supermarket for 6 cans of Green Giant.

- PC World - OK Thane has said he writes 'non extension' code, which makes it
portable between, I think, some 5 compilers. How many other PC contractors are
in the same boat. Donald are you totally with Fujitsu - or do you code for other
compilers. Same with Richard, do you use a mix of compilers.

If you *don't* switch between compilers why deliberately avoid using vendor
extensions which enhance their compilers, and which anyway, are subsequently
followed up with authorization by J4, albeit sometimes the syntax is slightly
reworded. If we stuck to the letter of the law about extensions - as yet there
would bo no code written in OO COBOL ! It has only now been 'sanctioned' as a
release in COBOL 2002.

- COBOL Libraries - we just don't have them, (bearing in mind the utitlities
available in Java, C++ etc., are one of their strengths - avoids re-inventing
the wheel). The majority of code is written for mainframes - and you immediately
come up against the legality issue. About the only way we could get useful
routines into a non-OO COBOL library is where the source is 'private' and the
third party offers something like a DLL - not being a mainframer, my guess is
that wont work in the real world.

Seems like a plug, but it's true - the only significant reusable libraries could
come from OO COBOL  - and to protect the authors we are back to DLLs.
Unfortunately that's got to be at least some two years down the road, (a) J4
must come up with Standard classes, and then (b) Some vendors offer compilers
compliant with the J4 Standard classes. And BEFORE (b) can happen, when Vendor X
refers to comp-5 it better mean the same thing to Vendor Y - otherwise third
party libraries are useless. Either that, or Vendors X and Y have to build in
some of the new data types.

Is the latter going to happen - I seriously doubt it. Bill, Donald  and Thane
put your 'memory caps' on - in a somewhat heated discussion a very 'senior'
official said he didn't think portability was a big issue. Of course not, ties
you neatly into one compiler !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard names (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Message-ID:** `<b3p8fh$cte$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <b3ljas$109r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jtqcnThLHobySMOjXTWcpw@comcast.com> <3E5EE6CD.E0917083@shaw.ca>`

In article <3E5EE6CD.E0917083@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

[snip]

>Question - how important is portable code, particularly as the majority here are
>probably mainframers ?

[snip]

>- Contractors - like DD and I think Skippy - do they deliberately write their
>code as 'portable' so that they can take it with them to another installation.
>Again legally the contractor owns the code not the contractee.

Actually... I try to write code as close to my client's standards as 
possible; if I'm doing something that I can't find an example for then I 
try to do it as close to 'textbook' as possible... after all, if I'm going 
to be wrong then I'll try to be wrong according to the textbook.

I don't recall ever taking an entire program that I coded for one client 
and installing it at another client's... minimal mods, major mods, any 
mods at all.  If I do something that piques my interest then I'll grab a 
copy of whatever-it-is and toss it into a .ZIP file... so next time I have 
to, say, create a copy of a Prod VSAM file with four alternate indices I 
know that I won't have to look up the syntax for MODEL again.

Speaking of cute ones... here's one that wasn't that I did today.  I'm 
doing some rather basic file-reformatting and had presented some results 
for the Project Manager's look-see... we had the following interchange, 
starting with him:

'Hey... you screwed up the output file's PROVIDER-CODE.'

'Odd... the mapping document showed it to be a direct move.'

'I dunno what the mapping document said... but look at the FileAid here, 
the field is 6/NUM and contains X'F0C3F5F4F5F7'.'

'Hmmmmm... 0C5457?  What's the source field for this... X(5)... let's look 
at the input... there, in the input file it holds C5457.'

'Oh yeah, I forgot... when I put together the mapping document I forget to 
say that this field needs to be translated, take a look at how this other 
program does it.'

... so I check This Other Program and I see something like...

01  FILLER.
    05  CHAR-NUM-TBL.
        10  FILLER PIC X(03) VALUE 'A01'.
        10  FILLER PIC X(03) VALUE 'B02.
        10  FILLER PIC X(03) VALUE 'C03'.
...
        10  FILLER PIC X(03) VALUE 'Z26'.
    05  CHAR-AND-NUM REDEFINES CHAR-NUM-TBL
        10  CHAR-AND-NUM-PAIR OCCURS 26 TIMES.
            15  A-CHAR PIC X.
            15  A-NUM  PIC 99.

... and then, of course, there is a separate paragraph (prefaced with an
appriopriate 4-digit number) that is PERFORMED UNTIL A-CHAR(WS-SUB1) IS
EQUAL TO PROV-CD-FIRST-CHAR (an appropriate WS field)... and then
A-NUM(WS-SUB1) is MOVED to another field so that the remaining 4 chars can
be appended thereunto.

(the fact that A-NUM(SUB1) and WS-SUB1 are equal did not seem to occur to 
the coder)

So... what did I do?  First I determined the program run-frequency and 
anticipated volume... nightly and 20K recs.  Since SEARCHes are frowned 
upon I then coded something like:

01  WS-MISC.
    05  THE-ALPHABET PIC X(26) VALUE 'ABCD...Z'.
    05  WS-CHAR REDEFINES THE-ALPHABET PIC X OCCURS 26.
    05  WS-SUB1 PIC 99 VALUE ZEROES.
    05  WS-PROV-CD-X PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.
...

*
***XLATE FIRST CHAR OF INPUT-PROV-CD TO NUMERIC
*
    MOVE ZEROES TO WS-SUB1.
    PERFORM UNTIL INPUT-PROV-CD(1:1) = WS-CHAR(WS-SUB1)
     OR WS-SUB1 > 26
        ADD 1 TO WS-SUB1
    END-PERFORM.
    IF WS-SUB1 > 26
        MOVE 99 TO WS-SUB1
    END-IF.
    STRING WS-SUB1, INPUT-PROV-CD(2:4)
     DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO WS-PROV-CD
    END-STRING.
    MOVE WS-PROV-CD TO OUTPUT-PROV-CD.

A fair number of 'rules' violated there, what with all those periods and a 
subscript of a DISPLAY numeric... but hey, somebody recently posted that 
folks here should Post More Code.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T10:32:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>
>Also note that the VARYING _will_ set the index to above its maximum
>value and yet there will be no abend.

The Micro Focus manual explicitly says PERFORM is permitted to go out of range
but SET is not. 

>Is this just another myth that you decided on decades ago and have
>never bothered to update ?

Yes. I stopped using indexes after discovering the annoying behavior of SET. 

>One reason why I think that you are quite wrong, yet again, is that an
>index can be used on several tables of different sizes:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>executed.

It will not be executed because it will not compile. Index-class variables can
only be set to another index. 

>And also specify how the system will 'know' not to bother checking the
>access to the table:
>
>            MOVE T2-Data(T-index)    TO ...

Nor can they be used to index a table. The index variable contains an offset,
not an ordinal. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T08:14:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net...
> >            SET T-index      TO 150
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> It will not be executed because it will not compile. Index-class variables
can
> only be set to another index.

REALLY?? I didn't know that!  Silly me!

ANSI X3.23-1974 indicates that the source for "SET index-name TO ..."  can
be an integer literal, an index data item, an index-name, or a numeric data
item (page III-11, 3.4 SET statement syntax rules 2 and 4).  ANSI X3.23-1985
says the same thing, as does ISO/IEC 1989:2002 in this respect.

> Nor can they be used to index a table.

Huh?  Index data items can be used to index tables, even when the table has
an index of its own!  An index data item contains an ordinal occurrence
number and is perfectly valid as a subscript as well as a container for
indices.

>The index variable contains an offset, not an ordinal.

If you've shifted gears to indices and away from index data items, some
implementations do it that way (and I would contend that's likely the most
efficient way to do it), but there's no requirement that they do so.  So
long as an index contains something that can be used to identify an element
of the table, in whatever format the implementor decides, and so long as the
implementor provides a mechanism to convert to and from ordinal occurrence
number to whatever form the implementor's decided on, then the
implementation meets the standard.  There is absolutely NO requirement that
an index contain an offset; I have run into implementations, in fact, in
which the contents of an index are indistinguishable from the corresponding
subscript.

    -Chuck Stevens
>
> Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T16:43:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3iqs6$sv5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

On 26-Feb-2003, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> >The index variable contains an offset, not an ordinal.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> subscript.

 I learn something every day.   Which compilers find the following test false?

MOVE INDEX-TABLE TO SUBSCRIPT-TABLE.
IF INDEX-RECORD (1) = SUBSCRIPT-RECORD(1)   DISPLAY 'TEST IS TRUE' END-IF.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T09:26:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3itco$26uv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3iqs6$sv5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I think you've misunderstood my point.  Given that INDEX-TABLE has INDEXED
BY AN-INDEX, I can reference INDEX-TABLE (AN-INDEX), but I can also
reference INDEX-TABLE (A-SUBSCRIPT).  There is no requirement in the
standard that the generated code for the first reference MUST BE different
from that for the second.  If the implementor decides to implement indices
as containing "the ordinal position of the specified field among the
components of the containing table" rather than "the offset of the specified
field from the beginning of the record containing the table, expressed in
units appropriate to the USAGE of the field", that's the implementor's
prerogative.  The amount of code necessary to convert from "index-type" to
"ordinal-position-type" and back again, via SET, is permitted by the
standard to be zero.

    -Chuck Stevens.


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b3iqs6$sv5$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 26-Feb-2003, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > implementations do it that way (and I would contend that's likely the
most
> > efficient way to do it), but there's no requirement that they do so.  So
> > long as an index contains something that can be used to identify an
element
> > of the table, in whatever format the implementor decides, and so long as
the
> > implementor provides a mechanism to convert to and from ordinal
occurrence
> > number to whatever form the implementor's decided on, then the
> > implementation meets the standard.  There is absolutely NO requirement
that
> > an index contain an offset; I have run into implementations, in fact, in
> > which the contents of an index are indistinguishable from the
corresponding
> > subscript.
>
>  I learn something every day.   Which compilers find the following test
false?
>
> MOVE INDEX-TABLE TO SUBSCRIPT-TABLE.
> IF INDEX-RECORD (1) = SUBSCRIPT-RECORD(1)   DISPLAY 'TEST IS TRUE' END-IF.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T12:54:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3j2g9$u3l$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Sorry to disagree with you on this one Chuck - but see (in the 2002
Standard - as it is easier to "search")  Page 361, SR9 which states,

"9) An index data item may be referenced explicitly only in a SEARCH or SET
statement, a relation condition, an intrinsic function argument, an inline
method invocation argument, the USING phrase of a procedure division header,
or the USING phrase of a CALL or INVOKE statement."

One may use an index data item to "store" the current value FROM an index -
but then one needs to "put it back" into the actual index (via SET) to use
it where a "subscript" could also be used.  At least this is true in a
STRICTLY ANSI/ISO conforming environment.

P.S.  It was this rule in the '85/'89 Standard that "disallowed" the use of
an index data-item in the FUNCTION LENGTH intrinsic function
```

environment.  Thanks for the correction.

SET

header,
> or the USING phrase of a CALL or INVOKE statement."

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T08:16:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CPj7a.72858$zF6.5118443@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3j2g9$u3l$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5d5fd5.11673524@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message news:3e5d5fd5.11673524@news.optonline.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> index data items?

For the same reasons that we need numeric data items, alphabetic data items
and packed decimal data items.  Proper definition of fields within a record.

One practical example of usage would be to capture the offset for a related
record within a hierarchical table, such as the offset for the associated
sub-assembly (parent) part of a component part within a Bill of Material table.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T22:38:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>subscript.

Here is a small test I compiled with Micro Focus which demonstrates everything I
said above is correct, to wit:

1.  Index data names can only be set to an index or another index data name. You
get a compile error when you try to set them to an integer or up by anything.
When an integer data name is set, no conversion takes place; it contains the
offset in the original table .. at least in the Micro Focus implemetation.

2.  When an index data name is used as an index, no conversion takes place. You
do not get an ordinal. If the element size in the referenced table is different
from the table whose index was saved, results are incorrect. If an index on the
second table is set to the index data name, no conversion takes place. Results
are the same as direct use. 

Note that the full-ANSI-compliance compiler option was set. 

Please try it with your compiler(s) and post the findings. 

Robert

      $SET SOURCEFORMAT"FREE"
      $SET ANS85
 identification division.
 program-id.                                 test19.
 author.                                     Robert Wagner.
*  Testing index data name.
 environment division.
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  unqualified-variables.
     05  index-data-name         index.
     05  table-1 value '123'.
         10  table-entry occurs 3 indexed index-name-1 pic x(01).
     05  table-2 value '1 2 3'.
         10  table-entry occurs 3 indexed index-name-2 pic x(02).
 procedure division.
 main.
*    The following two lines of code get compiler error:
*    Wrong combination of data-types
*    set index-data-name to 1
*    set index-data-name up by 1
*    Reason: index data names may only be set to indexes or
*      other index data names.

*    The following tests whether the index-data-name contains
*    an offset or an ordinal value
     set index-name-1 to 3
     set index-data-name to index-name-1
     display table-entry in table-2 (index-data-name)
     set index-name-2 to index-data-name
     display table-entry in table-2 (index-name-2)
*    finding: displays '2 ' -- third byte of the table
*    Reason: no conversion takes place when setting to or from
*      an index data name

     set index-name-2 to index-name-1
     display table-entry in table-2 (index-name-2)
*    finding: displays '3 ' -- conversion was done
     goback.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Micro Focus and "ansi compliance" (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T17:17:11-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3jhto$eqk$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net>`

```
As stated in another thread - but in case anyone other than Robert Wagner
doesn't understand what Micro Focus directives do and do NOT do.

The note that follows has the (incredibly) conflicting statements,

> Note that the full-ANSI-compliance compiler option was set.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>       $SET ANS85

First of all, the minute that you use free-form reference format, you are
AUTOMATICALLY outside ANSI'85 conformance.  (Micro Focus documentation makes
this clear).  Secondly, the ANS85 directive *only* determines which reserved
words are used (and unless you use the NOMF an other directives, it doesn't
even "limit" those.)

Now, let's read a little more about

  ANS85

compliance

according to the section " 6.5 The DIALECT Directive" at

http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/nx31sp1/nx31indx.htm

"In Workbench, files of directives (wb*.dir files) are supplied containing
sets of directives to make the COBOL accepted by the compiler match
particular dialects, and you can use the USE(filename) directive to specify
which one to use. This makes it easy to migrate programs originally written
for non-Micro Focus COBOL systems, especially for IBM mainframes.
In Net Express these files and the USE directive are replaced by the DIALECT
directive. In the DIALECT directive, you specify the dialect you want, and
the necessary directives are set for you.  ...

DIALECT(ANS85)
ANS85 NOCOBOLDIR COMS85 COPYLBR NODBCHECK NODBCS NODBSPACE NOFLAG FLAGCD"W"
FLAGSTD"H C2 D2 S2 R O" FOLDCALLNAME"UPPER" FOLDCOPYNAME"UPPER" INTLEVEL"2"
NOISO2000 NOMF NOMFCOMMENT NESTCALL NOOPTIONAL-FILE NORESEQ WARNING"3"
ZEROLENGTHFALSE"

Therefore, if you REALLY want (to use Robert's words)

"full-ANSI-compliance compiler option "

You either need to set ALL of those individual options or you should use
  Dialect(ANS85)

just like the documentation says.  (Actually, the "highest" priority
directive is the FLAGSTD option.  With it set correctly, then it will point
out any of the other problems - such as freeformat - that you might have)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus and "ansi compliance" (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T23:57:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5e95e7.91063006@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net> <b3jhto$eqk$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As you requested, I added FLAGSTD"H" (my 1999 Solaris Micro Focus doesn't have
DIALECT), deleted the free-format option, shifted everything right six card
columns, changed single quotes to double and goback to stop run. Having done
that, I got a compilation error on:

 display table-entry in table-2 (index-data-name)

The message said index data names cannot be used as subscripts. My post started
out, "It will not be executed because it will not compile. Index-class variables
can only be set to another index." It should have been clear from the context of
the discussion that "index-class variables" was a reference to index data names,
the thing I said wouldn't compile, rather than normal indexes.

After commenting out the offending line, compiling and running, results were the
same. The first display, formerly the second, incorrectly displayed "2 ",
demonstrating no ordinalization had occurred. 

Robert
