###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T16:09:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3jkv2$2mte$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net...

<<Here is a small test I compiled with Micro Focus which demonstrates
everything I said above is correct, to wit:>>

Well, I (and my compilers) have a few problems with your COBOL program and
its conformance to the standards.

One of these is the use of single quotes to identify literals (only allowed
in '02 COBOL).  That has recently been discussed at length in this forum.

Another is the use of "goback" (only allowed in '02 COBOL).  That too has
recently been discussed at length in this forum.

A third is the use of an index-data-name as a subscript, which, contrary to
what I said earlier, is expressly disallowed in standard COBOL (any
flavor!).  That too has recently been discussed at length in this forum.
See below.

Once these are fixed to bring your program into some semblance of standards
compliance:

> 1.  Index data names can only be set to an index or another index data
name.

That's true; it's also irrelevant.  I was talking about setting index-names
to a literal, to an index-data-name, or to a numeric data item.  All of
these are valid.

I think you have confused "indexing" and "index-names" with "index data
items".   "Index data items" are basically efficient containers for the
contents of "index-names".  That's about all they're used for.  It's quite
possible to do a great deal with indexing without ever declaring a single
index-data-name.

> 2.  When an index data name is used as an index, no conversion takes
place. You
> do not get an ordinal.

In standard COBOL (again, contrary to what I said before), you can't use an
index data item as an index.  You can use it as a container from which an
index can be retrieved, or into which one can be stored.  The only thing
index data items provide that I can think of that a numeric data item does
not is the possibility of very efficient access for such storage (and even
that's implementor dependent).

What I did here to circumvent the syntax error on the use of an
index-data-name as a subscript was to add a numeric data item and SET it to
the value of index-name-1, then use it as a subscript in the DISPLAY.

> If the element size in the referenced table is different
> from the table whose index was saved, results are incorrect.

No, not so.  The conversion (if any) on "set index-data-name to
index-name-1" from "implementor-defined" format to "ordinal instance format"
on the conversion.  Whatever the actual, physical contents of index-name-1
are is implementor-defined (my test using a raw program dump indicates ).

I took a program dump at EOT for my version of your COBOL85 program.  This
raw dump showed that index-name-1 *physically* contains the value 2, and
index-name-2 *physically* contains the value 4.  If, however, I DISPLAY
these two items, I get 3 on both, I SET a numeric-data-item or an index-name
to either one and I still get 3.  That's because the compiler knows how to
convert an index into an occurrence number and back again.

The program dump of an equivalent MCP/AS COBOL74 program at the same point
has the values 6 in index-name-1 and 11 in index-name-2.  Both would present
as 3 when converted from their internal formats, as for COBOL85.

And all four of these values -- 2, 4, 6 and 11 -- correctly point to the
third element in their respective tables, in their respective dialects -- in
context.

> Note that the full-ANSI-compliance compiler option was set.

I do note that.

I would comment that were it not for the possibility that the compilers I
work with might be guilty of ignoring breaches of standards compliance even
in the face of a request to eliminate even the most trivial such breaches, I
would note it with considerable amusement, but I dare not.

What is standards-compliant and what is not is NOT defined by what ANY
implementation says is standards-compliant; it is defined by what the
STANDARD says is standards-compliant.  Single quotes, GOBACK, and the use of
index-data-names as subscripts is NOT standards-compliant, whether Micro
Focus flags them as noncompliant (with a warning or a syntax error) or not.

>Please try it with your compiler(s) and post the findings.

Done.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T23:57:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5e9600.91088633@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net> <b3jkv2$2mte$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>its conformance to the standards.
<snip>

>A third is the use of an index-data-name as a subscript, which, contrary to
>what I said earlier, is expressly disallowed in standard COBOL (any
>flavor!).  That too has recently been discussed at length in this forum.
>See below.

Using index data name as subscript was the ISSUE IN QUESTION. You did it and I
replied, "It will not be executed because it will not compile. Index-class
variables can only be set to another index." Now, when I write a program to
demonstrate the truth of that assertion, I'm chastised for writing
non-conforming code. I wrote to show that the statement IS non-conforming. I'd
expected Micro Focus to flag it as an error, as most compilers do. It did only
after specifying the correct standard-checking option.

>Once these are fixed to bring your program into some semblance of standards
>compliance:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>these are valid.

You thought you were talking about both kinds of indexes when you quoted
language from the SET statement, which makes no distinction between the two
types. The difference is described under USAGE. The description of SET is
ambiguous and misleading, as you've learned.

>I think you have confused "indexing" and "index-names" with "index data
>items".   "Index data items" are basically efficient containers for the
>contents of "index-names".  That's about all they're used for.  It's quite
>possible to do a great deal with indexing without ever declaring a single
>index-data-name.

I have not confused anything, as evinced by the test program. 

>> 2.  When an index data name is used as an index, no conversion takes
>place. You
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>that's implementor dependent).

I wonder why index data names were invented. If one needs temporary storage for
an index, it is simpler and more symmetrical to declare another index on the
table.

>What I did here to circumvent the syntax error on the use of an
>index-data-name as a subscript was to add a numeric data item and SET it to
>the value of index-name-1, then use it as a subscript in the DISPLAY.

That doesn't demonstrate anything. I just ommented the statement out.

>> If the element size in the referenced table is different
>> from the table whose index was saved, results are incorrect.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>are is implementor-defined (my test using a raw program dump indicates ).

You neglected mentioning which number appeared on the second DISPLAY. If it was
"2 ", yes, it is so; if it was "3 ", you are correct. From reading your
description of internals, below, I know the answer is "2 ".

Whether or not the value is ordinalized (is that a word?) is important because
it makes the results correct or incorrect. If I wanted the language to
ordinalize, I'd use an integer rather than an index data name. 

>I took a program dump at EOT for my version of your COBOL85 program.  This
>raw dump showed that index-name-1 *physically* contains the value 2, and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>convert an index into an occurrence number and back again.

State of the variables at end of program is irrelevant because the third test
sets them correctly. However, your report of values shows the compiler is
internally using offsets. On the statement 'set index-name-2 to
index-data-name', there is no way for it to convert a 2, an offset into table-1,
into a 4, the corresponding offset into table-2, because it doesn't know where
the value in index-data-name came from. 

>The program dump of an equivalent MCP/AS COBOL74 program at the same point
>has the values 6 in index-name-1 and 11 in index-name-2.  Both would present
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>context.

As I said above, end of program values don't demonstrate anything useful. What
are the values after "set index-name-2 to index-data-name"? You can get them by
commenting out the third test. 

>What is standards-compliant and what is not is NOT defined by what ANY
>implementation says is standards-compliant; it is defined by what the
>STANDARD says is standards-compliant.  Single quotes, GOBACK, and the use of
>index-data-names as subscripts is NOT standards-compliant, whether Micro
>Focus flags them as noncompliant (with a warning or a syntax error) or not.

I like testing with real-world compilers for three reasons:

1. During debate, concrete evidence is more convincing to other readers than
quibbling over the meaning of standards language.

2. In the real world, we must live with compiler writers' interpretation of the
standard, whether correct or not.

3. It's faster. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message news:3e5e9600.91088633@news.optonline.net...
> >
> >In standard COBOL (again, contrary to what I said before), you can't use an
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >that's implementor dependent).

Using a numeric data item to store the numeric equivalency value
of an index data item's offset value requires 2 (unnecessary) conversion steps.  

> I wonder why index data names were invented. If one needs temporary storage for
> an index, it is simpler and more symmetrical to declare another index on the table.

There are at least 2 logical deficiencies in this statement.

1  Your classification of the storage - as temporary - is exclusive and erroneous.
2  Your hypothesis that a table index would fulfill any functional requirement 
     is unsupported and indeed unsupportable.

Declaring another index on the table may indeed be simpler, and more symmetrical (?).

There will however be numerous instances where it is not a solution,
as I explained with an example thereof in an earlier post.

.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T08:28:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3nrn7$d2v$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net> <b3jkv2$2mte$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5e9600.91088633@news.optonline.net> <qDy7a.3708$Uy4.295135@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3e5f4dfc.138195421@news.optonline.net>`

```
Two specific examples of where USAGE INDEX items *could* fulfill a purpose
that INDEXED BY items would not.  (BOTH of these *could* be met by
subscripts - but not by INDEXED BY items)

1) If one wants to store within a  single record of a file a "pointer" (not
the technical type of pointer) to a specific occurrence of a table.  The
"indexed by" data item would *not* retain its value in a WRITE then READ -
while a "stored" USAGE INDEX item would.

2)  In the CALL statement, when you pass a RECORD that includes a table with
OCCURS ... INDEXED BY - the value in the index is not "passed" (odd but
true).  While if you pass such a record ALONG with a USAGE INDEX item set to
the "proper" value, it does retain its value in the CALL.

   ***

Personally, I don't think I have "frequently" used USAGE INDEX items for
either of these purposes (but have used both on at least some occasions).
Also as I say, both of these can be "solved" by using a subscript to "store"
the value - but these are cases where using multiple INDEXED BY items
certainly would NOT work.
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302261959.1b7d6e05@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> Here is a small test I compiled with Micro Focus which demonstrates everything I
> said above is correct, to wit:

And this is, of course, deflection.

You completely failed to mention that when a table index item is set
to a literal outside the range there is _no_ bound check failure.

When the table is accessed using the table index item that has been
set outside the range by a literal there _is_ a bound check failure
and 'abend' (153 error).

So far from 'everything you said is correct', your 'all compilers will
abend on the SET and not on the access' is still quite wrong.

Try this in your demo program (for MF or Fujitsu): 

     display "set index now"
     set index-name-2       to 4
     display "use index now"
     display table-entry in table-2 (index-name-2)

and you will find that your assertion about what 'all compilers' do
was quite wrong.
```

     SET TP-MAX TO 1.

- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net> <b3l8t9$485$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3lkpv$fpr$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <b3lo08$e19$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b3lo08$e19$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 27-Feb-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > defined in WS?  The thing that you canNOT do in a conforming program is
to
> >
> >    Set  ???? To integer-literal
> >    Set  ???? up by 1
> >
> > where ?????" is defined as USAGE INDEX - not where it is in an INDEXED
BY
> > ???? phrase of an OCCURS clause.
>
> Ahhh, I figured that these were implicitly defined as USAGE INDEX.
>
> It's weird that there would be this distinction.

Not really. An item which is USAGE INDEX may be used on any table. Hence,
the amount added/subtracted on a SET  statement depends on the current table
to which that index is assigned. So, 'SET indexname TO' is meaningless
except in the context of a specific table.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T14:54:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3m502$1cee$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net> <b3l8t9$485$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3lkpv$fpr$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <b3lo08$e19$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <xkx7a.1867$rM4.334824@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:xkx7a.1867

> Not really. An item which is USAGE INDEX may be used on any table. Hence,
> the amount added/subtracted on a SET  statement depends on the current
table
> to which that index is assigned. So, 'SET indexname TO' is meaningless
> except in the context of a specific table.


That's why it's so important to distinguish an "index-name" (the name used
in an OCCURS ... INDEXED BY clause) and an "index data item" (an item
declared USAGE INDEX).    The way I see it:

"SET index-name TO ..." can have an integer numeric data item, an integer
literal, another index-name, or an index data item as a source field.

"SET index-data-item TO ..." can have an index-name or another
index-data-item as a source, but NOT a numeric data item or a numeric
literal.

"SET numeric-data-item TO ..." can have ONLY an index-name as a source
field.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T10:45:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302261045.53df7601@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >Is this just another myth that you decided on decades ago and have
> >never bothered to update ?
> 
> Yes. I stopped using indexes after discovering the annoying behavior of SET. 

So, you agree that you never bothered to update.
 
> >            SET T-index      TO 150

> It will not be executed because it will not compile. Index-class variables can
> only be set to another index. 

      SET {index-name}    TO {index-name}
          (identifier}       {identifier}
                             (integer}

> >            MOVE T2-Data(T-index)    TO ...
> 
> Nor can they be used to index a table. The index variable contains an offset,
> not an ordinal. 

I suggest that you read a basic introductory text on Cobol.
 
But regardless of your 'lack of recent knowledge' on the finer details
of the SET statement, you still need to answer your assertions about
when bound checking is actually done.

The actual issue that I raised is that bound checking and abending is
_NOT_ done  on the SET but _IS_ done on the usage of an invalid index.
```

Actually it _will_ compile on MF, with appropriate options, even the 2 
lines you had commented out, and will run correctly displaying 3 3 3.

Your claim was:

>> It will not be executed because it will not compile.

> You are correct. As Chuck Stevens helpfully pointed out, the 1989 standard
> moved the time of bounds checking from SET to reference. It was a subtle
> change in language that wasn't obvious.

No. There was no _change_ in bound checking.  The standard never required 
any bound checking, or specifically never required any penalty such as an 
abend.

- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Mon, 24 Feb 2003 17:00:48 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
wrote:
snip

>perform varying an-index from 1 by 1 until 
>    antecedent (an-index) = subject  or an-index greater than 100
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>What poor design.
Indeed.
Only a very inexperienced programmer would test the value of a indexed
variable before testing the index itself, regardless of whether you
use a subscript or an index, and regardless of whether you set it as
part of the perform, or within the performed code (inline in this
case).

Both examples will fail with the same error. (subscript error).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T10:08:22+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3e1m4$inh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <070l5vcekkbmcvqir73r9qmijmdp6ihem9@4ax.com>`

```

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

>perform varying an-index from 1 by 1 until
>    antecedent (an-index) = subject  or an-index greater than 100
>    continue
>end-perform

That is code that I would expect from a newbie rather than an experienced 
programmer, it shows poor style and bad defensive mechanisms.

It will fail if bound checking is on because the antecenent(an-index) will 
be tested first _before_ an-index.

   perform 
       varying an-index from 1 by 1 
       until an-index greater than 100
          or antecedent (an-index) = subject  

       continue
   end-perform

This will not fail because Cobol guarantees short-circuit conditional 
testing.  If an-index is > 100 then antecendent(an-index) will not be 
tested and no bound failure will occur.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T13:36:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3e391$201a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <070l5vcekkbmcvqir73r9qmijmdp6ihem9@4ax.com> <b3e1m4$inh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:b3e1m4$inh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...

> This will not fail because Cobol guarantees short-circuit conditional
> testing.  If an-index is > 100 then antecendent(an-index) will not be
> tested and no bound failure will occur.

Yes and no, or perhaps more appropriatly, No, then Yes.

ANSI X3.23-1974 and prior standards did not define the order of evaluation
of the components of a conditional expression, and did not define whether or
not components of that expression were evaluated once it was possible for
the truth value of the conditional expression to be determined.

ANSI X3.23-1985 (and subsequent standards) specifies the order of the
evaluation of the components (as left-to-right), and explicitly states that
evaluation ceases for a component or for the expression once its truth truth
value is determined (page VI-62, 6.3.4, Order of Evaluation of Conditions,
item 1).

The latter standard uses exactly this circumstance as a defense of this
"Substantive Change Potentially Affecting Existing Programs" (page XVII-58,
item 12, Justification, second paragraph):   "This change will allow a
program to safely test that a subscript is within range immediately before
using it as a subscript in the same statement; for example:  IF INDEX-A IS
LESS THAN 5 AND TABLE-A (INDEX-A) IS EQUAL TO 25.  "

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T15:58:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3e4ie$qip$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <070l5vcekkbmcvqir73r9qmijmdp6ihem9@4ax.com> <b3e1m4$inh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b3e391$201a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
and even the '85 Standard didn't finish this "debate".  See the following
*new* substantive change in the 2002 Standard, page 818

"25) EVALUATE statement, sequence of execution. The sequence of evaluation
of selection subjects and objects is now defined to be from left to right
and selection objects are evaluated as each WHEN phrase is processed.
When a WHEN phrase is selected, no more selection objects are evaluated.

Justification:
The previous COBOL standard did not define the sequence of evaluation of
selection subjects and selection objects in an EVALUATE statement. It was as
if they were evaluated all at once or in some undefined order. Also, the
rules indicated that all selection objects were evaluated even if an earlier
WHEN phrase was selected. The only place where the new rules would cause a
difference is when a RANDOM function is specified in one of the selection
objects that is no longer evaluated. In addition, some subscript bounds
conditions or other errors might not occur with the current specifications.

Because the new rules state that all selection subjects are evaluated at the
start of execution of the EVALUATE statement, some subscript bounds
conditions or other errors could occur during this evaluation that might not
have occurred before.

This change was made as a result of a request for an interpretation of the
previous COBOL standard. It is believed that few, if any, programs will be
affected by this change."
```

<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> enlightened us:

>and even the '85 Standard didn't finish this "debate".  See the following
>*new* substantive change in the 2002 Standard, page 818
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>affected by this change."

Well I don't know about standards, but on the IBM mainframe, every
time I've looked at the assembler code generated by Cobol in an "OR"
situation, i.e., IF this-condition is true or that-condition is true,
the this-condition is always checked before the that-condition and if
the this-condition is true, the that-condition is never checked.

Regards,

without your accordian."  - Donald Rumsfeld / US Secretary of Defense

Steve

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >That is code that I would expect from a newbie rather than an experienced 
> >programmer, it shows poor style and bad defensive mechanisms.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> You are correct. It was hastily written for this newsgroup. 

You have claimed to be an 'authority' on the practical use of Cobol. 
You shouldn't be making these fundemental and obvious mistakes, not
even when it is hasty code cut for demonstrating a point.  That is if
you really are such an 'expert'.

incompetently structured.

Also you are expecting us to believe that:

    perform 
        varying an-index from 1 by 1 
          until an-index greater than 100
             or antecedent (an-index) = subject  

          continue
     end-perform
     move antecedent (an-index)  to somewhere

will _not_ fail.

its usage, then the varying manages to by-pass the check to arrive at
101.

The subsequent move does not, according to you, check the index.

'demonstrations' on complete failure to know how it actually works.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T22:32:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o0pn5vcc98mmha3f4kp5npn4ntlf4jvc0j@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <070l5vcekkbmcvqir73r9qmijmdp6ihem9@4ax.com> <b3e1m4$inh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e5acb17.20393462@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Tue, 25 Feb 2003 03:48:36 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
wrote:

>Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>to Chuck Stevens.
Wrong as pointed out before.

The fact that your particular compiler may issue an error with the SET
instruction does not mean that COBOL itself requires that to happen or
that other compilers will work the same way.
Before doing such a statement you should be prepared to mention which
compilers you teste it on, with which options set, and if it something
that is mentioned in the standards (any or all of them) then you
should also mention which part it is on(paragraph, page, line or
whatever required to identify it).

So for your info.
with the following code as quoted by you
>set an-index to 1
>perform until antecedent (an-index) = subject or an-index greater than 100
>    set an-index up by 1
>end-perform

Fujitsu V7
With CHECK option disabled.
   Does not fail, but if source debuging you can see that an invalid
subscript is used in the perform evaluation bit (e.g. antecedent (101)
not on the the "set up by 1" instruction
With CHECK=CHECK=Yes 1,ALL 
   Fails with an error in the perform only, not on the "set up by 1"
instruction

Acucubol v 2.4.2
With no specific compiling option
   does not fail but once again the perform bit is referencing an
invalid subscript
With option -Za (Check array bounds at runtime)
   Fails with an error in the perform only, not on the "set up by 1"
instruction

RM/COBOL v7
Fail with a "COBOL data reference error 104" when it tries to
reference "antecedent (101)", not when the index is "set up by 1".

I could do the same validation in other compilers, but I don't feel
like doing it. Three is enough to show how wrong you are.



FF

Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

```

>On Tue, 25 Feb 2003 03:48:36 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>wrote:

…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>like doing it. Three is enough to show how wrong you are.

Compilers used to do the range check on SET. Evidently a subtle wording change
between the '85 and '89 standards, described in a related posting by Chuck
Stevens, moved the range check from SET to reference.

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> The program abends when the index is SET to 101, even though you never reference
> element 101. It doesn't matter whether the compiler option for bounds checking
> is on or off. 

> All compilers do it this way because the standard requires them to. 

Show me in the _standard_ ('85 or '2002) where it requiures this.

do and I will show you my list that _doesn't_'.

> What poor design.

When you have read the standard and discovered that you were clueless
about it then you can appologise to the standards committee.
 
> Moreover, indexes were invented to provide higher speed than subscripts, yet
> bounds checking cannot be turned off. 

On my systems bounds checking is entirely optional, show me where you
found this little gem of mis-information.

> Running on a modern machine, including
> mainframe, which can multiply in one clock cycle or less, it is quite plausible
> that subscripts will be _faster_ than indexes because of bounds checking.

Show me timings for any system where you have actually found this to
be true rather than just made it up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T10:32:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5c8f93.6470348@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251306.c60ed70@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
>> The program abends when the index is SET to 101, even though you never
reference
>> element 101. It doesn't matter whether the compiler option for bounds
checking
>> is on or off. 
>
>> All compilers do it this way because the standard requires them to. 
>
>Show me in the _standard_ ('85 or '2002) where it requires this.

I quoted Micro Focus and IBM manuals containing nearly identical language, which
was copied from the '85 standard. 

>When you have read the standard and discovered that you were clueless
>about it then you can appologise to the standards committee.

One of the committee members helpfully explained the change. 

>On my systems bounds checking is entirely optional, show me where you
>found this little gem of mis-information.

Some compilers did bounds checking at _compile_ time, where it could not be
turned off. For instance, 'set an-index to 101' would return a compilation
error.

I cannot demonstrate it because I no longer have access to such old compilers.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T08:31:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3iq4e$24q5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251306.c60ed70@posting.google.com> <3e5c8f93.6470348@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e5c8f93.6470348@news.optonline.net...

> Some compilers did bounds checking at _compile_ time, where it could not
be
> turned off. For instance, 'set an-index to 101' would return a compilation
> error.
>
> I cannot demonstrate it because I no longer have access to such old
compilers.

Hmmm.  I see a bit of a logical disconnect.

On one hand you decry the fuddy-duddy COBOL mainframe programmer who insists
on using constructs you describe as hopelessly archaic.

On the other hand, your definitions of "best practice" appears to be based
on your dim recollection of compilers that are no longer supported.

???

I can say that Unisys MCP/AS (ex-Burroughs Large System) COBOL (68) issues a
syntax WARNING if you attempt to SET an index using a literal that's outside
the range of the associated OCCURS clause, reserving the syntax ERROR for a
non-integer literal.  COBOL74 on that platform does the same.  Or perhaps
"best practice" in your Burroughs site was to set $WARNFATAL which would
turn the compile-time range check warning (along with all the other
miscellaneous warnings that so many programmers tend to ignore) into a
syntax error?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T00:00:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5d41d4.3990860@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251306.c60ed70@posting.google.com> <3e5c8f93.6470348@news.optonline.net> <b3iq4e$24q5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3e5c8f93.6470348@news.optonline.net...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>syntax error?

My personal best practice is 'no warnings allowed'. I dimly recall that I
wrestled with the problem on one of several Burroughs Medium Systems, so it
might well have been a warning rather than a fatal.

When a language feature proves to be flaky or unpredictable -- i.e. depends on
compiler options the applications programmer cannot change due to lack of
authority or knowledge, or works differently between compilers, or often doesn't
work at all -- I put that on my mental list of Features To Avoid. Recently
discussed examples are figurative constant QUOTE, optional files (another thread
in this newsgroup), and alphabetic/numeric class testing. 'Once burned, twice
shy.'

COBOL is called legacy programming. A part of the legacy it must live with is
inconsistency in the Good Old Days. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T16:17:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3jlf4$2n9m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251306.c60ed70@posting.google.com> <3e5c8f93.6470348@news.optonline.net> <b3iq4e$24q5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5d41d4.3990860@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e5d41d4.3990860@news.optonline.net...

> COBOL is called legacy programming. A part of the legacy it must live with
is
> inconsistency in the Good Old Days.

Not if the inconsistency no longer exists in any current supported
implementation.  What Medium Systems COBOLL (as distinct from COBOLV,
LCOBOL, B7COBL. L7COBL and COBL74, probably among other compilers that ran
on the B2500 and its successors) did -- correctly or incorrectly -- makes as
much sense to me as eschewing indexed files in COBOL because there were
frequent reliability problems with a Data Cell Drive if you put them there.
What Burroughs or IBM or Micro Focus did twenty years ago to put your head
into a dither over one construct or another is irrelevant if you stick to
COBOL constructs and usages the way they are defined in the standard, and
understand what is standards-compliant COBOL not by what some vendor says is
standards compliant but by reference to the standard itself!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-22T05:09:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iCD5a.45597$rq4.3736504@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com>`

```

Ace11 <member25371@dbforums.com> wrote in message news:2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com...
> 
> Hi cobol gurus there,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     since index values cannot be manipulated directly ?

Here's an example of saving and restoring  

In W-S

Table definition

01  TBL01-BOM10BLD                                   VALUE SPACES.
      05  TBL-BOM10BLD                                 OCCURS 50 TIMES
                                                                          INDEXED BY TBL1 TBL2 TBL3
                                                                          PIC X(430).
01  TBL02-BOM10BLD                                   PIC X(21500)        VALUE SPACES.
01  TBL03-BOM10BLD                                   PIC X(21500)        VALUE SPACES.
01  TBL04-BOM10BLD                                   PIC X(21500)        VALUE SPACES.
Etc, etc, etc

Index data name definitons

01  L01X                                                           USAGE INDEX.
01  L02X                                                           USAGE INDEX.
01  L03X                                                           USAGE INDEX. 

In the Procedure Division

== To set an index data name to a current offset value

      SET     L02X                    TO  TBL2.                    

== To reset an index to a previously saved offset value 

      SET     TBL2                    TO  L02X.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-22T06:25:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E571840.D02928A2@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <iCD5a.45597$rq4.3736504@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Hugh Candlin wrote:
> 
> Ace11 <member25371@dbforums.com> wrote in message news:2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com...
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>       SET     TBL2                    TO  L02X.

In IBM mainframe compilers since at least VS COBOL II, the
working-storage items L01X, L02X, and L03X can be any numeric integer
format, that is, USAGE DISPLAY, BINARY, PACKED-DECIMAL, COMP, or
COMP-3.  The occurrence value of the index will be stored, not the
address displacement value.

I'm not sure if this is standard COBOL, or an IBM extension.
```

## Thread: Periods - just my personal opinion — 31 this month / 79 total

### Periods - just my personal opinion

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T14:49:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Just because it might have gotten lost in all the MIS-information that has
been posted about periods, I did think that I might restate something that I
have posted before.

In my PERSONAL opinion, a style that uses a single period (best if on a line
by itself) per paragraph *is* the easiest style to maintain in an error-free
manner.  I see nothing "religious" about this - and it is best implemented
in NEW development or a "major" rewrite - not in the normal maintenance of a
program that currently has lots of periods within paragraphs (or sections).

The reasons that I would give for my preference are:

1) A period is an EASY part of code to "miss".  Sometimes insertions move
them past the R-margin and programmers don't see them.  Sometimes, they are
just hard to see on the screen or printed compiler listing. (Note: I am
visually impaired, so this is a real concern for me.)

2) A period in the middle of a paragraph (either inserted or deleted by
maintenance) *may* change logic flow in any paragraph with a NEXT SENTENCE -
and I believe in the use of the NEXT SENTENCE phrase  - except in compilers
that already support (as the 2002 Standard does) the EXIT
PERFORM/PARAGRAPH/SECTION features.  If you have a compiler with this
feature (and don't think you will EVER port your source code to one without
it) then I personally do NOT like NEXT SENTENCE as all its "valid"
functionality can be achieved by other and "more commonly understood"
features.

3) Avoiding paragraph internal periods, DOES encourage the programmer to use
scope terminators.  Again, I personally find this to be a "good" thing - as
it encourages the programmer to design the most "bullet-proof" code by
making him/her think about all the  conditional phrases available to various
statements.  It also allows for "easy" insertion of conditional phrases and
nested statements - during the maintenance of existing code. Again, in an
environment where the new EXIT facilities are available - and even the 2002
Standard feature of being able to "reverse" NOT and "positive" conditional
phrases, this MAY allow a programmer to both "optimize" their code by
placing the most common conditions FIRST as well as avoiding repeated tests
of "skip-the-rest-of this-paragraph's-logic" switches.

   ***

There have been reports (usually contradicted - so I don't know the truth)
about vendor products that insert periods within the Procedure division via
their "pre-compilers".   Obviously, in such an environment, it becomes
difficult if not impossible to get rid of "internal" periods.  On the other
hand, I did want to get back to the question of why (at least one person)
does RECOMMEND *not* require - that periods not be used except at the end of
a paragraph.
```

#### ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T21:18:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3653c$2ao$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 21-Feb-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> 1) A period is an EASY part of code to "miss".  Sometimes insertions move
> them past the R-margin and programmers don't see them.  Sometimes, they are
> just hard to see on the screen or printed compiler listing. (Note: I am
> visually impaired, so this is a real concern for me.)

That seems to be a valid concern to me.   But if you're doing maintenance and
don't expect a period, you are more likely to miss one than if you expect to see
a lot of periods.

> 2) A period in the middle of a paragraph (either inserted or deleted by
> maintenance) *may* change logic flow in any paragraph with a NEXT SENTENCE -
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> features.

More accurately - you (or the maintenance programmer who will be fixing the
program some midnight) don't always know what will be inserted.  You do know
that if the inserted code is followed by a full stop period - that nothing will
trickle out into your logic.

I agree that shop standards should be to disallow NEXT SENTENCE, and even
recommend that when doing maintenance that consideration should be given to
eliminating existing NEXT SENTENCE clauses.  I think this is good even when we
do not have EXIT facilities available.

> 3) Avoiding paragraph internal periods, DOES encourage the programmer to use
> scope terminators.  Again, I personally find this to be a "good" thing - as
> it encourages the programmer to design the most "bullet-proof" code by
> making him/her think about all the  conditional phrases available to various
> statements.

Here, the better solution is the more direct one.  Instead of having a style
standard designed to encourage terminators - make using scope terminators your
style.

> It also allows for "easy" insertion of conditional phrases and
> nested statements - during the maintenance of existing code.

But how much work is it to remove periods?    I want to see all my inserted code
on a screen, so there aren't many periods to remove.  If my inserted code is 
more complicated, create a new paragraph that gets performed.   In that case,
the inserted code is PERFORM NEW-PARAGRAPH   (now I want to end my sentence).

> Again, in an
> environment where the new EXIT facilities are available - and even the 2002
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> of "skip-the-rest-of this-paragraph's-logic" switches.

It's not obvious to me what this has to do with the use of periods.  (at least
not the way I code)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T19:20:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302211920.785f4471@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3653c$2ao$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> That seems to be a valid concern to me.   But if you're doing maintenance and
> don't expect a period, you are more likely to miss one than if you expect to see
> a lot of periods.

That sounds like an argument that goes:

   You are more likely to notice that one tree is missing from a
forest than if one tree is missing from an otherwise empty field.
```

#### ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

- **From:** "Calin @ Work" <dontemailme@work.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-21T18:12:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Aky5a.6240$hv3.580766@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I tend to agree with you on this one, although I think it's a matter of
personal preference (see all the flaming posts :-)).

As a COBOL shop with more than one programmer, we try to establish and then
adhere to standards that in time become the [substitute for] personal
preferences.

Maybe it's because of those standards, but, over time, I learned to like the
"less periods" approach.  The scope terminators make everything so much
clearer, I would say.  Again, personal preference, I assume.

Thanks for the calm input, I think we needed this!

Calin, TORONTO

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>...
> Just because it might have gotten lost in all the MIS-information that has
> been posted about periods, I did think that I might restate something that I
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I agree.

> 2) A period in the middle of a paragraph (either inserted or deleted by
> maintenance) *may* change logic flow in any paragraph with a NEXT SENTENCE -
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I agree in that I discontinued using NEXT SENTENCE when CONTINUE
became available.  I never used NEXT SENTENCE much anyway.  However
the use of NEXT SENTENCE depends upon the disciplined use of periods.

> 3) Avoiding paragraph internal periods, DOES encourage the programmer to use
> scope terminators.  Again, I personally find this to be a "good" thing - as
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I agree that the use of scope terminators should be encouraged, I use
them in conjunction with periods and indentation to make it easier for
the compiler and the coder to pick up missing terminators.

>    ***

Portion about pre-compilers snipped, because I don't know.

While I personally prefer to use periods at the end of statements that
are not part of a compound statement, I think that with the
introduction of the 2002 standard, it would be possible for separator
periods to precede procedure names on the same line, which would make
the convention of only using periods to terminate paragraphs rather
less cumbersome, compared with having to put the period on the
previous line all by itself.

E.g.  I have often seen constructs such as 

proc-name.

If  condition
    ....
else
    ....
end-if
.
next-procname.

What I think can now be done is

proc-name.

If  condition
    ....
else
    ....
end-if

. next-procname.

Also as a matter of curiosity and perhaps as a requirement for the
above, I think that procedure names no longer have to be the first
entity on a free-format line, does anyone know different?  Not that I
would want to do that, apart from the possible use of a preceding
separator period as shown above.

Robert
```

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-02-22T11:46:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b389gu$ek6$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

Hmmmmm... let me add a bit of fuel to all this.

>Just because it might have gotten lost in all the MIS-information that has
>been posted about periods, I did think that I might restate something that I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>manner.

[snip]

>The reasons that I would give for my preference are:
>
>1) A period is an EASY part of code to "miss".

[snip]

>2) A period in the middle of a paragraph (either inserted or deleted by
>maintenance) *may* change logic flow in any paragraph with a NEXT SENTENCE -
>and I believe in the use of the NEXT SENTENCE phrase  - except in compilers
>that already support (as the 2002 Standard does) the EXIT
>PERFORM/PARAGRAPH/SECTION features.

[snip]

>3) Avoiding paragraph internal periods, DOES encourage the programmer to use
>scope terminators.

[snip]

My apologies in advance, Mr Klein, if my editing has changed the nature or 
intention of your arguments... but I did it all for the sake of the 
bandwidth, rest assured.

How can I respond without looking like Ye Olde Farte... well, I guess I 
can't so here goes:

(the following, for the sake of convenience, assumes that 'thoughts' can 
be combined to form 'ideas' and that 'ideas' can be combined to form 
'concepts')

I consider the use of periods in COBOL to be analagous to the use of
periods in English, in that they should be used to define complete
sentences which do their best to relay a single, complete 'idea' even 
though this 'idea' might be composed of many simpler 'thoughts' ; much
as a SYNCPOINT in CICS or a COMMIT in DB2 should be implemented after each 
'logical unit of work' (as difficult as such a thing might be to define) 
so should a period be used in COBOL to signify, likewise, a logical 
completion.

Now... notice that in the preceding paragraph I used one period.  (notice 
that in the preceding sentence describing the preceding paragraph I used 
one period; I wonder if someone who responds to postings line-by-line will 
miss this and respond 'See... you used only one period per paragraph, nyah 
nyah nyah!')  In that what we deal with here is a language - albeit a 
computer one - there will be, obviously, differences in theories about 
what constitutes appropriate constructions to best suit both the 
limitations of the language and the purposes to which it will be applied.  
Consider English as an analogue (not *too* inappropriate as COBOL was 
designed to be 'English-like'); the English of a loan-contract is not the 
English of a VCR instruction manual is not the English of a 
romance-novel... and yet the goal of each, it can be argued, is the same 
in that each can be said to be intended to transmit ideas and concepts.

The use of periods in an address-label-printing program and a online 
inquiry program for long-term insurance disability claims might just 
possibly not be the same... but the goals of the use - to direct program 
flow and increase readability/maintainability - is the same.

It seems that this first goal of 'directing program flow' is that is being 
argued here.  If this is the case then the argument is a variant of 'GO TO 
or no-GO TO' and it seems that it will be as immediately and readily 
resolved.  It might be a sign of my education only but I was taught simple 
things... every paragraph has an -EXIT paragraph associated with it, all 
PERFORMs are done THRU this -EXIT, a GO TO is allowed only if it directs 
flow to the -EXIT or the paragraph-label above or the -ABEND paragraph and 
that a NEXT SENTENCE transfers execution of the program to the statement 
which follows.  These rules were not taught as an end-all and be-all of 
style, they were taught because most coders were thought to be able to 
implement and understand them... and 75% of your shop's budget goes for 
maintenance.  Development, of course, should be done in the 'shop's style' 
so that all code has a similar 'smell'... *not* 'identical', 'similar'; 
this allows for the introduction of different styles and techniques... 
little things, like scope-delimiters and EVALUATEs.

There... I have now voiced the opinions of All Reasonable Folks and now 
everyone can consider this debate concluded... oh, and I am the King of 
England, as well.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

- **From:** "anon" <no@no.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-22T23:41:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This might be off-topic or irrelevant... but I regularly code in at least 4
languages (COBOL, Java, Delphi, VB).  I've also been looking at C# lately.

If you don't end each complete statement with an explicit terminator, you're
bucking the norm of every other language out there.  It might not really be
a big deal to someone who never touches anything but COBOL.  But it would
annoy the heck out of me to spend a day working in Delphi, and then jump
into some COBOL that had "implied" terminators.

I also agree with the guy who mentioned that he didn't like adding new code
unless he knew it was at a spot where the logic was explicitily terminated.
I guess in a new project you could compartmentalize the code more (as in OO
coding), so back-tracing logic might not be an issue.

I agree that periods are sometimes hard to see.  And I think that is why
you'll see that most current languages use something more visible... ie: a
semicolon.  A missing period in a book, at worst is going to cause a run-on
sentence.  In a program it could cause critical failure.  I know COBOL is
supposed to be "english" like, but the period choice for a terminator was
just short-sighted IMO.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...
> Just because it might have gotten lost in all the MIS-information that has
> been posted about periods, I did think that I might restate something that
I
> have posted before.
>
> In my PERSONAL opinion, a style that uses a single period (best if on a
line
> by itself) per paragraph *is* the easiest style to maintain in an
error-free
> manner.  I see nothing "religious" about this - and it is best implemented
> in NEW development or a "major" rewrite - not in the normal maintenance of
a
> program that currently has lots of periods within paragraphs (or
sections).
>
> The reasons that I would give for my preference are:
>
> 1) A period is an EASY part of code to "miss".  Sometimes insertions move
> them past the R-margin and programmers don't see them.  Sometimes, they
are
> just hard to see on the screen or printed compiler listing. (Note: I am
> visually impaired, so this is a real concern for me.)
>
> 2) A period in the middle of a paragraph (either inserted or deleted by
> maintenance) *may* change logic flow in any paragraph with a NEXT
SENTENCE -
> and I believe in the use of the NEXT SENTENCE phrase  - except in
compilers
> that already support (as the 2002 Standard does) the EXIT
> PERFORM/PARAGRAPH/SECTION features.  If you have a compiler with this
> feature (and don't think you will EVER port your source code to one
without
> it) then I personally do NOT like NEXT SENTENCE as all its "valid"
> functionality can be achieved by other and "more commonly understood"
> features.
>
> 3) Avoiding paragraph internal periods, DOES encourage the programmer to
use
> scope terminators.  Again, I personally find this to be a "good" thing -
as
> it encourages the programmer to design the most "bullet-proof" code by
> making him/her think about all the  conditional phrases available to
various
> statements.  It also allows for "easy" insertion of conditional phrases
and
> nested statements - during the maintenance of existing code. Again, in an
> environment where the new EXIT facilities are available - and even the
2002
> Standard feature of being able to "reverse" NOT and "positive" conditional
> phrases, this MAY allow a programmer to both "optimize" their code by
> placing the most common conditions FIRST as well as avoiding repeated
tests
> of "skip-the-rest-of this-paragraph's-logic" switches.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> about vendor products that insert periods within the Procedure division
via
> their "pre-compilers".   Obviously, in such an environment, it becomes
> difficult if not impossible to get rid of "internal" periods.  On the
other
> hand, I did want to get back to the question of why (at least one person)
> does RECOMMEND *not* require - that periods not be used except at the end
of
> a paragraph.
> --
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-22T21:58:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
"anon" <no@no.com> wrote 

> I agree that periods are sometimes hard to see.  And I think that is why
> you'll see that most current languages use something more visible... ie: a
> semicolon.  

The good news is that you _can_ terminate each statement with a
semi-colon (;) if you want to, as long as you terminate each paragraph
with a full stop as well (and use scope terminators or full-stops
where required for the logic).

But my editor makes the full-stop very visible by making it have a red
background.
```

> you'll see that most current languages use something more visible... ie: a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> just short-sighted IMO.

Being first has its disadvantages.  Sentences end with periods.

Programmers found it hard to tell a period from a comma - so when it was time
for PL/1, they made the terminator easier to see, by changing to a semi-colon.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T08:37:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message news:<b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
> news:b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens

IMNSHO it isn't the visual appearance of the period, comma, or
semicolon, though that is a problem.  The core problem with the
full-stop is that it terminates everything, there is not a one for one
relationship with statements and statement termination when using
Cobol* periods like there are with other languages using their
specific terminators.

This unusual behavior requires source file position dependance and
encourages programmer laziness.  The visual aspect of the terminator
is at least third on the list of bad things about full stops.

Regards,

Joe Zitzelberger



* Valid variant according to the Merriam-Webster and American Heritage
dictionaries.  Like many other pronounceable acronyms, over time it is
converted to a proper name.  See also scuba, an acronym of similar age
turned word because of its easy pronunciation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T17:12:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com>`

```

On 25-Feb-2003, jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

> IMNSHO it isn't the visual appearance of the period, comma, or
> semicolon, though that is a problem.  The core problem with the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> specific terminators.

Occasionally there is an advantage in knowing that everything is terminated.   I
see this with the use of a pre-compiler that includes IF logic without including
END IFs.

But we have other commands that terminate everything - such as STOP RUN.

Could you illustrate a situation in a program using terminator commands (END
IF), where a full stop period could cause problems?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T06:39:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message news:<b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...

> But we have other commands that terminate everything - such as STOP RUN.

Stop run harks back to the days when the machine went into a halt
state until someone loaded a new deck and pushed the big red button. 
Those days are long gone.

Unless you have written a boot loader in Cobol and are running on bare
metal without an operating system then somebody called that program,
and that somebody ought to receive control back.  That is the purpose
of GOBACK.



> Could you illustrate a situation in a program using terminator commands (END
> IF), where a full stop period could cause problems?

The simpleist statement I can think of is:

	ADD 1 TO X.         <---- Note terminal period

I can't wrap that statement in anything because of the period.  I
might find a need to add something like:

	IF X is numeric
            ADD 1 TO X.
        ELSE 
            MOVE 0 TO X
	END-IF

but I can't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T12:26:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com>`

```
jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote

> Stop run harks back to the days when the machine went into a halt
> state until someone loaded a new deck and pushed the big red button. 
> Those days are long gone.

That may be an association that you make.
 
> Unless you have written a boot loader in Cobol and are running on bare
> metal without an operating system then somebody called that program,
> and that somebody ought to receive control back.  That is the purpose
> of GOBACK.

Actually, since the late 60s, I have been used to some Operating
Systems which start a new process for my program.  The OS may continue
running autonomously doing various tasks while my program runs
separately, it need not wait until the program passes control back.

GOBACK is an IBM thing that they have eventually managed to get into
the latest standard. The association that I have with it is a single
tasking OS that hands control to the program until the program hands
control back. One step above a boot loader it may be, but only one
step.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)

- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com>`

riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com>...
> jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> step.

Why would you want to tear down the entire process environment in your
code?  This effectively prevents anyone from using your code within
other code.  In the IBM case, use of STOP RUN almost requires that
said program is the program listed in the EXEC statement.

It is possible, with LE to register a condition handler and recover
from the STOP RUN.  But it is alot of work to gain otherwise normal
behavior (i.e. single entry and exit, expect control back after a call
statement, return control back to next statement after call, etc).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T22:03:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com>`

```
jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote

> Why would you want to tear down the entire process environment in your
> code?  

<Shrug>.  This is a different environment, it doesn't work like yours
does.

> This effectively prevents anyone from using your code within
> other code. 

My systems are interactive systems of one sort or another.  They
usually have a small core program that holds the the interface data
and sits in a dynamic CALL .. CANCEL loop until it requires to do a
STOP RUN.  Most systems, in fact, use the same core program so that
the systems can be mix-and-match.

The core program cause the loop to call an initialisation program,
possibly a user login, maybe a menu selection, often it depends on the
login as this may indicate the company, system, options, security
level, startup program.

All the other programs in the system rely of the core holding the
interfacing data.  The fact that this one tiny program does a STOP RUN
is of trival interest.  No one would want to use the tiny core program
in other code.  They may well want to use the dozens of reusable
programs that the core program CALLs.

> In the IBM case, use of STOP RUN almost requires that
> said program is the program listed in the EXEC statement.

<Shrug>, my system doesn't work like yours.  It _requires_ a STOP RUN
to exit the Cobol run-unit.  A GOBACK in the initial program simply
does the same as STOP RUN.
 
> It is possible, with LE to register a condition handler and recover
> from the STOP RUN.  But it is alot of work to gain otherwise normal
> behavior (i.e. single entry and exit, expect control back after a call
> statement, return control back to next statement after call, etc).

Not a problem here at all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T11:01:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com>...
><snip>
> All the other programs in the system rely of the core holding the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> programs that the core program CALLs.

It sounds as if we actually agree. I don't mind one tiny program doing
a STOP RUN.  But I hate finding a stop run 'maintained' into one of
those dozens of reusable programs...it can be troublesome to explain
why things just stop suddenly.

> <Shrug>, my system doesn't work like yours.  It _requires_ a STOP RUN
> to exit the Cobol run-unit.  A GOBACK in the initial program simply
> does the same as STOP RUN.

But, if a GOBACK operates the same as a STOP RUN at the initial
program level,  doesn't the choice to use STOP RUN only prevent other
systems from tail patching your systems?  Or prevent your systems from
being executed by an http server building responses?  Or prevent a
scripting package from gathering or maintaining data via your systems?

If STOP RUN and GOBACK are identical for initial programs, and only
GOBACK is usable in sub programs, why would you ever choose to use a
STOP RUN?  Doesn't such a choice require you to give up the useful
abstraction of not knowing which type of program you are working with?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T13:20:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com>`

```
As a follow-up on STOP RUN versus GOBACK, one of my personal "pet peeves"
(probably showing the same IBM mainframe background as Joe comes from -
below), is the Fujitsu *requirement* that you use a "specific" compiler
option (@OPTIONS MAIN - or its equivalent in the IDE) for the main program.

In my personal view (and the ANSI/ISO Standard doesn't seem to explicitly
say this),  COBOL - unlike many other languages - should NOT care if a
program is or isn't a "main" program - as long as it doesn't have a Linkage
Section and Procedure Division USING phrase.   (I am also used to compilers
that allow Linkage Section and Procedure Division USING - for a "main"
program - but that IS clearly an extension.)

Especially with the (new 18 years ago) EXTERNAL attribute, you don't need to
pass parameters in USING phrases of a CALL statement.  Therefore, it should
be totally "possible" to design and code a program that works "just fine" as
either a main program or a subprogram.  UNFORTUNATELY, the Fujitsu compiler
(unlike IBM, Micro Focus, and Realia compilers - on the same Windows
platforms) just won't let you do this.

I suppose, that this ties in (back to the original thread) with my tendency
to use GOBACK instead of STOP RUN (or even EXIT PROGRAM) because I am "used
to" an environment where I don't know (and do not WANT to know) whether my
code will be executed as a "main" or "sub" program.  GOBACK works great for
this.  If, on the other hand, I *must* decide that a program will be used as
a MAIN - then I might as well use STOP RUN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T14:15:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302281415.7e117d98@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote 

> Especially with the (new 18 years ago) EXTERNAL attribute, you don't need to
> pass parameters in USING phrases of a CALL statement.  Therefore, it should
> be totally "possible" to design and code a program that works "just fine" as
> either a main program or a subprogram. 

It may be that your appliactions are written for a rather restricted
range of situations.  For example it may be that you write for one
machine, one business, one company and you can predict exactly where
the program will be used.

The vast majority of my programs may run in different applications,
for different companies on different machines.  This means that it is
useful to know which set of file names to use, which operating system
is running, which company is being processed, which screen i-o
mechanism is being used, what the user's security level is, etc.

All these are established by various callable programs (usually user
logon).  If I were to run a program as 'main' then I would have to
arrange all this to be done.  It is much better, for me, to have all
this sorted out _prior_ to my program being run.  This is done by
providing a command line parameter to the 'core' program (which is
effectively the _only_ main) and have this call the user logon passing
the appropriate parameters to fake a login, and then call my program.

This was all programmed a couple of decades ago (with minor
refinements since) and means that I never need worry about all this
system information and where it comes from.  I can single-shot
programs from command-line or use these interactively.  But my
environment is quite different from yours.  Your solutions won't work
here, mine won't work there.  My solutions _do_ work with Fujitsu
extremely well, which indicates that they know the needs of this
environment (or these environments).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T15:05:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3opvn$4hl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In case it's of interest:  Unisys task parameters are at Lex Level 2,
library parameters at Lex Level 3.  A program that can be executed as either
has to copy the LL=3 parameters up to LL=2 (so they're visible to the likes
of SORT procedures along with the rest of the Data Division, as well as to
the "body" of the Procedure Division, both of which run by default at LL=3).
"Uplevel" pointers are dangerous (less so since the introduction of ASD's),
so COBOL85 avoids all the danger spots inherent in uplevel pointers by
requiring $LIBRARYPROG for programs intended as "libraries" (in MCP's sense
of the term); those that aren't libraries are executable as tasks.  There
are some ways you can seriously shoot yourself in the foot with COBOL74 that
the distinction between a library and a task disallows with COBOL85.  Nearly
twenty years ago now we had one of these foot-shots that took us nine months
to find!

Point of all this being:  There may be a valid, and appropriate,
architectural reason in the underlying Fujitsu operating environment that
makes this requirement ultimately, and perhaps even vastly, more attractive
than the alternatives in that context.

    -Chuck Stevens
