###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T23:51:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5ff60c.181226308@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>As a follow-up on STOP RUN versus GOBACK, one of my personal "pet peeves"
>(probably showing the same IBM mainframe background as Joe comes from -
>below), is the Fujitsu *requirement* that you use a "specific" compiler
>option (@OPTIONS MAIN - or its equivalent in the IDE) for the main program.

I agree. Fujitsu appears to have a communication program between their compiler
and linker. If you compile NOMAIN and try to link as .exe, the linker says it
cannot find an entry point. Evidently the .obj contains different language to
describe the entry points of MAIN programs vs. called. Rather than fixing the
linker to work right, they created a compiler option. 

>Especially with the (new 18 years ago) EXTERNAL attribute, you don't need to
>pass parameters in USING phrases of a CALL statement.  

Can you say 'blank common'? Can you say 'global'? 

Using the linker (or dynamic loader) to pass parameters is a Worst Practice. It
subverts the idea of localization by passing data through the 'back door'. C
programmers love to do this, while decrying COBOL's 'ocean of global data' in
working-storage. 

>Therefore, it should
>be totally "possible" to design and code a program that works "just fine" as
>either a main program or a subprogram.  UNFORTUNATELY, the Fujitsu compiler
>(unlike IBM, Micro Focus, and Realia compilers - on the same Windows
>platforms) just won't let you do this.

It's easy for Micro Focus because _all_ programs are called .. by 'the run-time
guy': cobrun. 

>I suppose, that this ties in (back to the original thread) with my tendency
>to use GOBACK instead of STOP RUN (or even EXIT PROGRAM) because I am "used
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>a MAIN - then I might as well use STOP RUN.

The problem with EXIT PROGRAM is that it must be alone in a paragraph. Why?
There is no logical reason. I'm offended by having to write a paragraph just so
I can exit. GOBACK provides the same functionality without requiring a paragraph
name. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T18:07:48-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3otkp$iv5$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5ff60c.181226308@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e5ff60c.181226308@news.optonline.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
 <snip>
>
> The problem with EXIT PROGRAM is that it must be alone in a paragraph.
Why?
> There is no logical reason. I'm offended by having to write a paragraph
just so
> I can exit. GOBACK provides the same functionality without requiring a
paragraph
> name.
>
> Robert
>

Robert,
  Any chance that you are confusing the "EXIT PROGRAM" statement and the
"EXIT" statement?  There is certainly no rule in the '85 Standard (or any
Standard conforming compiler) that "EXIT PROGRAM" needs to be in a paragraph
by itself.  (There is a rule - removed in the 2002 Standard - about it being
the last in a series of imperative statements - within the same sentence.)

There is a rule about the "place holder EXIT" statement needing to be in a
paragraph by itself.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T18:38:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302281838.6a34a04b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5ff60c.181226308@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> I agree. Fujitsu appears to have a communication program between their compiler
> and linker. If you compile NOMAIN and try to link as .exe, the linker says it
> cannot find an entry point. Evidently the .obj contains different language to
> describe the entry points of MAIN programs vs. called. Rather than fixing the
> linker to work right, they created a compiler option. 

What complete nonsense.

There is no 'communication problem', there is no broken linker that
needs 'fixing'.  .EXEs and .DLLs are quite different in several ways. 
A 'main' program must generate an entry point that is equivalent to
main() in a C program.  A .DLL must not have this and must have a WEP.

This is not a 'fault' in Fujitsu it is just the way that the
environment works.  With MF much the same is required for Windows
programs but it is just specified differently.
 
> It's easy for Micro Focus because _all_ programs are called .. by 'the run-time
> guy': cobrun. 

That depends on the environment and how the system has been built.  On
DOS and Windows I don't use 'cobrun', I use the shared or static
run-times.  In the case of Windows programs they, too, are built as
.EXE (main) and .DLL and different linkage parameters are used to
provide the required entry points.

> The problem with EXIT PROGRAM is that it must be alone in a paragraph. Why?
> There is no logical reason. I'm offended by having to write a paragraph just so
> I can exit. 

The reason for the paragraph is that the EXIT statement must be in a
paragraph by itself and this flowed over when they added EXIT PROGRAM.
 Of course the EXIT paragraph was required by archaic mainframe
compilers to have a place to put the return from a PERFORM.

> GOBACK provides the same functionality without requiring a paragraph
> name. 

GOBACK is an extension and is not part of standard Cobol until some
time in the future (mainframers have trouble understanding this).

EXIT PROGRAM is standard, as a common extension (for example in MF
since 1978) it can be used anywhere.  This is true in MF, Fujitsu, and
RM.  Probably most others.

If you are abiding strictly by the standard you would put EXIT PROGRAM
in its own paragraph, but then you wouldn't be using GOBACK at all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T17:05:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302281705.1a462cc1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote 

> Especially with the (new 18 years ago) EXTERNAL attribute, you don't need to
> pass parameters in USING phrases of a CALL statement.  

There is a practical problem with this, at least in my environments. 
If I need to add some extra fields to my interface block that is
passed to all programs then I only need to recompile the 'core'
program where it is held.  Al the programs that are passed this longer
block need not worry because they will only use the old, shorter, part
of it.

I only need recompile, retest and distribute the core and any program
that now uses the extra data.

If I change an EXTERNAL block then I need to recompile every program
that references it because a size check is done.  Every program must
be recompiled, retested and redistributed.

> Therefore, it should
> be totally "possible" to design and code a program that works "just fine" as
> either a main program or a subprogram.  

Of course the EXTERNAL block still needs to be filled somehow with
valid data when it is run as 'main'. Either you will repeat what other
programs do in filling it, or you will reapeat what my 'core' program
does in calling programs to fill it.

This will then be duplicated effort when run as CALLed as the data
area will already be filled.

Having programs that can run stand-alone or called just isn't
interesting (to me).
```

```
> to get into the 2002 standard.  IBM people don't seem to know this.

The IBM people I work with didn't know that it will finally be standard.   It
will be nice to not worry about it going away though.
```

### A How To Question

- **From:** IMSDBAGuy <IMSDBAGuy@nospam.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-22T00:00:13
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns9329C105378EBSnowCrash7antispamne@216.168.3.44>`

```
We are reading into a Cobol program an unsorted list of datasets that 
includes a space allocation value. We are also given x number of new DASD 
volumes to place these datasets on.

Currently the records are being read into a table, with a column for the 
space value.

The code to produce the correct delete define cards is done. However.....

We would like to consider ways to evenly distribute these datasets on these 
volumes, preferably by not using an internal or external Sort.

We are assuming that one way to do this would be to first deternmine the 
largest datasets and allocate those first. Is that necessarily correct?


Would appreciate any suggestions on ways to approach this.
```

```
to using SMS (System Managed Storage).  There are IBM Redbooks
explaining this and how to do it easily.
```

#### ↳ Re: A How To Question

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>

```
If you are on OS/390 or z/OS, SMS (System Managed Storage) can do most of this for you,
and it uses a very good algorithm. Otherwise, you have a problem in what amounts to computational
geometry.

The only link to a web available resource I have quickly available to me (here at home) is the following link.
It is not exactly what you are looking for, but it may give you some ideas. Also, the bibliography might give you some good
pointers.You could also try a good web search or two.

http://www.eecs.harvard.edu/grosz/SanPelGro.pdf


-Paul
```

```
"IMSDBAGuy" <IMSDBAGuy@nospam.net> wrote in message
news:Xns9329C105378EBSnowCrash7antispamne@216.168.3.44...
> We are reading into a Cobol program an unsorted list of datasets that
> includes a space allocation value. We are also given x number of new DASD
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> We would like to consider ways to evenly distribute these datasets on
these
> volumes, preferably by not using an internal or external Sort.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Would appreciate any suggestions on ways to approach this.

By 'evenly distribute' I assume you mean the same amount of storage per
drive.

Another more interesting, and probably more productive, criterion would be
the frequency of access - you wouldn't want to end up with several of your
most active datasets on the same drive. This makes the problem two
dimensional.

Sorry if I've complicated a problem you were trying to simplify.
```

#### ↳ Re: A How To Question

- **From:** Dirk Haar <news@mideal.de>
- **Date:** 2003-02-22T15:51:35+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030222154618.392D.NEWS@mideal.de>`
- **References:** `<Xns9329C105378EBSnowCrash7antispamne@216.168.3.44>`

```
> We are reading into a Cobol program an unsorted list of datasets that 
> includes a space allocation value. We are also given x number of new DASD 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Would appreciate any suggestions on ways to approach this.
Normally I'd say it is the best way to use an external sort BEFORE
/INSTEAD reading it into COBOL programs.
You don't say why you have different datasets and why you want to 
put them into different datasets. What are the criterias for that ?
Why don't you want to use a sort ?

Is it to do later processing parallel ? I did something like that at
Vodafone 1 1/2 years ago. By sorting one file first onto 10 datasets
and let the huge processing run parallel, I could reduce run time from
25 hours down to 3 1/2 (with use of Job Nets 3/3/2/2 jobs parallel).

Dirk
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A How To Question

- **From:** SnowCrash7 <SnowCrash7@anti-spam.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-22T20:01:30
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns932A988D4CE4DSnowCrash7antispamne@216.168.3.44>`
- **References:** `<Xns9329C105378EBSnowCrash7antispamne@216.168.3.44> <20030222154618.392D.NEWS@mideal.de>`

```
OK what is happening here is this, We are IMS DBA's. We have been tasked 
with creating multiple instances of database datasets in order to create 
a new series of test databases. 

Wanting to automate the process of creating the delete/define cards, my 
partner is writing a routine that breaks down an existing PDS library of  
dataset delete-define cards into a series of 80 byte 'records'. These 
records are being loaded into a table in a Cobol program.

We want to write a program that would create new delete-define cards for 
the datasets to be allocated over however many volumes we are given.

The program is basically done except for a procedure that would specify 
the Volume value to allocate them over the volumes we would be given.

If we are given 6 DASD volumes to put them on, my partner wants to be 
able to write out the new delete-define cards with the datasets allocated 
over the different volumes....with a given reserve of freespace left on 
each volume.

Now if one was to manually allocate a series of datasets over a series of 
volumes, I assume that we would probably work from the largest dataset 
down in size. Since we assume thats how a human mind would do it, we are 
asking ourselves if this is the best way to do it in a Cobol program.

My partner feels that it can be done efficiently without a sort, either 
before or during the program. I'd do a pre-sort but he is stubborn.


Having a lot of respect for the know-how I've seen demonstrated in this 
newsgroup I wanted to try to post the situation here and get some 
feedback.

*************************************************************************

To break the problem down into its simplest elements.

Let's say you were given 10 dataset delete-define cards.

The dataset records are NOT in size order.

The 10 datasets range in size from 6000 to 50 Cylinders apiece.

You are  given 6 DASD 3390 volumes.

Three Questions:

1.) Is it best to allocate the largest dataset first?

2.) Can you think of a way to do this without a pre or internal Sort?

3.) Is my partner being too stuborn for his own good? :)

Thanks in advance.

Gerry



>> 
>> Would appreciate any suggestions on ways to approach this.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dirk
```


#### ↳ Re: A How To Question

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T20:07:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5AC1C4.85A1F780@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns9329C105378EBSnowCrash7antispamne@216.168.3.44>`

```
IMSDBAGuy wrote:
> 
> We are reading into a Cobol program an unsorted list of datasets that
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Would appreciate any suggestions on ways to approach this.

I will assume that SMS controlled volumes have been ruled
out (I can understand why, I have more battles with SMS and
the ACS than I care to think about -- and I am one of the
ones that writes the ACS!).

First we need to understand the usage of the data sets. Are
they indexed, and do we want the index to be on a different
volume than the data set?

Next, we need to know if this is RAID based DASD or real
3390 type volumes (actually makes a difference in tuning and
VSAM parameters used!).

If we need the data to be scattered from each other, then
what we do is we take the volumes and we put them in some
kind of order (it would be nice to have them ordered such
that they aren't on the same paths...).

Data gets laid down, then the index gets allocated by
pulling from opposite ends of the volume list (we are
assuming that the opposite end of the volume list does not
share paths with the start). Meanwhile, keep track of the
storage used on the volume(s).

As we do this, we also use a round robin and last least used
methodology for allocating the volumes (yes, having them
sorted would make life easier!). Since you KNOW what the
volumes will have, you also know how much space is committed
on each volume. If the commit is over a certain % then you
go to the next least used volume.

If you can control the volumes, the ones with the LARGE
FILES on them (assuming that they would take up the whole
volume), then you do not want a VTOC-IX (waste of time &
space). Also, you will want the VTOC to be small and as
early on the VOLUME as is possible.

On the Volumes with lots of files, you may well want the
VTOC to be centered (again, with RAID based, this is a waste
of time!) and you will want a VTOC-IX.

This is just off the top of my head, not knowing many issues
that I would need to know to help you do allocation that is
also tuned for what you are doing.
```

#### ↳ Re: A How To Question

- **From:** "Don Higgins" <don.higgins@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T06:09:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3fins$61t$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns9329C105378EBSnowCrash7antispamne@216.168.3.44>`

```
I believe the simple answer to your question is yes there is a way to simply
distribute files to multiple volumes without sorting them provided you don't
need the "best", just a good approximation which will be true if file sizes
are not large compared to the size of the volumes.  I suggest keeping track
of the current total for each volume, and then add each file to the volume
with the smallest total.  This will ensure that each volume only differs in
total space by up to the size of the largest file.

Don Higgins
don.higgins@microfocus.com
"IMSDBAGuy" <IMSDBAGuy@nospam.net> wrote in message
news:Xns9329C105378EBSnowCrash7antispamne@216.168.3.44...

> includes a space allocation value. We are also given x number of new DASD
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> We would like to consider ways to evenly distribute these datasets on
these
> volumes, preferably by not using an internal or external Sort.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

```

<a id="t-6d16ed7a58"></a>
## Thread: Fitting more than 18 digits in numeric field. — 3 msgs

### Fitting more than 18 digits in numeric field.

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-02-23T11:11:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2564501.1045998691@dbforums.com>`

```

The IBM reference manual says that COBOL for OS/390 supports 31 digit
numbers. Packed and zoned decimal numbers may contain up to 31 digits if
you use the compiler option ARITH(EXTEND). I've never tried it (never
needed a number that large), but the manual says it works.

Microfocus Cobol
Actually the option is already in Net Express.
If you use the directive SET ISO2000 then you can use 31 numeric digits
in the PICTURE clause.
If you use the directive SET MF then you can have up to 38 numeric
digits in the PICTURE clause.

I hope that information can be useful to somebody.

respectfully
Marcos A.S.
Brasil
```

#### ↳ Re: Fitting more than 18 digits in numeric field.

- **From:** LSoens@t-online.de (Lothar Soens)
- **Date:** 2003-02-23T17:37:05+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<INZNCBOtXuBC-pn2-obQudYIMSysY@lsoens.dialin.t-online.de>`
- **References:** `<2564501.1045998691@dbforums.com>`

```
It works in COBOL for OS/390, but beware of ugly performance when 
using large numeric variables. Even if their actual values are small.

Regards,
Lothar
```

- **From:** Mike Hutchinson <mikehut@concentric.net>

## Thread: Subclasses from GUI-Classes like LISTBOX — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### Subclasses from GUI-Classes like LISTBOX

- **From:** mario_sinicki@msg.de (Mario Sinicki)
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T02:13:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b70219.0302240213.7c2373bc@posting.google.com>`

```
I have some problem with subclasses fro GUI-Classes.

We use NetExpress3.1 and Dialog-System. I built a subclass from
LISTBOX and made my changes in there. Now I want to integrate my new
class in Dialog-System but I found no way use my new class instead of
the class LISTBOX at Dialog-System.

My English is not that good but I hope someone understands my problem
and have a solution.
```

#### ↳ Re: Subclasses from GUI-Classes like LISTBOX

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T20:14:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5A7D9B.DBD624C5@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3b70219.0302240213.7c2373bc@posting.google.com>`

```


Mario Sinicki wrote:

> I have some problem with subclasses fro GUI-Classes.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> and have a solution.

Mario,

I don't use Dialog System so go to Micro Focus Answer Exchange and post
your question under Net Express. I'm pretty certain there are some DS
demos that cover Listbox contents.

Briefly how I do it using Dialog Editor and GUI classes - which wont be
dramatically different from Dialog System. As you indicted a separate
sub-class to create the Collection.

1. Set parameters to create Collection (ofReferences ???)
2. Initially create collection - read in records from COBOL file or DB
Table (Cursor Select)  and extract info to include in your collection
elements - do this with the "add" method.
3. Within your Dialog you have a control already designed for the Listbox
- so pass the Collection with :-

invoke YourListBoxControl "setcontents" using os-TempCollection

This is your list of EXISTING records.

4. If editing records, depends upon your design. Assume you allow user to
select EXISTING from the Listbox or key in an entirely new record. When
PB-OK, for safety's sake WRITE/REWRITE to your file or INSERT/UPDATE for
SQL.

5. Now you want to redisplay the 'changed' Listbox, (strings below are
object references) :-

(a) Add -
invoke os-TempCollection  'add' using ls-string (obj ref)

(b) Change -
       invoke os-TempCollection "replaceAll"
               using ls-string1 ls-string2 ls-index1 ls-index2

(c) Delete -
        invoke os-TempCollection "removeAtIndex"
                using lnk-index returning ls-string

Now pass the updated collection to your Dialog with :-

invoke YourListBoxControl "setcontents" using os-TempCollection

That's a 'potted' version - but go to Answer Exchange for more support.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Subclasses from GUI-Classes like LISTBOX

- **From:** "Calin @ Work" <dontemailme@work.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-24T18:31:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7Ux6a.1875$kf7.319777@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3b70219.0302240213.7c2373bc@posting.google.com>`

```
You should try browsing through the MicroFocus documentation (help file and
their web site) for the user controls features.

IF you don't want to go that way, create a "place-holder" object in DS and
in your cobol code do "fromHandleWithClass", using that place-holder's
handle and your class.

Good luck,
Calin, TORONTO
```

```
A place-holder can be any object you want that you can then access.  In your
case, I would recommend using a listbox in dialog system - no properties, no
script; then use fromhandlewithclass after your windows is created (hint -
use either after create-windwo my window statement or in window-created
procedure).

Good luck,
Calin, TORONTO
```

```
Hi,

Most of the time error 114 is you try to run a program which is not in
yout COBPATH.

check the environment variable COBPTH and see if the program is in your
COBPATH. If not add the path to the environment vaiable.

regards

Johan
```

```
mfcobol2002 wrote:

> I am using Cobol MF v3.4.23 in atmosphere Linux, together with
> FileShare, working with architecture Client/Server, the problem is that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com

I wont swear to it - but probably for all M/F compilers it is the same -
"RTS error 114 ("Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory")

More often than not it is associated with Linkage Section parameters
whether using CALLs or INVOKES - If appropriate, carefully check your
sending and returning parameters. Use your Debugger (Animator ?) to check
the *actual* values being sent or returned.

If you are still stuck go to the Micro Focus Answer Exchange and post your
query under the appropriate product.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** "Abdullah Kauchali" <someone@someplace.com>

```
Hi,

I am interested in finding out whether any of you write custom applications
to automate the creation of COBOL code?  This is probably among experienced
COBOL coders who have "seen it all before", so they'll probably keep or make
"clever tools" to do the dirty work.  (Not necessarily the gory details,
just interested in knowing how prevalent it is and whether there's an
official movement like Patterns out there for COBOL.)

Kind regards

Abdullah Kauchali
```

```
> to automate the creation of COBOL code?  This is probably among experienced
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> official movement like Patterns out there for COBOL.)
```

```
structures" so that it could produce your required COBOL src files - with
full SQL or whatever data access method you use!  That's automation at its
highest.  Don't you have a tool like that?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T15:03:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3g0kp$ad4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <b3ds73$aco$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3f6dd$4mt$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>`

```
On 25-Feb-2003, "Abdullah Kauchali" <someone@someplace.com> wrote:

> > The trouble with asking experienced COBOL coders is that we can code
> pretty
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> highest.  Don't you have a tool like that?

I use IDMS.   No.   But with any source code generator, I want it to be powerful
enough to do source code maintenance.  If it has all of that, the source code is
really irrelevant, as we don't need to ever look at it.   If it doesn't do that,
the source code should follow shop standards.
```

#### ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T00:07:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5AB3C8.76DF515@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>`

```


Abdullah Kauchali wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Abdullah Kauchali

As you've seen from Howard's reply, 'veterans can code quickly, so why
bother....'.
Possibly a sentiment others share, and yet amongst the mainframe crowd (the
majority), they must consistently be calling common routines, or even nesting
programs etc., which is on the same tack as your question.

OK - so I'm into OO - which I think offers far more possibilities. But first
let's back-track to non-OO. In previous messages Richard Plinston has said he
wasn't happy with the syntax available for Screen Section. So he wrote his own
routines, which I believe to be common to all his screen handling.

Initially starting on OO (Net Express) I really floundered with no comprehension
of what OO was about and how these damned GUIs fitted into the whole.
Arranga/Coyle firstly, and then Wilson Price 'Elements of OO COBOL' gave me the
breakthrough as to OO's simplicity once you get beyond the concepts - the $10
words, encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism etc.

When I first coded, each item selected from the Master Menu was perceived as a
separate entity with its own (business) logic, plus screen and file/DB handling.
As I got more comfortable with OO I latched on to the fact I was continuously
repeating myself - particularly with GUIs.

Stop coding. Put the brakes on and give it a 'real think'. Right back to design.
Quite naturally design is a very personal thing - and there is NO RIGHT way,
there are alternative approaches, just as successful as yours.. In the case of
large installations, even allowing for 'individual stylists' it makes sense,
even in non-OO if you can come up with an installation standard - master
template, that you can repeatedly use for say Editing a Masterfile, (Customer
Names, Chart of Accounts, General Ledger etc.).

Harkening to what Richard wrote, I realized I had to back-track to the beginning
on GUIs, as they inter-play with the rest of the N/E product. My visual design
tool is Micro Focus Dialog Editor - which follows very closely the Microsoft
Dialog Editor. You create your screen images visually then allocate IDs for the
controls. (Note that the Fujitsu POWERFORM and M/F's FORMS modules do a better
job setting properties). Nevertheless the Dialog Editor is not bad plus when
coupled with dynamically 'painted' tables (you try and do a 10 x 24 table, with
a 'static designer <G>).

So rather than pay lip service to the Dialog Editor I read the file and extract
a set of parameters for the controls. Can't do fancy diagrams here, but looks
like following :-

[A : EditProgram]----->[B : DialogParameters]-------->[C :Instances of Dialog]

"A" invokes "B" to pass the controls needed to "C". That being done  "B"  can be
dropped (finalized/destroyed) and the inter-activity from Windows events is
between "A" and "B". While t"A" and "B" are unique to a particular business
problem, "C" is common to all dialogs I create.

Same goes for COBOL file handling :-

[X : EditProgram]----->[Y : DBI]-------->[Z :Instances of file classes]

A copy file (e.g. CustomerRecord) is used in both X and Y (Database Interface)
and "Z" is a 'generic' file class ISAM, Sequential, Linesequential, Relative
achieved by those classes having a record :-

01 DataRecord.
    05 occurs 1 to n depending on ws-Recordsize    pic x.

Most messages bypass "Y" above which is primarily used for creating (a)
collections/dictionaries - prime requirement being Listboxes and (b) accessing
records in "Z" and selectively stripping off fields in "Y" to be passed back to
"X".
(Same sort of technique used with DBs and SQL - except - "Z" is not an instance
in the general meaning, but relates to a specific DB table).

We aren't quite there yet - but hopefully some seeds are starting to germinate.
So back to what Richard does with Screen Section. I read the Dialog Resource
File and generate the parameters for "B". I don't need to worry about "C" as it
will handle all dialogs (at least to date <G>). File handling "X", "Y" and "Z"
apply and for SQL "Z" is applicable to only one table.

I wrote "generate the parameters for "B"...". But coming up with my own design
style for editing, using an 'Edit Template' - I can generate 80% plus of the
code for "A". The model is fairly crude at the moment and I use 'search/replace'
to change copyfile references.

As crude as it may be, and obviously it can be refined to the nth degree, it
sure is going to save me a heck of an amount of time in the future. Mine is a
little guy's version of what those very expensive tools like SAP will do for
you. The only problem with tools like SAP is that they are heavily overloaded
with features to cover every eventuality and to appeal to as many potential
customers as possible.

Even without OO and its own particular techniques, I believe the above
short-cuts can pay off in non-OO development.

PS : I'm no expert but can claim to be a veteran if you take age into account
<G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** "Abdullah Kauchali" <someone@someplace.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T09:33:43+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3f68a$4i0$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3E5AB3C8.76DF515@shaw.ca>`

```
James,

Thanks for your answer, quite detailed indeed.

Regards

Abdullah
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T15:12:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3g140$an9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3E5AB3C8.76DF515@shaw.ca>`

```

On 24-Feb-2003, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> As you've seen from Howard's reply, 'veterans can code quickly, so why
> bother....'.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> routines, which I believe to be common to all his screen handling.

One of the things about OO, is that once you create a objects, you want your
program generator to really, really understand that method and know when to
incorporate it in future generation.   I'm thinking that designing a generator
designed around reusability of new objects (and easy enough to have programmers
actually tell the generator how to use their new objects), isn't trivial.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T00:34:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5C0C04.FE5210B7@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3E5AB3C8.76DF515@shaw.ca> <b3g140$an9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> On 24-Feb-2003, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> actually tell the generator how to use their new objects), isn't trivial.

"...you want your program generator to really, really understand that method and
know when to incorporate it in future generation....."

Not a bad objective - but we aren't there yet, but it does lean towards Pete
Dashwood's concepts of using components. Thane mentioned quite a while back, (he'll
have to confirm - it may have been Java), that as you are coding there are some (?)
languages which prompt you to select the right method - not totally what you are
after, but close.

I was curious that there currently didn't appear to be mainframe tools but both
Thane and Chuck made reference to that. The snag is generators tend to be
overwhelming to cover everything - just like SAP does. Needless to say the latter
comes at a hell of a price. I'm now aware of three SAP users in Calgary but they are
all 'big league' - two are oil companies and the third is into agriculture
fertilizers, primarily using natural gas to produce their end products..

The other message that came across with the 'generators' was - 'No touchee source
code otherwise you can finish up in trouble'. That's the sort of message which will
make the bravest of us back off <G>. Even given a tool like SAP - get your initial
design screwed up and you can make it crawl instead of run.

It's a snap to do the SQL stuff in Net Express because of ESQL Assistant doing the
COBOL source coding for queries. The PC world, I would suggest, tends to lend itself
to generators, because there aren't too many programs that don't include a Dialog.
Where you are using JCL on mainframes the PC equivalent for printing a report, very
likely contains a dialog for checkboxes, "print this, in this sequence..', 'Don't
print this one...' etc.

A Window and its controls are finite. Even for the N/E Entryfield Class - there are
some 46 methods, (and I'm assuming these match the API equivalents) - but it's
likely the average programmer possibly wont use more than some 20%. That being said
it becomes possible to set the focus on the controls being used and ensure that when
generating a business logic program, it at least contains the skeleton methods for
those controls.

Depending upon your design some are very generic - take my use of the Pushbutton OK
event returned to business logic from the generic Dialog handler :-
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "PB-OK".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 n                             pic x(4) comp-5.
01 n2                            pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-response                   pic x(4) comp-5.
Linkage section.
01 lnk-flag                      pic x(4) comp-5.

Procedure Division using lnk-flag.

move lnk-Flag to RecordChangedFlag

*>******* here we start customized coding **********

if   (Probe-ProbeID = spaces) OR
     (RecordNotChanged)
     invoke self "displayEmptyScreen"
     EXIT METHOD
End-if

set ValidationOK to true
perform varying n from 2 by 1 until n > ws-MaxEntryFields
      invoke os-Dialog "returnEntryObject" using n

*>.Above is returning the value of each specific control to the appropriate skeleton

*> method in this program - where the 'customized' validation occurs

      if ValidationError
         EXIT METHOD
      End-if
End-perform

set Field-RB-DUAL-ELEMENT to true
add 1 to Dialog-Field giving n2
invoke os-Dialog "returnRadioObject"
       using Dialog-Field, n2

if not ValidElement
       set Error-Element to true
       invoke self "messagebox" returning ls-Response
       set Field-RB-DUAL-ELEMENT to true
       invoke os-Dialog "setNextFocusClear"
              using Dialog-Params
End-If

*> ******* customized coding ends here ******

if ValidationOK and RecordChanged

       if     RecordFound
              invoke self "rewriteRecord"

       else   invoke self "writeRecord"
       End-if
End-if

*> Write/Rewrite above - same terminology for Files or DB Tables - except
*> within the methods for the latter the SQL syntax is INSERT or UPDATE

if ValidationOK AND ContinueProgram
   invoke self "displayEmptyScreen"
End-if

End Method "PB-OK".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T00:36:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5c00d8.99702055@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3E5AB3C8.76DF515@shaw.ca> <b3g140$an9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>One of the things about OO, is that once you create a objects, you want your
>program generator to really, really understand that method and know when to
>incorporate it in future generation.   I'm thinking that designing a generator
>designed around reusability of new objects (and easy enough to have programmers
>actually tell the generator how to use their new objects), isn't trivial.

To do that you need Introspection. You'll have to create it yourself because OO
Cobol doesn't natively provide it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T10:29:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302251029.6da792bd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3E5AB3C8.76DF515@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 

> In previous messages Richard Plinston has said he
> wasn't happy with the syntax available for Screen Section. So he wrote his own
> routines, which I believe to be common to all his screen handling.

Yes, I have written screen generators which take a text file of a
screen or a series of screen parts and build the working-strorage and
procedure division code.  I have used this for a number of program,
about 20, which were add-ons or replacements to older existing systems
using ADIS, so they fitted that style.

The original was done in 1996 I think during one of my 'what should I
be doing next' phases. Since then I use SP/2 for most new Cobol work.

Overall the screen generator didn't save much coding, but it was much
more interesting writing the generator than it was doing the grunt
work that was generated for me.

20 years ago I also decided that writing one large complex program was
preferable and more interesting than many small programs and wrote
script (text file) driven file maintenence program and report writer.

These don't generate code, they just use the control information to
specifiy the file dictionary, screen layouts for file maintenence,
report layouts and selection/sort criteria.

These allow me to get a new type of system up and running very quickly
to the point where data is being setup while I develop the processing
modules and specialised input for it.

These were pulled apart and new SP/2 front ends stuck on for TUI/GUI.
I mostly use SP/2 in text mode.
```

#### ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T02:27:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030224212731.29547.00000105@mb-cl.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>`

```
>From: "Abdullah Kauchali" someone@someplace.com 
>Date: 2/24/03 1:53 PM Eastern Standard Time

>I am interested in finding out whether any of you write custom applications
>to automate the creation of COBOL code?  This is probably among experienced
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Abdullah Kauchali

What I do and most of the other programmers I've worked with over the years
have done is 'clone' a program that's already been written.  Know what you want
to do, find one that does most of it, copy it and make necessary changes.  A
lot of programming is same old same old.  

As for nested programs and called programs for I/O etc etc I haven't seen this
done in any of the shops I've worked at.  In the current shop we have a
purchased accounting package that does have it's own subroutines for I/O but
that's it.   Most called programs have been for date conversions and one that
was an algorythm to determine which database to used to find subscriber
information when I worked in health care.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** "Kevin J. Hansen (Norcom)" <khansen@screenio.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T22:16:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dqn5vc9rpb2f6n3oineiqgche0997av22@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <20030224212731.29547.00000105@mb-cl.aol.com>`

```
On 25 Feb 2003 02:27:31 GMT, yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama) wrote:

>>From: "Abdullah Kauchali" someone@someplace.com 
>>Date: 2/24/03 1:53 PM Eastern Standard Time
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>official movement like Patterns out there for COBOL.)
============
We recently added a code generator to GUI ScreenIO, and people seem to
both use and like it.  It generates the basic code needed to run the
screen you create, using a template that you can easily modify to suit
your preferences/standards.

As it turns out, it reduces the (already small) learning curve for
using the product, too.

That having been said, we've been coding COBOL for so long that we
often can find something lying around to clone; always trying to
improve it, of course!  It just depends on the situation.  If I were
developing an application from scratch, I'd probably use the code
generator just to speed up the process and standardize the code.  It
makes maintenance a LOT easier.

Kevin
Kevin J. Hansen
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - Windows(tm) Programming in COBOL!
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

```
"Abdullah Kauchali" <someone@someplace.com> wrote in message
news:b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net...
> Hi,
>
> I am interested in finding out whether any of you write custom
applications
> to automate the creation of COBOL code?  This is probably among
experienced
> COBOL coders who have "seen it all before", so they'll probably keep or
make
> "clever tools" to do the dirty work.  (Not necessarily the gory details,
> just interested in knowing how prevalent it is and whether there's an
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I've written a few of those over the years.

The weirdest one was a tool that converted a sample report layout text file
(you know, the kind you show the user) to a Cobol working-storage report
layout.

Another one was a tool that, given two file layouts, would generate the MOVE
statements needed to copy all fields from one to the other.

My favourite was an ISPF edit macro that, given a DCLGEN,  generated any
required SQL statement.
```

#### ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T08:06:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>`

```
"Abdullah Kauchali" <someone@someplace.com> wrote in message news:<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>...
> Hi,
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Abdullah Kauchali

There are a lot of very nice COBOL based tools for this in existance. 
From the most rudimentary - template driven code generators to full
respository based system generators - like MPACT or CASEGEN or TOTEM.

I even used a non COBOL one years ago called "Black Box".

The problem I have had using these types of tools is the learning
curve necessary to make them useful.  In addition - when special
customizations are required you sometimes need to alter generated
code.  The SECOND you do that - the whole respository generated idea
goes out the window.

I don't have a solution - just my observations.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T08:19:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3g52v$ail$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com>`

```

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com...
> "Abdullah Kauchali" <someone@someplace.com> wrote in message
news:<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>...
> > Hi,
> >
> > I am interested in finding out whether any of you write custom
applications
> > to automate the creation of COBOL code?  This is probably among
experienced
> > COBOL coders who have "seen it all before", so they'll probably keep or
make
> > "clever tools" to do the dirty work.  (Not necessarily the gory details,
> > just interested in knowing how prevalent it is and whether there's an
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't have a solution - just my observations.

The EAE (formerly known as LINC) product generates complete applications --
COBOL code, data base specifications, and whatever glue is necessary to hold
the whole thing together -- from a user application description.  Originally
implemented on Burroughs systems in the days before the merger,
implementations are available on a number of platforms, including (but not
necessarily limited to) Wintel and Unix as I understand it, in addition to
Unisys MCP/AS systems.

Although it is possible to modify the generated COBOL source code, tools are
provided for debugging at the application generator level, and it is not
recommended that users get directly involved with the generated COBOL
source, concentrating instead on the specifications for the application and
debugging at the higher level.  The EAE / LINC developer need have no
knowledge of COBOL at all.

Many large corporations have significant EAE / LINC applications; it has
been a very successful product.

It isn't, strictly speaking, a program that generates COBOL fragments; it's
a 4th-generation system that generates applications, and the procedural code
that it generates to support those applications happens to be in COBOL.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T16:35:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3g60r$dgi$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com>`

```

On 25-Feb-2003, thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

> The problem I have had using these types of tools is the learning
> curve necessary to make them useful.

Heck - in most environments programmers don't even get comfortable enough with
CoBOL's REPORT WRITER.  Coding "the hard way" is just not very hard.

It's less obvious why testing tools don't get used more - after all testing is
much more difficult and time consuming than coding is.   (although most of that
is in determining valid test conditions and making sure the test is
comprehensive).

Plus, there is a much bigger demand for programmers who know CoBOL than who know
how to use tools to generate CoBOL.

> In addition - when special
> customizations are required you sometimes need to alter generated
> code.  The SECOND you do that - the whole respository generated idea
> goes out the window.

We've got three choices here:
1.   The generator works for new and maintenance code.   We don't look at the
generated CoBOL source any more than CoBOL programmers look at generated
assembler.   Of course with this generator, the language of the generated code
isn't important - why not generate it directly into assembler?
2.   The generator doesn't work for maintenance.  We have to maintain code
ourselves.   As this is the majority of CoBOL coding being done, this devalues
the advantage of having a generator coding to its own standards, and not having
good programmers do it.   It might be better than having poor programmers.
3.   The generator works for stand along programs.  But incorporating called
modules and library objects into its gestalt is not an easy task.   The question
is - do we have such a tool that is easy for the programmers to incorporate
these modules effectively?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T23:52:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2dd28$d66b9400$2accf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com>`

```
In my experience most COBOL generators are as hard or harder to learn than
COBOL.

For Y2K my organization approved usage of three approaches.  One of these
was "packed" dates where eight digit dates in packed/comp-3 format where
placed into the existing six digit dates.  I wrote a ROSCOE rpf that
generated pack and unpack routines for all the date fields in a particular
database.  It could handle dates in any one of four formats.  These
routines were then COPYied into the programs.

I also tend to clone programs from an existing one with similar features.

applications

make

details,
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T00:37:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com>`

```
thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>"Abdullah Kauchali" <someone@someplace.com> wrote in message
news:<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>...
>> Hi,
>> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>I don't have a solution - just my observations.

My thoughts on code generators: 

1. Don't use them. You can write higher quality code from scratch almost as
quickly. 

2. If you must use them (because the boss insists), run them once and thereafter
maintain the generated code. 

3. If the generated code is poor quality, as is usually the case, run it through
a beautifier. 

Code Generators are designed by Computer Science types, who disparage
programmers. They think they have a Better Idea which will make us obsolete.
It's about arrogance and ego, not about improving productivity, as claimed.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T14:22:57+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3h502$t7n$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> 1. Don't use them. You can write higher quality code from scratch almost
> as quickly.

<ironic>Such as your examples ?</ironic>
 
> Code Generators are designed by Computer Science types, 

Another vast generalisation which is easily dismissed because I myself have 
designed and written code generators.  I also have used other code 
generators that I know were written by 'programmers'.

> who disparage programmers. 

I can quite understand why this has been your personal experience, but I 
don't find that at all.

> They think they have a Better Idea which will make us obsolete. 
> It's about arrogance and ego, not about improving productivity,
> as claimed.

In most cases that I have used or examimed code generators it was about 
removing repetitive grunt work and the errors that arise from it.

For example, using embedded SQL and generating the specific database code 
is much more productive and portable than coding specific calls to the 
current system.  

I would recommend to avoid modifying the generated code because then you 
would get problems if it is regenerated, and especially you have removed 
its portability.

But then you would know abouit arrogance and ego.  Hmmm, or maybe you 
really don't notice.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T11:45:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5ca8f8.12971812@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <b3h502$t7n$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>> Code Generators are designed by Computer Science types, 
>> who disparage programmers. 
>
>I can quite understand why this has been your personal experience, but I 
>don't find that at all.

In other newsgroups, I've seen them write "I'm not a programmer, I'm a computer
Scientist." and "If I wanted to learn programming, I would have gone to trade
school."

>In most cases that I have used or examimed code generators it was about 
>removing repetitive grunt work and the errors that arise from it.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>current system.

The SQL pre-processor is usually thought of as a compiler, not a code generator.


>I would recommend to avoid modifying the generated code because then you 
>would get problems if it is regenerated, and especially you have removed 
>its portability.

Large shops commonly make 'customization' changes to generated and third-party
code, being careful to mark the changes in some way. When the code is
regenerated or a new version published, a low-level programmer is charged with
manually 'merging' changes into the new code. Talk about error-prone!

The same shops have version-control software which could automate the task, but
don't use it for that purpose. 

Robert
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message news:3e5ca8f8.12971812@news.optonline.net...
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

Well, I am a Software Engineer, not a programmer. However, I can and do program, both for profit and enjoyment.
The one thing I have found throughout the entire industry is that regardless of titles, people respect competenance.
Claiming false experience or ability is a fast track to unemployment. Unless of course, you are on the fast track
to managment, Dilbert style.



> >In most cases that I have used or examimed code generators it was about
> >removing repetitive grunt work and the errors that arise from it.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

By whom, may I ask? It is generally thought of as a pre-processor, which is a totally different thing from
a compiler. Nor is it  considered a "code generator" since it doesn't generate any code, it simply replaces
one set of code with another.

>
> >I would recommend to avoid modifying the generated code because then you
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is because Code Generators almost always generate (1) Nasty Code and (2) Code that does not completely satisfy the
requirements. Shops using code generators usually, sooner or later, find themselves, getting rid of the code generator.

-Paul


> Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T05:21:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clC7a.34451$Tz6.1458637@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <b3h502$t7n$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e5ca8f8.12971812@news.optonline.net> <na47a.32030$O41.1063730@twister.austin.rr.com> <3e5d4f28.7403425@news.optonline.net> <WBe7a.50490$F%.2686210@twister.austin.rr.com> <3e5ec467.102968778@news.optonline.net>`

```
I am an engineer in training and qualifications. I am a Software Engineer by choice.
There is no abuse of the word "engineer" there at all. I am most certainly as real an
engineer as my buddy who designs damns, or another buddy who designs hypersonic
aircraft wing surfaces.

Yes, there are licensing and qualification issues going on now about Software Engineers,
I agree with most of them. On the gripping hand, some of the issues are just plain silly.

By the way, I *had* to learn how to weld; it is an engineering degree requirement. And I
rarely pull cables anymore; I do design the networks and supervise the cable pulling. And
I also have a CompSci degree, and an E.E. as well. So what? A lot of the people here can
leave me far behind in COBOL subjects. I respect all of 'em for that.

It seems to me that either you are pretty bitter about something or else have lived a very
limited life. The latter seems not to be likely, given some of your statements on your experience.
Either that or you are real feisty young kid who likes stirring up witches brew and makes a lot of
stuff up as he or she goes. Isn't the Internet wonderful? <grin>

You might have a lot more fun if you learned to be at least a little more polite, and thing twice about
what you say before you say it.

-Paul


"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message news:3e5ec467.102968778@news.optonline.net...
> "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> I should have said 'The SQL pre-processor is usually considered a part of the
> compilation process.' Does that answer your objection?

Other code generators are also 'part of the compilation process' in
exactly the same way.  For example Decision Tables (which I used
decades ago), and Screen Code Generators are often done for each
compile, mine certainly are.

In the case of LINC the compilation is done directly after generation
of the code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T08:22:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0302260822.2487d09@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net>...
> 
> My thoughts on code generators: 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Robert

I just spent a week last month writting a generator that can generate
programs to convert 600-odd copybooks (each with anywhere from 10 to
10,000 fields) between xml document format and Cobol copybook format.

I don't suspect that you could write the 600-odd programs faster than
I can run said program 600-odd times, each with a different copybook
name...even if you had the extra week head start.

As the old saw goes -- "If all you have is a hammer, the world looks
like a nail" -- Code generators, like every other tool, have a place
and time to be used.  If you use them in the wrong place, you will
indeed get a mess, much as you will if you try to beat a screw in with
a hammer.  If you use them in the right place they can greatly
simplify repetive grunt work.

Where do you get your ideas about computer science types -vs-
programmers?  I'm not sure I've ever seen such a distinction made. 
Being a self-identified programmer of several languages and several
platforms with three degrees in computer science, which group do I fit
in?
```

```
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> name...even if you had the extra week head start.

I've done similar things.  But I never thought to call it a "code generator".

My definition of "code generator" is where I do my design (and sometimes my
analysis), on a computer and the computer writes new code based upon the
business requirements.

I don't know what others mean by that term.  Can I update my resume?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T02:55:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5eb28e.98399852@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302260822.2487d09@posting.google.com> <3e5d524d.8208933@news.optonline.net> <16e2f010.0302262023.271f085a@posting.google.com>`

```
psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message
news:<3e5d524d.8208933@news.optonline.net>...
>> jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:
>>  But I'm still
>not sure where the hostility toward 'CS' comes from...There seems to
>be significent crossover between that and programmers.

I used to work at a shop that had 20 superstar programmers. Each one would have
been the star in most shops. It was a dot-com kinda place, except we did
embedded rather than Web. Out of curiosity, I went around asking each whether he
had a degree in CS. Not a one did. Some were high-school dropouts like me, most
had degrees in music, philosophy, biology or business. All were self-taught
programmers. 

I also asked who was the best programmer in the shop. They nearly all pointed to
one guy who was a part-time programmer, full-time high-school student at the
time. He seemed like a dork to me, very straight boy scout type who didn't talk
much.  (After graduating high-school, he went on to Mormon missionary work.)
They said look at his code. I did and saw they were right. It was beautiful.
Just shows that you can't judge people from external appearance or lifestyle. 

>> > I'm not sure I've ever seen such a distinction made. 
>> >Being a self-identified programmer of several languages and several
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Probably both .. leaning toward intelligent programmer. You're the only
poster
>> to this newgroup with whom I generally agree.
>
>I'm not sure if I should thank you for that or not.

Probably not. I'm the black sheep here because I disparage mainframers who will
hold onto periods until their #2 pencils and coding sheets are ripped from their
bleeding fingers. 

It's all about Culture, not language. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T06:39:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5F0492.F511246E@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302260822.2487d09@posting.google.com> <3e5d524d.8208933@news.optonline.net> <16e2f010.0302262023.271f085a@posting.google.com> <3e5eb28e.98399852@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> (snip)
> Probably not. I'm the black sheep here because I disparage mainframers who will
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Pencils & coding sheets.  Maybe DocDwarf will appreciate my story.

When I got my first programming job in 1979, every programmer was
issued a mechanical pencil, a pad of coding sheets, and one "Pink
Pearl" eraser.  I think there were about 20 applications programmers
for one IBM 370/145 running DOS/VS.  This shop was a little behind the
curve, and they were just beginning to install CICS.  So there were
only three 3278 terminals for 20 programmers.  They used batch
Panvalet and card decks to modify COBOL programs.

I had just come from Junior College where I learned to program in
COBOL by punching hollerith cards on an 029, so my first sighting of
an IBM-3278 with COBOL code scrolling up & down was very exciting.

But my initial programming assignments for that company were on an IBM
Series/1 (4955 model D, 96K of memory with one 9-meg disk drive and
one 58-meg disk drive).  The Programming Supervisor insisted that it
was a waste of valuable programmer time to permit programmers to type
their programs directly into the Series/1 (or into Panvalet On-line on
the system 370), and we were required to write out coding sheets, turn
them over to the keypunch operators, wait one to three business days
for keypunching, then transmit the source code to the Series/1 over a
null modem connector, and finally begin the even more tedious work of
correcting the keypunchers' typos. 

One irritation on the Series/1 was that the ANSI-74 COBOL compiler
used double-quotes around literals, which had to be corrected whenever
mainframe programs were migrated.  We didn't even know about
APOST/QUOTE compiler directives or installation options.  I even wrote
an APAR for a compiler bug.

The big advantage to the Series/1 was that it was located in the
machine room.  That meant we could work on it without being in the
bullpen under the watchful eyes of the programming supervisor.  We
junked the coding sheets right away and just typed the programs
directly in, or copied and modified from an existing program.   

It wasn't until 1983 or 1984 that they got around to putting a
mainframe terminal on every programmer's desk.  They never got around
to putting a telephone in each cubicle. 

I haven't put pencil to coding sheet in over 22 years.  I still use
periods sometimes, but I don't get very excited one way or the other
over them. 
```

```
In article <3E5F0492.F511246E@worldnet.att.net>,
Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>Robert Wagner wrote:
>> (snip)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pencils & coding sheets.  Maybe DocDwarf will appreciate my story.

zzzzZZZZZzzzz... zzzzzzZZZAAAAWWWKKKKHHhhhhhh... whuh? bluh?  I'm sorry, I 
was just... resting my eyes, did someone call me?

[snip]

>But my initial programming assignments for that company were on an IBM
>Series/1 (4955 model D, 96K of memory with one 9-meg disk drive and
>one 58-meg disk drive).

A Series/1... hmmmmm, last time I saw one of those was... must have been 
'86, '87... the company was doing routing and delivery of snack-foods 
using the NORAND TTS/ACCA system.

(actually... I just had to look at my bragsheet, I didn't remember the 
acronym at all)

Kinda spiffy, for the time... all across the country they had truckers 
with handheld Z80 thingummies that they'd key their delivery information 
into... then they'd get back to the depot and put the Z80 machines into a 
cradle that was locally polled by an IBM PC/AT to upload the data... then 
the AT would call headquarters and dupm the data to the Series/1, which 
served as a front-end for the 3090, which would take the data, adjust 
inventories, run the forecasts and then spit back down this same pipeline 
the next day's orders, through the S/1, through the AT and into the 
handhelds.

>The Programming Supervisor insisted that it
>was a waste of valuable programmer time to permit programmers to type
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>correcting the keypunchers' typos. 

Heh... waste o' time, indeed... after all, you could be attending meetings 
or something!  Reminds me of the story - perphaps apochryphal - of the 
Corner-Office Idiot who sento out a memo requesting that programmers 
decrease their TSO connect-time to keep expenses down.

>
>One irritation on the Series/1 was that the ANSI-74 COBOL compiler
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>an APAR for a compiler bug.

I've avoided this single- or double-quotes discussion... but I was taught 
that if one wished to avoid troubles one used single-quotes and that was 
that.  Folks might notice that this has spilled over into my typing style, 
as well.

>
>The big advantage to the Series/1 was that it was located in the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>to putting a telephone in each cubicle. 

Heh... telephones in cubes... reminds me of another story that I'll forget 
about until I'm reminded about it again.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T15:21:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3nuql$pd2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302260822.2487d09@posting.google.com> <3e5d524d.8208933@news.optonline.net> <16e2f010.0302262023.271f085a@posting.google.com> <3e5eb28e.98399852@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 27-Feb-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Probably not. I'm the black sheep here because I disparage mainframers who
> will
> hold onto periods until their #2 pencils and coding sheets are ripped from
> their
> bleeding fingers.

I know a gal who missed a couple of periods and got into all kinds of trouble.


I don't think that's the reason you're the black sheep here.

I'm the person who argues that periods aren't the problem.   You don't like them
because you associate them with the old style of coding - but the only reason
you gave for them being "bad" is that when you cut and paste sentences into
blocks you have to remove them.  I like them because my pre-compiler sticks in
unterminated IF conditions.   I don't find removing periods hard, and take the
time to do indenting and prettying up of my code when I cut and paste anyway.

This is an old argument - and people advocating removing periods have never been
"black sheep" for taking that side.   No, it must be something else.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T19:50:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302260822.2487d09@posting.google.com> <3e5d524d.8208933@news.optonline.net> <16e2f010.0302262023.271f085a@posting.google.com> <3e5eb28e.98399852@news.optonline.net> <b3nuql$pd2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302281109.3941d608@posting.google.com>`

```

On 28-Feb-2003, jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

> At the risk of being a smart-ass (and I'm always worried about that)
> -- isn't the precompiler in question the IDMS
> 20-years-out-of-date-and-unsupported precompiler?
>
> I would kinda associate that with both "old" and "bad".

It's still supported, and it works well.  But periods are valuable for me here.

I am trying to find a good argument from the people who associate "new" with
"good" that removing periods can be correspondingly valuable.   The arguments I
have seen are:

1.   It's too much work to remove periods when I convert code to being in-line.
2.   My eyesight is poor enough that I can miss them.
3.   I want to make it more English like by removing periods.
4.   Failure to use terminators and use of NEXT SENTENCE can cause errors.
5.   It's what was used in the olden dayz, back when people coded poorly.

My replies:

1.   I edit such code anyway, aligning it  and such - but generally prefer to do
a PERFORM.
2.   Good argument.   But if you use terminators, and don't use NEXT SENTENCE,
the compiler will catch it where it matters.
3.   Huh?
4.   Then use terminators and CONTINUE.   I do.
5.   Not a reason.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T14:43:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302260822.2487d09@posting.google.com> <3e5d524d.8208933@news.optonline.net> <16e2f010.0302262023.271f085a@posting.google.com> <3e5eb28e.98399852@news.optonline.net> <b3nuql$pd2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302281109.3941d608@posting.google.com> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
OK - let me see if I can summarize (my view):

1) First and foremost, if your program has *no* "NEXT SENTENCE" phrases (or
statements - where such an extension exists), there can be (now and forever)
*NO* "logic flow" differences in your program no matter how many or how few
(except for the last one) periods you use within a paragraph.  Therefore,
everything else is STYLE and nothing more or less.

2) If your program *does* include some "Next Sentence" phrases, then the use
of periods *may* lead to programmer errors - when:
  A) the programmer THINKS (incorrectly) that a "Next Sentence" is always
the same as a  CONTINUE statement
  B) when the source code is "modified" and a new period is added or an old
one is removed
  C) when "nesting" an existing piece of code into a new conditional
phrase - without checking for periods and Next Sentence implications.

3) As long as the new "free format reference format" is available (and new
"construct" such as END-SENTENCE is added), then it is IMPOSSIBLE to provide
a method of getting rid of all periods in the Procedure Division - as it is
impossible to "syntactically" differentiate (in all cases) a procedure name
from a data name (without looking up every item in the data division - and
even then a "typo" may lead to the "wrong" compiler message)

4) "liberal" use of scope terminators (with or without periods and with or
without Next Sentence in the code - either in conforming or extension
places) seems like a "good idea" to most people.  (Whether to use them on
statements WITHOUT conditional phrases is still controversial)

5) For the visually impaired (like me) the use of "periods" is problematic -
but I am prepared to "search" for them in code that I have modified that was
created by those who use them.  When I create new code, I do NOT use them -
except at the end of each paragraph - and then I place them on a line by
themselves (sometimes even with an "inline comment" if the compiler accepts
such)

   ***

For code withOUT "next sentence" phrases, I don't think there is ever going
to be a "right or wrong" answer to whether or not to use periods in the
middle of paragraphs.  I do (personally) think that "consistency" in a shop
(or at least in a single program) is a GOOD thing as it will help in
maintenance - even by those used to "another style".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T21:21:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ojsh$51q$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302260822.2487d09@posting.google.com> <3e5d524d.8208933@news.optonline.net> <16e2f010.0302262023.271f085a@posting.google.com> <3e5eb28e.98399852@news.optonline.net> <b3nuql$pd2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302281109.3941d608@posting.google.com> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 28-Feb-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> 1) First and foremost, if your program has *no* "NEXT SENTENCE" phrases (or
> statements - where such an extension exists), there can be (now and forever)
> *NO* "logic flow" differences in your program no matter how many or how few
> (except for the last one) periods you use within a paragraph.  Therefore,
> everything else is STYLE and nothing more or less.

I think everybody can agree - a style that proscribes NEXT SENTENCE is certainly
worth while.


> 4) "liberal" use of scope terminators (with or without periods and with or
> without Next Sentence in the code - either in conforming or extension
> places) seems like a "good idea" to most people.  (Whether to use them on
> statements WITHOUT conditional phrases is still controversial)

The failure to use END-IF can make people who use periods have different bad
logic than for people who don't use periods, but both types of problems are bad.
  We agree that END-IF is a good thing.   I won't get into the controversial
part in this discussion.


> For code withOUT "next sentence" phrases, I don't think there is ever going
> to be a "right or wrong" answer to whether or not to use periods in the
> middle of paragraphs.  I do (personally) think that "consistency" in a shop
> (or at least in a single program) is a GOOD thing as it will help in
> maintenance - even by those used to "another style".

I agree here.   Certainly my experience of being a contractor has been to search
out preferred examples of local style and emulate them where they don't make a
significant difference.
```
