- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T22:31:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jrR7a.5153$Uy4.403340@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302260822.2487d09@posting.google.com> <3e5d524d.8208933@news.optonline.net> <16e2f010.0302262023.271f085a@posting.google.com> <3e5eb28e.98399852@news.optonline.net> <b3nuql$pd2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302281109.3941d608@posting.google.com> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <b3ojsh$51q$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message news:b3ojsh$51q$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> 
> On 28-Feb-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> worth while.

I think that everybody will agree - a style that conscribes the use of NEXT SENTENCE
is certainly worth while.

Theoretically, it is indicative of the programmer's awareness of the duality of the test, 
and the possible need for positive action on both sides of the ELSE.

In my opinion, such a shop standard encourages an environment where a programmer
takes the time to ask "What if", with respect to the default path.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T14:24:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302281424.61fc1997@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302260822.2487d09@posting.google.com> <3e5d524d.8208933@news.optonline.net> <16e2f010.0302262023.271f085a@posting.google.com> <3e5eb28e.98399852@news.optonline.net> <b3nuql$pd2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302281109.3941d608@posting.google.com> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> 2.   My eyesight is poor enough that I can miss them.

> 2.   Good argument.   But if you use terminators, and don't use NEXT SENTENCE,
> the compiler will catch it where it matters.

Get a better text editor that you can configure.  Mine puts full stops
on a red backgound block and are pretty hard to miss.  You could also
have colour highlighting, but I had to tone mine done somewhat.  Key
words, user words, comments, literals, numbers are in different
colours.  I also have keyboard macros for most reserved words which
sppeds up typing and accuracy.

Macros to strip/add full stops would be pretty easy (but I would only
use strip <grin>).

Also 19 inch monitors are cheap these days (<$US200) and should help
when used 80x25.
```

 ?
> I need one for a couple of weeks for my university project.
>
> Thanks in advance
> Tal

There are Tiny-Cobol and Open-Cobol projects which are free.

You can get a trial version of Kobol from www.TheKompany.com, the IDE
times out every 30 minutes or so, but it does compile and runs.  It is
only $60.00.

The Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux compiler will run for 30 days I think,
then will require licence (not free).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT]Job offers for COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Sean Eby" <gotchamail@sbcglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T19:14:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Qm87a.680$rM4.114417@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<b3fi1p$gvq$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <3e5c09f4.102034616@news.optonline.net> <b3i7uf$iqv$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <3e5cc02d.18913618@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e5cc02d.18913618@news.optonline.net...
> "Capellone" <capellone76@libero.it> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Another question: do you think a company would take someone, like me,
living
> >on the other side of the world paying for him a relocation, getting
the Visa
> >and do all the stuff concerning  the hiring?
>
> Regular employers would not. You might be able to find a contracting
company
> that would.
>

Agreed. Also, the 'economic climate' plays a big part in how much the
government makes it easy for domestic employers to hire foreign workers.
Typically, during booms they increase the foreign employment markets by
increasing the cap on H-1B visas and L-1 visas (is it L-1?? I cannot
recall...) for companies. So, right now, it is more expensive and costly
in terms of dollars and time for a company to bring in and hire a
foreign worker due to the domestic economic climate of the United
States. However, it IS not impossible, and depending on your skillset
and experience and whatever else that can set you apart from your peers
there as WELL as labor competition that exists in the US already, you
may STILL be able to find an employer here that would work through this
process with you. Still, there is no free lunch, so you'd have to work
pretty hard and do your own homework on this issue. If you've got any
kind of international networking you could rely on, I would use your
time right now to rely on it if at all possible. Also, all of this is
contigent on WHAT skills are in demand, and as has been said, the demand
for skilled COBOL programmers is rather stagnant, but stable, so there
isn't a rigorous need to bring in foreign hands. But, as I just said, it
isn't impossible. Foreign COBOL programmers are being hired right as I
speak...

Sean
```

## Thread: DISP for optional file — 6 msgs

### DISP for optional file

- **From:** abhikush@rediffmail.com (maverick)
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T05:15:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8501a81.0302250515.27a44c8c@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
What is the DISP parameter for optional file? I used DISP=OLD but that
did not work out. Got a JCL error.
```

#### ↳ Re: DISP for optional file

- **From:** Terry Sambrooks <terry@kmsitltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T08:40:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5b725e$1_8@news.onlynews.com>`
- **References:** `<8501a81.0302250515.27a44c8c@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

What is meant by optional file.

From a program's perspective a file may be optional in that it may be
OPENED or NOT OPENEd according to the logica flow.

If it is OPENED and there is no DD statement, then an IEC message of
some type will be issue. (An abend will also occur if I/O is attempted,
so DD DUMMY or DSN=NULLFILE would be the order of te day.) If it is not
OPENED then no DD statement is required.

From a JCL point of view OPTIONAL doesn't arise if the file is required
a DD statement must be present. The parameters will depend upon whether
the file exists or not. If the file exists, the disposition would
logically be either OLD or SHR. If the data sets doesn't exist it would
seem logical to code DISP=NEW.

For temporary data sets at least it is possible to edge one's bets and
code DISP=MOD, then if the data set exists the new data will be
appended, and if it doesn't exist, MOD will be treated as NEW.

The error message is not mentioned in detail, but was it perhaps "data
set not found".

Regards - Terry
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DISP for optional file

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T08:37:08+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ggne$kf0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<8501a81.0302250515.27a44c8c@posting.google.com> <3e5b725e$1_8@news.onlynews.com>`

```
Terry Sambrooks wrote:

> What is meant by optional file.

An OPTIONAL file is one that need not exist when the file is OPENed.

IF an OPTIONAL file is OPENed INPUT and does not physically exist then the 
file open is successful with a status of '05' and any READ statement will 
take the AT END or INVALID KEY branch.

That is, it will act as if the file is present but empty.

If the file is OPENed I-O or EXTEND it will be created as an empty file.  

Note that a non-OPTIONAL file that does not exist should fail with a status 
'35' - file not present (or other if not '85 standard).

Some systems, such as MF, have compiler options that cause non-OPTIONAL 
files to be created automatically when OPENed I-O or EXTEND.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DISP for optional file

- **From:** abhikush@rediffmail.com (maverick)
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T21:08:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8501a81.0302252108.d8c38b9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8501a81.0302250515.27a44c8c@posting.google.com> <3e5b725e$1_8@news.onlynews.com>`

```
I posted this question in www.mvshelp.com too and there too I did not
specify the error message. But for whatever it is worth give yourself
a pat in the back you did get the error message write. When I posted
this question I was in hurry dying to get back to home and hence the
lack of details.

The problem I have is I am not sure whether or not the file will be
present when I run the job. So I was planning to use "optional" in the
select statement. I gave disposition as OLD(SHR) in the JCL and there
I got the error which you have so successfully guessed. I was hoping
that with the file being optional this would work but as it turned out
this was not ANSI comittee had in mind when they designed this verb.

Anyway now I am coded an "IF" statement in the JCL checking whether
the file exists or not. I am executing this step only if the file
exists which renders the optional verb in my program useless.

Thanks for your time.

>

- **Message-ID:** `<HF37a.612$rM4.68362@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8501a81.0302250515.27a44c8c@posting.google.com> <3e5b725e$1_8@news.onlynews.com> <8501a81.0302252108.d8c38b9@posting.google.com>`

the OPTIONAL clause in a COBOL SELECT is totally, absolutely, completely
unrelated to the JCL.

(See also $ELSE)

MCM

## Thread: COBOL SORT within an online program (Non-File) — 22 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### COBOL SORT within an online program (Non-File)

- **From:** alexmcshane@blueyonder.co.uk (Alex McShane)
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T11:06:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dc8c3ee.0302251106.35ca006a@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi 
I would appreciate your advice on the following.

An online IMS/DC COBOL subroutine will receive in Linkage a array of
records. There is a need to Sort these records in a different
sequence.

Can the COBOL internal SORT facility be used to SORT non-file storage?
All the examples I have seen relate to File input 

Thanks
Alex
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL SORT within an online program (Non-File)

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T11:28:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3gg4d$i87$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dc8c3ee.0302251106.35ca006a@posting.google.com>`

```
Alex McShane wrote:

<<Can the COBOL internal SORT facility be used to SORT non-file storage?
All the examples I have seen relate to File input >>

"Table sort" is a feature of 2002 COBOL.  See ISO/IEC 1989:2002, page 524,
14.8.36 SORT Statement Format 2 (table).

Syntax example and summary is

    SORT data-name-1 ON <ascending/descending>... KEY data-name-2 ...

where data-name has to have an OCCURS clause, and data-name-2 must either be
the same as or subordinate to data-name-1.

I don't know which, if any, implementation provides this feature; I would be
surprised, however, were there none.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL SORT within an online program (Non-File)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T23:25:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5BFBCD.76B4EBF1@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<dc8c3ee.0302251106.35ca006a@posting.google.com> <b3gg4d$i87$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```


Chuck Stevens wrote:

> Alex McShane wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens

Might well have been in their DOS product but M/F have had the Table Sort as an
extension certainly since VISOC and inception of Net Express. I've only used it
once to sort six entries - extremely useful.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T15:06:00+00:00

#### ↳ Re: COBOL SORT within an online program (Non-File)

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T21:18:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R4R6a.1107$PK3.130245@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<dc8c3ee.0302251106.35ca006a@posting.google.com>`

```
"Alex McShane" <alexmcshane@blueyonder.co.uk> wrote in message
news:dc8c3ee.0302251106.35ca006a@posting.google.com...
> Hi
> I would appreciate your advice on the following.
>
> Can the COBOL internal SORT facility be used to SORT non-file storage?
> All the examples I have seen relate to File input

RELEASE and RETURN sorts (INPUT PROCEDURE: OUTPUT PROCEDURE) may sort
anything...the SORT has no clue from wence the data came... often used for
table sorts in compilers which do not natively support table sorts.

You are only restricted to "files" with USING .. GIVING sorts.

(FWIW, you can mix and match...  SORT sort-file ... USING filename OUTPUT
PROCEDURE xxxx... real handy for loading an unsorted disk file to a sorted
table).

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL SORT within an online program (Non-File)

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T16:20:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5C0860.840C50DC@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<dc8c3ee.0302251106.35ca006a@posting.google.com>`

```
I would not bother with the SORT statement.

Simply define another table of the same size, and copy the entries one by
one, putting them in the correct sequence.  This takes about 10 lines of
code, and if the entries happened to be in sequence, it is roughly equal
to a sequence check in time expended.

Basically, you code the following:
Working-Storage:
01  SOURCE-CODE-LIMIT PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE +4096.
01  SOURCE-CODE-SIZE  PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE ZERO.
01  SOURCE-CODE-TABLE.
 05 SOURCE-CODE-ENTRY
               OCCURS 1 TO 4096
            DEPENDING SOURCE-CODE-SIZE
            ASCENDING SOURCE-CODE
              INDEXED SC-X.
 10 SOURCE-CODE       PIC X(06).

Procedure Division:
Setup code:
To  guard against mistakes when table sizes get changed,
code the following statement in the 'housekeeping'  area
for each table that has a 'table-limit' item:
        COMPUTE 'table-limit' =  LENGTH OF 'table-name' /
                                 LENGTH OF 'table-entry'(1)
(In  the  sample  above,  'table-limit'  corresponds  to
SOURCE-CODE-LIMIT,    'table-name'    corresponds     to
SOURCE-CODE-TABLE,   and  'table-entry'  corresponds  to
SOURCE-CODE-ENTRY.)
The COMPUTE verb will set the 'table-limit' item to  the
correct  value,  even if the programmer forgot to change
it!  However, every effort should be made  to  keep  the
OCCURS  clause  and 'table-limit' in synch, because this
will aid maintainability of the program.

The sorting code:
Adding an item in sequence to a table:
The method is to move the last item in the table  to
the next occurrence, repeating this move until there
is  room  for  the  new entry at the proper point to
maintain the table's sequence.

          ADD 1            TO 'object'
          IF  'object'     >  'table-limit'
              PERFORM TABLE-OVERFLOW
          END-IF
          SET 'index'      TO 'object'
          SET 'index' DOWN BY 1
          IF  'object'     >  1
              PERFORM MAKE-ROOM-IN-TABLE
                  VARYING 'index' FROM 'index' BY -1
                  UNTIL   'index'   <  1
                     OR   'new-entry-key' >  'entry-key(index)'
          END-IF
          MOVE 'new-entry' TO 'entry(index + 1)'
          .
The MAKE-ROOM-IN-TABLE procedure reads:
      MAKE-ROOM-IN-TABLE                             SECTION.
          MOVE 'entry(index)' TO 'entry(index + 1)'
          .

Also possibly of interest:
Deleting an item from a table:
The method is to move the next  item  in  the  table
over the item being deleted, and repeating this move
until  the  end of the table is reached.  The result
is a table one item shorter than before, even to the
point of becoming 'empty' (zero occurrences).

The item to be deleted is pointed to by 'index', and
the size of the table is contained in 'object'.  The
value of 'object' must be  greater  than  zero,  and
'index'  must  be in the range of 1 to 'object', inclusive.

          IF 'object'  >  1
              PERFORM TAKE-ITEM-OUT-OF-TABLE
                  VARYING 'index' FROM 'index' BY 1
                  UNTIL   'index'   =  'object'
          END-IF
          SUBTRACT 1 FROM 'object'
          .
The TAKE-ITEM-OUT-OF-TABLE procedure reads:
      TAKE-ITEM-OUT-OF-TABLE                         SECTION.
          MOVE 'entry(index + 1)' TO 'entry(index)'
          .

Happy sorting!
=====
Alex McShane wrote:

> Hi
> I would appreciate your advice on the following.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Alex
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL SORT within an online program (Non-File)

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T09:01:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5CF2DE.6DD44C50@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<dc8c3ee.0302251106.35ca006a@posting.google.com> <3E5C0860.840C50DC@attglobal.net> <ZZGcnWShf_hvt8GjXTWchA@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse,
I thought you were being humorous in another post where you mentioned being
against using the COBOL standard index with arrays.

Maybe I could recode this example.  But why?  Your compiler and mine both
support everything in it.  The objective is to get the work done, and this will
do it.

Of course, I have personal preferences, too.  They run more to forbidding use
of ALTER and GO, using only one period per procedure, etc.

I think your argument was that indexes do not provide a significant performance
improvement, so why use them?  I could argue that likewise, subscripts do not
provide a significant performance improvement (in fact, I do not know of any
case where they offer any), they must be "correctly" defined as to usage and
the like, and they must be separately defined, and therefore could end up
getting used for multiple tables (which could be dangerous), so why use them?

There are some COBOL language elements (SEARCH / SEARCH ALL comes immediately
to mind) that require indexes.  Unless your programming environment has an
equally convenient method for table searching, I assume you grit your teeth and
use indexes some of the time.

The example I posted is torn from a larger document that discusses good table
handling techniques.  There, I extol the virtues of OCCURS ... DEPENDING
(because one never needs to initialize a table), limit checking when loading a
table and what to do when table overflow occurs (read the rest of the table
input, so a programmer knows how much to increase the table size), use of
SEARCH / SEARCH ALL, etc.  In the context of that document, the use of indexes
is integral.
Colin

JerryMouse wrote:

> Could you recode your example using subscripts? I distinctly remember
> forgetting about indexes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL SORT within an online program (Non-File)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T09:33:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3itq6$27aj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dc8c3ee.0302251106.35ca006a@posting.google.com> <3E5C0860.840C50DC@attglobal.net> <ZZGcnWShf_hvt8GjXTWchA@giganews.com> <3E5CF2DE.6DD44C50@attglobal.net>`

```

"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote in message
news:3E5CF2DE.6DD44C50@attglobal.net...

> The example I posted is torn from a larger document that discusses good
table
> handling techniques.  There, I extol the virtues of OCCURS ... DEPENDING
> (because one never needs to initialize a table), limit checking when
loading a
> table and what to do when table overflow occurs (read the rest of the
table
> input, so a programmer knows how much to increase the table size), use of
> SEARCH / SEARCH ALL, etc.  In the context of that document, the use of
indexes
> is integral.

Hmm.  I would caution that ODO entails more range checking for reliability.
Not only must you (or the compiler) ensure as for "normal" tables that the
index/subscript is in range, you (or the compiler) must ensure that the ODO
dynamic limit hasn't exceeded the physical array bounds.  Actually, we do it
the other way 'round; we check the ODO limit against the table upper limit,
and then if that passes we check the subscript against the ODO limit.  ODO
may not be suitable for performance-critical logic because of the overhead
of the bounds checking (user-supplied or compiler-supplied).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL SORT within an online program (Non-File)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T15:34:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5D4EEA.E64EBAED@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<dc8c3ee.0302251106.35ca006a@posting.google.com> <3E5C0860.840C50DC@attglobal.net> <ZZGcnWShf_hvt8GjXTWchA@giganews.com> <3E5CF2DE.6DD44C50@attglobal.net> <b3itq6$27aj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
I don't see any difference in the checking needed.  You can set the index to 1
(or move 1 to the subscript).  Then, you load a table from some external source,
and check as you load that the index or subscript doesn't exceed the table
limit.  The index (or subscript) gets incremented by 1.  When you have finished
loading, the index (or subscript) is one higher than the number of entries in
the table.  You set the index down by 1 (or subtract 1 from the index), and use
that to put a value in the object of the ODO.  From then on, you are set to use
the table, until such time as you may want to add more entries or delete an
entry.

There is a bit of run time checking of the ODO object, but I think in an IBM
COBOL implementation, this is done when the object is changed (so that isn't
often at all).  The object's value probably gets passed to run time subroutines
for SEARCH and the like.

I just know it works a heck of a lot better than filling a table with HIGH-VALUE
and then loading over 10% of that table, then doing a SEARCH ALL where the first
two or three looks are at "fake" entries.

Chuck Stevens wrote:

> Hmm.  I would caution that ODO entails more range checking for reliability.
> Not only must you (or the compiler) ensure as for "normal" tables that the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens
```

<postingid@dissensoftware.com> enlightened us:

>On 25 Feb 2003 11:06:13 -0800 alexmcshane@blueyonder.co.uk (Alex McShane)
>wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Why aren't they already sorted?

Beat me to the punch there.  Having been a CICS system programmer and
a VSE/VM system programmer before seeing the errors of my ways and
going back to application programming, the one thing I was always told
by those that knew, never, ever sort in a CICS environment.  Now,
under the newer operating systems and CICS, it is more tolerable of a
sort, but still not recommended.  Sort is best left to batch.

Regards,

         (o o)

without your accordian."  - Donald Rumsfeld / US Secretary of Defense

Steve

<a id="t-d15df0fa54"></a>
## Thread: COBOL indexes — 2 msgs

### Re: COBOL indexes

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T14:44:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3grl5$qce$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
I wrote:

> Indeed, I don't see just a whole bunch of unique functionality associated
> with USAGE INDEX data items offhand; when they are used to reference table
> entries, they function as subscripts, not as indices.

Actually, I'll take that back.  I do see significant functionality.

USAGE INDEX can I think reasonably be assumed to be implemented in a manner
that represents a given implementation's MOST EFFICIENT way of storing and
retrieving an occurrence number, and I'd further expect the implementor's
definition of "most efficient" to be in terms of processor time consumption
during execution.    All that's required of it is that it represent an
occurrence number; the rest is left up to the implementor.

To use a *reductio ad absurdum*:   An implementor COULD choose something
like
    01  USAGE-INDEX-STYLE USAGE DISPLAY PICTURE ***,***,***,*99CR.
as an appropriate model for storing indices.  It's even COBOL85 compatible
because of the de-editing features of COBOL85.  I would, however, contend
that would be a *bad* choice.

In the case of Unisys MCP/AS systems,
    77  USAGE-INDEX-STYLE USAGE BINARY EXTENDED PIC S9(11). *< or even PIC
S9(23)
would be a MUCH BETTER choice, and comes really close to how it's actually
implemented.  But such a declaration would not be portable to, or meaningful
on, anything but another Unisys MCP/AS system.  It'd be worthless on a V
Series, or a 2200, or an IBM system, or a PC, or a Unix box.

If the programmer has coded USAGE INDEX, we are free to use the above
format, IBM is free to use whatever's best for it, and so on.  A
standards-compliant program would be portable -- even in how it uses the
USAGE INDEX item, so long as it's not, say, in a file somewhere -- to any
other machine with a standards-compliant compiler, and be relatively
efficient (in this respect) on any of them.

That doesn't *guarantee* the most efficient implementation, but it does
suggest that a USAGE INDEX item is *most likely* to be more efficient than
"generic numeric", and that any significantly greater efficiency would
require the use of an "implementor extension" USAGE for that purpose.
It's portable because it's required by the standard, and no aspect of its
use places any specific format on it.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T09:14:49+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.hax68p0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`
- **References:** `<b3grl5$qce$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Tue, 25 Feb 2003 14:44:54 -0800, Chuck Stevens wrote:

>I wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In this discussion I am missing a very important argument in favor of
indexing.
An index belongs to one particular table and the compiler guarantees that it
will not be used for other purposes.
Am I the only one who has tried to find out why  and where a certain
subscript field was changed?

The performance issue is also very clear to me. Indexing will never be slower
than subscripting and takes the same time to code, so  I use indexing.
Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T13:22:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5cbe97.18507712@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com>`

```
If you are converting to Fujitsu, you may not need that .dll. Ordinary Micro
Focus indexed files store the data as ASCII text delimited by cr-lf. You can
sort them with a utility and load into Fujitsu files. However, if they use
EXTFH, they are in a proprietary format. MOST Micro Focus files are ordinary.

Robert


Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>I am trying to install Power 7 ( i have already use 6.1)
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Posted via http://dbforums.com

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T11:42:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302261142.3a10eded@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com> <3e5cbe97.18507712@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> If you are converting to Fujitsu, you may not need that .dll. Ordinary Micro
> Focus indexed files store the data as ASCII text delimited by cr-lf. 

Is this also something you 'learnt' decades ago and never updated ?

MF Level II Cobol (circa early 80s) indexed files used a fixed record
size file for the data. This was roughly based on C-ISAM format.

However it had no restrictions on containing binary, packed, or any
other data and _cannot_ be treated as if it were ASCII text.  Valid
records were terminated by cr/lf but deleted records were marked with
a null byte over the cr.  The file was extended by clusters and there
may also be slack space at the end where records have not been written
yet.

In the mid-late 80s MF Cobol 2 introduced a better indexed file
format, but also could use old Level II files.  The new format catered
for variable length, compression, and various other improvements. 
This file structure had a file header and a header for each record
that gave the length.

> You can sort them with a utility 

Extremely poor advice.  Simply wrong in most cases.

> and load into Fujitsu files. 

There was no indication that he even _wanted_ to load into Fujitsu
files, he may well want to share files with a MF system.

> However, if they use EXTFH, they are in a proprietary format. 

No. Wrong. Misleading.  Cobol/2 (mid 80s) uses EXTFH for all access to
MF indexed files regardless of whether they are Level II format or
Cobol/2 format.

> MOST Micro Focus files are ordinary.

They may have all been Level II format two decades ago, but even these
weren't 'ordinary' in any way due to binary or packed data, deleted
records, slack space.
```

riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

It is something I work with every day on my (almost) current Micro Focus
compiler. A plain vanilla indexed file uses this format. One must specify EXTFH
to get the External File Handler. Only one file in this shop uses EXTFH.

>MF Level II Cobol (circa early 80s) indexed files used a fixed record
>size file for the data. This was roughly based on C-ISAM format.
>
>However it had no restrictions on containing binary, packed, or any
>other data and _cannot_ be treated as if it were ASCII text.

Which is why my Best Practices says to store numbers in files in display format.


>  Valid
>records were terminated by cr/lf but deleted records were marked with
>a null byte over the cr.  

Any editor worth its salt will interpret 0A as a line terminator. 

>In the mid-late 80s MF Cobol 2 introduced a better indexed file
>format, but also could use old Level II files.  The new format catered
>for variable length, compression, and various other improvements. 
>This file structure had a file header and a header for each record
>that gave the length.

Probably so, but it did not become the default format. 

>There was no indication that he even _wanted_ to load into Fujitsu
>files, he may well want to share files with a MF system.

That's why I began "If you are converting..". It sounded to me like he was.

>> MOST Micro Focus files are ordinary.
>
>They may have all been Level II format two decades ago, but even these
>weren't 'ordinary' in any way due to binary or packed data, deleted
>records, slack space.

Most Micro Focus files are what you call Level II TODAY. What does slack space
have to do with anything?

Robert Wagner wrote:

> >> If you are converting to Fujitsu, you may not need that .dll. Ordinary Micro
> >> Focus indexed files store the data as ASCII text delimited by cr-lf.
> >
> >Is this also something you 'learnt' decades ago and never updated ?
>

Richard originally jumped in on this one with historical background. Exactly which
is your 'almost current' M/F compiler. I just didn't understand your reference to '
indexed files store the data as ASCII text delimited by cr-lf'.

Back to the DOS V 3.0 and then VISOC and Net Express - if I use an old PC Magazine
routine 'DR', (in a DOS Window), I can look at any file, (COBOL or otherwise). But
any ISAM, Sequential or Relative that has comp fields just shows up as a series of
gobbledegook characters - not dissimilar to the Rosetta stone. Naturally text fields
are readable.

When displaying the above three types they just display across the screen with
wrap-around - no intervening space indicating CR/LF. It is only Line Sequential that
'jumps' to a newline triggered by the CR/LF.

And so far as using that Fujitsu support DLL, the original enquirer must have been
referring to very current M/F formats. Sure, I have used the M/F Convert3 tool to
move RM/COBOL ISAMs to M/F - but that was the old RM/COBOL '74 V 2.0. Not worth
tiddly squat with the latest RM file formats.

> >However it had no restrictions on containing binary, packed, or any
> >other data and _cannot_ be treated as if it were ASCII text.
>
> Which is why my Best Practices says to store numbers in files in display format.
>

Now - there's a statement a couple of old hands here will agree with you on. Not me,
I'm much more interested in design than programming and bits and bytes. But based on
what they have previously written, I go along with the advantages that they
highlighted.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T22:27:27-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3mofi$3a$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com> <3e5cbe97.18507712@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302261142.3a10eded@posting.google.com> <3e5ead7e.97103717@news.optonline.net> <3E5ED99F.F42D96B0@shaw.ca>`

```
Let me jump into this thread and give you the information that when EXTFH is
and is not used - and what the "default" file handling facility is depends
(or at least did when I last checked) VERY MUCH on whether you are using a
Micro Focus "Unix-type" or "DOS/Windows-type" compiler.  For further
information on "Object COBOL 4.1.40 for UNIX" usage, see:

http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/ocds4140/oc41indx.htm

and look (for example) at:

"10.2 Using the Callable File Handler"

  which says in part,

"Your programs may already be using the Callable File Handler. If your COBOL
program uses Micro Focus COBOL syntax for all its file handling, the
Callable File Handler is used in the following cases:

 - If the CALLFH Compiler directive is used, all COBOL I/O statements are
compiled into calls to ExtFH, or to a file handler of your own, if
specified.

 - 32-bit:
On 32-bit systems, all COBOL I/O statements are compiled into calls to
ExtFH.

 - 16-bit:
On 16-bit systems, if you do not set the CALLFH Compiler directive, ExtFH is
used as follows:
 - ExtFH is called by the run-time system to handle I/O operations on all
indexed files.
 -The Compiler puts direct calls to ExtFH in your compiled code to handle
I/O operations on files of all organizations that have the ANSI'85 attribute
of EXTERNAL. "

and also of "interest" is

"10.1.3 Supported Files"

which says,

"In addition to the standard COBOL organizations, the following file types
are also supported:
 - LEVEL II COBOL
 - IDXFORMAT"4"
 - Btrieve format with ANSI emulation
 - Btrieve format without ANSI emulation
- C-ISAM (UNIX and 32-bit OS/2)"

    ***

Therefore, to understand which "file system" is being used when one says
"Micro Focus" - one needs to know

  - the platform (operating system)
 - the release of the compiler
 - what directives were used
 - whether "standard" COBOl syntax or other mechanism were used to access
the files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T12:39:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0302281239.39e3ccd9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com> <3e5cbe97.18507712@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302261142.3a10eded@posting.google.com> <3e5ead7e.97103717@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> It is something I work with every day on my (almost) current Micro Focus
> compiler. A plain vanilla indexed file uses this format. One must specify EXTFH
> to get the External File Handler. Only one file in this shop uses EXTFH.

No. That is wrong. There is _NO_ compiler option 'EXTFH'. If the
system is DOS or Windows or OS/2 and is MF Cobol later than the
mid-80s the it _WILL_ be using EXTFH for indexed files.

Ypou may be confused by the CALLFH compiler option which allows you to
specify a _different_ external file handler. If you do not use a
non-MF file handler, such as B-Trieve, then you _will_ be using EXTFH
whether you know it or not, whether the CALLFH is specified or not.

> Which is why my Best Practices says to store numbers in files in display format.

It may well be that you self impose this restriction on yourself, but
you advised others that the indexed file _WILL_ be ASCII characters
and you have no means of knowing whether this is true or not.

> Any editor worth its salt will interpret 0A as a line terminator. 

Exactly.  And deleted records will be brought back to life again.  If,
however, the delete marker is _not_ treated as a line terminator the
record after the deleted record will be appended to the deleted record
and if this is trimmed the deleted record becomes live and a valid
record is lost.

If the record contains binary or packed data (on which there is no
actual restriction) the data may cause many problems.

> Probably so, but it did not become the default format. 

Yes it did.  See the IDXFORMAT compiler option.  The default has been
'current system' since mid-late-80s.  It requires IDXFORMAT'2' to
create Level II format files, which are the closest to your
'ordinary', though still unusable in the way you suggested.

The IDXFORMAT applies when a file is created, so the program will
still use files created by earlier systems in the format they have.
 
> That's why I began "If you are converting..". It sounded to me like he was.

Do you think so ?  It didn't sound like he wanted to convert to me, he
was quite specific that he wanted "to read, write etc  a Microfocus
cobol File,"

> >> MOST Micro Focus files are ordinary.
> 
> Most Micro Focus files are what you call Level II TODAY. 

What surveys have you done to determine this ?  Given that it has been
the default for nearly two decades to _not_ use Level II then your
assertion is simply bogus.

> What does slack space have to do with anything?

You are supposed to understand these things before giving advice to
others.

When a new record is to be written and there is no space for it an
arbitrary amount of disk space is added to the file, sufficient for
several records and the one record is written somewhere in there.

When the file is read as record sequential then the unwritten parts of
that extended space are read as junk records.  Even if they get
ignored they may affect the alignment of any good records there.

MF indexed files are _not_ ordinary files and cannot be treated as
such even when they are the old Level II and even if they are only
ASCII data.
I think that you have excelled even your own best in this thread in
that _every_ point that you made was factually wrong, misleading, bad
advice or indicated that you had a complete lack of understanding of
the issues.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T21:36:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0302262136.604a8d58@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com>`

```
Carlos - chapter 7 of the Windows users guide tells how to do this.

You really only need do this:

assign..... "C:\xxxxx\nameoffile.dat,EXFH"

Then in your COBOL85.CBR add these lines:

@CBR_EXFH_API=MFFH
@CBR_EXFH_LOAD=MFFH.DLL

You might have to change the API to EXTFHSUB - I've not got it working
yet because I don't have the NetExpress runtime properly installed on
the machine I first tested on.  I'll check another - but this might
get you started.  Please report your progress here!


Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com> wrote in message news:<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com>...
> I am trying to install Power 7 ( i have already use 6.1)
> In documetatios fujtisu says that  its possible
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Carlos Lages
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5D0D45.15D790C6@shaw.ca>`

```


DeLaPena wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com

While possible, using the Micro Focus Screen Section - you really are
taking on a big challenge to try and emulate a scrollbar. Years ago I did
use the Screen Section to create a Menu Bar and Pop-Up menus - but
honestly, would suggest that's a bit of a toughie for beginners.

E-mail me if interested - I can give you a zipfile of some M/F DOS programs
that illustrate using Screen Section coupled with the Mouse. I also have a
second 'junk' zipfile which has some of my old Screen stuff - if you want
to wade your way through these, you might also get some ideas.

You didn't say which compiler - the DOS version 3.0 ??? Doubt you can get
your hands on a manual - but try the on-line books at the Micro Focus site.
Although now geared to Net Express etc., the appropriate manuals do include
all the Screen syntax plus use of ADIS(AcceptDISplay), directives etc.,
which you will need.

http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/nx31sp1/nx31indx.htm

The book you need is "Character User Interfaces'

Jimmy, Calgary AB

## Thread: COBOL Source for ISBN #: 0-13-081513-6 — 3 msgs

### COBOL Source for ISBN #: 0-13-081513-6

- **From:** "The_Master" <webmastermc@DELETE_MEiowatelecom.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-25T18:53:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3h36r$jdt$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```
Anyone know where I can grab the COBOL source for the afore-mentioned book?
Janelle Schneider sent me this book so I can help teach my small company
(Master WEB Design) some COBOL.

0-13-790817-2
and it's been lots of years since I had it in school!
(Note: am 44 now ...)
-----------------------------------
So any help would be appreciated.
-----------------------------------
Thanks.

pty0 wrote:

> Reports only in Fujitsu Cobol Files (indexed )
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

If you are specifically asking can I get reports from Fujitsu ISAM files - Yes.

Regardless of tool, COBOL Report Writer, Flexus SP2, or Crystal etc., - you have
to read in your ISAM records, (usually sequentially, or even against an
Alternate Key, but again sequentially) and fit the data into a template or
'mask', representing your print lines, in the appropriate tool.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

supplied with it and it is easy to use.
There is a consideration: It depends on what you need...If you use Sql
or Access database and you want to connect them to a report, Crystal
Report is the best solution..If you use native indexed cobol files,
Powerform is better, because you can design the print form easily.
Fujitsu has announced that in their last version (v7.0) is added a new
features to obtain the preview of print forms generated with Powerform.


Hope in this help.

Gianni

<a id="t-4a3ffbfbd1"></a>
## Thread: VMS Cobol COPY LIBRARY — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### VMS Cobol COPY LIBRARY

- **From:** samuli.raikkonen@enfo.fi (Sam)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T04:41:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1668a2c6.0302260441.b5c9112@posting.google.com>`

```
Hullo!
Just a simple - yes, a dumb and simple - question:

How can I tell VMS COBOL where to find the COPY LIBRARIES? I decided
against using a library and chose to use separate text-files instead
(suits our purpose better at this stage), and I'd like to put all the
separate text-files to a single directory. How can I tell the COBOL
compiler where these files are located?

So what to do without much ado?

- Thanks in advance, Sam
```

#### ↳ Re: VMS Cobol COPY LIBRARY

- **From:** Alain Chappuis <Alain.Chappuis@medecine.unige.ch>
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T08:31:41+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5dbe45@nntp.unige.ch>`
- **References:** `<1668a2c6.0302260441.b5c9112@posting.google.com>`

```
Sam a ï¿½crit:
> Hullo!
> Just a simple - yes, a dumb and simple - question:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> compiler where these files are located?


COPY "LIBRARY-FILE" FROM COPYLIB.

The COPY Statement, Dictionaries and Libraries

Including the COPY statement in your program allows separate programs to 
share common source text, reducing development and testing time as well 
as storage requirements. You can use the COPY statement to access 
modules in libraries. The COPY statement causes the compiler to read the 
file or module specified during the compilation of a program. When the 
compiler reaches the end of the included text, it resumes reading from 
the previous input file.

By using the /INCLUDE qualifier on the COBOL command line, you can set 
up a search list for files specified by the COPY statement. For more 
information, see the Compaq COBOL Reference Manual.

You can use the COPY FROM DICTIONARY statement in your program to access 
a data dictionary and copy Oracle CDD/Repository record descriptions 
into your program as COBOL record descriptions. Before you can copy 
record descriptions from Oracle CDD/Repository, you must create the 
record descriptions using the Common Data Dictionary Language (CDDL) or 
Common Dictionary Operator (CDO).

For more information about using Oracle CDD/Repository and creating and 
maintaining text libraries, refer to the Compaq COBOL Reference Manual 
and Using Oracle CDD/Repository on OpenVMS Systems.

AC
```

re compillers:
    for a good compiller, it will cost a small amount of money (relative to
value you will get).  $50 or $ 100 aprox depending on if you are a proper
student.
    I am still using for most part, the Micro Focus Personal Cobol (DOS)
compiller, but also have the windows version of this compiller.  If you use
both dos and windows MF compilers, then, you have to run a batch file to set
environment variables depending on if previously set to windows or dos. If you
dont use the simple batch files, then the DOS compiller will not work. Its just
part of the deal, I guess.
  Compiller comes with documentation.
  Or for free you can get cobol650 but not as good.  There is also a realia
classroom cobol compiller. (old one).

```
  Hullo!
  Just a simple - yes, a dumb and simple - question:

  How can I tell VMS COBOL where to find the COPY LIBRARIES? I decided
against using a library and chose to use separate text-files instead (suits
our purpose better at this stage), and I'd like to put all the separate
text-files to a single directory. How can I tell the COBOL compiler where
these files are located?

  So what to do without much ado?

  - Thanks in advance, Sam
```

## Thread: Plug: txt2pdf 6.4 — 6 msgs

### Plug: txt2pdf 6.4

- **From:** "Sanface Software" <sanface@sanface.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T15:38:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f1accd68b74080cf798f3c1ca6daccae.93245@mygate.mailgate.org>`

```
We would like to announce txt2pdf 6.4 version. 
http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
txt2pdf is shareware; it is a very flexible and powerful Perl5 script
that converts text files to PDF format files, so you can use it in every
operating systems supported by Perl5, including MPE/iX, OpenVMS and
EPOC.
It's simple to design background like invoices, orders etc.
Here nice examples made using txt2pdf PRO
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/Purchase_Order.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/invoice.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/hfmus.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/heraldbill.pdf
If you prefer we also distribute executables for Windows, Linux,
Solaris, AIX, HP-UX, and Mac OS X. Inside the Windows version is Visual
txt2pdf, a VB GUI.
Remember to read the Hachette Filipacchi Media U.S. project at
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/?AURL=%2FArticles%2FV%2FV40603%2Easp

What's new in this version

inputmod and outputmod features to make plug-ins
Visual txt2pdf 3.0

Test txt2pdf 6.4!
You can find it at http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
```

jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:


>> Adobe makes it easy for you to get IN, nearly impossible to get OUT. 
>> 
>> There is a company selling a product called pdf2txt which liberates you from
>> pdf. It costs $300 per seat. It's worth the price. I have a 'paid for' copy
on
>> my desktop.
>
>Cntl-A in PDF document followed by Cntl-V in text editor...

That's how we used to do it. We're in the business of normalizing THOUSANDS of
user reports into a database. Doing them one at a time is labor-intensive.
Pdf2txt lets us automate the process. 

For some reason, most Canadian mutual funds report their holdings in pdf whereas
US funds do not, preferring to come through Edgar in ASCII.
```

## Thread: NetExpress and HTML — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### NetExpress and HTML

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T12:03:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0302261203.425da5d2@posting.google.com>`

```
We've just got our first MF NetExpress (3.1) program working using
HTML. We're using Accept to read from the POST and using "extended
DISPLAY" with substitutions (lots of %) in the HTML file. It all seems
to work...

The query is about the size of the fields in the data division entries
for the EXERNAL-FORM structures. For the ACCEPTed EXTERNAL-FORM when I
set the PICs to the actual required size (same as maxlen) I seemed to
get memory allocation errors (certainly while Animating). Making the
sizes very generous it all seemed fine. Was this a real problem (it
could have been some other problem aggravated by Animator...)? And if
so is there some way of determining what the correct size should be?
(If this is a problem I wondered whether it may be related to the
POST's "name=value" strings)

graham
Olympic Computers
```

---

<a id="t-d196a767dd"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress and XML — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### NetExpress and XML

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T12:14:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0302261214.7dfb3ccd@posting.google.com>`

```
Further to my previous posting - we're no using HTML and it's fine BUT
it would be nice if we could use XML (with some XSL etc.).

Does the "extended DISPLAY" do things that mean that only HTML is
supported ? I gather from the documentation that it puts on headers
etc. for the web server. It would be rather nice if we could simply
get an XML record and pass that to the browser using XSLt etc to
generate the real output structure. As I mentioned we're using the
substitution features of the DISPLAY to do this and, in theory, we
could do the same in an XML document.

Alternatively we could create an XML document complete with all real
values and then use DISPLAY in some form. So would DISPLAY work either
on the XML file (as an extended DISPLAY EXERNAL-FORM); or reading from
the XML file and DISPLAYing it, either in bulk or in "records".

Any comments ?

graham 
Olympic Computers
```

## Thread: To "killfile" or not to "killfile" — 24 this month / 43 total

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### To "killfile" or not to "killfile"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T14:44:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3j8vq$j46$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Requesting input from other regular (and not so regular) participants in CLC
(but NOT from "you know who")

I am trying to decide whether to add a certain "recent" participant in this
NG to my killfile (for this newsgroup).

He is SO OFTEN wrong, that I am starting to get upset (when not bored).  I
have absolutely NO problem with his (or anyone else) submitting posts with
their opinions - when indicated as opinions - preferably with some "reasons"
behind them.  However, this person seems to FREQUENTLY make "absolute"
statements about what the Standard says or requires (current or past
Standard) - and/or comments about what "all" (or even most) compilers due -
which are simply NOT true for currently supported compilers.

On one hand, adding this person to my "killfile" would relieve my stress
level.

On the other hand, some of his "absolute" statements of fact (that are
simply in error as facts) might "slip thru" with no one bothering to correct
him.  This could easily lead to some "poor unexpecting" novice COBOL
programmer finding the post (either in the current NG or in its archives)
and getting wrong information.

So the question is:

Do others think that I should simply add this person to my killfile and
"ignore" his posts - or should I keep an eye out for his latest error(s) and
post corrections?  Obviously, if he continues posting erroneous "facts" (not
opinions) and others post replies - I will continue to monitor the replies
(whatever the original post says).

What are others doing?  What do you recommend?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: To "killfile" or not to "killfile"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T10:06:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3l9i3$25h$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b3j8vq$j46$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3E5D2B5D.61F2CF57@certcoinc.com> <3E5D8134.1020007@Sympatico.ca> <3E5E0F4A.4AC160A9@certcoinc.com>`

```
In article <3E5E0F4A.4AC160A9@certcoinc.com>,
Pat Hall  <phall@certcoinc.com> wrote:
>Some day some one will "smote" you for gloating like that.

Normally I don't comment much on grammar... but the examples (some from
http://www.dictionary.com) that can be dredged up for this'un are too good
to keep to myself.

Small nit: 'smite' is a fine, old word, a 'strong' verb with irregular 
inflections; 'smote' is the past tense/participle and a future use 
('will...') should take the uninflected form.  Consider:

And David . . . took thence a stone, and slang it, and smote the 
Philistine in his forehead. --1 Sam. xvii. 49.

(wow... not only a 'smote' but a 'slang', too!)

Saul . . . smote the javelin into the wall. --1 Sam. xix. 10.

And if thou refuse to let them go, behold, I will smite all thy borders 
with frogs:  --Ex. viii. 2.

The LORD will smite thee with the botch of Egypt, and with the emerods, 
and with the scab, and with the itch, whereof thou canst not be healed.  
--Deut. xxviii. 27.

(note 'will smite')

This all was triggered, actually, by my remembering a bit of Chaucer which 
used an even more archaic form in 'The Miller's Tale':

And he was redy with his iren hoot,
And Nicholas amydde the ers he smoot. 

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: To "killfile" or not to "killfile"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Pat Hall <phall@certcoinc.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T09:32:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5E2FA9.336E130B@certcoinc.com>`

```
I think I was using the Philistine reference.  As in some one  will 'smote'
him up side the head with a cue ball but perhaps I should have said 'smite'
as in I hope some one 'smites' you upside the head with a cue ball.
Smote/smite  I guess I should have used the universal "Whup'  as in I hope
some one 'Whups' you upside the head with a cue ball.  Thanks for the lesson
in grammar Doc.

PatH..who has been smote, smitten and whupped

#### ↳ Re: To "killfile" or not to "killfile"

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T21:17:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3jasp$6d0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3j8vq$j46$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 26-Feb-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> On one hand, adding this person to my "killfile" would relieve my stress
> level.

> On the other hand, some of his "absolute" statements of fact (that are
> simply in error as facts) might "slip thru" with no one bothering to correct
> him.  This could easily lead to some "poor unexpecting" novice COBOL
> programmer finding the post (either in the current NG or in its archives)
> and getting wrong information.



Sometimes one can make a decision that this will simply be a job that needs
doing.  Once that decision is made, you can get a certain zen where it doesn't
effect you.   Sort of like changing your baby's diapers - it's accepted as part
of the job of a parent (or grandparent).

I have a friend who found this kind of peace when he became a contractor. 
Somehow he no longer felt upset when his boss told him to program the boss's
way.  When he was an employee, he got uptight about doing it "right" even if
politics said to do it differently.   But not anymore.

If you wish to continue to quote facts in response to these statements, by all
means do so - but for the sake of your health, address it as a simple task and
learn to let it not bother you.
```

and source code for all to see and criticize.  I for one would like to see
more source code and such posted.  Sometimes he takes it well and sometimes
he doesn't. Some of his rebuttals are weak but some are thought provoking.

about mainframe programmers. His insistance that only his own terminology
and definitions are correct is irritating.
He seems to think he can change the world to his views.

You can't change him and I would not even try.  He is the only one that can
change himself. 

FWIW I have enjoyed the lively discussions and I personally appreciate your
and others  contibutions and corrections, especially as your knowledge of
the COBOL standards is much greater than mine.  I personally won't take the
time to correct all of his errors or even to dispute all opinions that I
disagree with.  I will pick my places as I prefer to use my time on other
pursuits. Everyone giving advice is occassionally mistaken and newbies will
find out when given advice is wrong the same way as we all do, by trying it
out in the real world.

Only you know how much all of this affects you personally.  I say do
whatever you feel in your gut is best for you personally.  Maybe if he
posts his fantastic "grep" program and his "radix/quicksort" and they are
any good it will make up for some of the "hot buttons" that he is trying to
press.

<a id="t-5c80e21014"></a>
## Thread: Interesting change in "bounds checking" — 22 this month / 51 total

### Interesting change in "bounds checking"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T15:03:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow-up on another thread, I thought that I would point out what may
turn out to be a MAJOR inhibitor for some shops (with some compilers) in
upgrading from an '85 Standard compiler (with an extension) to a requirement
of the new 2002 Standard.

The issue arises for 2 or more dimensional tables.

With all the compilers that I know about (IBM, Micro Focus, Fujitsu, Realia)
when their "extension" (or more accurately "debugging tool") boundary
checking is turned on, then one ONLY gets a "boundary violation"
notification for a 2-thru-N dimensional table if the COMBINATION of
subscripts (or indexes) references storage totally OUTSIDE the storage of
the "full table".

For example,

  01  Full-Table
        05  Level-1 occurs 10 times indexed by Ind1.
              10  Level-2 occurs 100 times indexed by Ind2.
                    15  AnElem   Pic X.
  ....
   Move AnElem (1 101) to Other-Field.

will *not* receive any "boundary violation" message (with the products that
I know of).

ON THE OTHER HAND,  the 2002 Standard "requires" that an

    EC-BOUND-SUBSCRIPT

exception condition be set.  See page 87, rule 2 that says (in part),

"The highest permissible occurrence number for any given dimension of the
table is the maximum number of occurrences of the item as specified in the
associated OCCURS clause. If the value of the subscript is not a positive
integer or is less than one or greater than the highest permissible
occurrence number, the EC-BOUND-SUBSCRIPT exception condition is set to
exist."

I can EASILY imagine that this difference - when actually introduced and
implemented by some vendors - may come as a fairly UNWANTED surprise to many
programmers and shops.  Obviously, vendors will be able to continue to
support the "older" behavior as a NON-conforming option - but when run in
"strictly conforming" mode, the new behavior will be required.
```

#### ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T21:19:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3jb1b$6d5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 26-Feb-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> I can EASILY imagine that this difference - when actually introduced and
> implemented by some vendors - may come as a fairly UNWANTED surprise to many
> programmers and shops.  Obviously, vendors will be able to continue to
> support the "older" behavior as a NON-conforming option - but when run in
> "strictly conforming" mode, the new behavior will be required.

Interesting.  I have seen programs that require the old option.  But that's not
(IMHO), the way CoBOL should be.
```

#### ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-26T13:19:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...

> The issue arises for 2 or more dimensional tables.
>
> With all the compilers that I know about (IBM, Micro Focus, Fujitsu,
Realia)
> when their "extension" (or more accurately "debugging tool") boundary
> checking is turned on, then one ONLY gets a "boundary violation"
> notification for a 2-thru-N dimensional table if the COMBINATION of
> subscripts (or indexes) references storage totally OUTSIDE the storage of
> the "full table".

Not true for Unisys MCP/AS COBOL85.  If bounds checking is not explicitly
disabled, on access to the table element each subscript or index is checked,
and a fault ("assertion failure on range test") occurs if any subscript is
out of range.

For MCP/AS COBOL74, you don't (and can't) get bounds checking for indices
anyway; the hardware prevents access outside the 01-level record containing
the table, but otherwise that record's fair game.   Subscripted access is as
for COBOL85 unless range checking is explicitly disabled (fault text is
"invalid index").

I used a four-dimensional table for my minimal test.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Who remembers "BUNCH" (was: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T14:51:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ltop$trv$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <b3lpmj$14oq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3lsjd$76b$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
I am pretty certain that Docdwarf was "joking" in his original question -
and that Chuck responded courteously (and knew it was a joke) and that DD
continued "politely".

Having said that, I did want to revisit "ancient" history a bit.  I suspect
many of those reading this newsgroup remember the days when the "data
processing" world was divided into IBM and everyone else (aka "BUNCH" - an
acronym of the various "other" vendors' names - including both of the
companies that EVENTUALLY became "Unisys").

In "ye goode olde dayse", it was medium common for IBM to do what they
wanted (conforming or not conforming to "the Standard" - COBOL or otherwise)
and have everyone copy them (because that is what SOME - not all - customers
thought "must" be done.)

Furthermore, when the "PC" world became more popular, MOST (again not all)
of the COBOL implementations had as a partial (often MAJOR) customer base
those who were doing IBM mainframe COBOL development.

Given these two facts ("follow-the-leader" for SOME features by "BUNCH"
vendors *and* PC "IBM mainframe emulation")  it is not too surprising that
some IBM mainframe COBOL compiler extensions (or even violations of the
Standard) became "de facto" Standards for some (still not all) COBOL
development.  The following are just a few "things" that many programmers
think are almost universal - but which are definitely NOT "Standard" (at
least thru the '85 Standard).

 - "single quote" (aka APOST) for alphanumeric literal delimitation (and/or
figurative constant QUOTE)
 - Comp-3 meaning Packed-Decimal
 - GOBACK being equivalent to EXIT PROGRAM if appropriate, STOP RUN
otherwise
 - REDEFINES of other than the "1st" entry in a series of REDEFINES
 - ENTRY statement (not to be confused with the '85 Standard OBSOLETE
"ENTER" statement)
 - Between column 72 and 73 being the R-margin (but allowing comments in
columns 73-80)

Chuck and others from non-IBM and non-IBM "emulation" environments, please
excuse me if some of these really DID get introduced by some other vendor
than IBM before IBM did them.

But all of this "boils down" to a semi-serious answer to DD's originally
(humorous) question

  "Mr Stevens, so much seems to be not true for/inapplicable to/shown to be
an exception by this Unisys MPC/AS  compiler that I feel it might be wise to
ask...

    ... are you sure it's COBOL?"

(Obviously, this also applies to Karl and Rosa's comments on the Siemens
implementation.)

The answer is that many (originally IBM) extensions "seem" to be a universal
part of COBOL - but only to those using features from "early" days of IBM
domination or from compilers explicitly targeting IBM COBOL development.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who remembers "BUNCH" (was: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T21:52:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lMv7a.73639$zF6.5171952@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <b3lpmj$14oq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3lsjd$76b$1@panix1.panix.com> <b3ltop$trv$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:b3ltop$trv$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
> 
> In "ye goode olde dayse", it was medium common for IBM to do what they
> wanted (conforming or not conforming to "the Standard" - COBOL or otherwise)
> and have everyone copy them (because that is what SOME - not all - customers
> thought "must" be done.)

I'd remove the "medium".  The phrase "The 800-pound gorilla" comes to mind.

IBM had the customer base.  The rest had to stay as close as possible
to being IBM-compatible in order to have any hope of wooing customers 
out from under the IBM "umbrella", as it was called.

If a 2nd or 3rd tier company could not lay claim to a relatively painless
migration from IBM to their own proprietary hardware, they were dead.
Many did die, or were assimilated, as the cost of keeping up proved too high.

It was true then, and it is true today.  

The world doesn't need or want a cornucopia of Operating Systems or Hardware Architectures.

IBM, and subsequently Intel and Microsoft, proved that fact, beyond debate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who remembers "BUNCH" (was: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-27T14:45:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3m4en$1c26$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <b3lpmj$14oq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3lsjd$76b$1@panix1.panix.com> <b3ltop$trv$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Yes, I definitely remember BUNCH.  I joined the Burroughs field support
organization while all five companies were still active in the marketplace.

Bill wrote:

> The following are just a few "things" that many programmers think are
almost universal -
> but which are definitely NOT "Standard" (at least thru the '85 Standard).
>  ...
> - Between column 72 and 73 being the R-margin (but allowing comments in
> columns 73-80)

This is the only one of your list that I see our implementations allowing.
I see *some* indications that this might have been a feature of First
Standard COBOL, based on the fact that I have usually found the IBM DOS and
Burroughs Large Systems COBOL (68) reference manuals taken in combination to
be pretty reliable indicators of what's standard and what's not.  Both show
the location of Margin R as fixed at Column 72, don't indicate that it's an
extension, and indicate either that the area following is used for program
identification (IBM) or that it may be used for anything the user desires
(Burroughs).

This leads me to believe that allowing the source code to "leak over" beyond
Column 72 was something people didn't expect to do, and that in earlier
COBOL (either by convention or by fiat) compilable code stopped there.

The last printed version of what started life as the Burroughs Large System
COBOL manual states, under the general introduction to the discussion of the
coding form "The format of the COBOL coding form has been defined by
CODASYL, ANSI, and common usage.  The A Series COBOL compiler accepts this
standard format.", which does indicate some possibility that this particular
margin, and the conventional use of what followed it, were "common practice"
rather than mandated by the standard.

I suspect these columns have been used for "patch marks" indicating program
change history since the very earliest days of external COBOL source file
management (merging of source changes into disk or tape master files by
sequence number) on Burroughs systems (Medium Systems "SOLT" and "SOLD"
files, and probably later, Large System SYSTEM/PATCH), but I can't say that
IBM's expressed use of these fields for program identification predates or
postdates that use.

The other features Bill mentions are not things our users have requested,
for IBM compatibility or otherwise, of Unisys, or of Burroughs before the
merger.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who remembers "BUNCH" (was: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T00:14:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E5EAA04.9FE9A8B2@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <b3lpmj$14oq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3lsjd$76b$1@panix1.panix.com> <b3ltop$trv$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <b3m4en$1c26$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```


Chuck Stevens wrote:

> Yes, I definitely remember BUNCH.  I joined the Burroughs field support
> organization while all five companies were still active in the marketplace.
>

While you folks are wandering down Memory Lane - which is absolutely
fascinating, care to comment on what we now call ISAM.

Purely from memory, so my figures and descriptions might be inaccurate. RCA (and
I note they don't appear in BUNCH above - or do they ?), produced their MCF
(Magnetic Card File) around the same time as NCR's CRAM - this just before the
introduction of the first IBM hard disk drives.

MCF was a big beastie say about 3 feet x 10 feet, standing at table level. One
unit had 8 magazines each containing 256 magnetic cards. Each card held 166,400
characters (I hope that's correct). So 166,400 x 256 x 8 - not bad storage for
mid sixties.

Programmatically, (and originally using either Assembler or perhaps some
proprietary RCA tool), cards were allocated for a particular indexed file. Each
card could be designated in 'buckets' (what I suppose we now call Blocks) in
sizes of 128, 256, 512 or 1024 per file. So given that you knew the size of the
records you were going to store, you picked the 'best' bucket size, and
allocated a set of cards to hold your file and anticipate expansion.. (Note -
records could not spill over into the next bucket).

In addition a separate area on the cards was allocated for the index. Depending
upon on your PrimeKey size, again you picked the appropriate bucket size. (Can't
be sure, but doubt there were Alternate Keys - we probably did it with Sorts).

Within the Index you had a bucket containing a group of PrimeKeys. I think it
functioned as follows. If there was space in the bucket then the new PrimeKey
was positioned in ascending sequence, shifting higher keys to the right.
(Obviously the key entry contained a reference to where the data record was on
another card). If there was insufficient room in the PrimeKey bucket then a
'Tag" record was allocated referencing the key, perhaps on an entirely new card.

So far as I can remember, actual records were added 'sequentially' in the data
area buckets.

There was a considerable amount of housekeeping done to get the indices and
records back into shape - plus the nightmare of failure - which made backups
ever present.

Now I know you can get books, and books, and..... on Indexing, Merging and
Sorting techniques - which is a very specialized area to the 'devoted'.

Question - just curious whether Indexing has moved dramatically from the above
model  ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-02-28T14:38:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net...

> I take offense to your insulting Mr. Stevens (and Unisys). He's been a
> fair-minded and HELPFUL poster here.

I'm glad you feel that way, Mr. Wagner.  But now that you bring it up:

It strikes me that your repeated characterization of Unisys' efforts to
conform to the applicable ANSI standards as being  far away from what you
deem as "standard practice" for COBOL users in general as well as for all
"mainframe types" in particular could be regarded as not a little insulting
to both Unisys and its many MCP-based-system customers.

That characterization also strikes me as not a little demeaning of my
personal efforts to further the cause of both fully-featured
standards-conformant compilers within Unisys and to encourage the practice
among our customers of writing ANSI-conforming code with careful
identification of any extensions in the programs.

And my efforts on INCITS/J4 (supported by Unisys, to the long-term benefit
of the COBOL user community) are motivated at least in part by a desire to
ensure that what is *truly* standard practice has been arrived at by formal
international agreement, as is the case for ISO/IEC 1989:2002 (and its
predecessors).  Unisys has some Really Neat extensions, and many of those
are VERY widely used among our customer base.  But I don't think very many
of our customers are so provincial in outlook as to deem such syntax
extensions as something everybody who's anybody does and no REAL programmer
would consider avoiding.  It seems to me you act as if the very idea of
offering Unisys users the same courtesy is beneath your dignity.

    -Chuck Stevens
```
